# Interaction between architecture and fine art

![](images/7fd507a2bd1f9bf2a6957047e6dd90b313a475cd101b660fea8eab2d838b22e6.jpg)

Leftist painting and new architecture

An important feature of the evolution of Soviet architecture during the first post-Revolutionary years was the extent to which new structural methods and materials exerted an influence on the output of painters, who sensed vast new aesthetic opportunities and the basis for a new style in simple geometic shapes devoid of decorative effects. It was from them, rather than from the adherents of Rational Architecture, that the Modernist architects absorbed these ideas. During the very earliest years of Soviet power, the new creative trend, which was being formulated as part of the fight against stylization, eclecticism and the Moderne style, took shape through close cooperation between architects and artists involved in the Leftist trends which had arisen before the First World War in opposition to official academic art.

A new style was crystallizing in European art as a result of a complex process which found its most intense expression at the frontier between figurative art and architecture. In the past, architecture had always been the determinant of stylistic change, but it lost this position during the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. This leading role was then for many years taken over by painting, which established tastes in art and aesthetic standards.

When the objective circumstances for the development of a new style materialized in the 1910s and 1920s, a discrepancy came to light between the state of fine art, on the one hand, and ofarchitecture together with applied art on the other. Architecture, industrial design and applied art were clearly lagging behind. Eclecticism and stylization prevailed, while innovation hardly ventured into the open. Yet, on a world scale, architecture was on the verge of an inevitable and radical break with accepted aesthetic values.

The attempt of the Moderne style to replace tectonic ornament by overtly decorative elements failed to satisfy architects. The Moderne approach helped many architects to grasp the futility of eclecticism and stylization. It promoted the integration of many technological achievements and methods of interior spatial organization, but failed to provide an aesthetic foundation for a new architecture and was never more than a transitional style.

A more radical revision of aesthetic principles was necessary. The stereotypes which had become established over the centuries needed to be swept away, so that the objective requirements of the 'new style', already integrated in many ways in the architecture of the early twentieth century, might receive artistic recognition from the architects themselves. Leftist painting supplied the requisite catalyst by accelerating the formulation of fresh aesthetic concepts in contemporary architecture.

New trends, such as Cubism, Suprematism, Purism, Neo-Plasticism and Cubo-Futurism, appeared from the turn of the century onwards, connected not so much with technical innovations involving only the figurative arts, but with the elaboration of new methods applicable to all spatial art. This trend in formal aesthetic experimentation continued to diverge more and more from the main line of Leftist painting. It also dispensed with certain basic features of figurative art and, as it were, prepared a 'new deal' for architecture and design, the artistic development of which had been retarded.

In the history of art as a whole, the last five years or so in Russian painting before the outbreak of the First World War stood out by the intensity, depth and diversity of the innovation involved. Soviet architecture could hardly have achieved such i forward in Russian painting.

## Mayakovsky: the cultural focus of the new art

Vladimir Mayakovsky was certainly the most significant figure of the new art in the early years of Soviet power. He left his mark on virtually all aspects of contemporary artistic activity, including architecture. He first joined the intellectually quite accessible Russian Futurist movement. When this split into a number of different groupings, such as Cubo-Futurism in Moscow and Ego-Futurism in Petersburg, he became one of the leaders of Cubo-Futurism, the supporters of which gathered about the Pervy zhurnal russkikh futuristov (First Journal of Russian Futurists) in 1914, among them Khlebnikov, Kamensky, the Burlyuk brothers, Sergei Tretyakov, Kruchenykh and others. Futurism was widely regarded as being largely for show, a matter of shocking the public, a destructive and negative trend. The Futurists themselves, on the other hand, believed that the rejection of the old order was no more than a stage in their artistic progress. As early as 1915, Mayakovsky wrote: 'We regard the first part of our programme - destruction  as completed. And so do not be surprised if you next see in our hands a builder's sketch rather than a jester's rattle.'1

Poets such as Mayakovsky, Khlebnikov, Kruchenykh, and others, indirectly influenced the solution of formal problems in the figurative arts and architecture by their approach to linguistic potential and an unprecedented freedom in the treatment of language. The Futurists did not merely regard language as an established system of communication, but intruded upon its ostensibly invulnerable foundations- syntax, for instance, and even the word itself. When they set out to reshape all the elements of poetic form, they considered it equally essential to reform the language itself, and rejected every suggestion that the latter was not susceptible to structural changes. By their freedom in the handling of language, and their experiments in the generation of new words in particular, the Futurists may be said to have removed all inhibitions restraining the creation of new forms in general. If poetry, working in a language that served the purposes of both literature and everyday communication, could successfully survive such change, then other forms of art with idioms of their own should all the more easily achieve such a transformation. This does much to explain the great influence of Cubo-Futurism on the new trends in the figurative arts in the pre-Revolutionary period, and on the Symbolist Romanticist experiments during the early Soviet era.

Mayakovsky's influence on Soviet art, including architecture, was not confined to purely aesthetic problems of form. Even before the onset of the Revolution, he was already linking a reform of the means of expression with the need to relate these to the new content of art. Thereafter, he shifted his emphasis to the subject matter and equated innovations in form with the changed role of art in the new social order.

The Futurists were probably frst among Leftist artists in attempting an approach to art based on its new social function. On 15 March 1918, the Gazeta futuristov (Futurists' Gazette) published 'Decree No. I concerning the Democratization of Art (Writings on Hoardings and Painting in Public Places)' signed by Mayakovsky, Kamensky and Burlyuk. They proclaimed the son'Allart orall he peole! called  r writers to 'seize their paintpots and use the brushes of their trade to illuminate and bedeck all the flanks, foreheads and b ailotrucks.roow , the izen will savour the profound thinking of his great contemporaries, contemplate the colourful brilliance of today's joyful beauty and listen everywhere to the music - melodious, thunderous or noisy - of superb composers.'

Two main lines of development may be identified in Leftist painting at the start of the century, one proceeding from Matisse, the other from Cézanne and Cubism. The divergences between the aesthetic principles underlying each of these appeared most clearly when figurative composition came to be rejected.

## Kandinsky: the concept of Monumental Art

Vasily Kandinsky, a distinguished representative of the first of these trends in Russia, sought to elucidate particular states of emotion through art and investigate the role of the subconscious in the artistic process. He set out to discover definite objective rules for deriving pictorial compositions from inner personal reality and subjective experience. Kandinsky studied the psychological perception of the means employed in various forms of art, in an attempt to synthesize a new and 'Monumental' or total art by bringing such effects into play, so as to produce kindred reactions among a widely varied public. Particular attention was paid to the link between painting and forms of expression primarily intended to convey individual emotional states, such as music, dancing and lyric poetry. Kandinsky was moved to combine painting with these other art forms in order to create mutually interchangeable means of expression capable of conveying a variety of moods within a single individual. He referred, for instance,to the movement of colour through space, the musical quality of colour, the interaction between non-representational form and colour.

As early as 1913, he wrote: 'The underlying identity of the means used by different forms of art ultimately becomes obvious and provides a basis for different attempts to stress and amplify the resonance of the special sound generated in a given art form by means of the identical sound available in another branch of art, thereby securing an extraordinarily powerful effect. . . . Every art tends to plumb its own depths and may be said to become specialized. Nevertheless, it also opens a door into a new world, the world which combines individual art forms within a single work, the world of Monumental Art."2

Kandinsky continued to develop his concept of Monumental Art throughout the period immediately following the Revolution. His interest in the special features and rules affecting the impact of works of art and in the application of new scientific and technological methods to the exploration of psychological and physiological perception of various art forms indirectly influenced the development of formal architectural innovations, especially those involving objective visual criteria.

The followers of the other main new trend sought their means of expression in the world about them, rather than within man's inner reality. Two approaches, fundamentally and explicitly opposed to each other, emerged from this, but both exerted a deep influence on twentieth-century architecture and design. One of these was linked with Kazimir Malevich's Suprematism, the other with the explorations of Vladimir Tatlin.

## Malevich:he sarch   path int te Lissitzky's Prouns

In his Suprematist compositions Malevich had tried ever since 1913 to free painting from anything that interfered with the direct effect of colour on the eye. He considered that colour and shape were closely related and set about enclosing patches of colour in simple and well-defined geometric figures on a white background. He believed not only that colour emphasized clear geometric shapes, but that form itselfinfluenced the effect of colour, so that, for instance, certain geometric shapes subjected a particular colour to maximum tension.

By the use of white backgrounds Malevich visually extended representation into depth, while integrating it into surrounding space. The geometric shapes on the picture surface seem to float in infinite space; dynamic tension is achieved by interrelating plane and colour. Suprematist painting with its characteristic relationships between colour and geometry promoted the development of a distinctive style in applied, decorative and monumental art, including textiles, posters and murals.

The further development of Malevich's Suprematism after the Revolution made geometric planes even more important as elements of pictorial composition, and colour was relegated to a secondary role. Colour-free dynamic Suprematist compositions began to appear in which the organization of space was based on geometric figures. By this stage Suprematism had virtually lost all connection with painting, as a result of its rejection of colour and its reduction to black and white planimetric figures. In the next stage, volumes and stereometric compositions pointed the way to Suprematist architecture. Volumetric Suprematism introduced novel architectonic relationships into formal aesthetic experiments, a field to which Lissitzky made an important contribution.

Lissitzky was a trained architect, and was among the first to grasp the significance of Malevich's experiments for the development of modern architecture. His output straddled architecture and the fine arts, and he was therefore able to apply to the new architecture those formal and aesthetic discoveries of the Leftist painters which could contribute to the formulation of a contemporary style. In 1919-21, Lissitzky created his Prouns (Proekty utverzdeniya novogo [Projects for the Affirmation of the New]), which were axonometric representations of variously shaped geometrical solids in a state of equilibrium, some standing out from a firm background, others seeming to soar into space. Lissitzky's Prouns were original models for a new architecture, expeiments inthe eneration orchitectonic forms, attmpts at new three-dimensional representations, a kind of compositional stock-piling for later use. Lissitzky deliberately stressed the architectural aspect ofhis projects. He saw Prouns as a staging-post on the journey from painting to architecture' and the e  h c   e fortuitous.

Later on, Lissitzky applied some of his Prouns to specific arcl scraper', a dwelling, and to exhibition displays.

The Arkitectons created by Malevich early in the 1920s heralded a new step in Suprematism's advance into architecture. These were by now real three-dimensional compositions, rough models of original architectural compositions in which horizontal and vertical parallelepipeds of different shapes and sizes coalesced or interpenetrated at right angles. Through three-dimensional Suprematism and the Arkitectons, Malevich actually came to behave as an architect in putting forward his Planits  the dwellings of the future.

Malevich's formal Suprematist experiments with geometrical figures anvolumes, icuding colour  ameans hivi ya ment of stylistic methods and forms in contemporary architecture and design. By the geometric purity of their abstraction

Part I/Aesthetic problems of design

Malevich's Suprematist compositions, his Planits and Arkitectons, acted much as a crystal does when immersed in a supersaturated solution. They precipitated the latent abstract tendencies in the Dutch De Stijl group, the German Bauhaus, and many Soviet architects, Ladovsky, Leonidov, Melnikov, Lissitzky, Ginzburg, Nikolsky, Ilya Golosov, Rudnev, Khidekel and Ivan Fomin among them.

As a painter, Malevich helped architects to see plain geometric shapes afresh and revealed the virtually inexhaustible opportunities for their combination in effective and complex spatial compositions. The juxtaposition of volumes in Malevich's architectonic compositions and Lissitzky's Prouns generated relationships and devices seldom or never before used for architectural purposes: the horizontal andvertical displacement of volumes relative to each other; overhangs of one volume over another; the siting of a large and bulky inchoate shape over smaller scattered ones; the soaring into space of a large volume supported only on a small area of its lower surface, and so on. A negation of symmetry, a fresh approach to gravity - with the visually 'weighty' above the apparently 'light'  the rich opportunities offered by light and shade, contrasting differences of scale, the constantly changing general spatial composition as an object is viewed in the round from every angle —all this gave an architect new means of achieving effects substantially different from thoseoftraditional architecture with its symmetry, its clearly defined facades, its upward lightening of the composition, its 'tectonic' decoration.

Malevich regarded his experiments as part of the search for a new language of art and saw his Arkitectons, for instance, as 'the Suprematist art of spatial construction'; as a species of 'Suprematist order'. He believed that after it had created new forms and new relations between them, Suprematism would break forth from the canvas and provide a new architecture. He wrote in 1928 that 'the whole path retraced by the new art in all ar, which is architecture'Malevich stressed that the processes of form-generation were connected with the aesthetic rule rn that vel for culdnot e ed 'without an aesthetic effect'.

Vladimir Tatlin, like Malevich, carried out formal aesthetic experiments on the borderline between painting and architecture. Malevich-was primarily interested in the opportunities provided by simple geometric forms and complex three-dimensional compositions, while Tatlin looked for aesthetic effect in the contrasting juxtaposition of different materials and new constructional shapes. In other words, both were concerned with aesthetic problems of form, but while Malevich aimed at the stylistic transformation of man's actual environment, Tatlin attempted to express an object's inner essence, implicit-as he thought - in the substance of its elementary form.

Tatlin and Early Constructivism

Gabo, Rodchenko, the Stenberg brothers,

## Medunetsky, Klutsis and others.

Tatlin was concerned with the properties of materials, such as their grain and their texture. He stressed the opportunities provided by a variety of materials in order to achieve a particular aesthetic effect.

His work moved beyond the bounds of painting. The saw, hammer, chisel and axe became his working implements alongside the brush. Applied colour increasingly gave way to the natural colours of metals and woods, and the 'picture' dealt with three-dimensional constructional problems rather than pictorial tasks. Tatlin's quest for the aesthetic potential ofactual materials led him to search out new paths whereby Leftist painting might move towards architecture.

Other artists were pursuing such a path simultaneously, though not always consciously. Miturich produced Spatial Painting as part of his experiments with Cubist compositions, Popova and Klyun produced pictorial compositions comprising three-dimensional elements, Bruny engaged in 'the pictorial treatment of materials'.

Tatlin's experiments not only taught artists to work in real space with materials quite unknown to painting, but also made them aware of the artistic potential of engineering structures. They became attracted to technology by the accuracy and efficiency of these structures, the unfamiliar spatial relationships involved and their rational utilization of materials.

Tatlin was among the first to make a deliberate transition from painting to the creation of a human environment. A tendency to switch from depiction of the world to construction of the environment had been evident in his work since post-Revolutionary years. In 1918-19, he taught in Moscow at the Svomas, where his experiments influenced young artists, such as Vladimir and Georgy Stenberg, Medunetsky and others. From 1919, he directed the workshop in the reconstructed Petrograd Academy of Arts under the slogan 'Construction, Volume and Material'. A bench, vice, anvil and assorted tools replaced palette, brushes and paints, and compositions assembled from metal, wood, mica and other materials were produced under Tatlin's guidance.

From Tatlin's point of view, these experiments with new materials and constructions were no mere formal investigations. To him, Constructivism was an organizational and formative principle, a fresh way oflooking at the world and its perception in three dimensions. Tatlin saw the creation of a new objective environment  a new perceptual world  as the ultimate object of his experiments. The method le called 'artistic production' would be extended to the design of all objects.

In 1919, Tatlin designed his Monument to the Third International, known as Tatlin's Tower, and in the autumn of 1920 he produced a model for it. Two young painters, Vinogradov and Meerzon, and a sculptor, Shapiro, helped with the detailed work involved. Both the latter eventually became architects.

Tatlin's Tower was planned as a vast, 400-metre-high framed structure in spiral form, housing three units of accommodation one above the other, which revolved at different speeds. The lowest of these, cube-shaped and turning at the rate of one revolution a year, was to house the International's conferences, congresses and legislative meetings. The middle unit, shaped as a pyramid, would revolve once a month and contained administrative and executive offices. The uppermost unit, a cylinder, would complete one revolution every twentyfour hours and act as an information centre.

The significance of this project in terms of innovation in Soviet architecture becomes obvious when it is compared with competition entries of the same year for the design of crematorium buildings in Moscow and Petrograd. These reflected that transitional stage in the emergence of new architecture in which the majority of architects still clung to their Classical heritage. Tatlin's project, on the other hand, enabled many architects to overcome the psychological barriers in determining the role which new constructional methods should play in shaping the stylistic characteristics of a modern building. In this respect, Tatlin's Tower was as significant for its period as the Eiffel Tower had been for the end of the nineteenth century.

A large number of young painters set about experimenting in this field under Tatlin's infuece.

Naum Gabo set out to produce volumetric, and then spatial constructions, and he and his brother, Anton Pevsner, announced in 1920, in their Realistic Manifesto, that they proposed to liberate volume from mass and that depth was the only pictorial and plastic form of space.

The Stenbergs and Medunetsky began their laboratory experiments at about the same time. They first produced colour constructions, derived from Tatlin's Counter-Reliefs, and then during 191920, volumetric constructions. These were openwork spatial compositions using metal angle brackets, T-, double-T- and U-section bars that included elements of glass, sheet iron, enamelled metal, brass pipes, drawn wire components and wood, the latter usually serving as a pedestal. The artists regarded this work as laboratory research in different forms of construction: as the process of inventing technically 'literate' and appropriate elements for constructive design.

This notion of laboratory work' was characteristic of those who supported the formal aesthetic experiments connected with Tatlin and the early Constructivists. All of them maintained that it is the engineer who produces new form', whereas to Spatial Suprematists, form was created through art. Constructivists contrasted their own spatial constructions with the hermetic Prouns and Arkitectons, and saw them as the quintessential expression of modern technological civilization.

Early Constructivist theory was reflected in a number of statements by the Stenberg brothers. It involved a form that was technically efficient in engineering terms, rather than an artist's invention. It meant space organized by means of an open structure, be it the linear sequence of rods in a girder or a lattice, rather than enclosed volumes. It meant a combination of frame and glazing rather than solid walls, all these devices being aimed at preserving the visual impression of undivided space.

Ioganson and Rodchenko experimented with spatial constructions at the same time as the Stenbergs and Medunetsky. Ioganson imparted a symbolic significance to his spatial compositions by basing all his constructional designs on a threedimensional cross. Rodchenko came to three-dimensional compositions by way of the Non-Objectivism - closely related to Suprematism - which he had proclaimed before the Revolution, In 1918, he produced a series of spatial constructions

Part I/Aesthetic problems of design

Chapter /Interaction between architecture and ine

made of paper, wood and metal representing basically symmetrical compositions set on pedestals or a flat surface; these could be collapsed and reassembled and were described by Rodchenko as 'non-objective' sculpture.

In 1920-21, Rodchenko produced a second series of original spatial compositions, without a specific upper or lower side, which might be seen as floating in space in a state of suspense. In these compositions, which Rodchenko described as conforming to 'the principle of identical forms', it was no longer a matter of a painter enthralled by the interaction of open spatial compositions, but of a constructor in search of new methods of deg  thei ma vailbe by the u of space. This second series contained the embryo of that new conception of 'transformable'furniture which was later aplied to domestic equipment and exhibition stands under Rodchenko's direction at the Metalworking Faculty of Vkhutemas. The theoretical importance of this second series of Rodchen-'s spatial construcions y in the method he used to escape from the picture surface into space. Malevich first depicted volumes in a plane and then transferred them into space. Tatlin may be said to have translated the illusion of plasticity in a Cubist picture into a three-dimensional object. The Stenbergs and Medunetsky first sketched out their constructions in a plane and then carried them out in space. Rodchenko, however, literally converted the picture surface into a three-dimensional composition without adding anything to it. The ease with wic the h   n b a plane stresses this. He took a plane of plywood or sheet metal with a specific geometrical shape such as a square, circle, hexagon, ellipse or triangle, and drew on it a series of regularly diminishing concentric figures, in accordance with 'the principl oenicl for He then   the urac o these lines and rotated the resulting figures in space to create three-dimensional compositions. These were shown, together with many spatial constructions by the Stenbergs, Medunetsky and Ioganson, in a separate room at a Moscow art exhibition in 1921.

During virtually the same period, in 1920-21, Rodchenko wooden rods as standard components.

GustavKlutsis produceanumberoconstructive composiand his designs for various street decorations in 1922 for the fifth anniversary of the October Revolution and for the Fourth Congress of the Comintern, among them amplifier stands, cinema screen frames, rostrums with screens, and kiosks. Klutsis was among the first to use extendable, unfolding, sectional and movable structural elements in these designs, and he devised a number of novel methods for varying the arrangement and dynamic impact of such spatial compositions.

Openwork metal constructions became increasingly popular among painters and sculptors for their artistic, as well as their technological, potential. Two designs by the sculptor Babichev early in the 1920s should be noted in this context. The first, a mobile sectional theatre, exploited the technical possibilities of a latticed structure, while the second incorporated two arched girders to impart a contemporary flavour to a city monument in a competition design for a memorial to Sverdlov in Moscow.

## From Leftist art to the new architecture

Thus the influence of experiments by Malevich, Tatlin and their followers reached out well beyond the realm of painting. The features identifying a new style common to painting, architecture and design probably first took clear shape in Malevich's and Tatlin's work, and they were the channels through which the formal achievements of Leftist art reached the archis.The explorations of these innovators decisively cossed the borderline between painting and architecture.

Suprematist compositions, Prouns, Arkitectons, Counter-Reliefs and spatial constructions - together with Mondrian's Neo-Plasticism and Le Corbusier's Purism - formed part of the mechanism whereby the baton of innovation passed from painting to architecture. They played a significant role in that interaction between art forms which was a characteristic feature of the early post-Revolutionary period. Within this interaction new forms of art, such as product design, came into being; formal methods were developed in previously unexplored arts such as the cinema and photography; existing art forms were enriched. Attempts were made to create fresh synthetic those underlying architecture, painting and sculpture), or by a fusion of time-regulated media such as music, dance and thea-i

For purposes of architecture, however, what mattered after the First World War and the October Revolution was the process whereby it took over the lead in formulating contemporary aesthetic ideas. This change took place in circumstances of close co-operation between painters and those architects who w  a e cumulated by Leftist painting during earlier decades.

The interaction that occurred between different art forms at that time affected not only experimentation, but the actual character of artistic associations. Collaborative groups of ar chitects and Leftist painters arose in France (Esprit Nouveau), in Holland (De Stijl) and in Germany (Bauhaus). Innovative architects co-operated just as actively with Leftist painters in the Soviet Union and this led to the creation of such unusual and multi-disciplinary organizations and institutions as Unovis, Zhivskulptarkh, Inkhuk and Vkhutemas.

## Unovis - 1919-22

Unovis, meaning Affirmers of the New Art, was an association of artists based on the Vitebsk Art School directed by Malevich, and it operated during the years 1919-22. Among its members were Lissitzky, Ermolaeva, Chashnik, Suetin, Khidekel, and Yudin. Suprematism was the Unovis creed, and the movement of Suprematism towards architecture was in its most intensive stage during this Vitebsk period, thanks chiefly to collaboration between Malevich as a painter and Lissitzky who, as an architect, taught building design there and was simultaneously producing his Prouns.

The students produced architectural compositions under Lissitzky's supervision, a notable one being the speaker's platform which Chashnik designed in 1920, on which Lissitzky based his Lenin Tribune of 1924. The interaction between Leftist painting and architecture, and the emergence of Suprematism into building and design, are vividly illustrated by the output of Unovis members. As an architect, Lissitzky radically altered his artistic approachunder the influence of Malevich's Suprematism but, reciprocally, he also influenced the formal experiments of Malevich and Unovis as a whole. The short period - 1919-20 - spent by Lissitzky at Vitebsk undoubtedly gave Malevich a better understanding of the architectonic implications of his Suprematist works, as wel as stimulating his interest and that of the Unovis students, such as Suetin, Khidekel, Chashnik and others, in architecture and design. Suprematism's shift towards architecture and design was already obvious in work of Unovis members before they left Vitebsk. It became even more marked when Malevich's group moved to Petrograd in the autumn of 1922 and went to work in the laboratory he directed at the local Ginkhuk, where he and his pupils produced the Planit and Arkitecton projects in the mid-1920s. Chashnik and Suetin immersed themselves in decorative and applied art, such as designing textiles and ceramics, and did exhibition design, while Khidekel continued his architectural training and made great use of Suprematist methods in his projects.

## Sinskulptarkh and Zhivskulptarkh - 191920

The Commission for the Resolution of Questions Bearing on the Synthesis of Sculpture and Architecture, known as Sinskulptarkh, was created in May 1919 and attached, initially, to the Subsection for Sculpture, then to that for Artistic Work, of the Fine Arts Department (Izo) of the People's Commissariat for Education. The Commission consisted of a sculptor, Korolev, who chaired it, and seven architects: Dombrovsky, Istselenov, Krinsky, Ladovsky, Raikh, Rukhlyadev and Fidman.

This was to be the first new association of avant-garde architects. Significantly, it did not arise under the aegis of the Architectural Art Department of the People's Commissariat, because that Department was headed by a dedicated Classicist, Zholtovsky. The young architects who disliked the general line then enforced by Zholtovsky setout to look for new ways ofgenerating form and rejected Classicism.

It was shared dislikes in the field of art, rather than common aims, which united the members of Sinskulptarkh from the start. They still did not see their way to a new architecture at all clearly, yet were convinced that the revival of Classicism could not provide a starting point for new departures, which they sought, instead, in the study of the formal achievements of Leftist art.

The Commission set itself the ultimate aim of synthesizing fined itself at the start to the resolution of problems arising from a fusion of sculpture with architecture. The actual experiments of the Commission were first directed towards the design and production of plans for a Temple of the New Cult - 'a building for communication among nations' — which was to be a new type of edifice intended for the performance of mass events.

At this stage the sculptor Korolev greatly influenced the experiments of the architects who made up the Commission. Korolev's own works were Cubist - the best known being the Bakunin monument in 1918 and the design for a monument to Marx in 1919 - and his experimental work was notable for its mixture of materials, such as metal with plaster of Paris.

Soon after the Revolution, Korolev also shifted his interests towards the boundaries of sculpture with architecture, and with the new field of design. He produced projects such as poster pillars and platforms for a variety of agitational purposes as well as domestic items like table lamps and ornamental combs. In his sculpture Korolev clearly displayed architectonic inclinations and a strong sense of the interaction of sculpture with its architectural setting. He invariably designed the pedestals for his monuments himself in order to establish an organic link with their environment, as for instance in his Monument to the Fighters of the Revolution in Saratov, of 1924-25, and the Lenin Monument in Tashkent, of 1933.

All this prompted a convergence of views between Korolev and the architects at that early stage and made him a central figure in 'passing the torch' to them from the arts. His influence on these architects bore first and foremost on such men as Ladovsky, the leader of Rationalism, and Ilya Golosov, the leading exponent of Symbolist Romanticism. Leftist art exerted its influence on these two men's work mainly through the intermediary of Cubist sculpture, as their artistic credos of the early 1920s clearly showed. This applies particularly to Ilya Golosov. He had not been a member of Sinskulptarkh, but was closely connected with the architects belonging to the Commission, shared their inclinations and enthusiastically identified himself, as they did, with the experiences and methods of Cubist sculpture. In December 1919, a group of sculptors, architects and students belonging to the Second Free State Art Studios founded a sculptural co-operative, which Ilya Golosov joined, together with the active members of Zhivskulptarkh (see below), Korolev, Ladovsky and Istselenov. Korolev led this cooperative, and under his direction the architects and students learned to apply the methods of plastic composition then current among Cubists.

Korolev's own work within Sinskulptarkh may also be regarded as an attempt to introduce sculptural methods into architecture, and even to impart to a building some distant association with sculpture of a generalized, usually Cubist kind, together with dynamic composition. There was a marked touch of anthropomorphism about these architectural designs, but a series of projects evidently dating from 1920 already point to a more clear-cut break by Korolev with a sculptural approach.

The influence of Cubist sculpture is obvious in the designs of the Sinskulptarkh architects during the Commission's early, 'plastic' period in 1919. In their search for new means of expression they drew upon the past; on the ways in which various geometric solids combine, and on visual associations with natural objects, such as rocks and plants. It was assumed that one of the distinguishing features of the new architecture should be pronounced dynamism in the composition.

Two painters, Rodchenko and Shevchenko, and then an arc  e9 ization changed its name to Zhivskulptarkh (Commission for the Synthesis of Painting, Sculpture and Architecture). The main emphasis during this second stage of the Commission's work, occupying the first half of the 1920s, was upon experimental designs for buildings with a new social function, such as the Housing Commune (Kommunaly dom) or the House of Soviets (Sovdep). The strongest influence from painting at this stage was that of Rodchenko.

When Rodchenko worked on the decoration of the Café Pitoresk in 1917, he was already attempting to apply Leftist painting methods to the design of a lamp. This would produce a transitional series of abstract compositions, which became the basis of sketches for the Café's lamps. In Zhivskulptarkh he was effectively repeating this process of escaping from Leftist painting by way of intermediary compositions, but now shifting into architecture rather than product design. Rodchenko first produceda whole serie ofabstracarchitectonicompositions, then based actual architectural projects upon them, to create a kiosk, a Sovdep, and so on. His output and that of Korolev vividly illustrate the way in which artists belonging to the Leftist trend sought their way out of Cubist and non-objective compositions into object design, and later into architecture — at the work of the architects in the roup.

The members of Zhivskulptarkh showed their work at an exhibition in Moscow during the summer of 1920; the Commission then dissolved and many of its members joined Inkhuk in the same year.

These interactions between fine art and architecture contributed to the emergence of new aesthetic concepts. But another and no less important aspect of this interaction involved the theory and practice of so-called Production Art. Industrial design activity was now emerging at the junction of architecture and fine art in order to create new consumer products.

Apart from their combined work on decorations for towns and public holidays, innovative architects and Production artists were also united by their Rationalist approach to the provision of an appropriate environment for the labour, leisure and everyday life of the working population. This activity was already reflected in Zhivskulptarkh and Unovis, but its two main and complementary centres were Inkhuk and Vkhutemas, both of which combined innovative architects and Constructivist painters. From these circles emerged the first designers, such as Tatlin, Rodchenko, Gan, Stepanova, the Stenbergs and Popova. It was there that the theory and principles of the two most influential movements in Soviet architecture of the 1920s - Rationalism and Constructivism - were elaborated amid arguments, experiments and debates.

## Inkhuk (192024)

The acronym Inkhuk stood for Moscow Institute of Artistic Culture. This was a unique association of creative individuals in Soviet art, bringing together painters, sculptors, architects, art historians and theoreticians of Production Art. It provided an important centre in which the concepts underlying Leftist tendencies in art were formulated.

Inkhuk had several dozen active members and was headed by an elected Presidium successively chaired by Kandinsky, Rodchenko, Brik, Arvatov and then Brik again, while Babichev, a member of the Presidium virtually throughout its existence, provided continuity. Lectures on general topics were read and discussed at plenary meetings of the Institute. Particularpint hy  eiwor wei er depth within individual sections and working parties. Inkhuk went through several stages of development during its short existence. Embittered arguments took place; some opinions gained the upper hand, while the supporters of others left the Institute; the general orientation of Inkhuk's work and its tasks changed; so did its membership; its structure and methods altered; and new people came to the top.

Once past the organizational stage, the first period of Inkhuk's activity-from May 1920 until the autumn of that yearwas concerned with the work of its then unique Section of Monumental Art, devoted to the pursuit of Kandinsky's concept of a synthetic Monumental Art. Attention focused on perceptual psychology and the mutual relation between means of expression proper to painting, poetry, dance and music.

Kandinsky's approach to problems of form-generation and questions concerning relations between the arts provoked protests from a number of Inkhuk members, most of whom had come from Zhivskulptarkh. Opposition to Kandinsky was headed by Rodchenko, whose supporters created the Working Group for Objective Analysis, consisting of Rodchenko himself, Babichev, Stepanova, Popova, Ladovsky and others, in November 1920, and tabled a proposal for a change in the orientation of the Institute's work.

Disregarding the more intricate contradictions involved and the finer shades of difference between a variety of opinions, it would be fair to say that the split in Inkhuk towards the end of 1920 occurred between two groups of Leftist painters with different views about the further evolution of non-representational art and the part it should play, both in the development of art as a whole and within ordinary life. Both sides believed that non-representational art should provide the basis for a new synthetic art. Kandinsky, however, saw the future of painting as involving its transformation through interaction with timerelated arts (music, dance and poetry) into a specifically dynamic form of expression in which colour, music, movement and speech would combine. Rodchenko, on the other hand, believed that painting would contribute to the creation of a new objective environment by interacting with the space-related arts (sculpture and architecture). When Kandinsky and a group of his followers left Inkhuk in January 1921, Rodchenko became its chairman.

The work of the Group for Objective Analysis followed a programme drawn up by Babichev. The Group's most important event was a discussion held in January—April 1921 on 'The analysis of the concepts of construction and composition and the dividing line between them'. Each participant was expected to contribute a thesis supporting his or her point of view, and if sition and construction as he or she saw them. This debate revealed fundamental disagreements between Rationalist architects and Constructivist painters concerning the approach to the generation of form. The first stressed the role of composition, the latter based form-generation on the manifesting of constructive rationality.

These differing approaches resulted, among other things, in the creation at the very start of 1921 of two new working groups. The first consisted of architects - Ladovsky, Dokuchaev, Krinsky, Efimov, Petrov, and others - the second of Constructivists - Gan, Ioganson, Medunetsky, Rodchenko, the Stenbergs and Stepanova.

Alexander Vesnin, the future leader of architectural Constructivism, at that time himself a member of Inkhuk, joined neither group, but in May 1921, entered the Working Group of Objectivists formed in April and comprising Drevin, Popova and Udaltsova.

Until the summer of 1921, these four working groups broadly covered the work done at Inkhuk. That autumn, further developments once more changed the Institute's orientation. Militant members of Lef and Production Art theorists headed by Brik joined Inkhuk, and sharply criticized the direction the Institute's work was taking. They branded it as Veshchizm (Thing-ism, or the Culture of Things), and regarded Constructivism as an intermediary stage between Veshchizm and Production Art. The proponents of Production Art summoned the painters to work in real production, effectively abolished Inkhuk's division into separate working groups, and concentrated all its activity on the plenary meetings. The main emphasis of Inkhuk's work on professional problems concerning form-generation shifted to sociological questions propounded in lectures by Arvatov, Brik, Kushner, Tarabukin and other Production Art theorists. From then on, working groups were set up mainly in order to promote Production Art and Constructivism bd he  Iu. It  ey r t that a student group for the architectural faculty of Vkhutemas was created by Inkhuk in February 1924, with Brik, Alexander Vesnin and Lavinsky representing Inkhuk while Barshch, Simbirtsev, Krasilnikov, Lavinskaya, Komarova and Semenova were among the students.

Throughout its activity Inkhuk was closely connected with a large number of artistic, educational and scientific organizations, such as Zhivskulptarkh, Obmokhu, Unovis, Lef, the Museum of Painterly Culture, the Workers' Preparatory Faculty (Rabfak) of the Arts, and others, some of whose members and contributors also belonged to Inkhuk. The latter thus became during the early 1920s a sort of doctrinal centre and debating club for like-minded creative artists and promoters of new trends in the figurative arts, architecture and the newly emerged field of product design. The aesthetic outlook of both leaders of the two most influential trends in Soviet architecture during the 1920s - Ladovsky for Rationalism and Alexander Vesnin for Constructivism - was in fact shaped in Inkhuk. It was also there that the first organizations representing these trends took shape — the Working Group of Architects in 1921 and the Vkhutemas Architectural Students' Group in 1924 - on the basis of which the main associations of creative architects, Asnova and Osa, were established in 1923 and 1925 respectively. Inkhuk acted first and foremost as a centre for the formulation of new theoretical concepts, many of which were subsequently embodied in experimental work at Vkhutemas.

## Vkhutemas and Vkhutein - 1920-30

In the first years after the Revolution all major architects with any claim to originality devoted a good deal of attention to higher educational establishments, where virtually all the new trends and schools were born in the 1920s. This was not a matter of chance. When the professional associations in Moscow and Petrograd renewed their activity after the Revolution, the prevalence within them of traditionally minded architects forced the innovators to turn to the young. Their leaders were all aware that the outcome of the struggle between them and the traditionalists would mainly depend upon the way in which the rising generation of architects had been trained. The battle for cntrol over higher rchitectural eucation became an issue of principle on which architecture's orientation in the immediate future would largely depend.

The student body, still open-minded and lacking any set models, became a target for anyone wishing to launch a new trend or found a school. That is why Zholtovsky, Ladovsky, Ivan Fomin, Ilya Golosov, Alexander Vesnin and other architects with a clear system of aesthetic views devoted so much time and trouble during this first period of development to teaching and regarded it as crucial in determining the future direction of Soviet architecture.

Art education throughout the country was totally reorganize in the autumn of 1.The variety of schools and colleges was replaced by Free State Art Studios, offering either a broad general curriculum or a specialized one in which the students were allowed to pick their tutors in the various subjects. Painters, architects and other types of artists all had the right to claim a studio of their own. The Stroganov Industrial Art College and the College of Painting, Sculpture and Architecture in Moscow were accordingly reorganized as the First and Second Free State Art Studios (SGKhM). Individual studios in the Second SGKhM were supervised by Zholtovsky, Shchusev and Rylsky. Methods of teaching were similar in all three studios and were laid down for each faculty by a single Curriculum Commission. Training began with study of the architectural orders and exercises on Classical subjects.

The first academic year in the Architectural Faculty of the Second SGKhM, 1918-19, passed reasonably peacefully, coinciding as it did with the Classicist period among Soviet architects. By the end of the second year, however, student ferment broke out in earnest. The Classical bias of the teaching now failed to satisfy many students, and an exhibition of designs by members of Zhivskulptarkh greatly contributed to this. Ladovsky's and Krinsky's projects attracted the young as exceptionally dynamic compositions, and as forms completely breaking with tradition and pointing the way to an architecture of the future. Balikhin, Mochalov, Krasilnikov, and others, organized an action group within the Architectural Faculty and called upon fellow students to reject Classicism. Leftist studios were set up in the autumn of 1920 as a combined venture by students both within Vkhutemas and outside it, in Zhivskulptarkh and elsewhere, in which two separate streams of protest against the imposition of Classicism were united. The development coincided with the fusion of the First and Second SGKhM into a single educational establishment - the Higher State Artistic Technical Studios(Vkhutemas), renamed

Higher State Artistic Technical Institute (Vkhutein) in 192730.

Vkhutemas was a novel type of educational establishment in which faculties of architecture, production (wood processing, metalwork, textiles, ceramics, polygraphy) and art- painting and sculpture -were combined.

The composition of the teaching staff in the Architectural Faculty of Vkhutemas reflected the intricacies of the struggle between the traditionalists and the innovators. Three centres gradually formed within it between 1920 and 1923, each with its own set of artistic and educational principles: the academic studios, with Zholtovsky as chairman of the Curriculum Commission, and Shchusev, Norvert, Kokorin, Rylsky, Leonid Vesnin among the teaching staff; the United Leftist Studios (Obmas), with Ladovsky, Krinsky and Dokuchaev; and the independent Studio of Experimental Architecture, with Ilya Golosov and Melnikov. Many students switched from the old studios to the newly established Leftist ones.

The two new centres established in the Architectural Faculty during the academic year 192021, with teaching methods of their own, signalled substantial changes in the creative mood within Vkhutemas.

These new, forward-looking workshops each required a Curriculum Commission of its own to lay down teaching methods and, consequently, the orientation of the work as a whole. In Obmas, Ladovsky's psychoanalytical method provided the basis for the course, with Krinsky and Dokuchaev acting as lecturers. The Studio of Experimental Architecture relied on Ilya Golosov's theory of the construction of architectural organisms, since Melnikov had only just completed his studies and started out as an independent architect.

Obmas achieved independence first, and the Vkhutemas Architectural Faculty was thus split into two sections, each with its own Curriculum Commission. Then, in November 1922, the Golosov and Melnikov workshop gained recognition (and a Curriculum Commission) as a third independent group within the Architectural Faculty. Both these workshops rejected traditional architectural forms, and the Classical orders in particular, but Ladovsky's approach differed sharply from that of Golosov in terms of the development of form. Ladovsky regarded space as the basic material of architecture and subordinated questions of volume to the organization of space. Golosov, on the other hand, gave priority in form-generation to elements of volume. These differences in approach between Ladovsky and Golosov also extended into their views on architectural composition, and therefore into their teaching methods.

Despite all the differences between them, however, Ladovsky and Golosov were united in their attitude to the basic rules governing design in architecture. Both of them gave precedence to aesthetic issues, so that abstract formal tasks predominated in the initial stages of tuition in order to induce a deeper study of the possible rules and methods of architectural composition.

New architecture gradually consolidated its position in Vkhutemas from the start of the 1920s onwards. The new trends even began to affect the academic studios. An independent Foundation Department was created in Vkhutemas for introductory courses in which architects taught space, painters taught colour and sculptors taught volume. Ladovsky's psychoanalytical method became the basis of the 'Space' discipline, while in Obmas students supervised studies under the direction of Krinsky, even though most of them (including Balikhin, Korzhev, Turkus and Lamtsov) had still not graduated.

New student ferment broke out in Vkhutemas during 1923-24, reflecting developments throughout the modernist architectural movement. The Rationalists had strengthened their position and confirmed it by establishing Asnova - the Association of New Architects — but in the meantime another innovative trend was emerging in the form of Constructivism. The common front against traditionalism was breaking down, and divisions began to appear within the new architecture itself. Constructivism and Production Art secured much support among Vkhutemas architectural students. In Vkhutemas, as well as in the Rabfak of the Arts, where the Izo section under Babichev was preparing students for entry into Vkhutemas, the fight was on, with the support of Inkhuk and Lef, for the inclusion of Production Art concepts into the curriculum, in the Industrial and Architectural Faculties in particular.

This struggle for new ideas was at its peak in the innovative tack on abstract formal classroom tasks. The adherents of Constructivism and Production Art demanded that the study of formal problems should be replace by work n pracicalones, with a real purpose in view. The Lef group organized byInkhuk in the Vkhutemas Architectural Faculty was joined by some of Ladovsky's and Ilya Golosov's students.

As a result of these sharpening demarcations within the new architecture, and the intensive growth of the new Constructivist trend, Alexander Vesnin's studio emerged as a new focus of ideas in the Vkhutemas Architectural Faculty during the school year 1924-25. Groups of students transferred to him from the academic studios as well as from those of Ladovsky and Ilya Golosov.

From the mid-1920s onwards, when Golosov went over to Constructivism, two main centres with programmes clearly supporting the new architecture held their ground in Vkhutemas-Vkhutein: Ladovsky's and Alexander Vesnin's studios. There was a sort of silent competition between the course and diploma projects of these two studios run by the leaders of the main current trends in Soviet architecture — respectively, of Rationalism and Constructivism.

Vkhutemas was not just an educational establishment. It provided one of the most important centres for the development of a new orientation in Soviet architecture. The Vkhutemas students' course and diploma projects received much publicity in the contemporary Soviet and foreign press, with the most interesting projects coming from the students of the Vesnins and Ladovsky. They contributed significantly to shaping the new architectural ideas, and it can safely be said that the role of Vkhutemas in shaping twentieth-century architecture equalled that of the Bauhaus in Germany.

By contrast with the organizations in existence before the Revolution and revived early in the 1920s, such as Mao, Poa, Oakh, etc, the new groups and organizations that came into being in higher educational establishments and other institutions were not narrowly vocational in character. The senior architects who joined them were linked by a common creative aspiration an  igle  thec priciple athr tha y narrowly professional interests. This was as true of the first essentially artistic associations formed in 191920, as it was of the purely architectural groupings which grew from the former during the first half of the decade.

The direction then taken by art as a whole was the main reason why these mixed organizations and groupings emerged at all, and it also influenced the entire character of the work done by the leaders of the period.

Regardless of their original training, Malevich, Tatlin, Lissitzky, Alexander Vesnin, Rodchenko, Lavinsky, the Stenbergs, Exter, Klutsis, Gan, Krinsky and many others practised painting, architecture, graphics, design and theatre art simulnu.Temult-ar  the s not simply a reflection of their multiple talents. It was the consequence of an era in which the features of a contemporary style were emerging through the interaction between various types of art. Arkitectons, Counter-Reliefs, Prouns and experimental three-dimensional constructions helped architects to assimilatnd make creativeue theformal nesthesvr ies of Leftist painting. It could be said that in these experimental creations painting was moving from the representation of objects to the shaping of them.

![](images/30ebad4d748f19e97ebf28ed64d467de5120da73d8c5b619c5ec80d3c3e0943c.jpg)

Symbolist Romanticism

The lack of any actual building work during the first years of Soviet power led architects to concentrate on laboratory experiments. An evolution took place within their thinking that produced few outward signs, but several fundamentally new approaches were fast taking shape at this stage.

Symbolist Romanticism was not a homogeneous movement with a body of theory and a stylistically specific set of compositional methods and artistic forms. Instead, it should be seen as an early stage in the search for a new image and fresh lines of development for Soviet architecture in opposition to the traditionalism of the Classicists. The revolutionary uplift of those early years, War Communism and the Civil War, created unusual conditions for artistic experimentation. The dynamism of public life increased intensely. The people became the masters of streets and squares, Demonstrations, meetings, assemblies and solemn Red Funerals required a new kind of presentation. The intensity of the class war also affected attitudes to objects, to fashion and to forms in art. When the workers rejected the previous way of life they often also did the same for its material manifestations, which they perceived as the symbolic embodiments of the past.

The outlook of the masses at the time favoured the promotion of ideas concerned with art which rejected traditional aesthetic principles. The surge of feeling in favour of disowning the old order, its socio-economic relations, its ethics and the mercantile spirit, also embraced the arts. Buildings and objects represented more than mere material assets which had to be handed over to the working people. They were seen as the embodiments of aesthetic ideals inherent in the dethroned classes of exploiters, and were therefore put aside, rejected and sometimes physically destroyed. Later on, when such associations had lost their immediacy, another psychological factor came to theforeand theheagof b theuiding be came the subject of reinterpretation.

Certain basic features were common to almost all shades and schools of Symbolist Romanticism: the rejection of traditional aesthetic principles prevailing before the Revolution; the determination to express the revolutionary surge of the masses and the dynamism o public life in the function ofbuildings, in their organization in terms of volume and space, and in their architectural form; the recourse to severe or ascetic forms with a view to creating a new system of aesthetics which would contrast with the 'effeminate' and 'pompous' architectural forms of the past; the inclusion in the aesthetic vocabulary of features suggesting a symbolic meaning or even a story, in other words, the incorporation of architecture into Agitational Art; a generous use of industrial and mechanical motifs as symbols of the industrial proletariat's labour; the deliberate introduction of quantitative factors into the aesthetic canon so that squares, esplanades, halls, monuments and structures, and so on, became physically vast; a clear bias in favour of the collective, non-individualized client. In its stylistic dimension, Symbolist Romanticism ranged widely, drawing equally upon archaic forms and on the compositions of Suprematism and Cubo-Futurism.

## Archaicizing trends

## within the new architectural concept

Although Classicism continued to be seen by architects as the embodiment of certain standards of professional proficiency, it was already widely regarded immediately after the Revolution as a stylistic system too obviously associated with pre-Revolutionary ideals. However, two trends attempted to draw on the architectural heritage for forms and compositional methods to create a new 'heroic' image. One of these used Classicism as a starting point, the other rejected it. The two archaicizing trends ultimately clashed in the large competitions held in 1919-20 for designs of a Workers' Palace and district public baths in Petrograd, and crematoria there and in Moscow. Interesting contributions were submitted from many stylistic directions, with Classicists such as Ivan Fomin among them.

Those architects who preferred not to use Classical orders resorted in their search of the past to building types like fortresses and medieval castles, which had romantic and heroic associations. The new 'Workers' Palaces', be they Palaces of Labour or of Culture, copied traditional palace forms in order to stress an inherently majestic quality, while allusions to fortresses relected the poweand irreversibility of theRevolution.

Typical of these projects were Belogrud's reinterpretations of Roman architecture in compositions for the 1919 Petrograd Workers' Palace competition, and for the Moscow Palace of Labour competition in 192223. The former, entitled Workers'Fortress' was particularly characteristic a ru ture where the power of its external features was intended to represent the Revolutionary masses' firm resolve. With its rusticated castle walls, crenellated towers and the general effect of an impregnable fortress, this original and well-proportioned spatial composition incorporates elements that evoke such diverse medieval buildings as the Castel Sant'Angelo in Rome and the Palazzo Vecchio in Florence.

Several other architects experimented with the same kind of archaic romanticism, Buryshkin and Langbard among them, as well as some members of Zhivskulptarkh.Istselenov's sketches display a determination to produce a new type of building not dictated by tradition, but it is equally obvious that none could be found and that he was forced to turn to images from the past in order to escape from present stereotypes. He experimented extensively with Gothic shapes which attracted him by their dynamic upward motion, their compositional intricacy and the angularity and spikiness of their lancet forms. The resulting compositions were Gothic in plan and elevation, but to varying degrees stripped ofornament. Yet despite all Istselenov's efforts to transfigure and denude traditional forms, their stylistic imprint still shows through in his sketches.

He was clearly aware of this and tried his hand at complex hybrid structures, which fused Gothic and Classical features, or both styles-jointly or separately  either with modern metal lattice structures, or with elements inspired by even more ancient models, vaguely reminiscent of Egyptian pyramids.

A Gothic origin is also perceptible in many of Vladimir Fidman's sketches at Zhivskulptarkh, even though such stylistic ingredients are simplified to the limit and transformed into Cubist forms, or even into vegetative ones that disturbingly resemble fantastic flowers. Fidman experimented in particular with vaulted demi-ogival arcades incorporated into horizontally flattened and multiple-storeyed compositions. He also sketched out new, intricately composed towers made up of juxtaposed or intersecting cylinders and half-cylinders, or of cylinders cut back along an inscribed spiral, etc, with overlying tiers pierced by demi-ogives.

Sigismund Dombrovsky's sketches also display a search for inspiration in various medieval prototypes, such as castles and fortresses. Usually he drastically simplified such forms, however, and related them more closely to natural shapes, such as mountains and crags.

Dynamic compositions:

## the influence of Cubo-Futurism

The extreme Left wing of Symbolist Romanticism consisted of painters and architects whose output was clearly influenced by Cubism, and by Cubo-Futurism in particular. Their works conveyed the visually volatile and unstable effect of restless dynamism. They mostly worked on the decorative treatment of urban spaces and saw it as their main task to achieve a maximum transformation of the 'bourgeois' urban scene through monumental art and small, quasi-architectural structures. The Cubo-Futurist commitment to a chaotic assemblage of random, dismembered and dynamic forms proved very much to the point, when these artists were expected to break up the existing façades of bourgeoisbuildings solely by decorative means. When they decorated streets and squares for public holidays they did their best not to stress the architectonic features of existing structures and complexes, and to destroy them visually by superimposing a totally fresh artistic conception. Sharp lines and bright spots of colour produced new and consciously destructive' compositions. In his design for the decoration of a viaduct in Moscow, for example, Vladimir Krinsky overlaid its rectangular spans with a 'collapsing' arch.

The minor, quasi-architectural structures were designed to contrast sharply with the former pattern of city building, so as to disrupt the regularities, to inject Revolutionary dynamism and passion into a tidy bourgeois conception. In 1921, for instance, a project by Krinsky inserted a speaker's platform between houses so s to disturb the existing lay-out and bring movement into the aspect of the street.

In those early days, when existing architecture was still linked with the past in many people's minds, painters and architects did their best with arts at their disposal to explode, wreck and destroy earlier architectural forms and make the old buildings unrecognizable. Natan Altman used 20,000 arshin (more than 15,000 yards) of canvas to alter the Palace Square for the celebration of the first anniversary of the October Revolution. In his project, the Alexander Column was flanked by a speaker's platform whose restless forms were calculated to 'destroy' this imperial monument, while other emperors' monuments were entirely hidden by decorations.

Cubist and Cubo-uturisinlunces arebviusin the ojects of Zhivskulptarkh members, even in those based on older styles. The assertion of one of the fundamental features of the new architecture - visible dynamism combined with the rejection of symmetry and right angles — led to the creation of complex and novel plans and spatial compositions. Dynamic designs incorporating shapes with destructively acute angles, diagonals, sheared surfaces, cantilevers, curved and fractured forms, the contrasting utilization of light, all formed part of the varied means of expression employed in projects such as the competition designs for a kiosk by Rodchenko and Krinsky, the experimental projects for the Temple of Communion Between Nations by Ladovsky, Krinsky and Rukhlyadev among others, for a communal house by Ladovsky and Krinsky, for a Sovdep by Rodchenko and Shevchenko.

Unlike Istselenov, Fidman and Dombrovsky, certain Zhivskulptarkh members such as Ladovsky and Krinsky consistently rejected traditional forms and methods. Every one of them, however, independently contributed to the common cause of creating dynamic compositions of unusual form.

Nikolai Ladovsky concentrated on experiments with a variety of geometric volumes - parallelepipeds, spheres, cylinders, cones, pyramids, and so forth— which he linked in complex and most unusual combinations. He was particularly attracted by a dynamic, upward-surging movement, and experimented widely in this area: with solids overlapping by a cantilever effect, gradually tapering towards the top, surmounted by a cone, a pyramid or a spire; or with intricate many-tiered compositions which seem to spiral into the sky.

Vladimir Krinsky made no attempt to impart any particular direction of movement to his dynamic compositions dating from this period. Some of his building designs seem to be blown apart by an inner force, as though seen just as an internal explosion was in progress: the foundations appear to be firmly set on the ground, while the upper parts look like debris projected upwards and outwards, or like a chaotic conglomeration of items. Even when using simple geometrical solids, Krinsky invariably tried to impart apparent instability to the composition as a whole: as, for example, with a cube-shaped building stood on edge.

Ladovsky and Krinsky might have been expected to interact mainly withLetist sculpture hrough Korol. Instead, the in fluence revealed in their projects and sketches of this period comes from painting. This is particularly true of Krinsky, whose graphic compositions, sketches and projects of 192021 clearly show the strength of Rodchenko's influence at this stage.

In his early non-objective compositions Rodchenko had made extensive use of the combination of straight lines with planes rotated through different angles. In his architectural designs of 1919-20 for a kiosk and a Sovdep, this vocabulary was extended into dynamic compositions involving planes and volumes together, with a variety of lines acting as supports and tension members.

## The symbolism of artistic forms and of the architectural image

In its various manifestations and techniques art usually carries some symbolic meaning. This may be consciously grasped, or alternatively not perceived, so that it merely forms part of a complex of associations and representational stereotypes. Every compositional system may be treated as a language, each 'letter' and "word' of which has a symbolic as well as an aesthetic meaning. With the passage of time, however, the balance between symbolic content and artistic expression gradually alters, both within architectural forms themselves, and in the perception of the beholder. The associations that caused a specific meaning to be linked to a given form become blurred and fade, so that symbolic meaning increasingly yields in importance to the style's purely aesthetic properties. But if the forms and methods of art built up during the course of centuries should come to be swept away by a new era, attention once again focuses sharply on the symbolic function of art. It is as though all development reverts to fundamentals, so that fresh images emerge which hark back to a primordial meaning of fr an n ol  rgle against outdated stereotypes.

Attempts were made in the early years of Soviet architecture to give back to symbolism its commanding role in shaping the concept of art. Architects tried to make the symbolic content of their concepts more readily accessible by resorting to thematic and quasi-representational elements, to purely external associations and visual clichés.

Thus in 1919, in their joint competiton entry for a cremator n Moscw, Krinsky nd Fian basedtheresn n
