Great efforts were made in the early post-Revolutionary years toevolve universallytelligiblebolicagui monmental rt nrcitecture, by mean implem ric shapes and colour. Painters and sculptors contributed a great deal to these endeavours. Such symbolic forms also represented one of the intermediary stages or channels used by Leftist painting to make the transition from representational expression, via symbolic forms, into the language of architecture. The work of many leading artists, Malevich and Tatlin among them, passed through a'symbolist' stage.

At Unovis, Malevich worked out a system of 'signs' aimed at closing the gap between representational and non-representational media. He allocated explicit symbolic meanings to abstract geometrical figures and colour. The square stood for the world as a whole, the circle for the earth's movement, the triangle for a higher force. Extended rectangles which he called Supremy symbolized the interaction of forces, with black, white and red representing, respectively, evil, good and revolution. A red square thus symbolized world revolution, and so on.

Such a symbolism of geometrical forms and colour was used by the members of Unovis in their Monument to the Victory of the Radiant New, in Vitebsk, a parable for the significance and meaning of the October Revolution. A square platform, symbolizing the world, was surmounted by a half-sphere, representing the globe, into which three white and red wedges were driven to symbolize the victory of the 'Radiant New'.

This symbolism of geometry and colour was also used to decorate the town as a whole and to change its inherited image, just as the Cubo-Futurist compositions of painters and sculptors had done earlier in Petrograd and Moscow. Whereas the latter,however, remained minor visual events amongst the mass of city buildings, the Vitebsk scheme was consistent in style throughout, and did genuinely alter the visual character of this much smaller city. 'A strange provincial town,' wrote Sergei Eisenstein in 1920. 'Red brick - like so many towns of the western provinces. Sooty and dismal. But this town is uncommonly strange. The main streets here are covered in white paint over the red bricks. Green circles straggle over the white background, orange squares, blue triangles. . . .' It was like a sort of 'Suprematist confetti scattered along the streets of this bedazzled town'.1

A symbolism of simple geometrical forms and colour was wlyuein Artur the yr iaelylo the Revolution, It affected the decoration of cities at public holidays, because of the opportunities these occasions offered for the use of Revolutionary symbols and emblems, as well as the design of posters and memorials. Lissitzky's famous poster Cleave the Whites with the Red Wedge, of 1919, was one of these. This form of symbolism became so ingrained that it was incorporated in monuments even by such architects as Ivan Fomin, Rudnev and Gegello, who for all other purposes used traditional forms in their designs.

Ivan Fomin, for example, designed a series of monuments clearly influenced by the symbolism of simple geometrical shapes and the aesthetics of Cubism and Cubo-Futurism. The titles of his competition designs speak for themselves: Cube, for example, was one project for a monument to the victims of the Revolution at Lesnoy in Petrograd, consisting of a massive cube supported by short round pillars, carrying a briefinscription. Displacement (Sdvig) was a monument to Sverdlov in Moscow, with simple geometric shapes, dynamically displaced, resting on a base consisting of broken classical fragments - an allegory of the old world's destruction. The widespread use of symbolism in architecture was due not only to the function of the structures concerned - monuments, memorials and palaces  but equally to the fact that the investment of symbolic meanings in the new forms hastened the rejection of the old, while helping to familiarize the masses with the new simple geometric shapes and opening their eyes to the aesthetic values involved.

Simple, static, geometric solids, such as the cube, the sphere, and the pyramid had already been much used in the past. Cubo-Futurism added the dynamism of displacements, diagonals and spirals, the instability of 'crumbling'compositions, the stress of cantilever effects and suchlike. The story told by the symbolic interpretation of an architectural form was regarded as so important in Agit Art terms that a unified formal symbolism was applied across the boundaries of competing stylistic concepts and was given priority over all other aesthetic considerations.

What first struck many spectators in Tatlin's Monument to the Third International was less the unaccustomed architectural devices, such as its armature or the use of glass, than the symbolism of the spiral, an allegorical representation of the Revolution's upsurge and drive. This design was often imitated: a spiralling composition was combined with traditional forms, as in Yakulov's project for a Monument to the 26 Commissars of Baku, or with understated and subdued shapes, as in Lyudvig's project in 1922—23 for the Palace of Labour in Moscow, or with openwork metal structures like Shchuko's project of 1924 for a Lenin Monument.

The applicatiòn to architecture of the narrative symbolism used at that early stage in Agitational Art also clearly reflected the tastes of the masses. During the Revolutionary years, they had become accustomed to bright, poster-like images with a distinctly symbolic use of shape and colour, and to the widespread use of emblematic forms. The numerous projects and proposals for the perpetuation of Lenin's memory are indicative in this sense, especially the amateur contributions submitted to the open competition for the design of the Lenin Mausoleum in 1925, almost all of which incorporate a symbolic meaning in the architectural conception.

An All-Union competition was also held that year in Odessa for the design of a combined Lenin monument and mausoleum to local victims of the Revolution. Here too, many of the submissions relied upon symbolic devices common at that time, such as displacements, dynamism, and spirals, while a number of contributions resorted to directly thematic symbolism. In the project entitled Mayak (Lighthouse), for instance, a tower seems to be spiralling upwards through a domed building of emphatically classical design, like the Pantheon in Rome.

The symbolism of simple geometric shapes, combined with displacement and dynamism derived from Cubo-Futurism, turned out to be no more than a passing attraction in so far as most architects were concerned. Many such forms and compositional methods gradually shed their symbolic content and merely became yet another means of expression used in the new architecture.

Symbolist Romanticism, however, acquired such importance in the output of certain architects that they founded a new school (1920-24) based upon an architectonic reinterpretation of the symbolism of simple geometric forms and colour.

Ilya Golosov's influence was decisive in the formation of this school. He progressed from a simplified form of Classicism in 191819, as in his designs for a hospital, a crematorium and a school, to the application of Symbolist Romanticism in

1920-23. In Golosov's mind the strong dynamism of these works expressed the heroic nature of the Revolution in architectural form. Tension, upsurge and struggle were conveyed here by the interaction of volumes; the architectural masses display lines of stress, and displaced volumes, acute angles and slanting lines are prominent.

In the early 1920s, Ilya Golosov propounded a new design approach in his thery of the structure o arcitectural anisms', devoted to the general rules governing the structuring of large forms. He supplemented this with a theory of visual dynamics (literally of 'movement') in architecture, which dealt with the practical methods of generating a dynamic composition. Golosov's theories served as a foundation for the tenets of an independent Symbolist Romanticist architectural school. It had its own theoretical principles, elaborated in Ilya Golosov's works; its compositional techniques; its specific teaching methods devised by Golosov and implemented by him in the Architectural Faculties of Vkhutemas and the Polytechnical Institute; and its distinct set of followers. A group of Golosov's pupils from both establishments joined him because they believed that his pioneering work would lead to the creation of a revolutionary architectural concept. From their enthusiastic experiments with new forms and compositional methods, we have a range of student projects by Shibaev, Fedor Andreev, Vegman, Khiger and others.

## llya Golosov: theories of the structure of architectural organisms and of visual dynamics

Although Golosov was dedicated to the search for new architectural forms, by far the most important aspect of his creed was its emphasis on the expressive combination of large forms or volumes, rather than on detailed stylistic innovation, as the basis of design. He was a master of massive form, and practically all his works, regardless of their individual stylistic character, aim to manifest the geometrical specificities of a form of this type which dominates the spatial composition in question and subordinates to itself all other forms and details.

Inhi theory  the sucturerhitecuralaniss, Golosov gave great importance to a clear distinction between 'mass' and form' He defined 'mass' as the primary threedimensional shape devoid of all content. Form', on the other hand, was dependent upon the particular content and manifested in that content. Architectural form was thus mass with specific meaning. It was the totality of mass plus idea- the idea being the architectural intention which had brought it into being. Any piece of architecture could therefore be regarded both as a mass expressing its own relative position in space, and a form bearing the imprint of a specific inner content.

Golosov differentiated between objective and subjective mass. He defined the latter as independent of the relative position of other masses connected with it. As he saw it, the subjective mass was a solid that not only supplied the nucleus of an architectural organism's general structure, but represented the compositional centre in which the entire spatial concept was focused. In architectural terms, its function was to subordinate to itself all other masses within the composition, while retaining its own independence in relation to them. Since the subjective mass provided the very centre of a composition and was the form carrying greatest expressive responsibility, its design required the closest attention. The presence or absence of harmony in the composition as a whole depended on how wel it was conceived.

An objective mass always remained subordinate to the subjective mass, and dependent upon it.

Golosov also attached great importance to identifying the 'motion inherent in masses' — which he saw as a function of the geometrical properties, and as one of the starting points of the compositional process. He was particularly concerned with asymmetrical volumes, which in his view interacted with each other more intensively than symmetrical ones. The shape of a volume grafted on to one of these, and the position of the graft, were far less constrained than in the case of symmetrical, fully balanced volumes; and this fact further stimulated Golosov's interest in the 'motion inherent in masses'.

He introduced, moreover, the concept of the 'line of attraction', according to which every organism carried a built-in force consisting of the 'balanced tension between all the forces within an organism', and causing that organism to gravitate in a given direction. It was these lines of attraction which held individual architectural elements together as an integrated composition.Golosov distinguished between lines ofattraction that were 'active' or vertical, and those that were 'passive' or horizontal. The development of an architectural composition was based on such lines, which constituted the intangible compositional framework perceived by the designer, and which bonded together and unified the organism visually. The mark of a true artist was the ability to sense the lines of attraction and use them to establish a harmonious composition.

Golosov and his pupils used these principles extensively during the early 1920s, in dynamic compositions which vividly demonstrated their intensive pursuit of ways to express the 'movement of masses' and their attempts to manifest the close interaction of mass and space, both horizontally and vertically.

According to Golosov's theory, lines of attraction did not necessarily imply dynamism in a composition. They were merely a tool for revealing the possible roles of a given three-dimensional element within the general composition of an architectural organism. As such they could provide the architect with material for a variety of compositions: both static, with the lines of gravitation of individual elements balancing each other; and dynamic, in which the resultant of the lines of gravitation is clearly - even though notionally - located outside the architectural organism itself.

The 'principle of the motion ofarchitectural masses' became one of the most important features in Golosov's design theory during the early 1920s. He therefore supplemented his general theory of the construction of architectural masses by a special theory concerning movement in architecture. He connected the elucidation of movement in architectural compositions with the task of reflecting the dynamic character of an era, and thus naturally attributed particular importance to expressing this notion of movement in the design of large public buildings. In 1920, he produced a project for a city bakery in which the horizontal movement of the arches in the roof of the transport shed sweeps over into the spiral of the store and through it into a lvo

In his project for a radio station dating from 1921, the building seems to grow stepwise out of the ground as its moves towards the aerial mast, where the horizontal movement is arrested well beyond the confines of the building and switched to the vertical axis of the free-standing mast.

During the same year, Golosov also produced two versions of a project for an observatory, the main element in both being a vertical cylinder topped by a dome. In one variant, the horizontal movement developed within a complex stepped composition of prisms is halted at the tower and redirected vertically through two semicircular annexes, which oppose the horizontal movement and focus its lines offorce along the axis of the y-. I ral principle.

These early projects by Golosov, carried out in a spirit of Symbolist Romanticism, incorporate bulging volumes, slanting lines, clashes between horizontal and vertical movements, the dynamism of spirals and many other ways of generating a dynamic composition. The displacement of volumes in the plan features rarely. This is equally true of some later designs, in which a spatial composition conceived on a straightforward or even a strictly symmetrical plan is locked into position by a structural element which in itself is complex and restless - as in the project for a forge and pump house of 1921  or, alternatively, in which a rhythmic series of equally pitched roofs with an angular, saw-toothed outline sets up an accelerating horizontal movement within the composition as a whole, as in the project for the Ostankino stud in 1922.

1923-24, experimentation in colour became characteristic of many Golosov projects, notably the Far Eastern pavilion at the 1923 Agricultural Exhibition, the competition projects for the Soviet pavilion at the 1925 Paris Exhibition and the Lenin House of the People at Ivanovo-Voznesensk in 1924. He did use triangular projections in 1924 in two competition designs - for the Leningradskaya Pravda newspaper's Moscow office and the Arkos joint-stock company building - but these were no more than bay fronts, rather than volumes set at an angle on a rectangular plan.

spatial compositions by the followers of both schools. The Symbolist Romanticists, however, developed their own concept through the symbolism and dynamism of form; attributed a meaning to form as such; and introduced certain narrative and depictive devices. Their formal and aesthetic experiments were conducted on the borderline dividing architecture from sculpture, and they treated every structure as being akin to a monument. The Rationalists, on the other hand, sought to display the aestheic potential of the form itselfin the spatial compositions which they created; they kept to a strictly architectural approach in the means of expression and compositional methods which they employed.

![](images/47481d3d9b170bb9386be1c4f8934067f270c45d5595da4f22410778cb365fc7.jpg)

The problem of new form

During periods of transition, two requirements become paramount in art, and that includes architecture. They are the requirements for new means of expression and for a fresh aesthetic image or concept. It is on these problems that the innovative architects have always concentrated their energies. Those of them who have achieved a significant measure of success in this field have also been those who exerted the greatest influence on the development of new schools and trends.

The whole tone of Soviet architecture was set by the intensive formal experimentation which followed immediately upon the Revolution. The innovators were well aware that specific problems of function and structure, and questions concerning the new aesthetic imagery, could not be answered without mastery of the new formal possibilities. The rejection of eclecticism and stylization clearly led to the abandonment of traditional architectural forms, but new formal and compositional methods to replace them were still only in the making. As a result, projects and buildings remained primitively unprofessional, since no amount of progressive social attitude and new artistic conception could overcome these architects' lack of formal and compositional resources. Of all the aspects of the design process - the structural and functional aspects, the compositional aspects and the problems of ultimate aesthetic image — it was the issue of architectural form which proved to be the weakest link in the chain. Its retarded development held back the whole evolution of architecture as an art.

Two problems involving architectural form gave rise to special difficulties: the relationship of such form to the objective rules governing individual perception, on the one hand, and its relationship to the new functional and constructional bases of a building on the other. These were the problems which engrossed followers of the two main innovative trends in Soviet architecture in the 1920s: the Rationalists and the Constructivists. In practice, despite the doctrinal and artistic disputes that divided them, these two trends were engaged in a common task. But while the Rationalists approached form in terms of the objective laws of perception, the Constructivists approached it through the structural and functional aspect of building design: they were, so to speak, digging the same tunnel from opposite ends.

The evolution ofRationalism was protractedand fullofcontradictions. At the time when the doctrinal and artistic principles of this trend gradually took shape in 191822, its adherents were much influenced by Zholtovsky's teachings, by Cubo-Futurism, Symbolist Romanticism, experimental Suprematism and the ventures of Constructivist painters, as well as by Kandinsky's psychological theories. Nikolai Ladovsky became the acknowledged aesthetic leader, the theoretician and organizer of this Rationalist trend.

## Ladovsky, the leader of Rationalism

Apart from his role in the Rationalist movement, Ladovsky's name is also connected with the reform of artistic aspects of architectural education and with the development of an original town-planning concept. He attended a course of lectures by Zholtovsky immediately after the Revolution, at the same time as closely following current experiments in the figurative arts and establishing artistic contacts with Leftist painters and sculptors.

A working group of like-minded artists gradually formed around him, first in Zhivskulptarkh in 1919-20, then from the end of 1920 in Inkhuk's Working Group for Objective Analysis, and thereafter, from 1921, in its Working Group of Architects, as well as in Obmas from 1920 to 1923. These were the people who in 1923 would form the Association of New Architects  Asnova.

While still working in Zhivskulptarkh, Ladovsky was not only producing experimental projects but was already drawing up a set of theoretical tenets for Rationalism. He accorded great importance to modern technology, but regarded its new materials and structural methods as more significant for what they could contribute to spatial organizationthan or thersthetic potential.

The Zhivskulptarkh architects saw the creation of a new, synhert ba  riture, pig n sculptu in terms of the struggle for an architecture that would employ to tell hyclour, while rejecting stylization and eclecticism.

In the course of the debates at Zhivskulptark during 1919, on waysformulating the newiage  building, Lasy always stressed the role played in architecture by space. He pointed out that though form may be abstract and independent mi pnhe aos ex function ofthe material employed. When Ladovsky underlined the significance of abstract form in aesthetic experiments, he also emphasized that it was engineering which could endow such new form with material substance. 'We must look,' he said, 'at the latest technological forms; they can be borrowed and reworked because they have not so far been developed in decorative respects.'

He believed that the look of a building should not be solely determined by its function, and that the task of design involved more than a mere external reflection of its internal purpose; it also involved the creation of an image. The new architecture, as he saw it, would be marked by the use of simple solid forms without superimposed decoration, and he therefore stressed the need to evolve methods whereby the geometrical characteristics of each solid could be highlighted by architectural means.

Yet, in this context, Ladovsky did not merely call for the exclusive use of simple geometric compositions, but maintained that the architect should be able to stimulate the spectator's imagination through the conjunction of clearly intelligible forms.

In his arguments against the early Constructivists, who stressed the primacy of structure and materials, Ladovsky claimed that the most important factor in the generation of form was space. In April 1921, the Working Group of Architects in Inkhuk, which was headed by Ladovsky and included Krinsky, Dokuchaev, Alexander Petrov and Alexander Efimov, put forward a programme which covered such basic principles of Rationalism as greater attention to problems of space and the psychology of perception.

The basic factors governing the generation of form were listed in this programme in order, as Space - Form - Construction, a reflection of Ladovsky's belief that construction plays a subordinate part in this process. He stressed the priority of spatial considerations for the architect over questions of artistic potential.

The quest for a new artistic concept greatly concerned him. He used the latest building materials extensively in this connection, and accorded great importance to the pursuit of objective laws for composing architectural form. It was essential that there should be a scientific study of the psychological and physiological principles governing individual perception of architectural form, space and colour. Rational aesthetics and rational architecture - 'ratio-architecture', as he put it - were primarily the application of objective criteria, derived from scientific research, to questions of art in architectural design. In Ladovsky's view, Constructivists paid too much attention to the technological and functional efficiency of architectural form, while he and the Rationalists argued additionally for the need to elicit objective perceptual, psychological and physiological criteria.

The task, as Ladovsky defined it, was to discover not merely emotional but also rational principles for the artistic aspects of architecture, so as to enable an architect to apply objective perceptual criteria when he designed a building.

He called for research into new architectural principles and solutions, as well as a more direct individual involvement in this task. Creative imagination had a tremendous part to play in generating genuinely innovative architectural ideas, and Ladovsky therefore regarded it as a main responsibility of architectural schools to elicit and develop an understanding of space and volume among future architects.

## The psychoanalytical teaching method

By 1920, when he was engaged in the reform of artistic aspects of the curriculum in the Architectural Faculty of Vkhutemas, Ladovsky had already worked out a new, so-called psychoanalytical method of teaching. He put forward a new system of teaching architectural composition, devised compositional tasks for the construction of large spatial forms and introduced the practice of making students prepare models for their compositional exercises.

The object of the psychoanalytical method was to develop in students from their very earliest days at a higher educational establishment the spatial outlook required for architectural work. Ladovsky suggested that particular attention should be paid at the early stages of the students' education to the study of artistic compositional criteria based on actual psychological and physiological perception. They became familiar with the basic varieties of spatial forms, surface, volume, confined space (i.e. interiors), as well as with the means of architectural composition, such as proportion, rhythm, colour and dynamics.

Part I/Aesthetic problems of design

The students were set problems involving elucidation of the geometrical features of shapes; their physical and mechanical properties, such as mass and weight, mass and balance, etc; the identification of dynamism, rhythm, relationships and proportions, the description of volume and space, and so forth. The work set on each theme consisted of a twofold sequence, a first, 'abstract' part, in the form of a compositional task, the second 'productional'  the solution of the same compositional problem, but as part of the design of an actual object. In the tasks set by the course, the complexity of the spatial forms involved gradually increased, as did that of their environment and of their perception by an outside observer. In addition, more and more intricate compositional and functional demands were stated in the 'production' element of each task.

The main difference between the psychoanalytical method based on Ladovsky's set of principles, and traditional teaching lay in a different approach to the role of space and to problems of perception. In accordance with his views, Ladovsky taught his students that space was the basic material of architecture, rather than the structural elements. He also shifted the emphasis from the standard study of proportions and the hierarchy of architectural forms to criteria derived from individual perception.

One of the features peculiar to this system was its insistence onproblem-solving. Ladovsky did not cram his pupils, or needlessly intervene in the details of their design work, but invariably added some extremely complicated problem -or even a conundrum - to each set of abstract and production tasks. Regardless of actual content, the tasks he set his students radically chaned theattde to proect workand kept them in a state of creative tension throughout the process of design.

He knew how to draw out the very best in his students by setting them abstract and production problems which demanded for their solution the use of all the potential and talent at their disposal. His students have vivid memories of the brain-teasers he set, which nagged at them as, again and again, they went over the countless possible solutions. When outlining an abstract problem bearing on the determination of mass and weight, for instance, Ladovsky suggested that the solution must take account of the complex relationships between mass, weight and volume. In terms of architectural perception, weight was usually linked to size and also to mass, but only if the latter was obvious, since mass was not always linked to such factors as size. Even such a purely preliminary analysis disclosed the full complexity of what had appeared straight forward at first sight.

Many of Ladovsky's abstract problems lay on the borderline between the compositional and the technological, Students tended to shun compositional solutions and preferred the easier, technological path. In problems concerning mass and weight, for example, they would seek to demonstrate the destruction of form by mass and weight. Ladovsky would sharply criticize such evasions and would demand instead the properly architectural spatial solution of a spatial task, rather than a mere illustration of the effect of physical forces.

The solutions produced in Obmas often went to the limit of existing technology. But even though Ladovsky encouraged the quest for original, or even extreme spatial solutions, he was v  he   w technically sound.

The staff of Obmas included a consultant engineer, V.V. Kuzmin, who at Ladovsky's request carefully checked all designs and analysed them with the students from the technical and constructional point of view. Yet Ladovsky never insisted on simplification of form until all possible constructional solutions had been exhausted, and he himself often put forward solutions of great technical virtuosity.

Ladovsky tested the full psychoanalytical method on several groups of selected Obmas students. However, when the Vkhutemas Basic Course achieved independent status as the Basic Section, headed by Krinsky with Balikhin, Turkus, Korzhev, Lamtsov, Glagolev and Spassky, the first graduates and students of Obmas, as his assistants, the psychoanalytic method became the basis of teaching in the 'Space'discipline, which was common to all faculties.

Although 'Space' was taught on the basis of Ladovsky's method, members of the Basic Section's staf, especially Viktor Balikhin, gradually introduced into it additions and corrections of their own.
