![](images/e4a2ce2fb080d4e9f04927ea3ca350a4e57d1cc6f534fdf0ee84fe5cd6916b0d.jpg)

and he complemented each other well: the former a born theoretician and profoundly rational thinker, and the latter endowed with a vividly emotional approach to architecture.

In 1919-20, Krinsky was still producing distinctive designs displaying great originality in spatial composition and full of restless dynamism. By the early 1920s, he gradually shed Cubo-Futurist influence in his formal aesthetic experiments. His compositions were now increasingly aimed at expressive spatial solutions achieved by the greatest possible use of new building technology.

During this period he contributed to the formulation of a doctrine for Rationalism. In a lecture read in May 1921 to the Inkhuk Working Group of Architects on The Path of Architecture', he stressed the impact of rationality on the evolution of modern architecture, but also called for the independent study of architectural form. In December of that year he drew up a statement of his personal beliefs in which he developed these ideas. In 1922-23, Krinsky designed a skyscraper to test this theoretical concept, and it is significant that successive sketches display increasing restraint in composition and shape. The dynamic emphasis in the silhouette of the building gradually faded, and diagonal members of the armature were replaced by right-angles.

This progressive rationalization of artistic means and methods is llustrated in the projects carried out by Krinsky during the 1920s and at the start of the 1930s: the Palace of Labour in Moscow, Arkos, the Soviet pavilion at the Paris Exhibition, the Lenin House of the People at Ivanovo-Voznesensk, the House of the Soviets at Bryansk, the Columbus Monument in Santo Domingo (the last three in collaboration with Alexei Rukhlyadev), the Moscow sports stadium and the Palace of Soviets in Moscow.

These projects show that by the first half of the 1920s Krinsky had already rejected many of the methods characteristic of his earlier work. In his search for an effective architectural image, he boldly displayed ferro-concrete frameworks, contrasting juxtapositions of glazed and blind surfaces, colour and the symbolism of new emblems. At the end of the decade he tended to make increasing use of the centred and symmetrical compositional modes of Classicism, combined with a widespread application of modern types of construction and new architectural forms.

## Asnova

The Rationalists set up their own organization - the Association of New Architects (Asnova) - in July 1923. The founders of this first innovative union of Soviet architects, derived from Zhivskulptarkh and the Working Group of Architects in Inkhuk and Obmas, were Ladovsky, Dokuchaev, Krinsky, Rukhlyadev, Efimov,Fidman, Balikhin and Mochalov, together with two engineers, Loleit and Kuzmin. According to its statutes, Asnova's activities were confined to Moscow.

Ladovsky was Asnova's ideologist, its guide in matters of art, its organizer and leader, while Konstantin Melnikov became one of the most influential architects affiliated to it. It was the revival of the Moscow Architectural Society -Mao-founded before the Revolution and now gradually taking over the control of architectural competitions, which impelled the Rationalists to form an association of their own. As one might have expected, competition juries mostly consisted of Mao members who were often highly traditionalist. In 1922-23, Mao had been put in charge of the competition for a Moscow Palace of Labour, and the Rationalists, then mainly located in the Inkhuk Working Group of Architects and Obmas, regarded the jury as incapable of assessing innovative projects objectively and refused to enter the competition. Although a number of Rationalists, Krinsky and Lamtsov among them, had already done some sketches, the majority decided that no one should take part and everybody complied, except for Melnikov, who was not formally a Rationalist at that time.

As a result, Rationalism, the strongest innovative trend of that period, remained wholly unrepresented in this competition, which was so important for the development of new approaches in Soviet architecture. Traditionalism was countered only by projects emanating from the various schools of Symbolist Romanticism, submitted by Ilya Golosov, Lyudvig, Belogrud and others, and the recently emerging Constructivists, represented by the Vesnin brothers.

The task set out by Ladovsky for Asnova was to make a place in the community for new architecture and to establish a centre for creative experimentation in opposition to Mao. It was proposed gradually to attract all innovative architects to Asnova, to branch out into various fields of specialized architecture, to organize publications and to take over the competitions.

Asnova held general meetings and organized sections for the design of housing and of transport buildings, for architectural theory, town planning and so on, which all went into action. Copy was also prepared for an Association periodical.

Asnova, however, failed to become a single centre for the new architecture because of the way things developed in the Soviet Union. Rationalism proved to be only one of the more important innovative trends of that period. It largely took over the minor Symbolist Romanticist schools, but Constructivism, which was taking shape at about the same time, was in direct opposition to Rationalism in its approach to form-generation. The union set up in 1925 by the Constructivists - Osa - was joined by a number of Asnova members, and some others had left for the newly founded Vopra organization by the end of the 1920s.

Difficulties arose over publicity for Rationalist concepts and for Asnova itself in the press. The Rationalists failed to publish their own periodical. When the Constructivists' journal Sovremennaya arkhitektura (Contemporary Architectura)  SA  appeared in 1926, in which Rationalist work was not mentioned, Osa gained a clear advantage over Asnova in terms of publicity.

Asnova material mainly appeared in the oficially published prdical StroteltoMoskyoscowBilingnStrote promyshlennost (The Building Industry). Many Rationalist student projects produced in Vkhutemas were published in the school's own bulletin of 1927 entitledArkhitektura Vkhutemasa (Vkhutemas Architecture).

Lissitzky did much to disseminate the ideas of Asnova, both in the USSR and abroad, where he had been working when the Association was founded. He acted as its appointed external representative, kept in close touch with Ladovsky and published a number of Rationalist projects in ABC and other journals.

When Lissitzky returned to Moscow, he and Ladovsky tried to arrange for the publication of a regular Asnova bulletin to be called Izvestiya Asnova (Asnova News), but succeeded only in bringing out a single issue, dated 1926. This, however, contains the most detailed statement available of Asnova's theoretical positions, above all in two articles by Ladovsky.

The Rationalists pointed out the importance of objective psychological and physiological criteria in dealing with questions of formal aesthetics. They gave priority to the quest for a new architectural aesthetic, and for new kinds of dwellings and public buildings to celebrate the great and splendid changes taking place in Soviet society. The professional problems - of both theory and practice—discussed in Asnova documents and declarations were usually linked to newly arising social requirements. Asnova was a fairly small artistic organization numbering only thirty-five members, centred on Vkhutemas for purposes of collaboration among students and teachers, as well as for scientific debate.

The Rationalists were the first to compete with the traditionalists for the support of the rising generation of architects, and were therefore more closely involved in teaching work than members of other innovative trends. Virtually the entire strength of Asnova was engaged in academic work at Vkhutemas. The Rationalists gradually extended their activities within it and involved recent Vkhutemas graduates as well. As a result, Rationalists set a general direction for Vkhutemas training in various ways, especially in the Basic Section, and gained the upper hand there.

Despite its considerable influence on the development of the new architectural movement in the Soviet Union, the special circumstances prevailing in the debate about art during the years immediately following the Revolution led to Rationalism being underrepresented in practical work during the first half of the 1920s. The activities of Asnova members also contributed to this situation. They were mainly concerned at that time with problems of methodology and form-generation, with devising new types of buildings and a new concept for architecture. They could best formulate and explore these questions in Vkhutemas, where Obmas' Curriculum Commission provided full scope for a variety of experiments.

That is why such theoretical investigations were primarily bult intohour  themo tir w treated as part of the solution of 'abstract' and 'production' tasks. Experiments connected with new types of buildings, new spatial composition and the development of a new architectural aesthetic were conducted with great gusto by Ladovsky in his Vkhutemas studio and involved several students working together on the same set subject. Themes thoroughly explored under his supervision included an administrative skyscraper, a new kind of workers' dwelling and new types of industrial buildings such as an aircraft factory. Such work continued in Ladovsky's studitroughout the sond halofthe 120s.The resulting projects were therefore not merely training tasks, but also the experimental formulation of theoretical problems, and thus no less important for the development of Soviet architecture than projects intended for competition purposes or direct commissions. Indeed, major projects were often more broadly s theoretical solutions in such training tasks. When we later come to examine the various problems that faced Soviet architecture we shal therefore frequently refer back to projects carried out in Vkhutemas under Ladovsky's guidance.

## The two centres of Rationalism around Ladovsky and Balikhin

After the establishment of the 'Space' discipline in the Basic Section, two theoretical, methodological and organizational groupings developed within Asnova, one of which included the teaching staff of the Basic Section, while the other comprised Ladovsky's students. Gradually the outlook of these two groups came to diverge. Viktor Balikhin acted as ideologist for the new Rationalist grouping. He regarded Ladovsky's attitude to problems of art as excessively rationalistic and, although he accepted his general principles, believed that his theory needed to be supplemented by resorting to art. Whereas Ladovsky put the objective geometric properties of space and form first in his teaching, Balikhin stressed their artistic features.

Ladovsky proceeded from the principle of rationality in design and therefore concentrated on the organization of space and volume and on the form that manifested them, rather than on a composition's purely artistic aspect. His purpose was to teach future architects to convey to an onlooker the message inherent in their designs. An architect must be capable of stressing or elucidating by architectural means whatever form he or she had in mind, or whatever features characterized the space he or she had organized. In other words, as Ladovsky saw it, the perceptual criteria of form and space needed to be established and distinct means and methods found in order to enable an architect to assess such criteria and put them to good use.

Balikhin, however, felt that Ladovsky ignored purely artistic considerations in his rationalistic approach. He therefore elaborated the Basic Section's 'Space' course so as to introduce a degree of rationality into artistic methods of composition.

A comparison of training tasks set in Obmas and the Basic Section on the subject of, say, "Construction'or 'Balance', reveals the difference between the methods of Ladovsky and Balikhin. The abstract tasks carried out under Ladovsky's supervision invariably reflect the work and tension involved in each particular construction, whatever its outward expression, while those carried out in the Basic Section concentrate on outward balance, regardless of the actual characteristics of the construction concerned.

At first sight, it might appear that Balikhin genuinely developed and imparted greater depth to Ladovsky's theory by enriching it with artistic refinements while preserving its rational approach. In practice, however, the situation was more complex. Ladovsky was a confirmed Rationalist both by intellectual inclination and in his approach to form. His Rationalism was concerned with perception rather than function and structure, as in the case of the Constructivists. Yet, very much like the latter, he did not confine artistic creativity merely to its rational aspect. The Constructivist leaders claimed that the functional structure of a building only provided a basis for its artistic composition, and Ladovsky similarly saw in perceptual criteria the objective foundation for artistic composition.

Taking everything into account, Ladovsky recognized the existence of a clear dividing line in matters of art which could not be crossed by rational methods alone. In his 'ratio-architectural' theory, he did his best not to impinge upon purely aestetic problems, while Balikhin, though he remained an equally dedicated Rationalist throughout, often crossed every such borderline.

It is clear from Ladovsky's output and teaching that he was gifted with fine artistic intuition and was no mere Rationalist in matters of form. He consistently and strictly instilled in his pupils rational methods of formal development, but always knew when to pull up in time, before criteria that could not be formally stated came into play. In their personal contacts with him, his pupils sensed both the power of his unyielding logic in terms of form and the unlimited freedom of his artistic concepts. The secret of Ladovsky's teaching resided in his ability to induce this creative state of mind in a pupil. That is why almost all the Vkhutemas projects produced under his supervision stand out by the astonishing freedom of their spatial composition, combined with an unmistakably logical approach.

And so two centres for theory and organization developed within Asnova in the mid-1920s. Ladovsky increasingly allowed the alternative centre in the Basic Section more scope, while Balikhin and the rest of the teaching staff continued to improve upon the psychoanalytical method.

In 1927, Ladovsky created a psycho-technical scientific research laboratory in Vkhutein, the famous 'BlackRoom' with walls, floor and ceiling painted black. Various experiments involving objective psycho-physiological criteria of perception and the impact of architectural forms were conducted there. Instruments for the measurement of visual perception, spatial representation, and so on, were produced to Ladovsky's specifications. In the course of this work, almost as though he was still arguing with Balikhin, Ladovsky stressed the scientific justification for those aspects of form-generation which he considered capable of rationalization, but stopped short when it came to artistic methods.

The split in the Rationalist movement was formally recognized when Ladovsky and his followers let Asnova to set up a new organization, the Union of Architect-Planners - ARU - which will be dealt with in greater detail in connection with town-planning experiments. Asnova continued as a group of young teachers in the Basic Section led by Balikhin.

Asnova's history thus falls into three periods:

the first, 192325, when it represented the only grouping of innovative architects and included the majority of young progressive architects;

the second, 1926-28, when a number of members left it for whi lii    Ly and Balikhin;

the third, 1929-32, when one of these centres became an independent organization— ARU-in 1928, while some members joined Vopra in 1929.

Apart from those enumerated earlier, the members of Asnova included, at various stages, Borisovsky, Budo, Bunin, Bykova, Varentsov, Volodko, Gelfeld, Zalesskaya, Karra, Korzhev, Kruglova, Krutikov, Vitaly Lavrov, Lamtsov, Melnikov, Myslin, Viktor Petrov, Valentin Popov, Prokhorova, Spassky, Silchenkov, Travin, Turkus and others.

Asnova maintained contacts with innovative architects abroad such as Adolf Behne in Germany, Le Corbusier in France, Mart Stam in Holland, Lundberg Holm in America, Emil Roth in Switzerland, KareTeige inCzechoslovakia, Lubomir Micic in Yugoslavia and Muroyama in Japan.

Judging by the foreign press, European architects took a genuine interest in Asnova's activities during the 1920s and, in particular, in projects carried out at Vkhutemas under Ladovsky's direction.

## Asnova teams

The rejection of the individualism characteristic of academic studios in architectural faculties was mentioned in the earliest Rationalist Vkhutemas publications, under the slogan: 'Long live individuality, away with individualism!' In this context, 'individualism' primarily meant the subordination of students' opinions to the views of whoever was in charge of a studio and the resulting suppression of personal creativity. This was set against the ideal of a collective studio in which individualities combined and everyone was given a generous opportunity to develop and display an artistic identity, while the leader merely acted as the senior member of the team.

Asnova opted for collective work in modern architecture from the start, and the very first practical project undertaken by its members, the design for an international Red Stadium in Moscow in 1924, was a collective effort. Work on the stadium project (and then in more detail, within the Sport Structures Division) marked an important stage in Asnova's activity. It was used in competition entries by Obmas students, as well as in practical designs and schoolroom tasks, including diploma drawings. Moreover, the extensive assortment of building types involved made it possible to derive from it a number of problems bearing on experiments in formal aesthetics and new tes  uiig u s ellinht. This first practical project undertaken by Asnova helped to establish many aspects of the collective method promoted by the Rationalists. The stadium project as a whole was directed by Ladovsky, but each participant had a task of his or her own in the overall sports complex.

The second major Asnova project was to design and build the Shabolovka residential area in Moscow. When the Moscow City Soviet commissioned this work in 1927, a competition was held within Asnova, which picked Nikolai Travin's entry as the b.This becme the bass r the actual development wrk, but other Asnova members contributed to the final design.

Asnova was most active in the last phase of its history when the earliest Obmas students had graduated. At the turn of the decade, it became closely involved in the contract competitions which were then much in use. As soon as Asnova had secured a contract, it would first carry out an internal competition to elicit ideas. A design team would then be formed to represent Asnova in the open competition as the 'Asnova team'. But the project would be discussed, at various stages of drafting and before its final submission as a complete entry for the open competition, at Asnova's general meetings.

At the end of the 1920s and start of the 1930s, as professional groups - Mao, Asnova, Osa, ARU, Vopra - proliferated, projects were often entered in the name of such organizations. ARU used teams extensively to draft competition entries and other organizations followed suit. In Asnova, however, teamwork became an artistic principle and was regarded as a method of collective creativity appropriate to the new social conditions.

The team method was also used to implement the concept of synthetic art put forward by Balikhin, in the combination of architectural work with the output of painters and sculptors at the early planning stage. Combined teams of sculptors and painters as well as architects were formed and treated as creative co-operatives of artists concerned with space, volume and colour. Such collaboration was typical of Vkhutein's Basic Section, to which the Association's centre of creativity shifted when Ladovsky left Asnova.

Practically all important projects undertaken in the Association during its closing phase were carried out by teams. These projects, which had gone through a process of internal competition, followed by the approval of the Association as a whole, vividly illustrate the Rationalists' orientation at the end of the 1920s. Much in them derives from early projects carried out in Vkhutemas by young Asnova members under Ladovsky's direction, but there are novel features, such as the use of certain favoured methods of spatial composition, the occasional absence of a clearly indicated centre of composition for the general design within the space allotted to it and an extensive synthesis with figurative art.

The most interesting projects carried out by the Asnova teams, some of which are dealt with in later chapters, included in Moscow the House of Industry(1929-30), the All-Union Palace of the Arts (1930), the Proletarsky District Palace ofCulture (1930), the Palace of Soviets in the first-round competition of 1931, the School for the All-Union Central Executive Committee (Vtsik) in the Kremlin (1930), the Kharkov Massed Musical Performance Theatre (193031) and the Sverdlovsk Synthetic Theatre (1932).

![](images/91231850930c498ba260e0f1148feef8cadfbeb30798d058fd06687c2da5d7d8.jpg)

Constructivism, the second main trend in Soviet architecture during the 1920s, achieved maturity some years later than Rationalism. It reflected the requirements of the new architecture as real building work got under way again after the end of the Civil War. As an architectural movement, it was greatly influenced in its early stages by Constructivism in the wider sense, where it was a broad trend affecting early Soviet art as a whole, in book and exhibition design, theatre art, applied and decorative art, in posters, and so on. It emerged from the struggle to establish new forms of art and bring them into line to reflect the changes then taking place in society and the new production processes, and the development of fresh aesthetic ideals. As a phenomenon in Soviet art, Constructivism was the outcome of a unique synthesis of, on the one hand, Leftist experiments in painting, especially by artists searching for a way into architecture by imparting aesthetic significance to materials, textures and structural methods, and, on the other, the theories promoted in Production Art by Brik, Arvatov, Kushner and Gan, in particular.

## Constructivism and the theory of Production Art

Although the theories of Constructivism and Production Art very often coincided, they were by no means identical. In 192124, when the development of Production Art theory was at its peak, its supporters regarded the early Constructivism of 1920-21 as no more than an early stage on the way from Veshchizm or 'the Culture of Things'. The evolution of Constructivism itself, however, was affected by many extraneous influences and in its fully developed form, during the second half of the 1920s, it drew on Production Art for a significant contribution to its own theory. Production Art ideas had been formulated after the etablishment o Sovt power as part  nattempt by Leftist artists to acknowledge the role now devolving toat in society. One of the first such attempts, already mentioned earlier, was made in March 1918 in the Futurists' Gazette, when it published 'Decree No. I concerning the Democratization of Art'. In the same — and only - issue, Mayakovsky wrote in his 'Open Letter to Workers':

'The twin fires of war and revolution have taken hold of our souls and our cities. The palaces of yesterday's pomp stand as burnt-out skeletons. The ruined cits await new builders

To you who have accepted Russia's inheritance, you (I have faith in it) who will tomorrow become masters of the world at large, I address one question: what fantastic structures will you use to cover the sites of yesterday's conflagrations? ...

Only the explosion of the Revolution of the Spirit will cleanse us of the tatters of bygone art. ...

The revolution of the content . . . is unthinkable without a revolution of form. . . .'

In rejecting the old art that had served the ruling classes, Mayakovsky called upon painters to bring the new art within reach of the broad working masses. He adjured the workers not to be seduced by the old art's apparent beauty and to make the new art their own. Mayakovsky's poems and articles of this period not only embrace the struggle for the new art, but also display traces of Veshchizm and refer to the tasks of Agit Art and aspects of Production Art. As a great artist, Mayakovsky was at the centre of the web of complex and conflicting processes whereby the new art came into being, and he could not be identified with any single trend. He was, for instance, an equally authoritative figure both to Rationalists and to Constructivists, even though they were in bitter dispute with each other.

Mayakovsky's Veshchizm, with its appeal for an art of everyday usefulness, represents a stage in the development of the theory of Production Art. He wrote in 1921, in his poem 'Order No.2 to the Army of the Arts':

We shuffle and we argue

In search of mystic meaning

While things put up a clamour:

'Endow us with new shapes!'

The next attempt to define a social role for the new art was min the peridial skusstokomy Art otheComm between December 1918 and April 1919. Its editorials and articles by Brik, Kushner and Punin supplied the first identifiable tenets of Production Art. They suggested that the purpose of art was not to decorate life, but to shape it. The job of artists as  eajev aly om e thi  the environment, rather than to depict it. At this stage, however, Production Art was still in its frst, Veshchist phase. In fact, the term veshch (thing) was introduced by Art f the Coune.

Tl T as not dn t ole ws  e cn t prog useful objects. Artists were to 'make'things, and find their fuln Veist vi tur int bourction r were to switch their creative endeavours to producing objects of use, rather than mere art.

It was next proposed to alter the very nature of artistic activity: 'making'was to be replaced by 'producing', What had originally been a demand for the conversion of an art object into a useful item - the Veshchist stage of Production Art - now develope intbourconept inwhic r eualled lour. It was ot long befo Athe Coe requied ee ea tivity' to be merged with 'mechanical work'in a unified form of 'creative work'. The most articulate statement of Production Art's 'labour' concept can be found in a collection of articles, Iskusstvo v proizvodstve (Art in the Production Process), No. 1 (Moscow, 1921), in which Brik and others stated some fundamental Production Art principles, such as the denial ofany substantive difference between art and any other form of labour, and the assertion that the generation of form was no more than the creation of a useful object. This view of things triumphed at Inkhuk in the autumn of 1921. An artist was now required to abandon his or her calling and enter into production. Innovative architects, for their part, approved of the appeal for an art of the masses and the 'decoration of labour'. They did not object to Veshchizm because — in any event — architects had always created 'useful things'. They merely saw this as an opportunity to inject greater democracy into architecture and to devote their attention equally to single, 'one-off' projects and mass dwellings, communal buildings and industrial works. At the Veshchist stage, Rationalist architects, Ladovsky and Krinsky among them, successfully collaborated with future Productivists in Inkhuk, but as the theory of Production Art developed, their paths diverged. The Rationalists were not prepared to give up art in its own right and refused to accept the utilitarian making of a 'thing', such as a building, as the only valid reason for the generation of form.

Significantly enough, the strictest promoters of Production Art- such as Brik, Arvatov and Kushner - were pure ideologists, not practitioners. They urged artists to go into production, but saw them primarily as organizers who would raise its cultural level, rather than active representatives of art.

The term 'Constructivism' was current before Production Art theory developed its strict orthodoxy, and was often thought to include the concept of Veshchizm. This must be clearly borne in mind in order to understand how Lissitzky, a Constructivist when he left the Soviet Union in 1921, could represent the Rationalist Asnova organization abroad, but collaborate with the Constructivists in architecture and design when he returned to Moscow while nevertheless remaining a member of Asnova.

Lissitzky took Constructivism abroad when it was still at the Veshchist stage for theoretical purposes. He could not, in fact, have adopted the strict Production Art doctrine, since he was himself a practising artist in the relatively traditional sense. The very title — Veshch — of the periodical which he founded in Berlin, together with Ilya Ehrenburg, makes this point clear. The leading article of the first issue in 1922 promoted the Veshchist concept or, more accurately, the concept of Production Art at the stage when Veshchizm was turning into early Constructivism.

Early Constructivism developed as an independent trend, though not as yet in architecture, during 1921, and the bitterest controversy over it unfolded in Inkhuk. The Constructivist Working Group mentioned earlier was set up there in March 1921 with Alexei Gan, Konstantin Medunetsky, Alexander Rodchenko, Karl Ioganson, Vladimir and Georgy Stenberg, and Varvara Stepanova. Gan formulated the theoretical basis for the trendand later put rward his views in Knrtii, a book published in 1922. The programme of the Working Group s a Ganpovd   me 1 April 1921, with the fundamental aim of promoting the Communist expression of material values. The declared intention was to pass from laboratory work to real activity and practical experimentation. The basic means of expression provided by the materials available were said to be 'tectonic methods, construction and facture that would bring the material elements of industrial culture into play'. In this context, tectonic method was regarded as dependent upon 'the purposeful use of industrial materials', while construction 'was to be understood as the chosen function taken to its limit'. The Group proclaimed 'the incompatibility of artistic activity with the functional nature of intellectual labour', declared 'unrelenting war on art as a whole', and asserted 'the unacceptability of artistic tradition in so far as the Communist form of Constructivist buildings is concerned'.

The promoters of Constructivism and Production Art called upon artists who had rejected easel painting to undertake genuinely practical production work. They believed that the new art should, by means of production, foster the creation of a new material environment. They dreamed of a well-adjusted new human being surrounded by convenient things in a well-appointed city. They also contrasted objects in common use with artistic creations and equated the latter with the way of life of the former ruling classes. They thought that the proletariat would come to reject all 'useless' things, including objects of art, and would only require whatever was useful.

Boris Arvatov and other orthodox exponents of Production Art regarded Constructivism as no more than a transitional stage on the way to a new art which would, in accordance with the conceptions expressed in the journal Lef, become the art of living. Arvatov was probably the strictest among these theoreticians, and the ideologist of Production Art's final stage. Brik had been at the origin of this trend, and Gan was influential in formulating the theory of Constructivism, but - by developing the 'labour' concept - Arvatov in fact took Production Art beyond artistic creativity and replaced it by 'the structuring of life'.

Assessments of the part played in the formulation of architectural Constructivism by the theory of Production Art often fail to allow for thertisticaspect orchitecture. In sofar s r chitects were concerned, the appeals by the exponents of Production Art not to represent reality but to shape or construct a human environment instead, a world of relevant objects, did not in any way call upon them to abandon the essentials of their work. Architects have always devoted themselves to the creation of a human material environment, and they therefore regarded the Production Art demands as mainly a matter of social priority and orientation towards the mass consumer. As to the utilitarian view of form-generation, Constructivist architects saw in it little more than an additional argument to be used in the fight against the Classicists. If form is generated by function and structure, any elements of Classical order must certainly be discarded as antiquated - a very clear and useful point to score against the traditionalists, and therefore most popular with Constructivist architects.

The analysis and assessment ofany trend in art must obviously a movement's creative principles contain two inseparable ingredients: theoretical statements and the ideas embodied in the works which mark its successive stages of development. In such a context, statements of theory should be tested not only against the practice of their advocates, but equally in the light of counter-arguments put forward by their opponents. It is impossible to assess trends in Soviet architecture of the 1920s fully and accurately if one confines oneself to the logical and literal meaning of contemporary statements about principles, while disregarding their practical implications and the debates that surrounded these trends. One must grasp not only what was postulated in any particular set of theories, but what this actually meant in practice in each case. Thus, the Classicist line of argument should be borne in mind when one is confronted with the obdurate denial of art throughout the 1920s by a number of architectural Constructivist thinkers. Much that the Classicists claimed was correct, and it is difficult to understand nowadays why Rationalists and Constructivists should have reacted so sharply against the concepts they put forward.

The Classicists were stressing the need to take account of tradition, the importance of continuity, of national traditions, the inappropriateness of transplanting architectural forms from the past direct into the contemporary scene, the need to adapt one's heritage creatively, instead of copying it. All this, so it would seem, should have been equally acceptable to the supporters of the new architecture. But the true meaning of such Classicist statements can only be understood by examining the underlying practice.

When Zholtovsky's disciples, for instance, talked about a creative assimilation of the heritage, they apparently maintained that no direct copying of Classical models should be allowed and that allstylistic features must be modified, however slightly. Such a startling conclusion drawn from an apparently quite acceptable proposition led the Classicists' opponents to distrust all their suggestions about the assimilation of tradition. As a safeguard against such suggestions, these opponents rejected all claims in favour of tradition, and some even denied all art from the past. This was undoubtedly a-mostly verbalover-reaction, but it enabled the innovators to eschew hollow talk about questions of tradition. Only a knowledge of the realities of this debate about art explains why, for example, Ladovsky entirely excluded Classicism from his teaching, while sensitive artists such as Alexander Vesnin and Ivan Leonidov explicitly rejected the concept of art itself.

Bearing this in mind, it also becomes easier to understand the link between Production Art theory and architectural Constructivism. The artistic nihilism prevalent among orthodox supporters of Production Art met with no objection in principle from the architectural Constuctivists, who, in practice, harnessed it to a rejection of inherited forms and an emphasis on the ruturlly inal apet o a building in  ri form was concerned.

## On the term 'Constructivism'

The term 'Constructivism' has gained wide currency among art historians. It is, however, not altogether clear when and where it arose, what it originally meant and what art forms, including architecture, are covered by it. An attempt to answer each of these questions in turn follows, but without any claim to total accuracy.

Mayakovsky wrote in his 'Paris'essay: 'For the first time, an art term has winged its way from Russia, and not from France— Constructivism . . . , which regards the formal activity of the artist as merely a matter of engineering required for the design of our entire existence. It is from us that the French artists will have to learn. Here no clever stuff will prevail. The building of a new culture demands a cleared area. It requires an October broom.3

Although Mayakovsky touches upon all three of our questions in this passage, it is worth considering his answer to the first of them — did the term "Constructivism' in fact reach Europe from Soviet Russia? In the periodical G, No.3 (1924), its editor, Hans Richter, wrote in an article entitled 'Concering Constructivism': The concept of Constructivism arose in Russia. It is applied to an art that utilizes modern structural components instead of the usual materials, and pursues constructional aims. The concept of Constructivism was used in an extended sense by Doesburg, Lissitzky and myself at the Düsseldorf Congress in 1920.' When he used this quotation, Ivan Matsa correctly gave the date of the Congress as 29-31 May 1922.4

Alexei Gan wrote on several occasions that the first Constructivist group was formed in 1920.5 An information article in the architects' journal SA (No.2, 1926) also links this event, as does Gan, with the foundation of a Constructivist group in Inkhuk® and adds that the Constructivists' programme, together with photographs of their works, had been passed for informaio o   i and France.

This date, however, also requires correction, since Gan had ceary oten h he    was in Inkhuk in March 1921, not 1920. Works by members of the group were shown in a separate room at the Obmokhu exhibition in Moscow that year,' and photographs of these works were published by Lissitzky in the Berlin periodical Veshch, Nos.1-2 (1922), p.19.

It can therefore safely be said that the term 'Constructivism' appeared in Europe only in 1922, brought there from Soviet Russia by Lissitzky. As to its first appearance at home, documents in the archive of the Inkhuk's Working Group of Constructivists indicate that it was in use at least by the beginning of 1921, although evidence still undiscovered may push this date back to 1920, or even 1919.

The initial meaning of the term is important because art historians nowadays, consciously or unconsciously, overlook a social content which was inherent in the term from the start, and chiefly stress its purely formal aspect. The literal meaning of Constructivism carries a number of overtones - associations with technological constructions, with the structuring of a work of art (described in Inkhuk at that time as a 'construction'), with engineering work in the construction process, and with the task of organizing — or constructing - a new environment. All these expressions were current in Soviet art early in the 1920s. The promoters of Production Art, for example, regarded the construction of a material environment as the most important activity of all, while those who saw Constructivism as a fashionable new style were primarily interested in its visual connection with that external bareness of technically oriented construction work which became, in some sort, the hallmark of Constructivism. Soviet architects never saw Constructivism as implying the primacy of construction, but regarded it first and foremost as a new method of design.

As to the activities that the term 'Constructivism' covered, apart from its usage abroad, it would be wrong, especially in so far as architecture is concerned, to impart a purely stylistic meaning to it in a Soviet context, or to link it literally with the denudation of a structure. Yet this is precisely what often happens, and foreign writers attribute all innovative Soviet works in the 1920s to Constructivism, or treat as Constructivist all new structural innovations. In the Soviet architecture of the 1920s, Constructivism represents an influential but by no means unique trend, with identifiable creative processes which are peculiar to it, including its own approach to the generation of form.

## Constructivist artists

Production Art theory, which dominated Inkhuk from 1921 onwards, greatly influenced Constructivist principles in many branches of art: in cinema, graphics, the stage, literature and the new-born art of design. A factual approach in literature, documentary shots in film, abandonment of painted stage sets, insistence on functional requirements in objects of everyday use, all these were part of the new system of aesthetics promoted by Constructivist artists. Their fundamental principles included the rejection of artistic conceits and decorations, and of past forms of art, indeed even of art as such.

As noted earlier, the rejection of old-world art after the Revolution was connected with the prestige it had conferred on individuals in a class-ridden society, in which human values had been displaced by the value of things. Moreover, in the social climate of the times, the Constructivists contrasted things of everyday use with objects of art, which they regarded as luxury products involving wasted labour. Constructivist aesthetics took shape amid the complexities and contradictions prevalent as War Communism changed over to NEP, the New Economic Policy. An unrestrained burst of petit-bourgeois self-interest and the money-making tone set by traders further sharpened the conflict between the aesthetic ideals proper to the different layers in society. The workers set about showing up the profiteers by a deliberate restraint in dress, manner of living and arrangement of public establishments. Asceticism became the proletariat's ethical standard. Art soon followed suit, and artists conscientiously set about creating a material environment appropriate to these principles.

Mayakovsky was writing poster captions, instead of lyrical poetry. Varvara Stepanova and Lyubov Popova were producing textile designs. Dziga Vertov deliberately filmed only documentaries. Grigory Miller, Stepanova, Rodchenko and Tatlin designed working, sports and everyday clothes. Meyerhold dressed his actors in overalls and replaced his sets by 'the machine'. Gan introduced new methods of book design and new typefaces so as to use the lettering itself to express the content of a text. Klutsis and the Stenbergs applied photomontage techniques to poster work. In architecture, as in other fields, simplicity of form was associated with democratic sentiment and represented the new human relationships.

The denial of art and a marked utilitarian attitude towards form led to a generally ascetic approach to design. In addition it was entirely predictable, although Constructivist artists had not foreseen such an outcome, that the simple and severe forms which seemed to them strictly utilitarian and wholly devoid of ornament would soon be widely taken up as the outcome of a new stylistic trend. Restraint derived from social and moral considerations thus came to bolster artistic experiments directed at the search for a new form.

The Constructivist output most closely relevant to architectural interests was connected with the 'construction' of all aspects of the material environment— in everyday life, at work, in public buildings, for transport purposes and in town planning - and with Agit or Agitational Art.

The supporters of Production Art contrasted the creation of works of art with the construction of a material environment and, in doing so, often equated the production of objects with a structuring of everyday life. In its fully developed form, however, Production Art went beyond Veshchizm to herald the dissolution of object and form within the changing social environment. Thus Kushner linked the 'withering away of the object' with the progress of technology: the multi-purpose object was in the making and would adapt itself to actual needs, thus doing away with fixed shapes for particular objects. He believed that this evolution of the object would generate new ways of meeting requirements. A 'material setting' would come into existence and provide a unified system of equipping accommodation so as to satisfy generalized social requirements and remain adaptable as these requirements varied in time and space. The 'material setting' represented an ideal material environment cleared of individual objects and their intractable shapes. This ideal inevitably influenced the planning of a material environment in the 1920s. It also prompted interest among Constructiment. They designed - or 'constructed' - products such as mobile market booths, sectional exhibition equipment, combined table-cum-divans, adaptable seat-beds for use in aircraft, coaches etc; standard collapsible furniture for use in cinemas and clubs, cupboard-beds, folding wall-beds, sectional bus shelters, folding wall brackets, sectional cupboards, mobile cinemas, and multi-purpose equipment for clubs, shops and other establishments.

Gan, Lavinsky and others took a hand in designing such equipment, but the largest contribution to 'construction' in this field was made by Rodchenko, who incorporated the techniques concerned into the training schedule of the Metalwork Faculty which he directed in Vkhutemas.

Teaching at the Stroganov College and other pre-Revolutionary establishments had been based on the development of handicraft skills. As against this, the Constructivist Production artists working in the Vkhutemas Production Faculties introduced a fundamentally different method that relied on a rational approach and aimed to create, by the widest possible use of modern technology, things that were both useful and aesthetically valid. They fought for rationalization in the production of objects of everyday use and based their 'construction work' in this field on each object's function, component materials and make-up.

The first specialists in what they termed the 'artistic construction' of equipment graduated from Vkhutein towards the hRoT L their most important teachers.

In addition to their involvement in the 'construction'of everyday objects, the Constructivist artists were also active in Agit Art. They and the theoreticians of Production Art discerned a certain 'utilitarian' aspect in the need to stress ideological content and subordinate artistic formulation to agitational and propaganda purposes. They therefore included this in the list of functionally justified forms of creativity.

Alexander Vesnin and his followers assimilated a great deal of Constructivist Production Art theory and practice and then applied this to architecture. They were determined in their opposition to the Rationalist Asnova supporters and at one with Constructivist artists in their fight for modern functional architecture, new objects of everyday use and the rationalization of artistic labour, They therefore also adopted their allies' chosen description of themselves as 'Constructivists'.

Neither the artistic principles, nor the theoretical apparatus of architectural Constructivism could, however, be transplanted wholesale from another discipline and they evolved as part of a complex interaction between architecture and various other art forms and theories. The process whereby the fundamental beliefs of architectural Constructivism came to be formulated can be followed most clearly in the work of Alexander Vesnin, the leader of this movement.

## Alexander Vesnin,

## leader of the Constructivists

Until the Revolution, Alexander Vesnin worked with his brothers Leonid and Viktor in a variety of architectural styles. He was also active as a painter and worked for a time in Tatlin's studio. He had looked to the past, both as a painter and an architect, although even before the Revolution his output displayed a tendency to enrich Classical traditions with the achievements of the new art and technology. Thus the ceiling which he painted in 1915 in the Sirotkin House, built in Nizhny-Novgorod by the three Vesnin brothers, hints at Cé- zanne's influence among its Renaissance subjects and shapes while designs such as that for the Konovalov factory produced in 1917 in collaboration with his brother Viktor show traces of Rationalist influence.

In the early Soviet years Alexander Vesnin was mainly engaged in painting. He was interested in the formal aesthetic experiments of Leftist painting in which he sensed a great potential for the development of a new style. He turned out nonrepresentational works and showed these at a number of exhibitions. These paintings became, much as the Prouns had done for Lissitzky, not merely a matter of stylistic experiment, but a bridge of sorts linking the achievements of Leftist painting with the architecture of Constructivism. As a painter, Alexander Vesnin was influenced by a number of masters and trends during this period. He was drawn to Leftist art by the formal experiments in which the painters passed from colour to the organization of space. The experiments which interested Alexander Vesnin were not, however, those which sought a way into architecture through Counter-reliefs or spatial constructions, but rather those which linked painting and architecture by implanting the former into architectural space by means of architectonic compositions. This approach, adopted by Popova soon after the Revolution in a cycle of works which she described as 'painterly architectonics', attracted Alexander Vesnin to her output, in which he found a more organic interaction between the methods of Leftist painting and architecture.

As a result, when working at Inkhuk, Vesnin joined neither the Working Group of Architects, nor the Constructivists, but became a member in May 1921 of the Working Group of Objectivists founded a month earlier by Alexander Drevin, Popova and Nadezhda Udaltsova. The programme drawn up by its founders stated that their aim was to create 'substantial and concrete constructions, both in space and in a plane', and to work 'not on the representation of components, but on the creation of a concrete organism, both in space and in a plane'.

Discussions about theory within the group bore on problems such as the definition and analysis of colour, the materialization of colour, the analysis of the elements of colour, substantiality, and the quality of space. Special attention was devoted to subjects connected with the notions of 'production' in art and of 'facture'. Experiments in spatial construction were undertaken.

The Objectivist programme reflected the approach to Leftist formal experiments underlying the teaching in two Vkhutemas studios, one headed by Drevin and Udaltsova, the other by Popova and Alexander Vesnin, who had taught painting and drawing there since 1921.

Popova's influence is clearly perceptible in Vesnin's early non-representational work immediately after the Revolution. But in his paintings and graphic compositions he was also intent on taking in the widest possible range of those discoveries made by Leftist painting which pointed the way to architecture. He had produced a number of compositions in 1917 containing square planes of different sizes set at various angles to the picture surface and foreshortened, sometimes in a very cursory way. These planes seemed to be floating in neutrally grey space. They partly overlapped each other and displayed a variety of colours, such as red, white and black. Solids appeared in later compositons, together with some — barely identifiable - pieces of real objects and fragments of writing. The elements of the composition shrank in size, while their number grew, so that they gradually more or less squeezed out the empty areas from the picture surface. These elements were no longer discrete geometric figures floating in space, but jagged fragments incrusted in one another and solidly carpeting the entire picture. These compositions date from 1921, but by the following years there are already indications of the complex process whereby Vesnin assimilated for architectonic purposes the achievements of Leftist painting. He had closely explored the latter and made many attempts to apply them in the actual architectural work available to him at this time, such as the simple geometric volumes of the Red Square speaker's platform in 1918 and the Kamerny Theatre stage decorations in 1920-22. He was now, at long last, clearly finding his way from Leftist painting into architecture.

A growing tendency to organize the composition makes itself felt in works dating from 1922 as compared with those of the previous year. The component·elements have not increased in size, but a unifying, specifically graphic principle becomes apparent, even though the works as such remain wholly pictorial. The composition tends to be more closely structured, a square grid emerges from the medley of individual fragments and a large proportion of the flat elements combine to generate a kind of cellular framework. Depth is introduced and emphasized by the superimposition of another graphic grid forming a second framework closer to the spectator, but this time consisting of rods rather than planes.

In Alexander Vesnin's graphic works dating from 1922 a tendency to introduce a rectangular framework is even more pronounced than in his paintings. These works represent a store of architectural compositions for use, specifically as façades, in future Constructivist buildings. This is significant: unlike Lissitzky's Prouns and Malevich's Arkitectons, Constructivism came to architecture from non-representational painting by using a framework marked by a graphic rectangular grid, and not through simple geometric solids.

These non-representational compositions by Vesnin, which vividly illustrate the aesthetic approach of early Constructivism, were contemporary with the formulation of early architectural Constructivist doctrine and the movement's first projects.

Alexander Vesnin stated his personal creed at Inkhuk in April 1922 and further clarified a number of the points concerned in June of that year, as part of a course about the problems that face an artist. The early Constructivist theory was set out in these documents and the principles linking architectural Constructivism and Veshchizm, as well as Constructivist and Production Art, were clearly explained. Vesnin was at that time working in virtually all the fields where these ideas found an echo the stage, painting, book production, public holiday decorations and architecture  and he set out his personal understanding of the concepts on which early architectural Constructivism was later based. He wrote that the artist should neither represent nor interpret existing objects, whether produced by nature or human hands, but should create things that were new; that every object should structure perception; that the d  t w eterid by the ap tep modern life and its mathematically accurate rhythm, by its component materials and by its effectiveness; and that whatever an artist made should be constructive without any vestige of representation.

Work for the stage did much to shape Alexander Vesnin's outlook. The theatre acted as a sort of testing ground for architecture and design during the early years of Soviet power. The tremendous accumulated artistic potential could find no outlet inarhitecture t that time. Insted it switched to thesage, where architectural compositions and objects of everyday use could be produced lie-size, rather than n paper or s scaleddown models. The brilliant successes of the Soviet theatre after the Revolution were not only due to the pioneering work of directors such as Meyerhold, Tairov and Vakhtangov, but equally to the output of artists attracted to stage design at that time, among them Yakulov, Exter, Popova, Alexander Vesnin, Rodchenko, Stepanova, the Stenberg brothers and Viktor Shestakov. When Vesnin designed the setting for Claudel's L'Annonce faite à Marie at the Kamerny Theatre in 1920, he simplified medieval architecture to the limit and created a sort of Cubist Gothic. In 1922, the same approach led to a form of Neo-Classical Cubism in Phèdre.

Constructivist designs and sets appeared in Moscow theatres during 1922-23, the most original and radically novel of these being Popova's Magnanimous Cuckold, directed by Meyerhold at the Actor's Theatre in 1922, and The World Upside Down, directed by him at the Theatre of the Revolution in 1923; Alexander Vesnin's The Man who was Thursday, directed by Tairov at the Kamerny Theatre in 1923; Viktor Shestakov's Lyul Lake, directed by Meyerhold at the Theatre of the Revolution in 1923; and Exter's sketches for a production by Goleizovsky in 1922.

There had been various Constructivist holiday decorations, speakers' platforms and experimental structuresbefore this move into theatre work. They were, however, all produced by painters and, as a rule, lacked true architectonic quality and any awareness of the impact ofsuch shapes on the appearances of a modern city. Alexander Vesnin was probably the first to sense the urban quality these structures demanded.

In 1921, he and Popova designed an intricate set of open-air decorations on the Khodynskoe Field in honour of the Third Congress of the Comintern. These consisted of two symbolic cities, each of which stood for a social system. One of these — 'the citadel of capitalism' - consisted of blind Cubist solids, while the other — 'the city of the future' —was composed of blind and openwork dynamic elements: wheels, transmission belts, pylons, booms, struts and cantilevers. In form, both compositions were close to the Symbolist Romanticist experiments of that time, and the openwork elements involved were primarily used for symbolic rather than architectonic purposes. The restless dynamism of this composition by Vesnin and Popova matched the actual movement of various parts such as the wheels.

After completing this joint project, the artists, who jointly directed one of the painting studios in Vkhutemas, worked separately on two stage productions; Popova for Meyerhold with The Magnanimous Cuckold, and Vesnin for Tairov with The Man who was Thursday.

Popova undoubtedly set to work on The Magnanimous Cuckold only after the Khodynskoe Field project had been completed, by the first half of 1921, and Vesnin might be thought to have started work on The Man who was Thursday after her, since the first performance of The Magnanimous Cuckold took place on 25 April 1922. But the gap in time was not as great as is sometimes thought: the first performance of The Man who was Thursday was scheduled for 1922, though the departure of the company on a tour abroad delayed the performance by a year.

In other words, the designs for The Man who was Thursday embody Alexander Vesnin's artistic experiments during 1922. They represent a link between the dynamic compositions of the two cities in the Khodynskoe Field project, which hark back to Symbolist Romanticism and Cubo-Futurism, and the competition projects, submitted together with his brothers in 192223, for a Palace of Labour in Moscow. The stage designs, of which many have survived, show that Vesnin was relying on the Khodynskoe Field project  rather than on The Magnanimous Cuckold, a rural mill setting for Constructivist purposes— as a starting point. He had created on stage the generalized outline of a modern town -an artistic embodiment of the town-planning concept — by using frame structures, girders, cantilevers, lifts, moving pavements, various technological elements, such as revolving wheels, etc.

Dynamism dominated both his designs for the Khodynskoe Field decorations and his early sketches for The Man who was Thursday, both literally, as mobile components, and in compositional terms. As time went on, the frame structure gradually asserted itself and finally came to govern the conception of the setting as a whole. These designs are crucial in the formulation of aesthetic principles for architectural Constructivism. They show that early Constructivist ideas developed from openwork structures and mobile parts, thus combining the sense of space with that of movement.

The elimination from stage designs of thematic and representational elements was then regarded as the principal Constructivist aim in the theatre. The construction was merely required to provide 'a machine tol' or setting, as the sing then went  for the actors. Despite this, the Constructivist designs immediately came to be regarded as a new style of theatre art. The sets were seen to represent the industrial mood of the new era, while the structures and mechanical devices on stage acquired an aesthetic significance. Constructivism had reached the theatre starting from laboratory work and unadorned buildings. Now, or the t tme,  was mbodidin age structures deliberately endowed with aesthetic content. Architects and artists built into the Constructivist stage designs of the early 1920s the images of the city of the future and of a new way of life. They also initiated the aesthetics of town planning and mechanization. The latest structures and everyday things acquired a new dimension and an aesthetic quality when they appeared on stage. By the time they returned to real life for building or production purposes they had become elements of a new artistic environment, because strictly utilitarian items of t k which was often far from what the artists themselves had intended.

This increment of aesthetic quality through Constructivist work for the stage was particularly well illustrated by Alexander Vesnin's output. He was an architect and therefore created for the actors' use not just a 'machine tool', but a real and complex architectural composition, a fact which establishes his designs for The Man who was Thursday as marking an important stage in the development of architectural Constructivism's aesthetic theory.

Bare structural frameworks had been used earlier by some architects and in some small architectural items, but the aesthetic content with which they were now endowed here raised this method of construction to the level of a new system of composition. It was architectonically perfected in competition projects carried out in 1923-25 by Alexander Vesnin, jointly with his two brothers, Leonid and Viktor.

The Vesnin team finally took shape in 1923 and in the course of the next decade the brothers became the acknowledged leaders of the innovative direction in Soviet architecture. The team's strength lay in the organic collaboration of talented, uniquely creative, but also thoroughly diverse architects. The brothers successfully complemented each other, not only by their various individual talents, but also by their different professional experiences.

Alexander's quest for a new artistic form, Viktor's experience of industrial construction and Leonid's design work on housing all merged in the Vesnin team's approach and during the years 192332 imparted a fresh quality to their output that was very unlike the earlier work carried out by each of the brothers separately.

From 1923 onwards, the Vesnins generated a large number of projects, many of which became landmarks in Soviet architecture and influenced the output of other architects. When they were engaged on combined work, they dealt jointly with all artistic problems. But each had his own special area in which his influence proved decisive because he had taken the greatest trouble over it: Alexander was the authority on composition, Viktor on structural technology and Leonid on functional planning.

Architectural Constructivism first made itself felt as an independent creative movement in 1923 in the competition for the design of a Palace of Labour in Moscow. This competition - the largest since the October Revolution — in many ways determined the path that Soviet architecture was to follow. The majority of the entries-of which there were nearly fifty-offered a Palace of Labour on traditional monumental lines.(As we have already said, the Rationalists, headed by Ladovsky, refused to take part in the competition.) The Vesnins' new-style project stood out among the rest by its truly modern appearance, its clear and rational plan and the daring use of the latest structural components and materials.

The true road to creative experiment was opened for many architects by this Vesnin competition entry. The project proved outstandingly important for Soviet architecture,organically combining as it did a new building's social significance with a modern, technically functional approach and a different architectural image.

The Vesnin Palace of Labour project represents a landmark in Soviet architecture as a whole, and the evolution of Constructivism in particular. It is therefore more than usually important to provide an objective assessment of each brother's individual contributions, not so much in terms of elaborating particular structures or providing functional solutions for particular building requirements, as with regard to the creation of a radically new architectural image. This approach suggests that, in the last resort, credit for the design must go first to Alexander Vesnin, though it was executed with the assistance of his brothers. Alexander's experiments in 1921-22 led him directly to a new architectural image, and it is therefore not surprising that the surviving sketches for the Palace of Labour are from his hand. In fact, the spatial composition and architectural character of these Palace of Labour designs are closely related to Alexander Vesnin's previous output by the structural methods and stylistic features employed (as in the projects for the Khodynskoe Field decorations and The Man who was Thursday sets) and are quite unlike earlier works by Leonid and Viktor Vesnin. Leonid was a declared supporter of traditionalist tendences at the start of the 120s and taught in the aademicstudios of Vkhutemas. Viktor's work in the early post-Revolutionary years was closely related to Rationalist architectural concepts.

The position achieved by the Vesnin team during the 1920s was undoubtedly the result of Alexander's influence on the general direction adopted by it. This influence was decisive early on, in 192325, and the Vesnin projects of this period can therefore legitimately be regarded as expressing, first and foremost, Alexander Vesnin's principles.

This needs to be stressed because the three Vesnins are frequently treated as joint leaders of Constructivism, while no mention is made of Alexander's special role as the actual figurehead. In fact, it is sometimes assumed that, since Alexander Vesnin was a painter rather than an architect immediately after the Revolution, the decisive role in the creation of architectural Constructivism should be attributed to his brothers rather than to him.

In fact, given the circumstances prevailing during the early years of Soviet power when the torch was passing from Leftist painting to architecture, Alexander Vesnin's activity as an artist painter, stage and graphic designer- proved a strength in relation to his brothers, rather than a weakness. In his case, the interaction between Leftist painting and architecture unfolded within one individual's output. Alexander Vesnin reached new architecture by way of stage design, a marginal area, rather than simply through Cubism and non-representational art. This enabled him to adapt the formal aesthetic methods of Leftist art for architectonic purposes in the course of his transition to architecture.

The surviving sketches indicate that the best and artistically most brilliant Vesnin work - the Palace of Labour and the Leningradskaya Pravda building in particular  is in fact by Alexander Vesnin assisted by his brothers.

Furthermore, a number of Vesnin projects which do not carry his signature were executed by his brothers in the light of his advice, which clinched the definitive visual solution.

After the Palace of Labour competition, while Alexander Vesnin became the acknowledged leader of Constructivism, Moisei Ginzburg made a considerable contribution to the theory of this movement.

## Ginzburg's evolution towards Constructivism

Moisei Ginzburg taught architectural composition at Vkhutemas and the Moscow Institute of Civil Engineers (formerly the Polytechnical Institute), and played an active part at the beginning of the 1920s in the artistic and social life of the architectural community. In his onslaughts on eclecticists and Rationalists about problems of form he stressed and expounded the technical arguments for the new architectural designs in leading articles published by the periodical Arkhitektura in 1923 and in his book Stil i epokha (Style and Epoch) in 1924.

Alexander Vesnin established the basic principles of architectural Constructivism and Ginzburg's theoretical works dating from 192324 may be regarded as the next step in formulating a body of doctrine. They contained many statements of principles which became part of the basic theory of Soviet architectural Constructivism in its mature stage.

In his article 'Estetika sovremennosti' (The aesthetics of the present time') written in 1923, Ginzburg stressed the effect on architecture of the growth in production resources and progress in industry and technology. He chiefly looked for new architectural ideas in industrial and engineering structures because it was in these that architects had first abandoned traditional ornamentation and discovered an aesthetic value in its own right. Ginzburg, moreover, believed that the application of science and technology involved in the 'mechanization of life' was not merely a matter of new components, but implied the introduction of new and rational methods of design derived from construction engineering practice into architectural work.

Ginzburg did his best to sum up the experiments carried out by Sovit architects during the early 1920s in Style and Epoch, and the theories which he advanced concerning the influence on stylistic development of the needs felt in the various layers of society are of outstanding importance.

The book also illustrates Ginzburg's own progress towards Constructivism, a term the meaning of which he analyses as he goes along. As a historian of architecture, he was vividly aware of the important part that constructional considerations had always played in the evolution of form. But he did not regard structure and decoration as mutually incompatible, despite the different roles they performed at various stages of the formulation and development of style.

Ginzburg suggested in Style and Epoch that new architectural forms throughout history had usually been the product of changing structural or functional requirements and had been bare of decorative detail at the start. Such detail only appeared later, without at first disrupting the organic unity of form, but came to take over in time. 'The youth of a new style is mainly a matuciitaturi is ricn eeorative.' According to Ginzburg, the keen interest in structure displayed by modern architects should be seen in this light: if emphasis on construction characterizes the early stage of development of every new style, this must apply all the more strongly to modern architecture. Ginzburg therefore saw Constructivism at the start of the 1920s as the inevitable first phase in the development of a new architecture. It was only in his later writings that the term 'Constructivism' came to stand for a particular school.

Ginzburg himself was as yet only feeling his way into a new architecture, as his entry or the Moscow Palace o Labour competition in 1922-23 clearly shows, with elements of streamlined Classicism, Symbolist Romanticist influences and recollections of American grain silos, a form which attracted him at that time.

## Constructivism embodied in architecture: the foundation of Osa, the Union of Contemporary Architects

The innovative movements entered a new phase towards the mid-1920s when building work began in earnest throughout the country. Many questions of formal aesthetics came to be greatly clarified and the focus of experimentation in Soviet architecture gradually shifted to the design of buildings that were new in social terms.

It had become clear by 1923 that the theory and practice developed by the Rationalists and Constructivists had created a movement that grew and gained a foothold in Soviet architecture in its fight against eclecticism and traditionalism. It only gained the upper hand, however, in the mid-1920s, greatly helped in this by the competitions held in 1924-25 for building designs, such as the Moscow office of Leningradskaya Pravda (where entries by the Vesnins, Melnikov, Ilya Golosov, were particularly notable); the Arkos Joint-Stock Company (notably projects by the Vesnins, Melnikov, Ilya Golosov, Krinsky); the Soviet pavilion at the Paris exhibition (notably Melnikov, Ladovsky, Ginzburg and Ilya Golosov); the House of Textiles (Ilya Golosov and Ginzburg); the Moscow Central Telegraph Office (Shchusev, the Vesnins and Vegman); the Gospromyshlennost House in Kharkov (Serafimov, Felger and Kravets, Dmitriev), and a number of others.

Two Vesnin projects of 1924 must be singled out for the influence they exerted on the evolution of Constructivism. The first was the Leningradskaya Pravda design, extensively discussed in the architectural press worldwide and generally acknowledged as one of the artistically most accomplished pieces of twentiethcentury architecture. This product ofAlexander Vesnin's great talent brilliantly illustrated the vast aesthetic potentialofmodern architecture. Its well-balanced composition, the finely developed proportions of the elevations, the organic combination of ferro-concrete framework, glass surfaces and metal armatures, convey a sense of aesthetic perfection.
