51819 Krasin. Conveyer viaduct of Shatura Power Station, 1925. Detail of conveyer (518). General view of the power station (519), built by the architects Dubovsky, Ivan Burov and Brzhostovsky.

![](images/dd2dc272ebb5176b6c6e916e8ef62c1fe66aadc9a9fb297cafd8b1d8ccb58eb5.jpg)

![](images/b9fdf0b9050e8610644abccee55ddf3f438d348a6c01f3ba46254cbe78712763.jpg)

![](images/1d90c68c21d9c3958394148aef981dbef7231c18b9423070d7806a98a93156f5.jpg)

The other outstanding Vesnin project was for the Arkos building in Moscow, remarkable for its pronounced modern look. The building was located on a corner site, and their scheme offered an austere composition, including a clearly outlined ferro-concrete framework in which the first four storeys, nh tirely glazed.

The impact of these designs at a time of experiment in architecture was exceptionally powerful and immediately attracted attention on all sides. The Vesnin Arkos project greatly impressed architects at the exhibition of competition entries by the newness of its look and the freshness ofits architectural conception. Eye-witnesses recall its effect even now, so powerful that by comparison with it all the other entries seemed toshrivel and fade. It was a superb piece ofdraughtsmanship, strikingly coloured, which concentrated the movement in favour of the new architecture and provided a turning point in the artistic experiments of many architects.

The Vesnin Arkos project was awarded a first prize and was immediately imitated. While the other entries for this competition had substantially differed from it in appearance, the prize-winning entries for the next two major competitions - the Central Telegraph Office and the House of Textiles - held more or less at the same time in 1925, virtually all followed the Vesnins' Arkos entry. The Constructivist ranks were swelled even further in the course of many subsequent competitions held in 192627.

The success of the innovative trend in Soviet architecture at these competitions, and of Constructivism above all, caused it to be followed by other architects who merely regarded it as a new fashion. A Constructivist manner might well have spread and turned into a purely external Constructive Style. The Constructivist leaders were aware of this and fought stylization by concentrating on the formulation of a set of artistic beliefs and the foundation of an organization uniting the new movement's supporters. The Union of Contemporary Architects - Osa - was set up towards the end of 1925 with Alexander Vesnin as chairman, Moisei Ginzburg and Viktor Vesnin as deputychairmen, and Georgy Orlov as secretary. In their request for the registration of the new Union the representatives defined its principles as follows:

Osa brings together individuals closely linked by a common ideology and carries on collective theoretical, scientific, technical,and practical work in a truggle agaist inertia and the vestiges of the past. .

Osa collectively determines and gives practical substance to the new architectural form functionally derived from the purpose of a particular building, the materials used in it, its construction and other production requirements in accordance with the concrete demands arising from the socialist construction of the country."8

As noted earlier, architectural Constructivism had initially been organizationally connected with Inkhuk. A group of likeminded individuals gradually gathered round Alexander Vesnin in 192324, mainly recruited from his students, such as Barshch, Andrei Burov, Sobolev, Komarova, and Nikolai Krasilnikov, in Vkhutemas. A number of prominent members of Lef (the Left Front of the Arts) directed by Mayakovsky, also took part in the group, notably Brik and Lavinsky.

This architectural group was formally embodied within Inkhuk at the beginning of 1924, in fact replacing Ladovsky's Working Group of Architects, the members of which had founded an independent creative association - Asnova - in 1923. In its controversies with the Rationalists, the new group had consistently drawn attention by 1924 to the role of a building's functional purpose as the basis for generating its form (e.g. Ladovsky's lecture in February 1924), thus providing a link with the promoters of Production Art.

The Lef architectural group at Inkhuk thereby served as the nucleus for the Constructivist organization, Osa. Osa was joined by some of Ginzburg's followers from the Moscow Institute of Civil Engineers, e.g. Vegman and Vladimirov, a group of Viktor Vesnin's pupils from the Moscow Higher Technical Institute - MVTU - (among them Orlov, Nikolaev and Fisenko) and some Production Art Constructivists from Leningrad headed by Nikolsky. The membership of Osa included Alexander and Viktor Vesnin, Barshch, Andrei Burov, Vegman, Vladimirov, Gan, Ginzburg, Ilya Golosov, Leonidov, Nikolaev, Nikolsky, Nikolai Krasilnikov, Orlov, Pasternak, Nikolai Sokolov, Khiger, Yalovkin and two engineers, Loleit and Karlsen. From 1926 onwards, Osa published its own periodical called Sovremennaya arkhitektura (Contemporary Architecture) or SA, with Alexander Vesnin and Ginzburg, the founders and leaders of the group, as its chief editors.

## The Functional Method

The first issue of Contemporary Architecture (SA) carried the slogan 'Contemporary architecture must crystallize the new socialist way of life.' The drive to participate in producing types of buildings intended for new social purposes - 'the social condensers of the era' runs through all Constructivist theory and practice.

The movement's basic theories and methods were set out in its periodical. In the early days of Constructivism at the beginning of the 1920s, its followers mainly stressed the constructional effectiveness of the new architectural form. At this later stage, on the other hand, they concentrated on its functional quality, one of the main bulwarks against the threat of a stereotyped Constructive Style. Their doctrinal statements constantly emphasized that Constructivism was an artistic method, not a style, and that it demanded a specific attitude with regard to questions of construction, function and form. SA proclaimed: 'Architect, do not counterfeit technological shapes, but master the constructor's methods instead.'

In a series of articles about Constructivist theory, Ginzburg set out the principles of a so-called Functional Method which was then further elaborated and given wide currency. Function, in so far as the Constructivists were concerned, must be given priority in the preparation of a spatial composition for a building. The Functional Method proceeded from the functional requirements of a given structure, such as the siting ofindividual units of accommodation in relation to each other, the flow chart of the activity in question, and so on.

Constructivists made great use of the so-called 'pavilion method' of composition, whereby a building or complex was divided into blocks or units of space in accordance with their purpose. These were then linked by corridors or bridges in accordance with the requirements of the overall functional process involved.

The Functional Method was in fact a fully developed artistic programme. It confronted the architect with a host of challenges, such as taking part in the invention of new types of buildings; combating eclecticism; fighting for the industrialization of the building process, for standardization, the industrial manufacture of components and the conversion of building work into a process of assembling standard components. The Constructivists promoted the utmost use of scientific methods in the process of architectural composition and, as a result, sometimes oversimplified the complex interaction between the function of a building and its spatial organization. They also tended to exaggerate the importance of purely rational considerations in the creative architectural process, probably inevitably at a time when the latest technological achievements were being applied to building work.

The Constructivists made great use in planning and spatial compositions of new inventions from applied science in fields such as thermal insulation and acoustics, many of which were introduced into design and building work at that time.

Attempts were even made to quantify project work itself scientifically, so that the selection of a particular plan and spatial composition would largely be governed by the application of mathematical formulae, tables and graphs as proposed by Nikolai Krasilnikov.

Generally speaking, however, when dealing with form in functional terms, the Constructivists were most concerned with a structure's social function rather than its utilitarian aspect, though the very simplicity of the new architectural forms justified them in a sense for functionally structural purposes. Utility was no longer burdened by aesthetic considerations and the visual concept was derived from the structure's social context. For the Constructivists, the architect's basic task was to organize a new way of life. In so far as they were concerned, technology was the means of generating a new and functional form. Functionalist leaders, such as Vesnin and Ginzburg, believed that there was a qualitative difference between a merely utilitarian form and a functional form that had achieved aesthetic perfection and become an object of art. The application of the Functional Method did not, in so far as they were concerned, imply that only one single form could serve a given function and construction. They set great store by the artist's imaginative power and by the special effects of perception.

Osa's first exhibition was held in 1927, its first conferenc in April 1928 and its first congress in May 1929.

## Engineers and the new architecture

The creative principles of innovative trends such as Rationalism and Constructivism were well understood by engineers and constructors bent on applying the latest technological achievements. Great engineers such as Shukhov, Loleit and Krasin, were happy to work with the innovative architects. They jointly explored new ways of developing architecture, and engineers became active members of Asnova and Osa.

The switch at the start of the 1930s to an intensive application of methods derived from the past is often primarily explained by the inability of the country's building industry to meet the technical requirements set by the standards of architecture prevailing during the 1920s.

It would, of course, be wrong to deny that the Soviet authorities had inherited a low level of building technique from Tsarist Russia. It was no lower, however, than that obtaining in many other areas of the national economy which equalled performance elsewhere in the world during the era of the First Five Year Plans. Moreover, as pointed out earlier, Russia at the turn of the ny ot r theep tries of Europe and America in the number, standard and originality of its building and engineering works. There were therefore no good reasons for Soviet building work to develop more slowly than other branches of socialist industry. And if building technology was seriously retarded in the Soviet Union during the thirties, forties and fifties, this was due in large measure to the orientation of architecture during that period, which did not always encourage the introduction into building work of the latest improvements in technology.

In the twenties, on the other hand, Soviet architecture set tasks for the building industry which prompted the employment of new materials and structural elements, and raised building standards. Engineering technology in the building industry achieved great successes during this period, such as the metal structures by Shukhov and Krasin, Loleit's work in the field of ferro-concrete, the elaboration of modern timber work by Karlsen and the production of new hyperbolic paraboloid roofing by Makarova. The very latest structures and building materials, as well as modern production methods, were applied in the construction of engineering and industrial buildings, such as Shukhov's radio tower in Moscow in 1922 and Krasin's viaduct at the Shatura power station in 1925.

Engineers not only helped the architects to elaborate and implement their ideas in technical terms, but influenced their formal experiments, both directly and indirectly. Thus, the pronounced skeletal look of early Constructivist compositions is closely related to the latticed metal structures which gained widespread currency even before the Revolution. It is also worth stressing the influence of the quantitative theory taught in higher architectural and constructional educational establishments on the forms developed in the new architecture. This led to a maximum application of modern technology in many student projects dating from the 1920s and lent additional point to the formal experiments which they embodied.

![](images/cddc498ab3dded954c50c6b636786dc7abefded0f46b2ab94b24a3e6622534e4.jpg)

Creative innovation in the second half of the 1920s: teachers, schools, groupings

Canonization:

a threat to the formal tenets of the new trend

By the mid-1920s, the innovative trends came to exert a decisive influence on creative experimentation in Soviet architcture. The next stage involved the reconciliation of uncompromising attitudes in the quarrels between Rationalists and Constructivists. In the second half of the decade the main task was to achieve an organic synthesis of all that had been secured so far by the Constructivist Functional Method and Rationalist experiments in formal aesthetics.

The Functional Method undoubtedly consolidated what had been done in the field of spatial composition, but its strictly rational approach to functional construction did not in itself point the way to successful composition.

Innovative architects who were struggling against traditionalism during the 1920s relied on the achievements of science and technology, and constantly stressed the importance of a rational approach to architectural work. This led them to tip the scales in favour of rational factors in architectural design, as opposed to emotional ones. It also increasingly directed attention to the functional justification of a structure, often at the expense of artistic considerations, particularly in Constructivist designs. The technological factors introduced into a design by the architect were essentially rational, and tended very much to displace the emotional or intuitive aspects involved in the work. Inevitable as this would seem to have been under the circumstances, it involved a danger that this transient phenomenon might become a permanent feature of the new architecture.

Conservatism, as many innovators saw it, was chiefly a matter of drawing on the past for architectural forms and methods. These architects were not always clearly aware as they fought against eclecticism and Classicist stylization that an equally serious threat to every new movement from within was a conservative tendency to erect its own means and methods into canons. Such tendencies increasingly made themselves felt in the new architecture, not least because long years of eclecticism and stylization had conditioned architects in favour of canons that fetter and limit creative imagination. Orthodox Constructivists were also sometimes hampered in this respect by a simplistic view of how shape and the basic functional structure of a building were interrelated.

Complex asymmetrical compositions designed to satisfy the functional connection between various structural components proved to lack artistic unity. Moreover, stylization in the Constructive manner could mean that individual elements of the new architecture would be canonized and become merely external and formal features of buildings, imparting to them a stylistic stamp of approval.

An outdated architectural concept and a lack of compositional ideas were often cloaked by a fashionable covering of glass and concrete. Stylistic clichés apart, progress for modern architecture involved the solution of a host of problems by organic means, in particular by imparting expression to buildings.

## llya Golosov's Constructive Style

Golosov, as we have already pointed out, never based his work on any single style and worked with more or less equal success in a variety of idioms while remaining faithful to his own personal methods of spatial composition. The Vesnins' brilliant entries for the Arkos and Leningradskaya Pravda competitions impressed him deeply and converted him to the methods developed by the Constructivists in search of an artistic image. A clearly accented framework and a prevalence of glazed surfaces became characteristic features of Golosov's output after 1925. He turned out a series of artistically first-rate projects after joining the Constructivists and did much to spread an understanding of this trend. Golosov was the most successful interpreerCnstuctivist methods in the mid20s. He was cr tainly uniquely popular as a Constructivist architect in the years that separated the Vesnins' triumphal success from the flowering of Ivan Leonidov's work at the end of the decade. And yet, although he did brilliant work in the Constructivist manner, Golosov was never a true Constructivist when all is said and done. A close scrutiny of his output reveals that he treated Constructivism more as an outward trend than as a functional method. He nevertheless rose as a star in the Constructivist firmament and for a time outshone even such leaders of the movement as Alexander Vesnin and Ginzburg. Golosov's rise as a Constructivist started with the award of a first prize in the competition for a House of Textiles in Moscow in 192Thereafter, he took part in virtually all the competitions which were proliferating at that time and carried away what was probably  recor umber  prizes. He seeminsble in the  vy  worHeee bined ultra-modern Constructivist forms, boldly introduced a conjunction of contrasting volumes, such as a cylinder and a parallelepiped, with planes composed of glass and blind walls, and confidently modelled his façades. He displayed great mastery in demonstrating the artistic potential of Constructivism aq sal, still fairly limited at that time.

In 192527, when many architects were switching to Constructivism, Golosov even came to be regarded briefly as something of an artistic leader. Constructivism was becoming fashionable and many new converts began to work in a sort of 'Constructive' style. Golosov probably contributed a good deal to this development.

Careful inspection of Golosov's Constructivist projects, howevrtra i orha od, clearly shows that he was not primarily concerned with the externals of style, then as at all other times. In practice, the Constructive Style does not play a leading part in any of his projects dating from these years in so far as the appearance of the buildings is concerned.

It is true that all projects by Golosov dating from the second half of the 1920s are outwardly typical of Constructivism. But even here the spatial composition is invaria bly based on a large complex form which harks back to the methods of spatial organization elaborated by Golosov in his theory of the structure of architectural organisms. His principle that the basic architectural image must be a large spatial form which retains its power of expression even when some of its details are replaced by others, continued to dominate his output even during his Constructivist period. Golosov was attracted to the outward stylistic opportunities offered byConstructivism, such as the generously glazed frameworks. This is particularly characteristic of 1925-26 when Golosov designed a series of exquisitely proportioned office building projects, with beautifully traced frameworks and windows. Most outstanding in this respect were the competition entries for the House of Textiles in 1925, jointly with Boris Ulinich, the Rusgertorg in 1926 and the Elektrobank in 1926.

Golosov took special trouble in drawing these façades and designing frameworks and openings, but his idiosyncrasies showed nevertheless: he could not simply leave the façades plain, or put up with the wall as a 'theme', even though it was, to all appearances, an ultra-modern piece of glazed framework. Each of these projects also exhibited a touch of plasticity - a combination of right-angled prisms in the House of Textiles, a cylinder of glass at the angle of the Elektrobank and a halfcylinder in the Rusgertorg.

Golosov soon exhausted the possibilities offered by glazed frameworks and increasingly reverted, towards the end of the 1920s, to the large spatial elements which he always saw as the basis for outwardly expressive buildings.

## Symbolist and Expressionist responses to engineering structures - Lyudvig and Chernikhov

The Constructive Style and its ever more canonical aesthetic modes earnedpopularity and wide-ranging application,but also stimulated a search for ways of dislodging the aesthetic stereotypes connected with it. During the second half of the 1920s, the quest for variety in the approach to architectural composition developed not only within the innovative trend itself, but also at the interface between the innovative trends -Constructivism and Rationalism  and Symbolist Romanticism, as well as Classicism.

As to the Symbolists, one should bear in mind those architects who set out to integrate engineering techniques into Symbolist and Expressionist compositions in order to resolve functional, structural and technological problems. One of the most original of these was Genrikh Lyudvig. He had been trained as an engineer as well as an architect, and seemed to combine a strictly scientific, engineering designer's approach with that of an artist inspired by the dynamic forms born of revolutionary symbolism. Classicism marked his student work in 1920-21 and he came to new architecture by way of both formal symbolism and the extensive practice of building technology and applied science, a sort of 'Symbolist Functionalism' grounded in the achievements of science and technology.

This can already be seen in his first major work, an entry for the Moscow Palace of Labour competition in 1922-23, in which Symbolist shapes combine with others, derived from the technological purpose of the buildings: a spiralling tower of traditional Symbolist design, together with a ferro-concrete cantilevered pad answering contemporary engineering requirements on to which aircraft could be lowered in front of their hangars by means of a special crane-lift. The acoustics of the main hall have been very closely studied, with the speaker's platform as their natural focus, and with facilities for acoustically tuning the accommodation as a whole.

In 1924, Lyudvig submitted a design for the Soviet embassy in Ankara built up from simplified Neo-Classicist forms. He also submitted a design (which was not carried out) for an Afghan embassy in Turkey, based on a saw-toothed plan.

In 1926, he provided Kemal Pasha with a project for the Turkish President's summer palace at Ceflik, consisting of a combination of offset rectangular apartments diagonally intersected by corridors and galleries, with a large number of terraces under a vertical dominant consisting of a mooring tower for dirigibles.

The originality of Lyudvig's talent is best exemplified by designs in which the symbolic shapes which he favoured, such as the spiral and the five-pointed star, are combined with a novel functional and technological solution embodying some characteristic engineering concept. The designs for a Columbus Monument in Santo Domingo in 1929 and for the Palace of Soviets in Moscow in 1931-32 are typical of his competition entries.

True to his convictions, Lyudvig covered the engineering aspect of the Columbus Monument, as well as the symbolism of this memorial complex. He lined the shore with a green vegetative acoustic barrier, its width calculated according to a formula, together with rows of trees, the height of which was governed by a graph.

For the first round of the Palace of Soviets competition, Lyudvig designed a pentagonal building surmounted by a stepped pyramid. For the second round, he proposed a gently tapering cylinder on a pentagonal foundation and, wound around it, a spiral car ramp for tourist access to the viewing platform on the roof and ease of maintenance of the outer walls while the building was in use.

Lyudvig's brilliant command of engineering considerations inconstruction and architecture is demonstrated by two projects for platform shelters in leisure parks. The first is a temporary guyed structure with wooden uprights, wire struts and a tarpaulin cover, which was erected in the Moscow Central Park for Culture and Leisure in 1929. The second was intended to cover a stadium holding an audience of 6,000 for stage and musical performances. The structure was based on a ferro-concrete ring, one side of which was supported at the bottom by the foundation of the building and at the top by the segmented cone of metal girders and glass forming the side of the amphitheatre. A circular screen enclosed in the ferro-concrete ring reflected the sound towards the amphitheatre.

The work of Yakov Chernikhov exemplifies another approach to the use of technology in order to impart outward expressiveness to buildings. He was well versed in matters of engineering, had perfect command of both perspective drawing and architectural draftsmanship, and created a large number of 'architectural fantasies' during the second half of the 1920s.

These 'fantasies' display an original amalgam of Symbolist Romanticism, Rationalism and Constructivism. Many of them reveal an inventive talent, but, despite superficial differences between them, the vast majority are no more than variations on familiar themes. There are few original discoveries here or any great attempt at the higher reaches of form-generation, such as characterize the output of Melnikov and Leonidov, for example.

## Zholtovsky's Harmonized Constructivism

Overtly traditionalist concepts could no longer hold their own against innovations when the new movement came to dominate Soviet architecture in the second half of the 1920s. As a result, many traditionalists rallied to the new architecture. Even such stalwart Classicists as Zholtovsky and Fomin came to experiment on the frontier between innovation and Classicism.

As we have noted earlier, although they both harked back to the Classical tradition, Zholtovsky regarded proportion and harmony as the most important elements in Classical composition, while Fomin laid stress on the individual idiom of the Classical order involved. This determined the difference between the means and methods they employed when they attempted to find a place or their work in the new arhiteure, starting from a Classicist position,

In the second half of the 1920s, when Zholtovsky returned from Italy, where he had lived during 1924-25, he again tried to found a Neo-Renaissance school by gathering about him a group of talented young architects. Its mainstay was provided by Georgy Golts, Mikhail Parusnikov, Ivan Sobolev and Sergei Kozhin, described in architectural circles of that period as 'Zholtovsky's Quadriga'. The first two had completed their training under Zholtovsky at the start of the 1920s, while the others had graduated in Vkhutemas from Alexander Vesnin's studio and had therefore been trained in the spirit of Constructivism.

Given the complex circumstances prevailing at that time, hooH produced basic models incorporating many compositional methods and forms derived from the Italian Renaissance and, at the same time, took up the new architecture with the intention of artistically harmonizing its utilitarian forms. He used the Renaissance compositions in the design of public buildings - the Makhachkala House of Soviets, the Gosbank (State Bank) building in Moscow — and the new forms for industrial purposes - the Ivanteevka factory, the power station boiler houses for the Moges, Kievges and the Dneproges competition projects.

Zholtovsky came to set great store by industrial architecture. He could see that changing aesthetic criteria had made his Renaissance designs uncompetitive and that his skill would be best and most convincingly displayed within the latest manifestations of new architecture.

As a result, a peculiar trend, a sort of 'Harmonized Constructivism', emerged on the borderline between Neo-Renaissance and Constructivist formal experiments during the 1920s. The industrial structures designed by Zholtovsky's group were characterized by an aesthetically refined composition of façades. The Renaissance wall became a model for a carefully proportioned and strictly symmetrical façade. In his polemics against new architectural concepts, Zholtovsky defended the leading part played by the façade in the creation of an architectural image against innovators who increasingly rejected it as a central element for architectural purposes, not only in industrial design, but in other types of construction as well.

He reserved the central place in any composition to the front elevation, Glazing was widely used to provide a neutral background, while the structural framework was only picked out when this was required in order to articulate the composition, or as a stylistic component.

The Neo-Renaissance school certainly injected a potent cultural element into industrial architecture. The buildings conceived by Zholtovsky and his pupils stand out to advantage among the many only too drearily utilitarian industrial structures of those times. This fact in itself did much to establish the authority of Zholtovsky's school among young architects.

The rising generation's close interest in Zholtovsky's ventures into industrial architecture was also due in part to the threat of canonization which hung over the innovative forms and methods described earlier, and the resulting disillusionment with the Constructive Style.

## Fomin's Proletarian Classicism

Ivan Fomin's determination to combine Classicist and new architectural concepts led him to undertake yet another attempt in this direction. We have already seen how he had tried to adapt the Classical order to early Soviet circumstances by picking out the most 'heroic' and elementary forms of what he described as Red Doric. By the mid-1920s, he was switching from the selection of Classical forms to their simplification, a process which he described as the reconstruction of the Classical order. A system of composition gradually emerged in his projects and became the basis of his new conception, of 'Proletarian Classicism'. He superimposed a Classical order on the armature of a façade which was largely glazed and without concealing the framework imparted a Classical look to it. By 1928, Fomin's Proletarian Classicism had become a full-blown artistic system backed by an elaborate theoretical justification.

Fomin believed that a modern style should be at once international and democratic. He suggested that such a style should be based on a severely simplified version of a Classical order, standardized and stripped of all ornament. In other words, he proposed that only the framework of a Classical order should be preserved. He regarded it as essential, for instance, that the window area should be extended so that the whole surface between the supports - or columns - of the building's framework was glazed. All versions of an order should be simplified to the limit. Complex cornices should be replaced by simple lintels, all fascia, moulding, capitals and plinths should be suppressed and tapering columns replaced by simple cylinders.

Fomin used this system in his design for the Dynamo building in Moscow, in which he linked six of the storeys by paired columns, while the seventh acted as a sort of entablature pierced by rounded windows. Fomin regarded paired columns, a basic component of Proletarian Classicism, as among his personal artistic creations. These allowed the design of a single multistorey building to be unified by means of a single order, thereby creating the effect of one monumental pile.

The new Mossoviet (Moscow Soviet) building, erected in 1929-30, was regarded by Fomin himself as the most successful embodiment of his Proletarian Classicist concept. The attractively proportioned paired columns enclosed square windows and were topped by a plinth surmounted by an attic storey pierced by low rectangular windows. Fomin used Proletarian Classicism in a series of projects dating from the early 1930s, such as the Moscow Workers' Faculty, the Ivanovo-Voznesensk Polytechnical Institute and the NKPS (People's Commissariat for Transport) in Moscow.

In justifying his choice of Classicism as the basis for a new style, he pointed not only to the universal acceptance it commanded, but equally to the regularity and discipline inherent in the compositions derived from it. He sought in the 'reconstructed' order a means of providing both variety and continuity in a way that would not conflict with new structural technology. Fomin strove to impart a renewed sense of architectural relevance to the tectonic approach of Classicism by combining an order based on it with the use of ferro-concrete, the proportions involved having been adjusted to the efficient use of this new material. As he saw it, far from conflicting with the principles of Classical order, a ferro-concretefamework would oen up new potentialities for this age-old architectural system.

Proletarian Classicism was akin to the Neo-Classical experiments then being carried out by, among others, Auguste Perret in France, Gunnar Asplund in Sweden and Marcello Piacentini in Italy. Two main movements concerned with form developed in Europe early in the twentieth century as a reaction against eclecticism and the Moderne: a Classical revival and a new functionally constructive approach to architectural design. The Neo-Classical variant of this trend  to which Fomin's school, among others, belonged-strove to achieve both the rationalizing aesthetics of Classicism and a rational use of new materials. This was no mere Classical revival, but a determination to make a ferro-concrete framework serve the working principles implicit in a Classical order.

## A New stage in design

It was, however, not Golosov's Constructive Style, Chernikhov's Symbolism and Expressionism, Zholtovsky's Harmonized Constructivism or Fomin's Proletarian Classicism that impelled the new architecture on to a higher level in the second half of the 1920s.

An influx of fundamentally new ideas was now required, not least in matters of form. Yet all the experimentation described so far during the late 1920s, and much else besides, was either a matter of developing and perfecting what had been devised so far or of injecting means of expression derived from symbolism, or the art of the past, into the new methods and forms.

The new architecture had been launched by artists, such as Malevich and Tatlin, and architects, such as Ladovsky and Alexander Vesnin, whose creative concepts and artistic experiments were marked by originality of mind and true innovation. Their successors merely developed their ideas within the scope of such talents as they possessed, a respectable and necessary activity. There is nevertheless a fundamental difference between original talent and a mind, however brilliant, which develops and interprets what another has discovered.

As the signs that the formal methods of the new architecture were being erected into canons became ever more threatening, masters capable of discovering fundamentally new ideas and creative methods, fresh artistic thought and innovation were needed, capable of imparting to architectural concepts uniquely personal and pristine qualities. Two outstanding personalities, Konstantin Melnikov and Ivan Leonidov, made major contributions to the innovative forays of Soviet architects in the second half of the 1920s.Their output displays broadly original artistic concepts. These two architects were both capable of deep emotional response, but they bowed to the requirement for rationalization, in terms both of the functionally structural conception of a building and of its architecturally aestheticimage. Their experiments in formal aesthetics implied a wish to discard the established stereotype of what a building should look like.

![](images/dc406907a8cf1e26ab64f135af75b71a3ae960685f174dc34de3fe4615ed780f.jpg)

the twentieth century. He designed the Makhorka pavilion at the 1923 Moscow Agricultural and Handicraft Exhibition as an original spatial composition, with a blind, overhanging upper structure, anopen winding staircase, a vertical glazed shaft and a roof with a sharp diagonal slope. He developed this new approach to the design of exhibition buildings even further in the Soviet pavilion at the Paris International Exhibition in 1925, the first and immediately triumphantly successful appearance on the world stage of the new-born Soviet architecture. This pavilion stood out among the rest by its modern appearance- simple geometric forms, bold use of colour, glazed ltt agonally across a two-storey building laid out on a rectangular plan.

During the late 1920s and early 1930s, Melnikov produced a whole series of original designs which helped to extend the scope of experimental work and intensified polemics about form. Projects by Melnikov such as the Rusakov Club, his own residence, the Columbus Monument and the Palace of Nations demonstrated the possibilities of creating expressive spatial compositions by such means as overhanging elements, unusual forms of symmetry - such as symmetry about a horizontal axis, a generous use of diagonals etc.

Melnikov's merit during this complex period in the development of Soviet architecture was that he got rid of many conventions through his innovative projects and helped to remove psychological barriers hindering the acceptance of new forms.

He attached great importance to the functional approach and to achieving the most rational organization possible in any composition. But his main concern in connection with every building was to devise an appropriately expressive appearance for it, an artistic image that would incontrovertibly link its spatial organization with a novel approach to its interior design. All Melnikov's designs are marked by virtuosity in the treatment of their interiors. What is more, the means of spatial organization are never repeated and remain entirely original in each individual case.

Melnikov boldly extended the range of means and methods of expression in modern architecture and set great store by novelty in an architect's output. As he put it, 'Creativity begins at the point where one can say: "This is mine." This attitude to the production of original forms and images is evident throughout his work. His spatial compositions were striking by their novelty. But Melnikov never distorted a geometric form. Cylinders, cones, parallelepipeds, rhomboids and other elementary volumes freely combine, intersect, interpenetrate and are articulated with each other in a variety of ways while retaining their clearly defined geometrical identity.

The hallmark of Melnikov's architectural forms is their inner tension, rather than any showy dynamism, emphatic structure or excessively obvious balance. There are no idle elements in his compositions: everything is thoroughly resilient, but no display is made of the great inner strength held in reserve. In so far as he was concerned, architectural form should be like a tensed muscle, and this accounts for the overhanging components which he favoured, because they supplied the most loadbearing shape in visual terms.

## Leonidov, a poet of pure form

During the second half of the 1920s, Soviet architects not only successfully fought off the threatened canonization of stylistic features, but made a major contribution to the solution ofa series of problems affecting the treatment of form and a new approach to spatial organization in the architecture of their period.

One of the most outstanding creative personalities involved in these developments was Ivan Leonidov, an exceptionally gifted artist, inventor and original thinker. Le Corbusier called him 'a poet, and the hope of Russian Architectural Constructivism'.

Leonidov's graduation project for a Lenin Institute, drawn up in Alexander Vesnin's Vkhutemas studio, was shown at the first Moscow Exhibition of Contemporary Architecture in 1927. Alexander Vesnin saw in it the birth of a new architecture, and it was also highly commended by Ladovsky and Ginzburg.

The project provided for a large building complex on the Lenin hills comprising a library, a scientific research institute, an auditorium and other supplementary accommodation. The treatment of the large auditorium was unusual and was regarded at that time as breaking new ground. It consisted of a vast sphere raised above ground level on an openwork metal armature; the top half of the sphere was glazed over, while the lower part housed the auditorium. The whole structure was fankd by a vertical parallelepiped, an element in the same spatial composition, intended for a book store.

In 1928-30, Leonidov became the effective leader of Constructivism. He single-handedly represented this movement in a series of outstandingly important competitions and matched the entries submitted by entire teams working on behalf of various other organizations. During this period he produced a whole series of competition entries which stood out by the depth of their conception, their technical mastery and their graphic presentation, among them the Tsentrosoyuz (Central Union of Consumer Co-operatives) building in 1928, the House of Industry in 1929-30 and the Palace of Culture in 1930, all for Moscow, and the Socialist Settlement attached to the Magnitogorsk Industrial Combine in 1930. These projects tackled in an original way problems such as the siting of a cultural and social centre in the construction of a new town, the lay-out of a modern city complex and the relationship between areas of housing and their environment.

The principles of spatial organization in modern buildings worked out by Leonidov were to exert great influence on the further output of Soviet architects. He was among the first to sense, as early as the 1920s, the basic tendency of modern architectural design towards simplicity in the treatment of volume, which only secured widespread recognition during the 1940s and 1950s. He demonstrated in his projects that an extremely laconic use of geometrical volumes devoid of decoration was no less expressive in its effect than more elaborate compositions. Such utter restraint in the organization of space was truly innovative in the 1920s, at a time when many architects were still exploring the expressive potentialities of modern architecture by bringing greater complexity into compositions.

## Suprematist Constructivism - Nikolsky and Khidekel

Other means of overcoming the stylistic stereotypes which had established themselves were adopted by the leader of the Constructivists in Leningrad, Alexander Nikolsky, and Lazar Khidekel, a pupil of Malevich.

Nikolsky discarded Classicism soon after the Revolution, then came under the influence of the Cubo-Futurist methods employed by the followers of Symbolist Romanticism. Early in the 1920s, he produced a number of projects inspired by Cubo-Futurism, often oddly combined with themes derived from ancient Russian architecture. By the mid-1920s, however, his experiments took a different stylistic turn.

The designs then produced in Nikolsky's studio sought to create expressive compositions by means of large rectangular horizontal and vertical volumes contrastingly juxtaposed, with a generous use of colour, blind surfaces, either strictly symmetrical or sharply asymmetrical compositions, and violent contrasts of light and shade, at the expense of intersecting forms, overhanging elements and any extensive use of ledges.

The originality and polemical nature of Nikolsky's formal aesthetic experiments is largely accounted for by the situation that had arisen in the architectural world of Leningrad. In altering the city's appearance during the early post-Revolutionary years, the 'destructive' Cubo-Futurist experiments had shaken Classicism to its roots, but failed to destroy its compositional basis. By the mid-1920s, the new architecture was consolidating its position with every passing day, but had to cut through the canons imposed by the Classical order. Leningrad architects gradually succeeded in sloughing off traditional forms and ornaments, but they retained many ingrained habits — symmetry, for instance—and preferred the use of straightforward geometric volumes in the external design of buildings to Constructivist means of expression, such as exposed frameworks and large areas of glazing. The experimental designs of Malevich's group- the Arkitectons and the Planits - contributed to this approach after the move to Petrograd from Vitebsk in 1922.

Malevich's experiments with form, in which his students — Suetin, Khidekel and Chashnik - shared, were well known in Petrograd, where they had greatly influenced decorative art and architecture. After the Unovis move, Lazar Khidekel began ts rhiture  was thusbleiS-ideas it it from withi, rather thaidrecy, as in Nikolsky's case.

Khidekel's first projects go back to 192224, although he did not begin to concentrate on architecture until the middle of the decade. His Suprematist forms gained in realism as his interest in architecture increased and his compositions, such as his Aero-Club designs in 1922, are the first originating from this scol tnclueoorndwnoweingin what ha e viously been blind geometric volumes, thereby imparting a measure of architectural actuality to Suprematist projects. Khidekel studied in Nikolsky's studio from the mid-1920s onwards and the increasingly Suprematist Constructivist flavour of its output may well have been partly due to his influence.

It was in fact Khidekel who produced the first fully developed architectural project - a Workers' Club - in a Suprematist spirit in 1926, a design which already reflected all the basic features of Suprematist Constructivism. He went on to produce a whole series of designs in the later 1920s and early 1930s based on the formal aesthetic discoveries of the Suprematists. Suprematist Constructivism was an original approach to form and style in composition which left some mark on many innovative ventures by Leningrad architects in the 1920s.

At the same time, however, Classicist influences were also more significant in the output of Leningrad architects than in that of their Moscow colleagues. This was most obvious in the competition entries submitted from either side in the mid-1920s. The asymmetrical composition, glazing and bare frameworks of the Central Telegraph Office and House of Textiles in entries originating from Moscow contrasted with the symmetry of compositions incorporating simple geometrical shapes in designs submitted from Leningrad, as in those for a House of Culture in the city's Moscow-Narva district, and for the Gosprom (State Industry) building in Kharkov.

Such differences between the creative concepts prevalent in these two cities led to the application of conflicting criteria in the assessment of entries for competitions organized by the two local architectural associations. A split developed among these architects, so that many of those in Leningrad refused to enter competitions launched in Moscow, and vice versa.

As a result, the work of Nikolsky's group became fundamentally important because it helped to consolidate the process of creative experimentation by Soviet architects wherever they might be. It provided a synthesis of the artistic experimentation carried out in the three main Soviet artistic laboratories— in Moscow, Petrograd and Vitebsk - though Nikolsky rejected the increasingly stereotyped features identifying the various schools. This enabled him and the members of his studio to design truly original projects for clubs, schools, baths and stadiums. Nevertheless, even though it had assimilated the ideas of Moscow-based Constructivism, the Suprematist Constructivism which Nikolsky's group adopted was closer in terms of design to Malevich's experiments and Khidekel's architectural projects.

## The graduates of MVTU

Pre-Revolutionary Rational Architecture, as we have already said, did not provide a basis for an innovative trend immediately after the Revolution. It would, however, be wrong to deny it any role in the evolution of Soviet architecture.

In the 1920s, interaction between the tradition of Rational Architecture and Constructivism led to the formation of a school of industrial architecture, the representatives of which made a significant contribution to resolving problems not only in the lay-out of production and engineering structures, but also in their artistic formulation. An architectural section was set up in the Moscow Higher Technical Institute - MVTU - in 1918, which had by the mid-1920s trained a group of talented architects under the guidance of such distinguished representatives of Rational Architecture as Alexander Kuznetsov, as well as Viktor and Leonid Vesnin.

The teachers of architecture at MVTU differed from their opposite numbers in Vkhutemas by a more practical approach to the problems in hand and this largely determined their graduates' subsequent choice of work in the field of industrial and housing construction, as well as structural design. Nevertheless, the training of the MVTU architectural students was, Rational Architecture apart, influenced by the contemporary mood in Soviet art as a whole. A Creative Group gradually formed among these students. They arranged 'Wednesday' fixtures at which reports were read and information provided concerning architecture, the visual arts and poetry or new stage productions by Meyerhold and Tairov. These 'Wednesdays' began in 192223 and carried on until 1928, even after the members of the Creative Group had graduated. Among those who took part in them were Vladimir and Gennady Movchan, Nikolaev, Kalish, Fisenko, Meilman, Evgeny Popov and Sergei Turgenev.

These fixtures helped to shape the outlook on art of the young architects and to found a school, mainly in the field ofindustrial architecture.

The clearest examples of this school's orientation can be seen in projet aried out  Mosow by MVTUruats, such as the complexes of buildings for the Central Institute of Aero- and Hydrodynamics, Tsagi, and the All-Union Electro-Technical Institute, Vei; the laboratories of the Moscow Textile Institute and the headquarters building of the All-Union Electro-Technical Association.

These buildings combine functional and structural efficiency with the application of new architectural methods, such as the geometrically accurate articulation of volumes, contrasts between gazed and blind walls, and the juxtapositon o rec tangular and semicircular elements.

## Shchusev and the adoption of new architectural principles

The innovative trend achieved a dominant position in Soviet architecture during the second half of the 1920s and the early 1930s. One after another, architects belonging to an earlier generation, who immediately after the Revolution had seen the way forward in the greatest possible reliance on traditions, began to change sides. The evolution of Alexei Shchusev's output is a case in point. He was not a strict Classicist and found the picturesqueness and rich plasticity ofancient Russian architecture more attractive. He freely combined its forms with the new types of structures and the Moderne, which he found more tolerable than Classicism.

Shchusev was an uncommonly gifted artist, whose enthusiasm was readily captured by new styles. If some architectural conception attracted him even though it conflicted with his own at that moment, or if he considered that it answered present requirements better, he was apt to switch to it.

This degree of adaptability took him to the verge of eclecticism, but also enabled him to appreciate innovation more broadly and objectively than many other architects of the older generation. It was due to his support, for instance, that Melnikov's Makhorka pavilion was included in the 1923 exhibition and, unlike Zholtovsky, he did not consider the Vesnins' Palace of Labour project unacceptable.

The evolution of Shchusev's approach in the 1920s is clearly illustrated by the process of desgning and building the Lenin Mausoleum in Moscow's Red Square.

Shchusev's first design for a temporary wooden mausoleum piit ument consisting of four columns carrying an entablature. Tight construction schedules and building difficulties prevented the project from being fully carried out and the first mausoleum remained unfinished, with only the base completed.

In the course of designing the second wooden mausoleum, Shchusev attempted to return to his initial idea by crowning a stepped base with an extended colonnade, either circular or rectangular in plan. But the spare forms of the unfinished first mausoleum fitted well into the surroundings of Red Square and the very fact of their presence influenced all further design. As sketch followed sketch, the colonnade gradually shrank until it ultimately became the uppermost tier of the stepped design. Stylistically, the second wooden mausoleum was treated in the Classical manner for compositional and formal purposes, with columns, pilasters, consoles, and so on.

Between 1924, when one wooden mausoleum followed the other, and 1929, when the stone one was designed, Shchusev's artistic outlook completely altered. This change followed the success of the Vesnin Arkos project. Shchusev allied himself with the innovative movement and produced a competition entry for the Moscow Central Telegraph Office in its spirit. This new approach equally affected the design and construction of the stone mausoleum in 1929-30. He was set the task of perpetuating the composition of the wooden one, which had become a familiar feature of Red Square,and of translating it into stone. Nonetheless he completely discarded the traditional architectural elements incorporated in the previous monument and concentrated on spatial composition, so as to stress the simplicity of the geometric forms employed, the proportions of the composition and the relationship between the colours involved.

Many other older architects, such as Vladimir Shchuko and Vladimir Gelfreikh, who had previously worked with traditional forms, similarly went over to the innovative movement during the second half of the 1920s.

## A unified new trend

Thecy  n ther So chitectural devement, c with itwn s leary rmu lated tenets, helped to impart shape and cohesion to the innovative movement. By the end of the 1920s, however, these indivil ual thnc points for the innovators, though not before they had playedan important part in the struggle against eclecticism, the shaping of a new artistic orientation and the establishment of an identifiably Soviet architecture. By the beginning of the First Five Year Plan, in 1928-32, objective circumstances favoured joint action by all the innovators at a time when the construcion of new towns and settlements, as well as industrial premises, dwellings and public buildings, was being carried out on a grandiose scale. The existence of different associations was beginning to hamper united action by Soviet architects.

One sign of the times, among others, was the abandonment of the fundamental ideological struggle between Rationalists and Constructivists, who all turned to the solution of the most important practical problems thrown up by industrialization. As we have already noted, Ladovsky left Asnova in 1928 with some of his followers and founded the Union of Architect-Planners, ARU, whose members set out to solve the theoretical and practical problems involved in socialist town planning.

In the same year, Ginzburg also left his theoretical researces in O to hed a rouprchitects in theTyoy Section of the Committee for Construction of the RSFSR (Russian Republic), investigating in depth the design of new types of inexpensive dwellings.

Early in 1929, the Presidium of Osa, which comprised Alexander Vesnin, Ginzburg, Leonidov, Khiger and Orlov, suggested that individual architectural associations should be merged in a single Federation of Revolutionary Architects. Not only did this prove impossible, but a further independent grouping was founded in the same year — the All-Union Proletarian Architects'Association, Vopra, which numbered among its members Alabyan, Baburov, Vlasov, Zaslavsky, Ivanov, Kochar, Alexander Krestin, Kryukov, Mazmanyan, Matsa, Mikhailov, Mordvinov, Nikolai Polyakov and Simbirtsev.

The part taken in competitions at the turn of the decade by representatives of the various groups brought an element of creative rivalry into their debates. Each organization followed the example of Asnova and ARU in setting up a special team for each major competition, although their various entries hardly diverged from the general stylistic orientation of the innovative movement. There was also a good deal of cross-fertilization between the groups concerned.

The closeness of the views held by these groups became apparent whenever they combined to resist traditionalist trends. Thus a joint protest by Osa, Asnova, ARU and Vopra was organized against the decision to build the Lenin Library on Classical lines. A joint meeting of architectural associations to discuss participation in the design for a Palace of Soviets resolved on a united front in opposition to conservative tendencies. All this contributed to the consolidation of artistic and organizational attitudes among innovative architects.

## Growing controversy within the new trend

Despite the gathering momentum of the movement for creative unity among innovative trends, dissensions within the new Soviet architecture actually sharpened, largely owing to the activity of Vopra members. The creation of this new group, at a moment when objective circumstances already pointed the way to a common programme uniting all progressive Soviet architects, greatly complicated the situation and converted polemics about art into internecine warfare. In the initial statement of their organization's aims, and a number of articles by Alexei Mikhailov and Arkady Mordvinov among others, the supporters of Vopra claimed the sole right of their group to speak for Proletarian Architecture and classified all other creative trends as 'openly bourgeois', 'fellow-travelling', and so on. They promoted a vulgarized version of Marxist-Leninist teaching about the role of art in social life, adopted cliquishness as a guiding principle and set about expelling from the world of socialist art all who were not of a like mind with them. They used rabblerousing methods of criticism under the pretence of fighting for Proletarian Architecture.

Many influential members of Vopra conducted a group warfare of sorts: they replaced intellectual leadership by crude administrative procedures, put forward inflated assessments of the - professionally inadequate  output of Vopra members and conducted destructive criticism of work by members of other organizations. Architects such as Leonidov, Melnikov, Ladovsky and Krutikov, whose work best displayed signs of experimental activity, took the full brunt of attacks from the Vopra side. Leonidov was one of the earliest targets of what became a genuine persecution. The expression Leonidovshchina 'Leonidovishness' was coined to describe formalism and wilfulness wherever it was found.

In some cases, Vopra members not unreasonably picked on a degree of narrowness in Constructivist and Rationalist positions and rightly stressed the need for more attention to artistic ideology in architecture. But they unwarrantably attempted to translate disagreements between individual architectural trends into political terms. The lack of an articulated and welldefined Vopra programme increasingly shifted the debate into the area of scholastic disputations about formulae and definitions. By declaring that the Constructivist and Rationalist movements, which included some of the most prestigious creative architects of the period, were 'unproletarian', the supporters of Vopra contributed to a marked weakening of the main trend in Soviet architecture of the 1920s and early 1930s, and to an increase in the influence of those who favoured a recourse to tradition.

The traditionalist trends contemporaneous with the innovative movement had lost most of their influence by the mid-1920s. They revived at the end of that decade: old tendencies and schools were resurrected, and new ones developed as part of a move to revert as far as possible to the architectural heritage.

![](images/e19db1d6f0e45f2fc824027d6277d42c6dae79acaaae8a23ed2913bdcac5ac86.jpg)

![](images/a35eee4269391cd21b8aef3d9dccd36de52cf1bc44e67a85698ae49dfea0b4d2.jpg)

# The problem of nationalism and internationalism

## Changes in the relationship between the national and international

T are at once international and national in character. There are fatures which link and unite it with that ofother nations, just as others make it distinctive,impartoriginality to it, and even a measure of uniqueness. Given favourable circumstances, a national architecture may very effectively produce features that are initially uniquely national, but are subsequently adopted by other nations.

The part played by international features in Soviet architecture grew sharply after the October Revolution. This was already obvious in Tatlin's Monument to the Third International, in 1919-20, and remained true of Rationalist and Constructivist designs. Pride of place, in this sense, goes to the Soviet pavilion at the Paris International Exhibition in 1925. It stood out among the majority of other national pavilions, whose designers had relied on a stylized approach to the heritage of their past, by the way in which it highlighted the innovative nature of its international image. Tatlin's tower, Melnikov's pavilion and many other designs by Soviet architects of that period were greeted by progressive architects abroad as the natural expression of the international nature of the world's first socialist architecture. They influenced the international development of the new architecture.

As compared with the past, however, the balance between national and international elements in Soviet architecture changed in the 1920s. Within the stylistic unity of the new architecture the designers undoubtedly reflected features proper to their nationality, not to be found elsewhere. Thus if one compares the works of Alexander Vesnin, Ladovsky, Melnikov, Leonidov or Ilya Golosov with those of their contemporaries abroad, one is struck by the specifically Russian character of their output, despite the deliberate internationalism which was one of the most marked features of Soviet architecture during that period.

## The search for a 'national' style

Between 1917 and 1934, the innovative movement had to contend not only with Classicism, but also with stylization in the national spirit' which had become widespread at that time, especially in the first decade after the Revolution.

Many constituent nationalities of the former Russian empire owned rich architectural traditions of their own, but were prevented from developing them unhindered. In a number of cases, in fact, the development of local architecture was forcibly broken off and another line imposed which the local people regarded as colonialist.

The Soviet creation of Union Republics equal in rank and the grant of autonomy to former colonial peoples stimulated an intense growth of national cultures and thereby  apparently quite inevitably -led to the rebirth of certain national architectural forms.

In these circumstances, recourse to traditional national forms did not necessarily imply inherent professional conservativism on the part of an architect. Traditional forms, for instance, often acted as symbols of national prestige.The 1923 Moscow Agricultural and Handicraft Exhibition provided vivid illustrations of this: the pavilions of Kirgizia, Turkestan, Azerbaidzhan, Armenia, Georgia, Tartary, the Crimea and other republics, with their traditional forms, spoke for local architecture by their appearance and represented original architectural exhibits in an attractive display of individual local cultural features. But difficulties arose when traditional local forms were picked as a basis for the development of a national architecture, especially when such forms harked back to some high point of architecture in the past.

The quest for national styles which began during early Soviet times reached its peak in the mid- 1920s. The new architecture had so far made no headway in a number of republics and left the field free for traditionalist trends drawing on the past. Wherever groups of trained architects existed locally, trends of this sort usually burgeoned into schools with explicit theoretical programmes. At the end of the 1920s and start of the 1930s, the rapid spread of the new architecture to cities and republics throughout the Soviet Union led to an increasingly bitter clash between the upholders of the various national styles and the innovators. The professional support of Moscow and Leningrad architects proved very important in the course of this conflict.

Schools of modern architecture developed during the 1920s in extremely adverse conditions due to: the widely differing levels of local traditions and degrees of development; a shortage of trained architects; the dominant position occupied by eclecticism and stylization; and the still rudimentary approach within the new architecture to problems of art in composition. It was not only a matter of overcoming eclecticism and 'national' stylization, but also of surviving the initial reaction to the introduction of the new architecture, which was apt to be regarded as an attempt to get rid of distinctive national features. A new national architecture could only be created within the general innovative framework after all the functional, structural and compositional aspects involved had been clearly worked out.

A new architecture did develop in the national republics during the period under review, but this process was hardly completed anywhere and was arrested early in the 1930s at various stages of evolution. The entire process may be divided into three broadly equal phases, each lasting some four to six years, in so far as many republics are concerned. In the first stage, while the Soviets were still fighting to establish themselves in many areas of the country, eclecticism and stylized historical traditions ruled. The second phase, in 192328, witnessed an intensive search for national styles based on local architectural traditions. When the third phase was reached at the turn of the decade, innovative trends gradually established themselves in the republics, and modern national schools of architecture took shape. These developments had been prepared throughout the 1920s by theoretical studies concerning the existence and nature of international and national factors in modern architecture, and by the training of young architects for the republics.

## Innovators and the problems raised by national and international considerations

The innovative architects did not deny that the national aspect ofarchitecture needed to be explored. But while the protagonists of "national styles' sought to draw on historical forms, the innovators mainly took account of the local way of life, climate and environment. They rejected, as a matter of principle, architectural forms associated in the past with the ruling classes and religion, because they believed that progressive national traditions were more accurately reflected in popular building work, less in its ornamentation than in its adaptation to local circumstances, such as climate and living conditions, and in its spatial organization.

The Constructivists were the most consistent in following this policy both in theory and practice. Ginzburg, for instance, based his design for the Crimean Republic pavilion at the 1923 Moscow Exhibition on a traditional Tatar dwelling, while Fedor Shekhtel modelled his Turkestan pavilion on a medieval mausoleum, Nikolai Bunyatov on a local shrine for Armenia and Yakov Syryshchev on mosques and palaces for Azerbaidzhan.

The best insight into the Constructivist attitude towards the role of national and international factors in modern architecture is afforded by the buildings designed for the various republics by leading Constructivists, such as those provided by the Vesnins in Baku and by Ginzburg in Alma-Ata.

## 'National' style and new architecture in Azerbaidzhan

In the mid-1920s, architecture in Azerbaidzhan was ruled by traditionalism based on the fifteenth-century Shirvan school. The Sabunchinsky railway station in Baku, designed by Nikolai Baev in 1926, represents the centrepiece of this national style, in which such 'eastern' elements as lanceolate arches, peshtaks or ornamental portals, domes and pendants were widely used.

By the turn of this decade, however, the new architecture had gained the upper hand. Its followers designed buildings that were modern in plan, composition and appearance, but tried to take account of local environmental and social characteristics.

The Workers' Clubs built in Baku and its surroundings from designs by the Vesnins are typical in this respect. They are laid out as a set of pavilions and occupy a complete block, with an internal courtyard or deep forecourt. Their spatial organization is complex and they are organically integrated into their surroundings by means of galleries, open terraces, loggias and balconies.

A whole series of large public buildings in Baku were desined in the spirit of the new architecureduring the late 1920s and early 1930s, such as the Press Palace by Semyen Pen, food factories by Sadykh Dadashev, Mikael Useinov and Konstantin Senchikhin, and some fine school complexes.
