made up of standard component units and designed for the accommodation of two to four thousand inhabitants.

The archetypal dwelling combine evolved towards the end of the 1920s. It was the outcome of two somewhat remotely connected developments. On the one hand, collective housing became increasingly complex and space was made within it for the requisite communal facilities. On the other hand, dwellings and public buildings became ever more closely and organically linke within each ity district. Adwelling combine maythere fore be regarded either as a collective dwelling that has grown tte physically communicate with each other.

Fully developed collective houses and residential areas, complete with public buildings, were already being designed by the mid-1920s.Interesting diploma projects for dwelling complexes included in city construction were designed in Vkhutemas in 1926 by both Turkus and Lamtsov under Ladovsky's direction. In Turkus' design the dwelling complex consisted of five small residential areas of two- to four-storey houses, linked by gangways at second-floor level with a communal building containing a kindergarten, a crèche and a canteen. All these residential areas, each of which represented a kind of embryonic dwelling combine, were grouped around a cultural and sports centre with a club and stadium. Lamtsov based his design on the wholly communal upbringing of children and feeding of the inhabitants in the complexes. He set aside a sector for buildings to serve children. The dwelling houses for adults were assembled out of two standard elements, the combination of which provided a large number of buildings differing from each other in the composition of their ground plan, elevation and outline. The club and canteen were located in a building at the centre of the complex.

One of the first designs for a fully developed dwelling combine was submitted at an internal Osa competition in 1927 by Sobolev. This consisted of a city district of ten blocks, each six or seven storeys high, linked by gangways both with each other and with a group of communal buildings — a canteen, club, library, shop, crèche, and so on. A stadium, sports grounds and gardens were sited at the centre of the complex.

Subsequent projects aimed at the inclusion in the dwelling combine of an ever-increasing number of public buildings, so as to turn it into an independent component urban unit. Phalanstère-type combines were also designed as self-contained settlements, such as Nikolai Kuzmin's project of 1928-29 for the Anzhero-Sudzhensk coal mining community which, when completed, was intended to house 5,140 people and provide all the public services required.

The tendency during the 1920s to create a structural unit - the dwelling combine - intended to be virtually self-contained within a city may be seen as a more or less straightforward attempt to convert a self-contained independent workers' settlement into a city district, with a minimum of adaptation. The supporters of the Sotsgorod idea articulated their designs for a city quite independently of industrial working conditions and concentrated on everyday requirements for social services. They treated a city as an assembly of independent elements of construction, rather than an integrated entity. Social contacts in dwelling combines conformed to the patterns of life in independent settlements, so that the outlook of a village dweller with his or her narrow neighbourhood view was transplanted to the big city. The latter was to be turned into a sort of machine pattern of independent worker's settlements and of the qualitatively different town-planning system generated by dwelling combines, which represented the cross-connected, but self-contained constituent elements of a city.

The concept of Sotsgorods consisting of identical dwelling combines was widely applied during the period of the First Five Year Plan. Standard component units were designed for such Sotsgorods in the form of district communes, competition projects were drawn up for new industrial towns and dwelling complexes were built.

The most consistent application of the Sotsgorod concept, with the dwelling complex as its component unit, can be found in competition designs submitted in 1929-30 for new socialist towns and living complexes.

In their project for the lay-out and construction of Kuznetsk, Alexander and Leonid Vesnin offered a compactly planned Sotsgorod for 35,000 inhabitants with clearly identified communal and cultural centres. Its districts were to consist of dwelling combines, each composed of four accommodation blocks, two for families and two for single people, a communal block with a canteen, assembly hall, reading room, gymnasium etc, blocks for children's institutions, and a school block with provision for boarders. All the blocks communicated with each other by gangways. Two dwelling combines formed a city district with a clump of vegetation at its centre on to which opened the children's institutions and the school. The settlement  the Sotsgorod - was entirely made up of these standard city districts.

The project submitted for the Avtostroi competition by MVTU students under Mordvinov's direction resembled this Vesnin design closely. The town was composed of standard districts built on an almost square ground plan, each of which consisted of two fully developed living combines: six dwelling blocks, a school block, club, kindergarten and two blocks for crèches.

The Vesnin project for Stalingrad was likewise an autonomous Sotsgorod consisting of large dwelling combines. The first version of this scheme, for a town of 64,000 inhabitants, consisted of twenty dwelling combines each housing 3,200 people and each comprising eighteen blocks. The second version, for 32,000 inhabitants, consisted of twelve dwelling combines housing 2,600 people in each.

A complete dwelling combine was also proprosed in one of the competition projects for Magnitogorsk submitted by a group of students from Vkhutein under the direction of an architect named Brilling.

Golosov submitted an unusual design for a dwelling complex in Stalingrad. Instead of a single compact block, it provided a set of three parallel blocks, linked at the centre by gangways. Two blocks were for communal use, offering a communal centre and a children's section, while the central and longest block, stretching for 450 metres, provided living quarters.

Another imaginative scheme for the spatial organization of a dwelling combine in Leningrad to house 2,000 people was put forward by a team from the Leningrad Institute of Civil Engineers, Ligi, consisting of Pavel Zhukovsky, Alexander Knyazev, Olga Proskurina, Boris Rubanenko, Nikolai Khomutetsky and Nikolai Shadrin. In their design for 2,000 people in Leningrad, a communal block built on a V-shaped plan was linked to two rows of parallel dwelling blocks.

The Gorodok chekistov (Special Police Cantonment) at Sverdlovsk, designed by I. Antonov and Venyamin Sokolov in 1931, provides an instance of a design that was actually executed.

Standard dwelling combines were also used to make up the New Kharkov Sotsgorod, an autonomous complex of 50,000 inhabitants designed by Aleshin in 1929 and begun in 1930 for the Kharkov Tractor Factory. This new town, eight kilometres from Kharkov, was planned as part of a large industrial district. Its basic component unit - a dwelling combine or town quarter — was intended for 3,000 inhabitants and consisted of eight dwelling blocks, six of which were segmented for the use of families, while the other two were laid out along corridors as accommodation for individuals, and four blocks housing institutions for children, a club and a canteen. Unlike the fully developed dwelling combines in Kharkov itself, this industrial housing district offered only those communal facilities required for everyday use. In this instance, the inhabitants were dependent for public services on the general communal facilities provided within the Sotsgorod as a whole. This Kharkov complex clearly marks an intermediate stage in the change-over from huge housing communes to the traditional city district as the basic component unit of the Sotsgorod. This evolution in the dwelling combine concept was connected with the transition from experimental design work to the actual construction of Sotsgorods to serve new industrial undertakings during the First Five Year Plan.

The component units designed for the Sotsgorods at New Gorlovka in 1929-30 and Kominternovsk in 1929, the latter by Petr Yurchenko, Mikhail Grechina and others, closely resemble the New Kharkov dwelling combines in that their network of public services is closely integrated into the city's public services system as a whole.

The practice of dispensing. with the dwelling combine's selfsufficiency and integrating its requirements in the general scheme regulating the public services of the city concerned, is clearly illustrated by a competition project for a Housing Commune submitted in 1930 by Nikolai Baranov, Leonid Galperin, Evgeny Ilin, Venyamin Notes, Mikhail Rusakov and Andrei Chaldymov. These architects did not treat the Housing Commune as an independent social unit, including it, instead, in the Sotsgorod's multi-tiered organizational scheme. They distributed the various component elements of the public services in accordance with an ingeniously conceived hierarchy within the city structure. The dwelling combine, a community of 2,000 inhabitants, was designed in this case as a city district without covered gangways, and the graduated system of public services was designed to serve both component elements larger than a dwelling complex, such as a group of communes or a whole Sotsgorod, and smaller than it, such as the nucleus of a commune, a commune element or two such elements together.

The further evolution of the dwelling combine concept is well llustrate by the sixth settement t Zaporozhe whe the overall division of the town into districts was already well marked, but where the residential areas still preserved their basic component units, including housing and communal blocks.

Another original interpretation of the concept of a Sotsgorod consisting of dwelling combines was used in laying out residential areas on empty sites in large cities as micro-districts complete with dwellings, children's institutions, a school, shops and educational establishments. Examples of such city districts can be found in the former Dangauerovka area of Moscow built in 1929-33 by Motylev, Boris Blokhin and others, at Armeniken in Baku, and in the residential areas of Erevan built in 1931-32 by Kochar and Mazmanyan.

## Disurbanization:

## Okhitovich's 'new settlement'

The second most influentical concept of socialist settlement — disurbanization — is linked with the lectures and articles of the sociologist Mikhail Okhitovich. The principles on which he based his 'new settlement' concept differed radically from those underlying the Sotsgorod theory, and were in some ways at the opposite extreme to them.

Okhitovich and Sabsovich were at one in their rejection of big cities. But while Sabsovich regarded the creation of towns strictly designed to serve a given purpose as the basis of socialist settlement, Okhitovich rejected all forms of compact town planning and countered the principle of urbanization with a consistently 'disurbanizing' concept. In so far as he was concerned, town and country needed to be eliminated as contrasting notions and replaced by the principle of decentralized settlement. Modern inter-city transport facilities would render the traditional patterns of settlement out of date. Moreover, new technology and the ability to carry power over long distances would lead to the dispersal of industry. This would make it possible to replace existing dense concentrations of industry by a linear distribution of sites and, consequently, of dwellings in close proximity to the production plant they served. Okhitovich suggested for the immediate future that existing compact industrial sites should be served by ribbon development of workers' housing along the main routes linking interdependent production units.

Individual prefabricated and mobile units were to make up such linear settlements. As a corollary of the decentralization of housing advocated by Okhitovich, social and public service centres would be replaced by a network of services brought as close as possible to the consumers. Large public service establishments would provide the output that would then be channelled to small distribution points intended to serve areas of restricted size.

Okhitovich's town-planning ideas were most closely followed in the Socialist Settlement Section of Gosplan (the State Planning Commission) for the RSFSR, where town-planning problems were studied by Barshch, Vladimirov, Georgy Zundblat, Milinis, Pasternak, Nikolai Sokolov and others, under the direction of Ginzburg and Okhitovich.

A general settlement scheme and two designs for actual locations - Magnitogorsk in the Urals and Green City (Zeleny Gorod) near Moscow — were produced there. The general settlement scheme provided for a regular network of major roads for the transport of raw materials, fuel, semi-finished and finished products, and labour. Production plant would be installed at the apexes of the triangles, at the intersection of these roads. The sides of the triangles would be bordered by strips of parkland 50-100 metres deep with local roads beyond them and, at some distance along these, dwellings for the workers employed at neighbouring factories. These dwellings were to range from communal blocks to one-family homes, with gaps some fifty metres wide between buildings. There would be no settlement in the inner areas of the triangles, which would be reserved for agriculture and extractive industry, the workers in either of these being also housed along the main routes. The network of public service establishments - post offices, libraries, children's institutions, canteens and the like - would be located in the parkland strips. A Park of Culture and Leisure, with a club, cinema, sports complex, exhibition area, swimming pools etc, would b sie t the mos vourable natural location aon each line or 'ribbon'.

The design for Magnitogorsk drawn up in 1930 by Okhitovich, Barshch, Vladimirov and Sokolov was an attempt to plan the actual settlement of an industrial area as an integrated system of industrial plants, mines, ancillary plant and agriculture. Eight main ribbons of settlement, with an average length of twenty-five kilometres each, drawn along the main highroads converging on the metallurgical combine, provided housing and a network of public services for the entire district. Each ribbon included a cultural centre with its club and park, while a central Park of Culture and a single administrative and communal centre were also provided.

A competition was launched in 1930 for the design of a Green City -Zeleny Gorod—in the Moscow suburban area, to serve as a health complex or 'city of recuperation'. Barshch and Ginzburg submitted an entry, but disregarded the terms of reference to some extent and treated Green City as a part of the 'new settlement'.

In this project, Green City would represent one of the suburban districts of Moscow, a ribbon of settlement strung out along the main Yaroslavl road and housing 100,000 inhabitants, with a strip of parkland 200250 metres deep to border it. Beyond this parkland would lie a continuous band of living quarters consisting of bunched housing units raised on pillars, with a covered passage running underneath it from end to end. Covered walkways would lead from the regularly spaced bus stops along the main road to the housing, by way of various communal establishments. Children's institutions, schools, technical colleges and cultural establishments would be located on the other side of the main road, amid undisturbed clumps of vegetation.

The Green City project revived to some extent the idea of syphoning off the population of Moscow into its suburbs. It treated the new town as part of the proposed reconstruction of Moscow, and specifically as the first move in the measures to relieve congestion there. Its authors suggested a ban on new construction and the enlargement of existing industrial enterprises in Moscow. These, along with scientific, educational, administrative and other institutions, would gradually be removed beyond the city's limits, while the population would be resettled along the main lines of communication linking the city with other neighbouring centres. Within Moscow itself, most existing buildings would be demolished as they became obsolete and replaced by green areas, while architectural features andmonuments of historical and artisticinterest would be pre served. Moscow would thus in effect become a Central Park of Culture and Leisure towards which the ribbons of socialist settlement would converge. This grandiose park would accommodate the few remaining free-standing administrative institutions, scientific institutes and higher educational establishments serving only the local population, along with concert halls, sports stadiums, swimming pools, zoological and botanical gardens, horticultural institutes and nurseries.

In 1931, a group of architects headed by Ginzburg also designed a settlement for the Chernikov industrial district in the Bashkir Autonomous Republic. The 'new settlement' concept sought to disperse the large cities and resettle their population in natural surroundings by laying out ribbons of development amply supplied with social services. Unlike the Sotsgorod concept, this was to be achieved through conditions that thoroughly favoured individual development, since Okhitovich was categorically opposed to anything fostering a regimentation of the way of life.

## In search of flexible planning -

## Leonidov, Miliutin

During the period of the First Five Year Plan, Soviet architects were faced with having to design new towns of apparently fixed size and population, while it was perfectly obvious to them that the further growth of towns could not be artificially restrained. They therefore sought for some planning principle that would enable a town to evolve without conflicting with its functional order or requiring a radical revision.

Theoretical planning schemes for such a flexible structure of the developing Soviet city were worked out at the end of the 1920s by Leonidov, by Nikolai Miliutin, and by Ladovsky. These three separate schemes were published almost simultaneously in 1930, at the height of the debate on the problems of socialist settlement. Neither the Sotsgorod theory of Sabsovich nor Okhitovich's linear resettlement concept took into consideration problems arising from the evolution of the planned structure over a period of time, A Sotsgorod consisting of dwelling combines could, seemingly, only grow by the addition of further standard districts or dwelling combines, while its layout remained otherwise unaltered. Disurbanizing linear development gave greater scope for the creation of the flexible planning structure: in theory, the line of settlement could grow both lengthwise, and by increasing the density of population per linear kilometre. However, in the disurbanizers' scheme for Oitovich'intermeiat age, theribbons ofhousing inked industrial nodes, and the linear structure was therefore closed off at both ends.

In earlier linear projects of settlement  by Soria y Mata in Spain and Benoit-Lévy in France-existing compact inhabited localities, which acted as centres of employment and culture, were linked by newly created lines of residential districts stretched out along main roads. By contrast, the 'new settlement' disurbanizers provided for cultural and social facilities along the line of the residential zone.

The concept of linear settlement influenced the schemes for flexible planning elaborated by Leonidov and Miliutin. Leonidov used as his base line Okhitovich's transitional stage, with industry in compact locations and settlement planned along ribbon zones. Miliutin, on the other hand, adopted the final stage outlined in this theory in which industry itself would be sited linearly. Leonidov's and Miliutin's projects included elements of both disurbanization and the Sotsgorod concept, even though the main influence on both was that of Okhitovich's theoryof linear settlement. This dual origin was chiefly obvious in their attitudes to building height in housing, and to building density.

Leonidov may be said to have detached a district from the general scheme of disurbanization and treated it as an independent linear town developed along one or more of the main lines of communication radiating from a compactly laid out industrial centre.

In 1930, Leonidov produced a competition entry for Magnitogorsk based on the generalized planning scheme for a linear town. It consisted of a strip of residential districts containing two types of buildings, some low-rise and some tower blocks, alternating with districts reserved for children's institutions. The detached public buildings, sports facilities and parks were sited on either side of this strip. Freight and passenger communication lines ran along the outer boundary of the town.

As it developed along the highway leading to the industrial area, the town cut into the surrounding vegetation, and could grow in any given direction without departing from its planned structure, although as it grew its residential districts came to be ever more remote from the places of work.

Leonidov's design, to which Vkhutein students had contributed under his direction, was submitted for competition in the name of Osa. The Constructivists were active participants in competitions for the design of new towns or residential districts during 1929-30. The projects submitted by Constructivist teams were mainly variants of the linear settlement concept, from a settlement strip - as in Magnitogorsk and Green City - to various forms oflinear cities. The only exceptions to this general rule were probably the designs for Kuznetsk and Stalingrad, which proposed compact Sotsgorods. Thus the design submitted by Osa's team for the Avtostroi competition embodied the linear planning principle, in that the residential strip consisted of a row of dwelling combines in each of which ten six-storey blocks were connected at second-storey level by buildings with a cultural or social purpose. The design submitted for Chardzhui by a team from Osa's successor organization, Sass, provided dwelling combines consisting of two-storey buildings on a square ground plan disposed on a single axis alongside the main transport line leading to the industrial area.

In the same year, Miliutin drew upon Okhitovich's 'new settlement' concept, developed Leonidov's design, and published his town-planning scheme based on the principle of functional flow. By siting the industrial plants parallel to the residential buildings, he not only brought the place of work and the living quarters more closely together, but enabled the linear town to develop in two different directions.

Miliutin proposed the zoning of the urban area in parallel strips arranged in the following sequence:

(1) railway lines;

(production and communal facilities, as well as scientific and technical educational establishments connected with them;

a green zone concealing a highway;

residential area consisting of three strips  public service buildings, dwellings, and children's institutions;

a park zone with recreational facilities, sports grounds, swimming pools etc;

market garden, dairy and fruit-growing zone.

In addition, Miliutin thought it desirable that a stretch of water, such as a river, lake or large pond, should adjoin the residential zone of the linear town. This would be important in terms of recreational, sporting and health amenities, as well as providing additional communications facilities to link the different parts of the linear town by water transport. He suggested that the residential zone of his linear town should consist of three-storey buildings, either lining the highway or abutting on it.

Although Miliutin's fluid functional scheme allowed the town to develop in two different directions, it made no provision for the creation of a fully developed social centre. The town-planning designs put forward by Ladovsky which provide the basis for the fundamental principles of the Union of Architect-Planners, ARU, display a radically different approach to the search for a flexible urban planning scheme, and in particular to the role played by the city centre.

## Ladovsky's conception of an expanding city ARU's urban planning proposal

The town-planning principles applied in the linear settlement and Sotsgorod concepts influenced the design and construction of new industrial towns and promoted a more thorough study of many planning problems, such as that of transport between work and residential areas, the improvement of public health conditions within the area of habitation, the inclusion of the natural environment in the latter, the creation of a single network of public services, the design of a basic component element for dwelling complexes, and the formulation of a system of settlement for larger areas.

Nevertheless, their rejection in principle of large cities made it difficult for the supporters of these concepts to fit them into practical town-planning work. As a result, designs for settlement within an economic district based on the linear development concept lacked any economic focal point, while those which envisaged a spatially organized solution for the design of a city were restricted to one residential area, or a small town at best. Meanwhile, the requirement for an accelerated rate of industrialization countrywide demanded ever greater attention to the problems generated by large cities and industrial conurbations.

In these circumstances, the views and designs of architects who adopted a consistently urbanizing position carried much weight: in particular those of Ladovsky and the group of his followers who left Asnova in 1928 to found ARU, with Ladovsky as president. Founder members of ARU were Fridman, Vitaly Lavrov, Krutikov, Sakulin, Zazersky, Grinberg, Sergei Lopatin, Saishnikov, Glushchenko, the public health specialists Nekrasov and Kovalev, and the economist Zhmudsky.

The problems of socialist settlements as a whole, of industrial conurbations, of flexibility in planning and the component elements of large cities were all studied in theoretical writings and experimental designs by the ARU supporters.

Ladovsky's town-planning concept was characteristically complex. As opposed to many town planners in the 1920s, for instance, Ladovsky paid attention to questions such as a city's aesthetic quality and the increasing intricacy of its lay-out as it grew in size.

In his project for the industrial settlement of Kostino near Moscow in 1927, Ladovsky had already suggested a design which allowed the settlement to grow without impairing its layout and made it possible for the residential and industrial areas to develop freely together. This project contained in embryonic form the ideas which Ladovsky later developed in a radically new scheme for the flexible planned lay-out of a city.

He stressed that town planning must now be given priority in architecture by combining a thorough study of the theoretical problems with practical work. Individual buildings should only be treated as elements of an entity —in other words, a city— and design should proceed from city to building, not the other way about, as many architects were in the habit of doing.

He thought that it was essential as a matter of principle to produce a planning scheme which would not only allow for the quantitative expansion of a town's territory, but also take into account the inevitability of a qualitative increase in the complexity of its lay-out as it developed.

In 1930, Ladovsky elaborated a new scheme for the planning of a developing town, based on a careful analysis of the merits and demerits of linear lay-out and those based on main lines of communication radiating outwards through concentric rings. The scheme Ladovsky put forward may for purposes of argument be regarded either as a radial-concentric system broken at a given point, or as a curved version of Miliutin's functional-flow scheme. Ladovsky's design represented a parabola in which a social centre aligned on its axis was enclosed by successive residential, industrial and green zones. As a result, the new planning scheme combined to some extent the advantages of both radial and linear lay-outs, while it lacked their drawbacks. Ladovsky's 'parabola' provided an opportunity to develop a centrefor the city as a whole, while preserving its role as a nucleus for planning purposes.

Ladovsky's parabola influenced not only theoretical studies l

Thus, in the project for replanning Tashkent produced in 1930-32 by Ladovsky's pupil Silchenkov, together with Gelfeld, the industrial zone surrounded the residential area and the city centre like a horseshoe. This allowed for the further development of the residential area towards the open end of the horseshoe.

Ladovsky asserted that the lay-out of a city was a matter not nism. A city could be said to be growing organically when its parts increased in step with the whole and both formed part of a coherent time-and-space structure.

Unlike the followers of Okhitovich and Sabsovich, the ARU membership directed by Ladovsky took a broad view of town planning and did not reduce complex questions of settlement to a single type such as a linear settlement or a Sotsgorod. It was a mistake, they believed, to design standard houses and residential districts without reference to the size of the settlement concerned, as was widely the practice at that time, since qualitative differences in the lay-out of a settlement are governed by the number of its component elements.

The ARU supporters were active in competitions for the planning and construction of new socialist towns. Their designs stood out by their great spatial impact and the attempt to endow individual districts with the greatest possible variety of special treatments. Their residential districts - or dwelling combines— varied in composition, with circular districts, diagonally or radially sited buildings, and the types of houses included differed in their ground plans and the number of their storeys.

The ARU town-planning concept was reflected in a whole series of projects put forward by its supporters: in the Green City project of 1930 by Ladovsky; at Avtostroi in 1930 by Krutikov, Vitaly Lavrov and Valentin Popov; at Chardzhui in 1930 by Kalmykov and Leonid Grinshpun; in the industrial settlement near Ufa in 1929-30 by Vitaly Lavrov, as well as in a series ofdesigns produced within Vkhutein under Ladovsky's direction, such as that for Avtostroi in 1930 by Kalmykov, Petr Revyakin and Panteleimon Fedulov.

## The problem of the big city: the competition for replanning Moscow

When all is said and done, and despite the polemics involed, the debate in 1929-30 about socialist settlement played an important part in Soviet town planning. It helped architects to identify and discuss in contemporary terms a large set of - among other—social, economic and public transport problems. It was on opportune moment for the investigation of questions such as the creation of a network of public services, the transition from the planning of a single inhabited locality to that of settlement within an entire district, the development of the primary components within a residential district and a city as a whole, or the formulation of a flexible city lay-out.

In June 1931, the Plenum of the Communist Party's Central Committee held a discussion 'Concerning the management of the city of Moscow and the development of urban management within the USSR'. The basic principles applicable to urban development during the next few years were established. Proposals for the liquidation of big cities were rejected, but it was also decided not to build new industrial plants within the confines of large urban centres. A requirement was stated for the reconstruction and rehabilitation of existing cities and the construction of new socialist towns. The resolutions of the Plenum set architects the task of producing a scientifically based plan for further development and building work in Moscow.

The elaboration of a general plan for Moscow at the beginning of 1930 went far beyond the creation of a design for replanning a single large city and focused attention on the general problem of developing a large city and the whole conurbation connected with it.

Two radically different proposals were published in the press in 1930, even before a competition was announced. One was the disurbanizing design by Barshch and Ginzburgfor Green City, discussed above, whereby Moscow would gradually dissolve into the ribbons of settlement converging upon it. The other was a coherent urbanizingdesign by Ladovsky which opposed the dispersal of Moscow and assumed that it was capable of developing further as a political, administrative, cultural and industrial centre. But Ladovsky also believed that unless the closed radial-concentric lay-out were radically reshaped, it would set solid limits to the further expansion of the city, and that the constant topping-up of the old planned

Part II/Social tasks of architecture

lay-out by new insertions would break up the aesthetically satisfying lines of the streets and squares. He thought it essential to plan for the possibility of the social centre turning outwards in order to preserve the historic pattern of buildings by not overloading with new functions the central complexes and street pattern inherited from the past. Ladovsky suggested that Moscow should be developed in accordance with his proposed scheme for an expanding city, using the line of Gorky Street and the Leningrad Highway as the axis of his parabola. The new, growing and changing functions of the centre were to be given an opportunity to escape beyond the historic nucleus of the city by breaking through its ring roads in one sector. The axis of the parabola, shaped like a comet, along which the centre was to develop, would incorporate all the basic dynamic alterations, thereby protecting the historical centre from further overloading.

The grouped garden-city theory also continued to exert a marked influence on the formulation of projects for the reconstruction of Moscow in the early 1930s. An outline scheme for replanning Moscow was prepared at the end of 1930 under Semenov's direction. It suggested that the city required radical reconstruction and represented an ill-defined urban form. Semenov described it as 'the Moscow blob . . . a historically generated conglomeration of factories, buildings, streets and green spaces'. It needed to be deliberately broken up into a system of 'city complexes' arranged around the old city centre in accordance with a predetermined plan. Each city complex was to be given a significant degree of autonomy in planning and administrative matters and immersed in a protective and healthgiving green zone.

In an outline plan produced in 1930, Le Corbusier proposed that the area of Moscow should be cut back and filled with greenery by redeveloping it with high-rise buildings, while preserving the best historical settings, substituting a rectangular grid pattern for its radial-concentric plan and drastically reconstructing the bulk of existing buildings.

A closed competition to find suggestions for the reconstruction of Moscow was carried out in 1932. Seven teams took part.

In his competition design, as in his article published in 1930, Ladovsky advocated the application of a parabola-shaped planning scheme so as to gradually transform the radial-concentric lay-out of Moscow into a dynamic structure developing in a particular direction.

The design submitted by Vladimir Kratyuk's team also proposed the development of the city beyond the historically conditioned radial-concentric lay-out, but - as distinct from Ladovsky's proposal  the growth ofits area was planned in several directions at once with differing degrees of scale and intensity.

The remaining designs combined the preservation of the city's historical nucleus with the decentralization of the entire structure of Moscow, ranging from the articulation of the city into districts to its conversion into a set of independet settlements.

The Vopra team project proposed to develop Moscow as a city consisting of five district complexes representing independent planning units, each with its dwelling blocks, service industry and district centre. Kurt Meyer divided the city area into five districts converging at the centre and growing outwards into the suburbs. In the design submitted by Krasin's team, the city's territory pushed out even more vigorously into the suburban area with broad wedges centred on the main highways. Hannes Meyer's team put forward a project in which 'wedges' and 'rays' were distributed among ten satellite settlements strung out along the main transport routes. They formed a single conurbation, with Moscow, the big city, still retaining its importance. In Ernst May's design, the compact big city was in fact replaced for all intents and purposes by a system of satellite towns linked with a common centre.

Ther the0uher ment of Mosow, and the designs submitted for its replanning, stimulated additional work on the preparation of a general plan for the city's reconstruction.

Much experience of experimental design and the theoretical elaboration of the problems of town building and urban development had thus been accumulated by the end of the period under review. As a result, the great expansion of new town building during the 1930s did not catch Soviet architects unawares. The experimental design work, scientific study, competitions and discussions of theory in the preceding years helped them to enter upon practical design and construction work.

# Reconstruction of the way of life and development of new forms of dwelling

![](images/1b5b4da61c21e7a7a8e44d8a73d02d8a01a4fddbd102565063353ddf3cf62038.jpg)

The reconstruction of domestic life and involvement of women in social production processes

The October Revolution set architects the task of creating a socially new form of dwelling, an aim strenuously pursued from the very beginning as part of the process of establishing a socialist way oflAll thespets an every sage ofthe develom propriate form of housing.

Soviet architects closely followed the social changes taking place around them as they worked on their new designs. They studied the proposals of utopian socialists, such as More, Campanella, Veiras d'Allais, Morellet, Dézamy, Fourier, Owen and Saint-Simon, as well as those of the Marxist-Leninist classic writers. The latter treated the housing problem and its solutions under socialism as part of the much wider problem of socialist resettlement, in connection with the resolution of the conflict between town and country and the involvement of women in the social production process.

Engels wrote that 'the liberation of Woman, the achievement by her of equality with men is impossible . . . so long as Woman is kept away from socially productive labour and confined to domestic work'.1

Lenin also devoted much attention in his writings to the reconstruction of the way of life in which he saw the opportunity for a genuinely socialist position on the status of women. He wrote in March 1917, as part of an analysis of various aspects of the structure of life under socialism: '. .. without drawing women into social service . . . and political life, without drawing them out of their stultifying domestic and kitchen situation, it is impossible to ensure genuine freedom, it is impossible to build even democracy, let alone socialism'.²

After the October Revolution, Lenin repeatedly returned to the problem of the socialist reconstruction of the way of life. He regarded practical measures in this field as imperative and urged that all embryonic manifestations of such restructuring should be sought out in everyday life, to be fostered and propagated.

At the First All-Russian Congress of Working and Peasant Women called by the Central Committee of the Communist Party in November 1918, Lenin said: 'Woman is crushed by her domestic work, and only socialism can save her from this situation." After a thorough examination of the problems involved in women building a new society, the Congress adopted a resolution which stated that: 'In the place of kitchen pots and wash troughs we must put, in town as in the country, communal kitchens, communal eating places, central laundries, workshops for mending clothes, collectives for the cleaning of linen and dwellings, etc."4

'Woman,' wrote Lenin in 1919, 'remains a domestic slave despite all liberating legislation, because menial domesticity presses on her, strangles her, renders her mindless and humiliates her by fettering her to kitchen and nursery, squandering her labour in an unbelievably unproductive, mean, enervating and depressing way. The genuine liberation of women begins only at that place and time at which the struggle against such demeaning domesticity is undertaken, or more accurately, the mass reconstruction into large-scale socialist management begins."5

On 8 March 1920, in its special edition devoted to International Woman's Day, Pravda published an article in which Lenin yet again stressed the close connection between the genuine liberation of women and the radical reconstruction of domestic life:

'To involve woman in social-productive labour, to extract her from"omestic slavery" to lierateher from the subj tion which dulls and humiliates her in the ceaseless and unremitting environment of kitchen and nursery - that is the main task.

This is a long struggle which requires a radical reshaping of both social practice and ethics.'6

## The growth of communal living: a new social brief for housing

The acal cans whic theSovit way f le asegoing at that time exerted a strong influence of their own on the search for a new type of dwelling.

On 20 August 1918, the Presidium of the All-Russian Central Executive Committee issued a decree 'Concerning the Abolition of Private Real Estate in Cities'. All the more valuable dwellings were transferred to the local councils. A mass resettlement of workers began, moving them out of shacks and cellars into the houses confiscated from the bourgeoisie. In 191824, nearly 500,000 people were moved into comfortable apartments in Moscow, and some 300,000 were likewise moved in Petrograd.

Housing Communes sprang up of their own accord for social, political or simply domestic purposes, as part of the massed move of workers into bourgeois apartments. The victorious proletariat was intent on converting the expropriated houses into springs of communist culture. The properties originally built for investment and rental were renamed Housing Communes-workers' houses or communal houses—and treated as new forms of workers' dwellings, the domestic arrangements and organization of which would help to develop collective habits among the population and educate its communist consciousness. During 1918-20, before the introduction of the New Economic Policy, the workers paid no rent and each house set up an administration of its own which would not only manage the building, but also organize collective arrangements, such as communal canteens and kitchens, kindergartens, crèches, rest rooms, library reading rooms, laundries, and so on. All these, as well as the cleaning and maintenance of communal premises, were carried out collectively.

The Housing Communes which spontaneously developed in 1918 were officially recognized by local councils from 1919 as an approved method for the use of dwellings by a workers' collective. Such communal self-management of housing by the workers was very widespread in the early Soviet period. There were 865 Housing Communes in Moscow at the end of 1921, and 242 were registered in Kharkov during the years 192225. Housing Communes played an important part in the upkeep of the nationalized building stock that had been handed over rent-free to the workers. They did much to improve living conditions and to consolidate new political principles in the way of life of the labouring classes. The very term 'Housing Commune' described a new social focus and a new relationship among the inhabitants of a building.

The domestic communes formed in the early post-Revolutionary years were voluntary consumer associations of workers with a set of rules to regulate the lives of all their members. Fully fledged communes were governed by a general assembly and a Communal Council. The domestic communes varied widely in size. In Petrograd, for instance, there was in 1919 a commune consisting of thirty-two people sharing their everyday existence, while in Kiev, in 1922, the workersof the Arsenal factory set up a commune in an old house in which each of the eighty flats housed two or three families, and in which a club, reading room, bakery, baths, laundry and storeroom had been set up.

When the Housing Commune movement gathered momentum, the conversion of communal houses into centres of the new communist culture, the creation of domestic collectives and the transformation of the way of life through self-administered communal institutions were among the tasks set for it. But even when this movement was at its peak, communal ways of life developed very slowly in nationalized houses. It was thought at the time that the main reason for this lag was that old houses were ill-suited to the new way of life and that the provision of specially designed communal dwellings, complete with the requisite social services, would lead to a more rapid movement in this direction.

There was, however, no agreement about what architectural form the new dwellings should take. Three basic concepts were in competition. The first was linked to the garden-city theory and aimed at a communal settlement consisting of individual houses, together with public buildings. The second mainly stressed the role of communal housing complexes, and a socialization of the way of life in which not only the domestic economy but the family itself would be radically reconstructed. The supporters of the third concept believed that buildings specially designed for fully developed Housing Communes could not be built immediately, but that a transitional type of dwelling — the collective house  must be designed so as to assist with the gradual integration of new habits into the existing way of life. Such a house would continue to offer accommodation for individual families, but would incorporate collective institutions, such as communal kitchens and dining rooms, crèches, laundries, and so on.

The Housing Communes that arose spontaneously in nationalized accommodation served as a basis for the formulation of the new social dwelling requirement. They were regarded as an important advance in the field of housing, representing prototypes for the dwellings of the future and actual embodiments of the new way of life. The experience they offered was sdiand the indications aboutaew way olerising from them were spotlighted as models.

This new way of organizing the life of the workers had the support of the Party, the trade unions and all the other social organizations in the country. The programme formulated by

Lenin and adopted at the Eighth Party Congress stated that: 'Without confining itself to the formal statement of women's equal rights, the Party is intent upon liberating them from the material ties of an outdated domestic economy through the provision of communal canteens, central laundries, crèches and the like."7

The movement for reshaping the way of life arose among the workers themselves. When they took over the homes and real estate investments of the bourgeoisie they rejected the entire social system developed by the old society. Thus self-management by the residents replaced private ownership of housing. There was rent-free accommodation for workers, instead of high payments for living space; an explicitly collective approach to everyday life, instead of the isolation and segregation of individual families; broad mutual co-operation, instead of the friction and rivalry aroused by the pursuit of prestige in, for instance, the 'cult of material things', and voluntary self-help, instead of the employment of servants and maintenance staff.

The Housing Communes served as a kind of test-bed for trying out new ways of organizing life. It was here that the systems of communal social services which were to develop later first arose spontaneously and manifested themselves in a variety of ways. This principally involved aspects of communal and cultural activities with a bearing on such crucial socio-political tasks as the liberation of women from domestic work, so as to bring them into the field of production and social life, with the help of canteens, communal kitchens, laundries, kindergartens etc, and the launching of a cultural revolution through inauguration of such facilities as reading rooms and Red Corners.

## Communal houses

The experience acquired in Housing Communes led to the formulation of a programme for the development of new dwellings suitable for this purpose, and the appearance of corresponding experimental and competition designs during the first half of the 1920s.

One of the earliest projects of this sort was put forward by Ladovsky and Krinsky in 1920 at Zhivskulptarkh. This involved multi-storey buildings of complex design in which accommodation of various kinds was grouped around a covered courtyard.

Acompetiton was launced at the end of 1921 for thedeign of a standard collective dwelling for use in the development of the Petrograd suburban area. The specification involved the construction of a transitional type of dwelling for thirty families combining the normal domestic amenities with some communal facilities. The first prize went to David Buryshkin and Lev Tverskoy for a building of complex design the central part of which was to house a canteen and kitchen, a library reading room and a crèche, while four wings with 'cottage-type' living quarters (but no cooking facilities) radiated from the centre.

A competition held at the end of 1922 for model workers' houses in two Moscow residential districts also specified a transitional type of dwelling. Requirements for family and single living quarters were clearly defined: the two- and three-room family flats were to be provided with a full set of domestic offices, including kitchens; adjoining communal facilities included a kindergarten, crèche, library reading room, storage rooms, baths and showers. Childless couples and single people were to have individual rooms of appropriate size, with wash basins. The accompanying communal accommodation included reception rooms, library reading rooms, canteens, kitchens, storage rooms, baths, showers, lavatories etc. The district was also to be provided with a surgery, garage, laundry, housing management office and meeting hall. In the majority of competition entries, the family accommodation consisted of three-storey buildings where standard dwelling units were repeated around staircases, as in the projects by Leonid Vesnin, Sergei Chernyshev, Ilya and Panteleimon Golosov, Edgar Norvert and others. In many cases, the district institutions were planned either as separate buildings or grouped functionally.

The project submitted by Melnikov was of outstanding importance. It provided separate blocks for families, while combining all communal services in a single building of complex design, linked by covered walkways at second-storey level with four-storey blocks intended for childless couples and single people. It was the prototype of a communal house, with its distinguishing features already present: a well-developed communal sector with provision for eating, rest and culture, education and domestic requirements; dwelling blocks for single people, without kitchens or other facilities; covered walkways carried on piers to connect the dwellings with the communal block, and a modern design radically different in aspect from the old tenements.

From 1923, students in Ladovsky's Vkhutemas studio worked on the design of 'The Communal House for Workers'. The task set by Ladovsky was to create a dwelling which would combine the advantages of individual housing with those of a well-developed communal building. He occasionally varied the requirements and in a number of projects, for instance, demanded access from each dwelling unit both to the communal facilities and direct to the street, at no increased cost. Turkus provided a brilliant solution with the design for a three-storey house in which all the living units for childless couples and individuals communicated directly with the outside world and with inner corridors. Lamtsov suggested a novel way of linking individual and communal accommodation by routing all communications through the front of the house. Silchenkov provided horizontal communication along low corridors carried in overhanging outer galleries. He later elaborated this scheme and patented it in 1930. He also designed a two-storey unit of two rooms, a component for a communal house with a corridor serving two storeys at once.

An All-Union competition was held in 1924-25 for a communal house in Kharkov. Its specification required a new type of dwelling incorporating a fully developed communal component. In the outcome, Trotsenko's design was implemented.

In 1925-26, the Moscow City Council held an All-Union competition for the design of a workers' dwelling, which specified 'a type of house suitable both for single workers and working families with no housekeeping facilities of their own'.

The design submitted by Georgy Volfenson, Samuil Aizikovich and E. Volkov, which secured a second prize, involved a building on a complex ground plan, consisting of living quarters sited along corridors and abutting on each other at either side of an open courtyard with a communal block at its far end. This project was carried out with some modifications on Khavsko-Shablovsky Lane. The dwelling blocks consisted of flats and single rooms, while the communal part contained a canteen, a club with a reading room, a crèche and a kindergarten.

Communal houses were also designed - and some built - for ohercit in the i20sAcu ortagelige, however, meant that the use for which they had been planned was disregarded. Functionally designed communal houses could ensure comfort for their inhabitants if all the communal amenities worked well and if they were used for housing the kind of families for which they had been designed. Any disregard of these conditions - such as service failures, the use of communal accommodation for dwelling purposes, or the use of blocks intended for childless couples and individuals by families with children - made any objective assessment of the advantages and drawbacks of such experimental communal dwellings impossible. Worse still, it inflicted discomfort on the inhabitants and so made them sharply critical of communal houses as such.

The failure to establish the communal house as a model for mass workers' housing was also attributable to other causes. Thus the conditions and forces that had brought the domestic communes into being during the early years, the reasons for their evolution and gradual disintegration, had not been adequately studied before new design briefs were formulated.

During the period of War Communism it had been thought, for example, that the buying and selling of goods would be replaced by barter through a highly developed network of consumer collectives or communes. The exchange of goods and labour would underpin not only the distribution of industrial products and foodstuffs, but the provision of public services and housing as well. In the barter system that arose under War Communism, with its appropriation of surpluses, its distribution points and its ban on private trade the commune became the basic economic unit of the distributive system for goods and services. All this was radically changed by the New Economic Policy.

Moreover, the creation of Workers' Housing Communes had also been prooted by the proletariat's assertion ofits political role in previously hostile residential districts. This fostered the organization of domestic communes which became the mainstays of Soviet power among the population of cities, helped to bring sympathizers together, instilled a new attitude to material possessions among the people at large and played an undoubted part in setting a new socialist ethical and communal standard.

In many ways, therefore, the first Workers' Housing Communes were not, as later critics wrote, an 'untimely move', but genuinely reflected the circumstances of their day. Attempts to preserve them and secure their future failed because economic, social and political circumstances changed rapidly as socialism was bein built. Somefature veryday lie fdwy and others emerged. This was as true of Housing Communes as of everything else.

This process already began to show itself in the first half of te 0s hen the  t te Ne cic ic an to the principle of rent-maintained urban housing substantially changed the very basis of the Workers' Housing Commune economy, founded as it was on a totally free use of each building and complete self-service. A new approach to co-operative housing took its place, under which members contributed fiy .

## The housing associations

The co-operative housing associations took over from the Housing Communes of the War Communism period and introduced a new set of collective principles. A resolution of the First Congress of Housing Co-operation, held in December 1923, stated that the tasks of housing associations included both the maintenance of buildings and the provision of various domestic amenities for general use, such as study rooms, reading rooms, canteens, bathrooms and facilities for children.

The housing co-operative buildings planned and erected in number of cultural and general domestic amenities. As a stage in the collectivization of the way of life, they approximated more closely to transitional type dwellings than to fully developed communal houses.

Typical buildings of this kind include the Dukstroi co-operative house in Moscow, built by Alexander Fufaev in 1927-28; the co-operative house in Zachatevsky Lane in Moscow, designed by Samoilov in 1925; the design for the Novkombyt (New Communist Way of Life) building, by Ilya Golosov in 1928, consisting of family flats, single rooms, and communal accommodation; and the co-operative house built on the Tverskaya in Moscow (today's Gorky Street) by Ladovsky in 1930.

Co-operative housing, along with most other new types of buildings developed during the 1920s, was a subject of study in Vkhutemas, where Alexei Zaltsman designed a building for a housing association in Alexander Vesnin's studio.

As a rule, housing associations were connected with a specific working community. Efforts were made during the mid-

1920s to accommodate within a single building both the living quarters and offices of the organization to which the members of the housing co-operative belonged. The Dom Kozhsindikata (the Leather Syndicate building) in Moscow, for instance, designed by Golubev in 1925, housed offices on the three lower storeys and flats for employees on the four upper ones. In the same year, the ElektroCo-operative commissioned from Ilya Golosov a combined dwelling and office building in which the first three storeys were reserved for offices and the top four for living quarters, while the basement housed the club accommodation.

## Experiments in the use of traditional dwelling types

The interest displayed in early Soviet days in the fully developed communal house stemmed partly from the sharp contrast drawn between it and private housing in the course of polemics, since the single-family house with its plot of land had been treated as the model for mass workers' dwellings by the supporters of the garden-city concept. Small houses for workers were first designed immediately after the October Revolution. In 1918, Belogrud produced a project for a 'proletarian dwelling' consisting of a single-family house arranged on three levels. In 1921, Markovnikov designed an experimental two-family, split-level, brick house. His designs were used in 1923 for a settlement by the Sokol housing association, comprising a variety of small self-contained houses for single families, terraced houses and small blocks containing three dwellings.

Early in the 1920s, architects were trying to keep down the cost of small houses while preserving their cottage character through inclusion of direct outside access from each housing unit and a garden plot for each family. This led them to design a variety of houses, both free-standing, with two, four or eight flats each, and in terraces. Some of these designs displayed great inventiveness in the search for rational planning of the accommodation and spatial organization of the flats. In competition entries for a dwelling complex in Moscow in 1922—23, for instance, Belogrud and Melnikov designed family flats in terraced houses set out in rows on a saw-toothed ground plan, with the smaller flats on the ground floor and the larger on the floor above, while the attic floor was provided with outside stairs at the back of the blocks.

Early in the 1920s, small houses became the usual type of workers' dwellings in towns themselves, as well as in outlying settlements. In Moscow during the first half of the decade the dwelling complexes that were built consisted chiefly of small houses, such as the workers' settlements of the AMO factories, comprising two-storey terraced houses by Zholtovsky in 1923; the Krasny Bogatyr settlement in 1924-25; and the Duks settlement, with two-storey four-, six- or eight-flat houses, by Venderov in 1924-25. The Stepan Razin settlement at Apsheron, by Samoilov, the first sector of which was occupied in 1925, consisted of single- as well as some two-storey houses, containing one, two, four or eight flats. The first workers' settlement in 1924-26 at Ivanovo-Voznesensk consisted of standard small houses.

It became obvious by the middle of the decade, however, that neither small houses nor communal ones could provide the basic model for mass housing. The construction of small houses, especially with a plot attached, proved uneconomic in urban conditions. Communal houses were still only in the early stages of their evolution as a form of dwelling. Doubts and disputes arose about many aspects of life within them, while the inclusion of a complete communal component greatly increased their initial cost.

The increasingly acute shortage of housing necessitated a switch to mass building methods for workers' dwellings and to an economically viable type of building. The use of standard dwelling units around staircases provided the answer, and this type became known as 'sectional' housing. A major factor in this development was that local councils had become the main commissioning authorities for housing by the mid-1920s, while the volume of private commissions, as well as those from trade enterprises and co-operatives, shrank sharply.

The growing part played by local councils in commissioning urban housing influenced both the type of dwellings built and the method of allocating living space. Private building aimed at individual dwellings. Co-operatives represented pre-selected groups of shareholders, and therefore often commissioned well laid-out garden settlements, or houses with elaborate communal elements operated on a self-service basis. A local council, on the other hand, did not usually have any particular group in view for housing purposes and supplied accommodation to those workers' families which needed it most. As Alexei Meshkov, an architect actively involved in the planning of housing on behalf of the Moscow City Council, wrote in 1925, the slogan here was: 'Maximum living space at minimum cost'.8

In housing commissioned by the Moscow Council, public services were provided at district and area level. The closed system of the communal house was being replaced by large dwelling complexes made up of sectional housing, shops, children's facilities, canteens etc.

In Moscow, Leningrad, Baku and other towns, the first dwelling complexes consisting of sectional housing were built with specially designed units of accommodation for each project. The introduction of standardized and mass-produced components, however, made it necessary to devise standard solutions. The first standard sectional units began to appear in the mid-1920s. These underwent substantial changes in design of occupancy of the newly built housing.

Thus in Moscow, for instance, in 1925-26, two-room flats predominated in the first sectional units of four flats each, with only rudimentary domestic facilities, thereby setting a limit to the number of occupants per room. By 192728, the standard module contained two flats, usually of three rooms each. The standard of amenities in the flats also substantially improved — with bathrooms, through ventilation and no rooms without an independent exit—and the total area of each flat increased. On the other hand, the general tendency during the second half of the decade to build flats with larger numbers of rooms, combined with relatively little new construction and an acute shortage of housing, affected the allocation of living space. As a rule, new housing came to be occupied room by room.

The transition in cities, during the mid-1920s, to dwelling complexes consisting of sectionally designed housing required the architects to produce new standard plan elements suitable for adequately compact dwelling complexes, yet capable of ensuring a varied spatial organization in city districts and allowing for plenty of fresh air and vegetation. With this end in view, the widely used linear, abutting, angled, T-shaped and cruciform sectional units were supplemented by new types arranged as three-pronged elements or elements set at an obtuse angle.

The three-pronged standard sectional unit was first used in a design by Ladovsky in 1924 for a small residential area consisting of three-storey masonry houses composed of three types of elements- linear, three-pronged and square— offset in relation to each other.

In 1925, Ladovsky and Lissitzky submittedajoint entry in competition for the design of dwelling complexes at Ivanovo-Voznesensk. They used wide-angle sections in the design of family housing laid out in strips on curved or undulating ground plans. The rooms were all rectangular and the wide angle was used to fit in stairwell on a trapezoidal plan. By altering the orientation of the latter, the component sections could be made to point in a variety of directions.

## Osa's internal competition

## for the design of communal dwellings

The switch in the mid-1920s to sectional construction of mass housing for the urban working population made communal dwellings a suitable subject for experimentation, especially in so far as the design of a new type of housing was concerned. Much work was done in this field by the Constructivist architects' group Osa, which put forward the slogan 'Modern architecture must crystallize the new socialist way of life' in the first number of its periodical SA in 1926. The periodical then went on to announce 'an internal competition for the outline design of a new workers' dwelling'.

Practically all the designs submitted in 1927 for this competition represented transitional forms of housing, comprising both family dwelling units and a fully developed communal component. The designers were particularly concerned to establish the cheapest way of providing these units and communictons to the communal facilities  uc s corridors, ng ways and stairs.

Ginzburg designed a communal house consisting of two sixstorey dwelling blocks linked at the top by the communal component, with a canteen, library reading room, club and assembly hall. The ground floor of each dwelling block was flanked near the stairs by small annexes for children - a kindergarten and crèche. Corridors provided internal communications and ec served to foors, since all flats were built on two levels.

In two of the designs, the architects— Sobolev and Andrei Ol - tried to save space horizontally by making a single corridor serve no less than three floors of a dwelling with split-level flats. Such flats were also used in designs with external galleries encircling the buildings, by Nina Vorotyntseva and Raisa Polyak, and in sectional buildings, by Vladimirov.

In virtually all the entries featuring split-level flats the main living rooms rose to the full height of both levels, while small bedrooms and ancillary accommodation were kept to half that height and fitted in either at the level of the living room or on half-landings.

The five-storey sectional houses in Vegman's entry were linked at the top by covered gangways, and the top floor of every seventh block was reserved for communal purposes. In Pasternak's project all the ground floors of each six-storey block held communal accommodation, so that corridors could be combined with a sectional lay-out.

The internal Osa competition supplied a wealth of new ideas about the spatial organization of dwelling units, the combination of residential and communal elements within a single building and the rational use of floor space. Two projects of similar date by Leningrad students were closely related to the Osa internal competition entries. The first of these, by Mikhail Krestin, involved five four-storey dwelling blocks, consisting of split-level flats with a kitchen, bathroom and lavatory, each lik ed with two communal blocks. In the second project, by Konstantin Ivanov, Petr Smolin and Fedor Terekhin, the dwelling and communal elements were combined in a single sevenstorey block on a trefoil ground plan, with the canteen, kindergarten, crèche and reading room located on the ground floor.

## Transitional housing designs

## incorporating new uses of space

Work on the rationalization of housing and the development of a transitional design for communal dwellings was carried out in 1928 in the Typological Section of the Committee for Construction, RSFSR (Russian Republic), by a group ofarchitects, including Barshch, Vladimirov, Pasternak and Grigory Sum-Shik, directed by Ginzburg. Problems involving a scientific approach to the organization of the way of life were tackled here at government level virtually for the first time. Housing units were to be developed so that a separate flat could be allocated to every family. After a thorough study of the practical possibilities then available, the architects in the Typological Section decided that the only viable solution to the housing problem lay in the production of a standard, small (initially oneroomed) flat intended for an individual family.

Part II/Social tasks of architecture

Ways of reducing the cost of flats in sectional housing were thoroughly investigated. Particular attention was paid to their rational planning and, more especially, to the reduction of ancillary floor space. The housewife's work in the kitchen was subjected to close time-and-motion analysis and a planned arrangement of the equipment involved made it possible to save some otherwise wasted space.

Several versions of a rationally planned sectional flat were produced which the architects called Type A, as well as a fresh spatial lay-out for a sectional flat termed Type B. The designers succeeded in reducing the height of ancillary accommodation by alternating the accommodation within a building from side to side, in other words, by following a mirror-image plan from one floor to the next.

Apart from this rationalization of the lay-out of sectional flats, the Typological Section developed various versions of the four ways of arranging housing units along a corridor serving a single floor to produce their Type C, or on two floors in Types D and F and three floors in Type E. A comparative analysis of the economic efficiency of the various types of housing units showed that Type F was most cost-effective for purposes of mass resettlement of single families in small flats.

In spatial terms, a building composed of Type F units required a corridor.Asdistinct from Types C, Dand E, howevr, such corridors did not reduce the area available for dwellings. Each corridor served two floors and fitted into the space saved by reducing the height of the ancillary accommodation and sleeping alcoves in the flats. Moreover, such corridors secured daylight, while every flat could be naturally ventilated.

Several models were worked out in the Typology Section for single-room Type F flats with living areas of 27 m², 30 m² and 31 m². Each of these had a small entrance hall, from which stairs led up or down to a main living room 3.50 m or 3.20 m high, including, behind a folding partition, a kitchen recess containing standard units - range, sink etc. Accommodation with lower ceilings at 2.25 m or 2.15 m included a sleeping alcoveand adjoining toilet facilities containing a bath, showerr wash basin.

A number of designs for transitional communal housing were prepared by the Typology Section in which various types of dwelling units were combined in different ways.

The Section's work in 192829 resulted in the formulation, published in 1929, of 'model designs and assemblies for the construction of dwellings recommended for use in 1930', as well as the construction in Moscow, Sverdlovsk and Saratov of six experimental communal houses of transitional design. Various designs of housing units, methods of linking the dwelling and communal components to each other, new structures and materials, and methods of organizing building work were tried out in the course of their construction.

The most interesting of these houses is that designed by Ginzburg and Milinis, with Prokhorov acting as engineer, built in 1928-30 on the Novinsky Boulevard (now Tchaikovsky Street) in Moscow, with the inclusion of dwelling, communal and shopping elements. The dwelling part consists of a six-storey block with two corridors, one on the first, the other on the fifth floor, and two staircases. The ground level is open save for pilotis. Three types of flat make up the design: thirty-two reduced Type F units, several doubled 2F units, eight units for larger families approximating to Type D, and hostel accommodation with rooms for one or two people. The flat roof carries a sun terrace and a garden. At first-floor level, a covered gangway leads from the dwelling block to a separate building housing a kitchen and canteen (midday meals were taken home) and kindergarten.

New structures, materials and building methods were tried out in the Novinsky Boulevard house, as well as new dwelling units. These included weight-bearing ferro-concrete frames, thermo-insulated outer walls, fire-doors,sliding windows, standardized prefabricated structural elements, such as pillars, girders, panels, windows and doors, and their assembly on sie.Other experiments were also carrid out: in spatial organization, to find a human scale for the rooms, a maximum permissible limitation of space etc; in lighting, to establish the relationship between the dimensions of rooms and the lighting required for them, and to create the apparent enlargement of interiors by the rationalized distribution of window space; and incour b the diffential hading  wall  ce stimulate the perception of interior space, and by the functional use of colours for purposes of orientation within the buildings.

The second of the experimental houses was built in Moscow on the Gogol Boulevard by Barshch, Vladimirov, Milinis, Orlovsky, Pasternak, and Lyubov Slavina. It consisted of two dwelling blocks and a free-standing communal building. The third was situated at Rostokino in Moscow.
