Designed by Ginzburg and Solomon Lisagor, it consisted of three blocks: one with five storeys, comprising paired tworoom flats; another comprising Type F units; and a communal building, containing an assembly hal, foyer, canteen, kitchen, laundry etc. The fourth, erected in Sverdlovsk by Ginzburg and Pasternak, with Prokhorov as engineer, was laid out as four buildings on a square with a garden in the middle. The fifth, by Vladimirov and Yulian Gershtein, in Moscow, comprised fourteen large Type 2F flats, and a hostel with individual rooms and a communal kitchen. The sixth, by Lisagor and Evgeny Popov in Saratov, consisted of two buildings: a dwelling block made up of Type F and 2F units and a communal building. In addition to these, a large number of designs for communal, transitional housing were produced towards the end of the 1920s. Some of these designs were carried out, mostly on the strength of commissions from housing associations, in Kiev, Kharkov, Baku, Tashkent, Smolensk and elsewhere.

## The family and communal accommodation: the debate about transforming the way of life

Work on the design of new towns and dwelling complexes to serve the industrial plants being built under the First Five Year Plan focused the attention of architects on the problem of mass housing, as well as on resettlement. A keen debate developed on this subject both in the press and in specialized publications. All aspects of life were debated: the future of the family, relations between parents and children, the nature of everyday social contacts, the collectivization of domestic life and consumption, and many other such problems. A diversity of social models were put foreward, each of which involved its own special new requirements in housing.

The charaçter of marital and family relationships, including their implications for the planning of new dwellings, came under particularly close scrutiny at that time.

The difficulties of everyday life immediately after the Revolution had further aggravated a series of moral problems arising from the rejection of the traditional view of the family, while its new interpretation had not yet taken shape. The new Soviet concept of the family emerged among the disturbed circumstances of Civil War, economic chaos and the popular upsurge of War Communism and was therefore often prevented from functioning normally. Day-to-day existence lost much of its stability as a result of the Civil War, the new family was often altogether unable to take root in it, and marriage itself frequently became impermanent in character. Although such instability had been caused by the complex circumstances of those early Soviet days, it was nevertheless often regarded as the expression of a new socialist ethic. And so, when the stabilization of economic life during the first half of the 1920s restored to the family its role as the basic economic and consumer unit in society, the young revolutionary generation saw in this a threatened rebirth of conformism, the jeopardizing of the collective spirit and an attempt to replace it by petit-bourgeois individualism. Stormy discussions raged in the press and at youth meetings throughout that decade concerning a new sexual morality and alternative models of family relationships.

Theories gained currency which linked a new way of life not merely with the wholesale collectivization of domestic existence, but with the rejection of the family as the basic unit of society. In the new collective way of life the existence of every member of society would be subjected to minute regulation.

This approach to a new way of life was most coherently stated in the theoretical writings of Nikolai Kuzmin and found its architectural expression in 1928—29 in his design for a communal house. Kuzmin completely discarded family flats. Instead, he divided the residents into age groups and designed separate accommodation for each of these. The rooms provided in special blocks were reserved for sleeping, and the remainder of free time had to be spent by everyone in a cultural centre. The family as such did not exist. Children were brought up collectively, in separate age groups. Meals were taken communally. Life was strictly regulated, and the programmes prescribed for each age group were reckoned out to the minute.

The 'Theses on Housing' put forward by Nikolai Kuzmin at the Firt Osa Congress in , which declared flats to be a material manifestation of petit-bourgeois ideology unacceptable in the new society, were adopted by the gathering as a whole. The fully developed communal house, involving the utmost collectivization of all aspects of life, was erected into the model of new socialist housing.

The communal house designed in 1929 by Barshch and Vladimirov reflected the Osa concepts as embodied in these Theses'A complex consisting of two sets of buildings intersecting each other at right angles was divided into three interconnected basic blocks: a six-storey block, housing 360 people, for children of pre-school age; a five-storey block, housing 320, for school children; and a ten-storey block for 1,000 adults. Each block contained all the facilities required by the relevant age group.

Designs by Alexander and Leonid Vesnin, Ilya Golosov and others, for actual dwelling combines, regarded as the structural units of a new town in the Sotsgorod planning concept, were undoubtedly influenced by such proposals for a fully developed communal house catering for a completely collectivized way of life. Sabsovich, the proponent of theSotsgorod theory, favoured the wholesale collectivization of the way of life and the rejection of the family. He even opposed the provision of shared rooms in communal houses for married couples: rooms, in his opinion, were primarily there for sleeping in, as well as for individual rest and study. Sabsovich believed that the family would disappear as the basic unit of society and be replaced by communities founded on other principles. As he saw it, in a socialist society the problem of parents and children would be replaced by that of adults and children, or society and children, and resolved thereby.

## Youth collectives and communal dwellings involving wholly shared living

The theories advocating a wholesale collectivization of life and the liquidation of the family, as well as the architectural designs derived from them, claimed to reflect a current social need. Their promoters pointed to individual examples of Housing Communes with wholly shared living and voluntary elimination of the family. Such communes did in fact exist, but were usually no more than a temporary form of domestic organization by groups of young people.

The young, owing to their position in the family, at work or in training, are veryinclined to associate with their contempo raries and to form independent groups with values and an outlook of their own. They need close contact with their own age group as they develop from dependent children into fully fledged adults. This urge gradually fades, new family ties develop and individuals become absorbed into working communities. Hence the objective circumstances which favour the development of youth groups, but hence also their temporary nature, their instability and their inevitable decay.

This tendency among the young to form groups of contemporaries was intensified during the 1920s by special circumstances. The young often saw the struggle for a new way of life as a challenge to family ties: these gradually weakened in any case and were replaced by comradeship among contemporaries.The creation of communes among the young was also greatly stimulated by the fact that large numbers of them were directed to workers' colleges and institutes of higher education, or employed in the construction of new cities. These were the circumstances in which domestic communes for the young came into being. Lonely students or building workers, a long way from home and their childhood peer groups and communities, as yet without new families of their own, naturally chose company over solitude and preferred to live communally.

Many aspects of the new way of life were exemplified in the everyday existence of young workers and students during the 1920s. Certain sociologists and architects belonging to that decade allowed themselves undue scope, however, in their interpretation of specific life styles, ethics and relationships when they studied the Housing Communes of the young. In practice, many desins ofcommunal houses catering for a fully collective way of life and the rejection of the family were no more than attempts to provide an architectural framework for youth hostels and to rationalize the way of life appropriate to them. The subsequent history of communal houses built to serve such communities is indicative in this respect.Those designed for student communes survived successfully for many years as wellequipped hostels, because their inhabitants consistently met the age and family requirements for which the buildings were initially conceived. On the other hand, communal houses designed for young workers' communes gradually developed into unsuitable dwellings as their inhabitants acquired families of their own and the occupants no longer bore any resemblance to the youth communes for which such buildings had been designed. It must be stressed, however, that youth communes came into being before any communal houses specifically intended for a fully collectivized way of life were designed and built. In other words, these houses met a genuine social requirement, even if its scope has been grossly exaggerated.

In the mid-1920s, for instance, the students of the Leningrad Eletro-Technical Faculy ome  destic cin which housing, basic expenditure, everyday activities, food and study were collectivized.Total self-service was introduced. All parts of the hostel premises were reallocated and provided with new functions —bedrooms, access to which was completely forbidden in daytime, a dining room, a rest room and studies. All books were 'nationalized' and incorporated in a selfservice library. A self-service shop was organized. The creation of a commune was helped by the age and common social origin of the students, all of whom were former workers, their joint interest in study and their equal incomes.

The movement for the establishment of domestic communes among young workers entering higher educational establishments, and the spontaneous emergence of such communes, strongly influenced the design and construction of student hostels towards the end of the 1920s.

An experimental communal student house was built in Moscow in 1929-30 for 2,000 residents, to designs by Nikolaev. A vast eight-storey block contained small double bedrooms measuring 6 m². This block communicated with a three-storey communal building containing a sports hall, an assembly hall seating 1,000, a dining room, a reading room seating 150, a study room accommodating 300, and numerous individual study cubicles. The design also included a laundry, a repair workshop, a crèche accommodating 100, a surgery, sun terrace, club rooms and showers.

Other well-equipped student hostels in Moscow, contemporary with Nikolaev's design, variously reflected the research into communal housing. A student hostel built on the Kotelnicheskaya Embankment in 1930 consisted of two dwelling blocks linked to a building inserted between them and housing a dining and dayroom, as well as a self-service kitchen, on each floor.

Similar hostels were also built in other cities. The hostel of the Zoological Veterinary Institute designed by Kochar in 1931 in Erevan, for instance, consisted of three buildings — two dwelling blocks and a communal block lying between them, connected with each other by gangways.

An All-Union Intercollegiate Competition for the design of a communal students' house in Leningrad, with accommodation for 1,000 residents, was held in 192930, when the commune movement was at its height among students. There was a marked difference in the spatial composition of the communal building between the projects submitted from the Leningrad

Institute for Communal-Construction Engineers (Liiks) and those from Vkhutein in Moscow.

The Leningrad students adopted an approach which had already become traditional for this purpose: a multi-storey block or blocks linked to a communal building or buildings. The designs produced by the Vkhutein students under Leonidov's direction, on the other hand, attempted to reflect the inner structure of the life of commune members. Most of the designs split the communes into groups of ten, fifty or a hundred students. Each group was architecturally provided for by a building complex with individual rooms intended for sleep, rest and study, together with communal and ancillary accommodation. In addition, there was a common cultural centre. In Ivan Kuzmin's design, each such basic community was allocated a separate circular two-storey building on pilotis. In the design by Leonid and Nikolai Pavlov, it was part of a low complex with individual small inner courtyards. For Grigory Pyankov, it was a single standard 'section' in a tower block, and for Viktor Kalinin, as for Alexander Maximov and Zanvil Korsunsky, it was part of a multi-storey block.

Leonidov's theories about the reconstruction of the way of life, which hadinlenced the Vute students'desins, we best illustrated by his design for a socialist settlement attached to the Magnitogorsk Industrial Combine. The dwellings in it represented a form of hostel for individuals and childless couples, in which each section consisted of sixteen small singleroom flats for two people. These sections could be used as separate dwellings or incorporated in a multi-storey tower block. At the centre of each section, a hall provided with a double light was intended for use as a common rest room, cultural meeting place, morning gymnasium and dining room. Leonidov believed that the new dwellings should not recall hotels, where thousands lived along noisy corridors far removed from nature. Instead, they should provide a framework for small communities in which the individual would not be submerged, but be able to develop to the full and communicate with others by graduating from small groups to increasingly larger ones.

In Leonidov's design, the dwellings were surrounded by gardens, sports grounds and fountains. Each dwelling complex or district would house 250 people, and a free-standing building for children's institutions would be sited between every two complexes. Only the most elementary forms of public accommodation and communal services were located in the immediate vicinity of the dwelling units. All other communal institutions in Leonidov's design were located in public buildings beyond the confines of the residential districts.

In 1930, the Leningrad City Council launched an All-Union open competition for a communal house - 'a collective dwelling combine' — to house 2,000 workers. The bulk of the entries offered dwelling combines comprising housing and communal blocks linked by gangways.

The design by Simonov, Notes and Rusakov was ultimately selected and provided a communal dwelling for 1,750 people. It was a complex design consisting of interconnected housing blocks, each with its strictly defined purpose - for individuals and childless couples, families, school boarders  and a communal centre containing a theatre, sports facilities, a dining room, and so on.

The communal house designed by Ol and built in Leningrad on Rubinstein Street in 1930 was commissioned by an association of young engineers and writers who themselves decided at their general meetings the degree of collectivization to be applied in the building. All domestic work was to be communalized, and leisure was to be collective. None of the flats in the building, whether larger, with three to four rooms; small, with two rooms; or hostel accommodation for single people, included a kitchen. The ground floor housed a dining hall, reading room, children's rooms, rest room and other public premises. However, difficulties arose in the life of this domestic commune after the first flush of collective enthusiasm. New families developed, and the house which had been planned for a community of young people proved to be completely unsuitable for ordinary domestic purposes.

This fate also overtook a whole series of other communal houses built for the working youth, In contrast to communal houses for students, the family circumstances of the inmates changed as the years went by and with them the entire life style.

## Individual personal requirements, collective living and the economy: ideal projects and actual circumstances

In their model for the future way of life, the disurbanizers scorned designs for communal houses involving total collectivization where an individual would constantly be surrounded by other people. They advocated a combination ofindividual freedom with a provision for everyday needs through a centralized supply system, as opposed to the communalist concept of a minutely regulated way of life.

In his settlement scheme, Okhitovich assumed that people would always eat, dress, and so on — in other words, engage in consumption-as individuals, and that individual demands for consumption would grow as society evolved. The disurbanizers' attitude to the socialization of everyday life stressed the need to socialize the more labour intensive domestic tasks within the house and outside it, but denied that consumption itself must be collectivized. They did not set out to create permanent social contracts between fellow occupants of a dwelling, but stressed instead the autonomy of each individual's existence in every possible way. The disurbanizers designed small individual standard units which could either be free-standing in natural surroundings; linked in continuous ribbons, as in the Barshch and Ginzburg Green City design; or included in housing association and hostel projects, such as those drafted for various types of dwellings in the Socialist Settlement Section.

However, even though the disurbanizers rejected extremist attitudes towards the socialization of domestic life and the collectivization of consumption, their approach to the design of a basic housing unit was much the same as that of the supporters of communal housing involving a fully socialized way of life. Okhitovich believed that the unit of housing should be designed for a single person, since wives and husbands would live apart in the future, while children would be brought up collecively. As he saw it, 'the family hearth' as such would vanish in time.

The debate at the end of the 1920s about restructuring the way of life unfolded at a time when resources for new housing had to be allocated most sparingly in order to meet all other demands for the rapid industrialization of the country. Any betterment of the workers' living conditions required an improvement of the housing as such - rationalized planning, modern equipment, increases in the per capita quotas of floor space etcas well as the development of a network of communal facilities. These two basic requirements, however, proved conflicting when a decision had to be made as to which of them should be given priority in funding. A majority gave clear preference to the socialized amenities, since the restructuring of life was link s theyoug otny wih arallssothe 'ol a  it the primary element of society. The family was not rejected outright, but merely regarded as an unstable form of association between individuals. This made it necessary to adjust the design of housing units to the 'mobile' nature of the family, in order to takecunt  both jontanindividualccpy Miliutin, for instance, asserted in his theoretical statements and in a design for a dwelling block that living quarters in building called upon to reflect the mobile character of the family in a socialist society must be planned flexibly.

Most of those concerned with restructuring the way of life types of housing, concentrating on the search for the correct planning and spatial organization of the dwelling of the future. The development of a new way of life was intimately linked in their minds with the provision of certain forms of housing, and all 'old' types of dwellings were usually rejected as being socially unacceptable.

Ladovsky saw the connection between the way of life and housing rather differently. He thought it important to shift the emphasis in pursuit of the new way of life on to the design of districts and entire cities, and away from the preocupation with individual dwellings. He considered it wrong to identify particular types of residential building with specific social purposes, since the social identity of any dwelling - be it a flat, say, or a hostel embodying one measure of socialization or another - may be successfully accommodated in every known type of building, including a skyscraper. The distinctions between such buildings were simply a matter of minor differences in everyday practice and of the way in which a dwelling fitted into the network of communal services, rather than any point of social principle. This attitude was reflected in Ladovsky's own designs and those of his students in Vkhutemas-Vkhutein. The designs originating from the latter included dwellings that might be conceived sectionally, or in galley form, in blocks of towers, along corridors or in some other form, but always firmly as part of the new social organization of a dwelling complex or a city.

This approach to the connection between housing and a new way of life proved justified by experience. In terms of everyday life towards the end of the 1920s, districts consisting of sectional housing and free-standing communal buildings did not, in fact, differ greatly from the intermediate type of dwellings with communal accommodation built for the housing associations.

The total socialization of life and the withering away of the family were advocated in radical theories. Meanwhile, the communal houses and dwelling combines that were designed, and especially those that were actually built, increasingly proved to be intermediate dwellings, hotel-like buildings and groups of sectional dwellings and communal blocks, either adjacent or linked by gangways.

The advocates of communal houses catering for a wholly socialized way of life were searching for a new guiding principle for housing and a new primary component of society to replace the traditional family. The latter had developed within its own form of accommodation. This was now to be replaced by a fresh spatial design to fit the new everyday personal relationships within a new primary community. The actual course of events soon made it plain, however, that two of the basic features of the proposed 'ideal' housing communities, which would need to take root and spread before the family flat could be replaced by a different housing component, were unrealistic. For one thing, thefamily provedmore lasting as the basicunit ofsociety than many had imagined in early Soviet years. For another, the Housing Commune as a consumer collective based on completely voluntary self-help by its members, operating in a socialist economy on the principle 'From each according to his ability, to each according to his labour', proved to be mere utopia. It took no account of the real economic relations between people, such as differential earnings among the members of the community and the resulting material inequalities in opportunities and needs, or the impossibility of assessing individual contributions to the self-service system as a whole , and the resulting artificiality of egalitarianism. The Housing Commune, therefore, simply did not take root as a new basic unit of society. However, the demand for a special type of dwelling - the communal house - which arose during the early Soviet period in connection with the spontaneous emergence of Housing Communes, acquired an impetus of its own. It formed part of the search for a restructured way of life and came to be regarded as an ideal model, however unattainable it might be in the actual circumstances prevailing at the time. Many sociologists and architects treated it as a matter for the future and believed that at some stage it would be necessary to forgo the ideal communal house as a model and temporarily resort instead, for purposes of mass accommodation, to a transitional type of building. Yet even the latter did not achieve widespread use. In real life, the burden falling on the housewife was eased by taking some of the heavier domestic tasks out of the home - by such means as ready-made foods, for instance— and by streamlining work within the home through new equipment. Hopes that the majority of domestic chores could rapidly be eliminated proved illusory, however, and the family continued to provide not only the basic component of society but also its primary economic unit. Furthermore, experience failed to justify the expectation, which had seemed so incontrovertible in early Soviet days, that everyday consumption itself would gradually become collectivized. Finally, the growth of increasingly complex social relationships outside working life put an end to the hope that neighbourliness would become an important factor among residents and lead to the gradual evolution of a living community within a house.

In the outcome, not only did transitional housing fail to provide a basis for a gradual evolution of communities to the 'higher' level of Housing Communes, but even such elements of self-service as they incorporated failed to develop and often vanished after a few years. A house would then either simply shed its communal accommodation, or become part of a dwelling complex with public services of its own. Alternatively, it might survive as a hotel-like dwelling offering a high level of service, rather than encouraging self-help, for the benefit of residents who for one reason or another had no domestic facilities of their own.

A large number of houses and complexes designed and built towards the end of the 1920s included features variously taken from communal houses, dwelling combines, houses of a transitional type, hotel-like establishments and districts with their own networks of public service, and often extravagantly combined. Among these were a house on the Petrograd Embankment in Leningrad, designed by Simonov and others in the early 1930s, consisting of blocks internally connected with each other and composing an entrance hall, foyer, auditorium and dining room on the ground floor; a complex of family hostels in Pirogovskaya Street in Moscow by Rukhlyadev and Osipov in 1928-30 and consisting of eight interconnected blocks: one for single residents, six with two-room flats for families and one with larger flats, together with communal kitchens, toilet facilities and laundry rooms on every floor at the butt-ends of the buildings; a dwelling complex by Boris Iofan, in 1928-30, on the Bersenevskaya Embankment in Moscow, in which the public rooms — film theatre, kindergarten, crèche, dining room and shop - adjoin the dwellings, but do not communicate with them internally; a housing complex in Kiev, in Revolyutsiya Street, built in 1929-30 by Anichkin as architect and Zholtus as engineer, which consists of a five-storey building with a complex ground plan, having public rooms on the ground floor and a laundry in the cellar; and a dwelling on Karpovka in Leningrad by Evgeny Levinson and Igor Fomin in 1931-34.

These and many other such examples of housing designed towards the end of the 1920s clearly show that the mass urban dwelling was then still only in its experimental stage, Architects were no longer content with sectional houses consisting of large flats with single-room occupancy, or communal houses made up of dwelling cubicles lacking ancillary facilities. A kind of convergence was taking place between these two types of dwelling which involved a search for a family housing unit and ways of linking a dwelling with public amenities.

In May 1930, the Central Committee of the All-Union Communist Party adopted a resolution 'Concerning Work on the Reconstruction of the Way of Life' which stressed the importance of shaping a new socialist way of life and pointed to errors committed so far in the course of this process.

The development of a socially new form of dwelling displayed many contradictory features during the period under review. Yet the intensive investigation of creative opportunities existing in this field greatly influenced the evolution of Soviet architecture. The reconstruction of the way of life was debated and tested in numerous designs and buildings. New approaches to the planning and spatial organization of dwellings and housing complexes were worked out. In order to plan and equip dwelling units in a rational way, new scientifically based methods were introduced into the design work. The set of communal institutions required within a dwelling complex was carefully studied, the structure of the public services involved was elaborated and the various public buildings required could thus be provided.

## Manifesting the new collective way o li inthe appanc ousg

When architects designed workers'dwellings, they tried not only to promote a new way of lie among the inhabitants, but also to impart a new look to the buildings, so as to make them visibly different from theinheritance of the pst.

The visual stereotype for the new communal dwelling - a complex composition, consisting of blocks of different sizes linked by gangways, which gradually achieved almost universal currency - did not establish itself immediately. Earlier on, the appearance of the dwelling of the future might be marked by its palatial character, as in Tverskoy's 1920 competition des DwellingorPolianin Por;orbyhat ically dynamic composition, as in Ladovsky's forceful design for a communal house; or by contrasting outward austerity with the pompousness of a bourgeois investment property, as in Malevich's Planits of 1923-24 and Khidekel's communal housing designs of 1927-31; or by the way in which the workers' dwelling was seen to be thrown open to sun and fresh air, as in Leonidov's glazed prismatic houses designed for Magnitogorsk.

Nevertheless, an emphasis in the appearance of the dwelling on the collective nature of the new lie within it and the provision for general social contact within housing units, became the essential elements of the spatial composition of new dwellings in the 1920s. This approach was first exemplified clearly in Melnikov's competition design for a dwelling complex in Moscow, of 1922-23.

Covered gangways — on pilotis or in spans — between dwelling blocks and communal buildings were widely used during the mid- and late 1920s in designs for new types of housing. The use of gangways to connect buildings led to the introduction of new spatial solutions. A new town-planning dimension developed from the design of residential areas. Many fresh ideas were incorporated in designs for communal houses, transitional buildings and dwelling combines, where the external evidence of the connection between the various blocks created a new architectural image for them and for the new life itself.

The fully developed communal house or dwelling combine was usually designed on a near-square ground plan and took up an entire city district or block. In practice, the intricacy of the spatial composition could not be fully displayed if the component buildings were set parallel or at right angles to the sides of the site. The spectator would then only have a partial view of them, and would perhaps fail to take in the collective nature of the new urban element.

In an attempt to impart greater external impact to communal houses or dwelling complexes, their architects sometimes set the component blocks diagonally, or strung them out with their connecting gangways along the street, instead of laying them out in depth within the site. Two designs by Ilya Golosov are typical in this respect: the UMS communal house, drawn up in conjunction with Boris Mitelman in 1931, and the socalled Collective House for the First Workers' Settlement in Magnitogorsk in 192931.

Experiments in the spatial composition of dwellings were not confined to the creation of a new image for a socially novel type of dwelling. They also involved the design of new types of sections, new forms of spatial organization for dwelling units, the formulation of spatial compositions for dwelling complexes and the use of colour.

The Moscow City Council approached Asnova in 1927 with a request to design and build an experimental residential district on the Shabolovka. At that time, the construction of dwelling complexes based on standard component sections and designs embodying the latter was in full swing in the city; their appearance satisfied neither those who had commissioned them nor their prospective users. This forced the City Council in 1926, first to launch a competition for the design of façades for dwellings, and then to resort to Asnova as a creative organization with a reputation for research into artistic form. Asnova carried out an internal competition and selected Travin's design, which was used as the basis for the design actually carried out by an Asnova team. Spatial organization of the district by buildings sited diagonally and the application of colour by a conjunction of brick and coloured rendering were principally stressed. As a result of this original artistic presentation, the district built on Shabolovka in 1930 was pleasingly different from other dwelling complexes built by means of the very same component sections.

## Experiments with curved forms

During the 1920s, Soviet architects resorted to semicircular a p  uilig t ar tional planning and expressive spatial composition.

Krutikov designed a competition entry for a three-storey hostel on a semicircular plan in 1924; a standard semicircular plan in 1924; a standard semicircular housing unit in 1926; and in 1927, as part of a scheme for an academic community designed in Ladovsky's studio at Vkhutemas, he used a semicircular section for a two-storey individual house, for a housing block composed of two sections, and for a seven- and eightstorey multi-section student hostel.

In 1928, both Mazmanyan and Kochar, also working in Ladovsky's studio at Vkhutemas, designed two-storey and multistorey dwellings on a circular ground plan. Mazmanyan designed a two-storey domed house, while Kochar's design was for a nine-storey hostel-type house on a circular plan, either single, or twinned with a shared stairwell.

Towards the end of the 1920s, Melnikov evolved a method of combining a set of intersecting cylinders. He used this in a number of designs for housing blocks and public buildings, as well as in the house he designed for himself in Moscow. Here, the curvilinear plan and the different ways of articulating the various elements generated a set of complex interior effects. Two rooms identical in their shape and dimensions, for instance, produced wholly different effects, because one was provided with a large plate-glass screen while the other was lit by a combination of forty hexagonal windows combined in a complex pattern.

The 'paraboloid' communal house for northern districts, by Bunin in 1930, provided another instance of originality in spatial organization. It consisted of a six-storey dwelling for communal use in the form of an upward-pointing paraboloid. It was set on pilotis with a central stairwell, from which circular corridors led on each floor to the doors of the individual rooms, as well as to a reading room, hall and open terrace. On the top floor, a sports hall was roofed over by a transparent dome.

## Equal opportunities for the consumer:

## a new approach to rationalizing accommodation

The tendency prevailing towards the end of the 1920s to design communal houses for a fully socialized way of life, on the one hand, and the provision by local councils of sectional housing with flats intended for single-room occupancy, on the other, undoubtedly delayed the development of a rationally planned standard town flat which took maximum account of functional family needs and the efficient organization of everyday life.

However, the new social circumstances and the living conditions which they imposed demanded the development of such a standard unit. The introduction of a single norm for the allocation of housing space required a fundamentally different approach to the design of flats: the consumer considerations governing living space no longer involved satisfaction of the needs of various layers of the urban population in accordance with their levels of income. They were determined instead by scientifically based standards of public health and other parameters, due attention being paid to the economic circumstances of the country as a whole. Equality of opportunity in securing accommodation was in practice, if not in theory, the decisive factor in the shaping of a new way of life, and radically altered the entire approach to the design and construction of urban housing.

The rationalized planning and equipment of flats became a matter ofintensive study towards the end of the 1920s, especially by the team ofarchitects which Ginzburg headed in 192829, first in the Typology Section of the Committee for Construction, RSFSR, and later in the Socialist Settlement Section.

The flat with its basic and ancillary accommodation was treated as a single spatial entity in which all the main processes of family life unfolded. These processes were closely studied so that they might be rationally organized within a restricted living space.

Built-in equipment, its design and efficient distribution, was given high priority as a means of improving comfort in a confined space. The economic circumstances of the time did not allow the allocation to every family of a flat with purpose-built rooms, so that the latter had to serve a number of purposes. In an effort to meet this need, adaptable equipment and furniture were designed, as well as flexible-purpose flats.

The Typology Section team devised an economical flat of laT tensiveeearc intoscentially bas andar ousing; studied the rationalized planning of housing units in the light of various basic features of everyday life; and took into account individual perceptions ofhousing in terms of size, lighting and colouring.

Side by side with this work by the Typology Section, Lissitzky at Vkhutein was directing a prramme for the development o mode built-n furniture, adaptable furniture sectional furniture and individual components of standard furniture, many of which were designed to fit the housing units evolved in the Committee for Construction.

The complexity of such an approach to the rationalized design of mass type flats and standard equipment and furniture for them not only reflected a highly professional approach to housing problems, but was also dictated by the new social conditions. Work done in the Soviet Union during the 1920s on the rationalization of urban flat dwelling was not directed, as elsewhere, towards the provision of cheap housing for low-income earners, but towards the design of standard flats for mass use. This was a fundamentally different approach to the design of a housing unit and was linked, in particular, with the establishment o  standard for the allocation of living space.

## Prefabricated accommodation and mobile dwellings

Suggestions for the use of standard elements in prefabricated buildings and for various forms of mobile dwellings crop up early in Soviet times. Thus Lavinsky assumed in his City on Springs design of 1921 that standard prefabricated components made of materials such as steel, aluminium, glass and asbestos would be used in construction.

A group of architects who had learned to use new timber structures when building the pavilions of the Moscow Agricultural and Handicraft Exhibition of 1923 worked in the design office of the Standard Joint-Stock Company in 1924-25. The company therefore organized the prefabrication by wood-processing plants of standard two-storey houses for workers' settlements, such as those used in 1925-26 at Ivanovo-Voznesensk.

Building materials were primarily reserved for industrial purposes towards the end of the 1920s, and were in very short supply. The construction process itself was converted as far as possible into the continuous industrial production of standard components for subsequent assembly. Both these features stimulated research and design work on the construction of prefabricated housing from local materials and industrial waste.

The first dwelling built of small breeze blocks was designed by two engineers, Krasin and Loleit, and erected in Moscow in 1927. Burov and Blokhin began a long run of designing breezeblock housing at the end of the 1920s. Research into the use of large building blocks was launched in 1929 at the Kharkov Structural Institute under the direction of the engineer Andrei Vatsenko. These investigations resulted in the construction of experimental city districts consisting of three-storey houses built with large breeze and concrete blocks in 1929; a trial sixstorey dwelling built of large blocks in 1930 in Kharkov, and a settlement consisting of similarly built houses at Kramatorsk in 1933.

Simultaneously with the construction of increasingly tall dwellings using large building blocks, a team headed by Ginzburg investigated the use of factory-made standard components for the assembly of low-rise timber houses. The scientific study and experimental design of standard prefabricated wooden houses was carried out initially at the Socialist Settlement Section of Gosplan, the State Planning Commission of the USSR, and later by the Group for Prefabricated House Building and Planning of Giprogor, the State Institute for Urban Design, in 1929-31. Designs for various kinds of dwellings built of local materials were produced. Provision was made in a number of these for variations of the basic lay-out by means of movable and collapsible partitions. It was proposed to set up special organizations for the construction of two-storey standardized housing with local materials. Building work was to be fully industrialized by the production of lightweight components in factories, for assembly on site with the help of small cranes. The basic structural components were to consist of prefbricatemberme nd fbrolte peli.

Designs incorporating prefabricated housing units began to appear at the turn of the decade. Varentsov's New City diploma project already included multi-storey dwellings with housing units suspended on brackets from a central pillar. In 1930, Ladovsky suggested in his Green City competition entry that a fully equipped housing unit or cabin, produced in two alternative designs, should be used as the basic standard element, rather than walled boxes or structural members. Such units would be factory-produced and brought on site for assembly in a wide variety of patterns ranging from individual houses to multi-storey buildings, offering both dwelling space and communal accommodation for general or specialized purposes. Ladovsky outlined a procedure whereby all communications would first be laid, a fully standardized framework would then be erected and a crane used to put the assembled cubicles into place, which would then be connected to all the services availa-

New types of buildings for social and administrative purposes in the Soviet Union

Part I/Social tasks of architecture

ble. The arrangement of the standard components of the framework allowed a choice of spatial compositions for such dwellings, while communal accommodation could be located anywhere within the framework and allowed as much space as necessary. This proposal was officially recognized as an original invention in 1931 and awarded an Author's Copyright Certificate 3714 No.21406.

A little book called My i doma (Ourselves and Houses) had been written by the Futurist poet Velemir Khlebnikov in 1914-15, but was only published in 1930. It contained many stimulating ideas about future housing, which only obtained wider currency during the second half of the twentieth century. Khlebnikov wrote that in times to come the housing unit or cabin would not only provide a fixed residence, but would move about freely with its occupant, who could thus carry on his or her usual existence wherever he or she went.

Khlebnikov suggested that all dwelling cabins should have strictly standard dimensions. All cities would be provided with metal cellular structures, or 'Frame Houses', with mooring bays for the cabins. All means of passenger transport, whether by road, rail or water, would be designed to carry these standard cabins. The dwelling cabin could be fitted into a Frame House bay in every city by special means of propulsion and lifting gear. Similar 'Frame Hotels' would be erected at leisure centres, where the bays for dwelling cabins would alternate with public accommodation, such as dining rooms, living rooms etc.

Khlebnikov considered it essential to vary the spatial composition of Frame Houses, so as to diversify the visual appearance of a city. He suggested several basic types of Frame House for this purpose, all of which could be assembled out of standard dwelling cabins. These included a Poplar House, a communications tower to which circular tiers of dwelling cabins could be attached; a Tape House designed for 1,000-2,000 cabins and representing a single or double row of cabins strung out along a vertical partition between two towers; a Bridge House consisting of towers and spans connecting them above ground or water, with bays for dwelling cabins; a Funnel

House, which represented a circular frame with a large inner courtyard; a Book House formed by two vertical planes (with bays), set at an angle to each other and resembling an open book; a Whisker House consisting of one, two or three vertical sets of dwelling cabins supported by a central metal rod; a Bowl House, in which several cabins were arranged under the cover of a glass dome along a high metal pylon; underwater houses; floating houses; pyramidal houses; housing complexes with a random distribution of dwelling cabins in natural surroundings; moving cabins; Chess Houses, with bays occupied by cabins in a chequerboard pattern; Cradle Houses; Field Houses, and so on.

The quest for a new form of housing during the period under review was marked by the boldness of its approach and the broad, clear and courageous statement of the problems.

The new type of housing was most closely connected with thel lh paey t the restructuring of the way of life. But other questions  concerning function, aesthetics and structure—were also raised by many designs for new housing. In fact, during those years Soviet architects intensively studied all aspects of the opportunities they encountered, such as novel methods of spatial organization, the alternatives available in combining housing and public accommodation, spatial models of housing units, rationalization in planning and equipping, new types of housing - single flats, blocks, single- and multi-sectional houses, prefabricated and mobile dwellings - and much else.

![](images/84213692bb75a679f11d7ac0654f49e5324b65f6f476eac2a6ed63fff6f4ba94.jpg)

Special social requirements

in the immediate post-Revolutionary period

The bitter political struggle which the working class had to undertake in order to establish a new system of government and radically reorganize the social and administrative systems was reflected in the elaboration of new kinds of public buildings.

During the early post-Revolutionary years, trade unions and Housing Communes played an important part in the evolution of a network of cultural and civic institutions. These proletarian organizations, in conjunction with the local councils, supplied the basic constituents of a new urban life and brought into existence a whole set of new mass institutions and, consequently, of new types of public building.

The Housing Communes which had sprung up spontaneously in the first years after the Revolution, and the communal houses for workers which they called into being, demanded new kinds of communal service accommodation, such as canteens, public laundries and children's facilities. Multi-purpose buildings characteristic of those early years - such as Palaces of Labour, Workers' Palaces, Palaces of Nations etc - played a leading part in the creation of a new kind of mass cultural establishment.

In the circumstances of the times, when the proletariat had only just set up the new structure of social organizations and mass cultural services, these multi-purpose buildings became the workers' social and cultural centres. They were accommodated in former private houses, palaces, Council Halls of the Nobility and other nationalized buildings. The range of activities for which they catered made it possible to determine the design specifications for new buildings of this kind. These covered a wide spectrum and included the provision of accommodation for social organizations, educational work, a theatre, club, museum, library reading room, and so on.

The very varied purposes fulfilled by such public buildings reflected a particular stage in the development of the network of new social organizations and institutions, as well as the functionally undifferentiated nature of the developing system of cultural and civic provision for the workers. The determination to bring together as many institutions and organizations as possiblewithin a single public building was, however, also strongly influenced by a wish to make the new proletarian public building conspicuously huge and majestic, to be seen as the unique centre of the workers' political and cultural life.

The upsurge of those early years shed a glow of revolutionary romanticism over the new requirements for public architecture. The workers were determined that the evidence for a bright and joyful future should be embodied from the very start in splendidly majestic buildings, which must essentially be 'Palaces': Workers' Palaces, Palaces of Nations, Palaces of Labour, Palaces of Culture, and so on. These were seen as symbols of the new power. They were also visible representations of a free working people's future city, often described in those days by such adjectives as 'huge', 'gigantic', 'bright', 'radiant", 'stupendous', 'luxurious', 'marvellous', 'majestic', "wellshaped', 'beauteous'.

The worker had become used to being in company during those stormy times, at meetings, assemblies and demonstrations. Having achieved power, the proletariat sought to express its monolithic unity and vastness in its public buildings.

All these considerations influenced the architecture of public buildings, representing as they did a set of social requirements arising out of actual circumstances. Architecture provided a spatial embodiment to the truly revolutionary way of life of those heroic years and created a framework for the activity of the revolutionary masses. The individual was seen as a member of a team, in constant motion - in processions, at meetings or marching in the ranks. This also accounts for certain characteristic features of early designs for public venues: vast open spaces for demonstrations, grand halls for meetings, and monumental flights of stairs.

## Palaces of Labour

Palaces of Labour were officially the venue of trade union organizations. In early Soviet years, however, they became typical of public buildings serving a wide range of purposes, many of them well outside the range of trade union activities.

A competition was launched in 1922 for the design of a Palace of Labour in Moscow, intended as a symbol of the new society. The list of functions specified for the building provides the clearest illustration of the complex uses to which these institutions were then put; it included: a large hall holding 8,000 people; a number of smaller halls for a variety of purposes, such as meetings, lectures, concerts, performances and films, for audiences of300, 500 and 1,000 people; sets of offices for the Moscow City Council and the Moscow Party Committee, including conference rooms and suites; a Museum of Social Sciences; a dining hall seating 1,500 people, and more. The upper levels of the building were intended to house a radio station and an observatory, among other things, while the possibility of laying an aircraft landing strip there was also to be investigated.

A variety of approaches were used to convey the spirit of the social changes then in progress in some fifty designs, submitted, among others, by the Vesnins, Trotsky, Hlya Golosov, Lyudvig, Melnikov, Belogrud, Ginzburg with Grinberg, and Sergei Toropov with Alexander Kuznetsov.

The requirements set in the Moscow Palace of Labour competition served as a model for a whole set of competitions for similar official buildings during the years 192326.

Thus the Rostov-on-Don Palace of Labour competition required the inclusion in a single building of a theatre seating 3,500; two concert halls, seating 500 and 1,000; a dining room for 1,000 people; a library reading room; rooms for group activities; and other accommodation. Designs for this were submitted by, among other, Boris Korshunov, Ilya Golosov with Ulinich, Sergei Ovsyannikov, Grinberg, Ginzburg and Aleshin. Much the same requirements were set in the competition for the Palace of Labour at Ekaterinoslavl (now Dnepropetrovsk) with entries submitted, among others, by Sergei Krestovnikov with Vasily Voinov, Grinberg, Grigory Barkhin, David Kogan, Raikh with Fridman, and Ginzburg with Korshunov, All the prizewinning designs provided for a Palace of Labour building consisting of functionally different components welded into a single complex composition.

Such Palaces of Labour represented centres of mass cultural activity with clearly differentiated sectors reserved for theatrical, club, sports, library, lecture and other purposes. Many public buildings serving a variety of functions first took shape within these Palaces of Labour complexes and then increasingly developed a separate existence during the second half of the decade, as clubs, libraries, sports buildings, theatres, Houses of Soviets, communal dining rooms or museums.

A competition was held in 1932 for a Moscow District Trade Union Palace of Labour. Among the most interesting designs submitted were those by Kornfeld and Milinis, with the main building opening in a semicircle on to the main road; Egorychev, Karra and Lamtsov, with a main hall formed by rhythmically arranged semi-cylinders of glass; and Kalmykov and Osipov, in whose design the wings of a thirty-storey building descended to street level in steps forming a series of terraces.

## Houses of Soviets

During early post-Revolutionary years, local Soviets, or Councils, were housed in existing buildings adapted for their use while the organization of the new authorities was being worked out and specifications were devised for specially designed buildings. Experimental designs at that early stage usually sought to provide a visual identity for buildings embodying the newly acquired workers' power, such as those by Rodchenko and Shevchenko for a Sovdep in 1920, and by Nikolsky for a rural district Soviet in 1921.

The development of a building type for a House of Soviets began in real earnest towards the mid-1920s, when the construction of buildings to house the new People's Authorities began in the Republics and administrative areas. Competitions for the design of Houses of Soviets exerted a considerable influence in this field.

One of the first of these, launched in 1924, was for a House of Soviets in Bryansk. Grinberg's design was implemented in 1926. Ilya and Panteleimon Golosov, Fomin, Rukhlyadev and Krinsky were also among those taking part. According to the specification, this House of Soviets was required to accommodate the Provincial Soviet, as well as Party, trade union, and Young Communist institutions and organizations, together with a group of public premises: a small and a large hall, seating 200 and 1,000-1,200 respectively, both for House of Soviets purposes, such as congresses and conferences, and for club activities, such as shows and film performances; together with a library and section offices. In most of the competition designs, the architectural image established by this building was mainly developed from its cultural and social component, since this offered greater scope for spatial composition than the strictly official part of the premises.

Two fundamentally different approaches to the design of a y tion for the Dagestan Republic's House of Soviets at Makhachkala. The clearest examples of the contrasting trends were

Zholtovsky's design, which was adopted, offering an impregnable castle with a closed inner courtyard, and that by Ginzburg, who attempted to stress through his spatial composition the democratic character and freedom of access promoted by the new kind of official premises, and the accessibility of the new authorities to the broad layers of the population.

Ginzburg further developed this approach in his competition design of 1927 for the Government House of the Kazakh Republic at Alma-Ata, which was adopted and carried out in 1929-31. It consisted of several blocks linked by corridors and a covered gangway. In his organization of interior space, the architect took special trouble over the reception areas. The interior composition is centred on a high entrance foyer, lit by paired windows from either side, which acts as a connecting link between all the main parts of the building. This foyer is laid out as an indoor patio, complete with a fountain and plants. Its glass partitions could be slid open in summer to let open air into the building.

Other interesting Government House designs can be found a Elista in Kalmykia, by Ilya Golosov, at Minsk in Belorussia, by Langbard, and at Tashkent in Uzbekistan, by Polupanov.

Houses of Soviets were also built during this period in Khabk by IGooUli;inGork, yGig; in Novosibirsk, by Gordeev, Turgenev and Andrei Kryachkov, while a new block was added to the Moscow Soviet building by Ivan Fomin.

The Krasnaya Presnya district of Moscow was among the first to acquire a local Council or Soviet building of its own, built in 192829 from a design by Golubev and Nikolai Shcherbakov. Leningrad architects made a major contribution to the design of District Houses of Soviets at the end of the 1920s and beginning of the 1930s. Great importance was attached to the actual siting of these buildings within the general lay-out of the district communal centres then being built. The Leningrad Narva District Soviet, located in a large open area, was designed by Trotsky and built as a flattened volume, with a contrasting high-rise component which provided the dominant accent in the entire district centre complex. Closely related to this was the design by Valerian Daugul and Viktor Tvelkmeier, awarded a first prize in the competition for a Vyborg District Soviet building in 1931. A different conception was used for the external elevations of Leningrad's Moscow District Soviet, by Igor Fomin, in which the main element of the spatial composition was a block, built on a circular ground plan with an inner courtyard, set against two counterbalancing three- and fourstorey blocks. The whole building was erected during the first half of the 1930s.

## The search for an aesthetic image for the country's 'Supreme Building'

The designing and building of 'palaces' of every kind in early Soviet times reflected the proletariat's determination to assert its position as the new ruling class. It was also proposed to create a grandiose monument to the Great Revolution. This urge was further manifested in the search for a national "'Supreme Building' which would become the symbol of the Revolution and the new society.

It was envisaged that this 'Supreme Building' would act as a nificance. Various institutional buildings were suggested at that early stage as suitable political symbols for the country as a whole, because, whatever its primary designation, the 'Supreme Building' was intended to provide for an assortment of functions.

A candidate for the part was, for instance, the Palace of Nations in Moscow, the foundation stone of which had been ceremonially laid in the autumn of 1918, and which was conceived as a grandiose edifice housing assembly halls, theatres and a wealth of accommodation for a variety of purposes. Another was the building forVesenkha, the Supreme Council for the National Economy, for the design of which a competition was also decreed that autumn.

To mark the contrast with the past, the 'Supreme Building' had to present a totally new artistic image. This largely accounts for the widespread support secured by Tatlin's Monument to theThird Inteatial nd  emphatically nove coc tion.

The First Congress of Soviets of the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics met on 30December 1922 and adopted 'A Resolution and Treaty for the Creation of the USSR'. Sergei Kirov moved a proposal that this occasion should be marked by 'undertaking in the very near future the construction of a monument in which the representatives of labour could meet'. 'It is often said of us,' he declared, 'that we wiped the palaces of

Part II/Social tasks of architecture

bankers, landowners and tsars off the face of the earth with the speed of lightning. That is correct. Let us now erect in their place the new palace of workers and labouring peasants.'

It was such a 'palace of workers and labouring peasants' that architects set out to design when they took part in 192223 in the competition for a Palace of Labour in Moscow, then intended to become the 'Supreme Building'

Great activity was devoted in Vkhutemas-Vkhutein to the design of a 'Supreme Building'. Three buildings vied for this role: the Central Palace of Labour — with designs by Kozhin and Sobolev in Alexander Vesnin's studio, and by Leonid Teplitsky in Golosov's studio - in 1926; the House of Congresses of the USSR - with designs by Rashel Smolenskaya, Glushchenko and Travin in Ladovsky's studio- in 1928-29; and the Comintern building  with designs by Komarova in Alexander Vesnin's studio, and by Kochar in Fridman's studio - in 1929.

## The competition for the Palace of Soviets, 1931-33

A competition for the design of a Palace of Soviets in Moscow was carried out during 193133 in four successive rounds. Its significance was outstanding in terms of the development of a new type of government building, the establishment of an artistic model for the country's 'Supreme Building' and the further evolution of Soviet architecture as a whole.

In the first, preliminary, round of the competition, which served to clarify the requirements, many architects treated the Palace of Soviets as a People's Forum, a place for mass collective occasions, such as demonstrations, meetings, political festivals, and both military and sports displays. The business part of the Palace, including the smaller of two halls, was accommodated in a separate set of premises subordinated in compositional terms to the main block of the building incorporatng a large hall. The ARU, Vopra, Asnova and Sass teams' entries were typical in this respect.

The ARU design, by Nikolai Beseda, Georgy Krutikov, Vitaly Lavrov and Valentin Popov, clearly distinguished between two sets of premises in the Palace, one reserved for official use, the other for mass purposes, which communicated through the outdoor area left open for demonstrations. The main component of the accommodation was a large rectangular hall capable of holding 15,000 people and arranged so that it could open on to the demonstration area or provide access to

In the Vopra design, by Alabyan, Karra, Mordvinov, Revyakin and Simbirtsev, the main hall which represented the pivot of the composition was sited and organized so that all the congress delegates' seats faced the main square, from which columns of demonstrators could move towards them when the glass front partition was opened.

In the Asnova design, by Balikhin, Budo, Prokhorova, Turkus, Romuald Iodko and F. Sevortyan, the main element of the building, which housed the large hall, represented a cube intersected by an inclined plane. This formed an amphitheatre, within the building and continuing outside it, in the open air, so as to enable the delegates to greet the demonstrators.

The large hall in the Sass team's project, by Leonid Pavlov and Mikhail Kuznetsov, was a glass and metal structure provided with walls that could be raised and a roof that could be lowered.

Some interesting individual elements for the Palace were also submitted by Ladovsky, in the form of a hemispherical dome with sloping tiers of external balconies; by Nikolsky, with a tent-shaped roof; and by Fidman, with a building drawn out lengthwise and composed of overhanging tiers.

The second round attracted 160 entries, including twentyfour from abroad. Spatial and stylistic treatments of the subject varied greatly in the designs submitted. There were simple monumental forms, by Boris Iofan and Hector Hamilton; symbolic compositions by Lyudvig, Vlasov, Kurovsky, Lamtsov and Igor Yavein; the application of traditional methods and forms by Zholtovsky, Golts, Sobolev and Parusnikov; and compositions following the forms of the new architecture by Le Corbusier, as well as by Alabyan and Simbirtsev. In the statement by the Council for the Construction of the Palace of Soviets concerning the results of the open competition, which awarded top prizes to the designs by Zholtovsky, Hamilton and Iofan, guidelines laid down for further work on the projects stressed 'monumental quality, simplicity, integrity and elegance in the architectural presentation', and the application 'both of new methods and the best employed in Classical architecture'.

The variety of approaches displayed in the open competition bcenoarhe thcooni thirteen entries. Three different conceptions stood out: a nationwide forum designed in the forms of the new architecture, as in the entries of Ginzburg, Gassenpflug and Lisagor, Ladovsky, and the Vesnins; a monument governed by simplicity of line, as in designs by Alabyan, Kochar and others, Golosov, and Iofan; and a set of closed compositions relying heavily on traditional forms, as in the designs by Zholtovsky, Dmitry Chechulin, and Shchusev.

Five teams competed in the fourth, closed round, and they all treated the Palace of Soviets as a single monument set on a compact ground plan, regardless of the external stylistic features adopted in each case, whether these were the forms of the new architecture by the Vesnins; simplified forms in the spirit of monumental Neo-Classicism by Iofan, Alabyan, Yakov Doditsa and others; or a generous use of traditional forms by Shchuko and Gelfreikh, and Shchusev. The trend established in successive rounds of the competition, which gradually converted the whole conception of a Palace of Soviets from that of a national forum into the image of a majestic monument, was further developed in the continuing work on Iofan's design, which had been adopted as the basis for the project.

## Official and administrative buildings

Intensive work on the design of a new type of administrative and business building began about the middle of the 1920s. The economic life of the country was reviving, a network of official and administrative institutions was being established, and trade relations were developing with many countries. All this required the construction of modern office premises for the newly created industrial, sales and foreign trade organizations.

During this period, competitions were held for the design of offices for the Arkos organization in Moscow and for Gosprom, the State Industry bureau, in Kharkov. The Vesnins' design of 1924 for the Arkos building was awarded the first prize, and stood out among the rest of the entries by its rational approach to the solution of functional requirements by structural means and its uncompromisingly modern look. It set a standard for further research into the design of office buildings and influenced many competition entries for the House of Textiles in 1925, for the Rusgertorg (Russo-German Industrial and Trade Commission), Orgametal (Trust for the Rationalization of Manufacture in Machine Building and in the Metal Processing

Industry) and Elektrobank buildings in 1926. The latest construction techniques such as ferro-concrete frames were used in these buildings, and this to a large extent determined the treatment of their façades.

When designing such office buildings, architects were closey portunity for functional experiments. They therefore devoted much attention to formal and aesthetic aspects, the more so because the commissioning agency as a rule insisted on buildings with a distinctive look. This led to the production towards the middle of the decade, largely by Constructivists, of some very striking office building desigs. Horizontal strips of glazing were combined with normal windows; solid areas of glass on the lower floors contrasted with blank walls in the upper storeys; colour was applied; and the lettering of signs and advertisements was used for decorative purposes in Russian and foreign-language inscriptions.

Office buildings in the mid-1920s also frequently fulfilled various additional functions. The specification for the House of Textiles, for instance, included, apart from office space, a restaurant, general store and hotel, while a shop, café, film theatre and lodgings were provided in the Rusgertorg building. One such variant of a multi-purpose office block was designed by Alabyan and Zalesskaya in Ladovsky's Vkhutemas studio, with shops at ground level, offices on the next two floors and a hotel on the top three.

Rationalist designers also essayed high-rise structures. As early as 1922-23, Krinsky took up a suggestion by Ladovsky and produced an experimental design for a Vesenkha skyscraper. This work was carried further in Ladovsky's Vkhutemas studio with designs by Volodko, Glushchenko, Vitaly Lavrov and others for a Vesenkha building of between forty and sixty storeys. In 1929, Ladovsky designed a 56-storey skyscraper on a cruciform ground plan for the Columbus Monument competition.

In the mid-1920s, there emerged office building designs which attempted to combine the Constructivist and Rationalist approaches. They were divided for purposes of spatial composition into two elements, one lower, on the lines of Arkos, the other a high-rise tower akin to the Rationalist concept. The outstanding examples of this approach were designs for the buildings of Izvestiya, by Grigory Barkhin, and of Gostorg, the State Trading Office for Import and Export, by Boris Velikovsky, both of 1925-27. In both cases, ferro-concrete frame buildings were to combine a main six-storey block with a central component of twelve to thirteen storeys.

However, a regulation issued in Moscow in 1926 banned the construction of buildings more than six storeys high within the Sadovoe or Garden Ringroad, although some structures rising to ten storeys had previously been erected there, such as a dwelling block by David Kogan.This made it necessary to stop high-rise construction while building work was already in progress and accounts for the fact that not a single high-rise building was constructed in central Moscow during these years. Such buildings nevertheless continued to be designed, in particular for publishing houses. Examples from Vkhutemas included designs byLeonidov, in Ladovsky's studio, and by Alexander Kurovsky, in Shchusev's, in 1926. Three- to five-storey buildings took their place, such as the offices put up in 1930 for Mosproekt, the Moscow Organization for Architectural Projects, on the Leningrad Highway, and for Transtroi, the Construction Organization for Refrigeration Works, on the Rozhdestvenka.

A competition was held in 1930 for a Pravda Publishing House building, for which Golosov and Kurovsky designed a structure with a high component at the angle of the building, while Lissitzky's entry, for instance, contained no high-rise elements. Yet the project adopted and carried out in the 1930s was that by Golosov, though shorn of its high-rise component.

The competition held in 1928 for the design of the Tsentrosoyuz headquarters set a trend in the design of office buildings and Leonidov's entry attracted particular attention. He offered a functionally well-conceived building comprising an entrance hall between the two parallel streets on either side, a rationally laid out working area, and a sober but nevertheless striking spatial composition. The Tsentrosoyuz building was finally bult incance with adesgn byLCorbus, thogh ny after he had considerably altered it.

During the period under review, appropriate People's Commissariats - or ministries — had not yet been set up, and industry was directed by republican and local Economic Councils called Sovnarkhozy, for which Houses of Industry and State Industry, or Gosprom, buildings were erected.

The Kharkov Gosprom building, designed and built by Serafimov, Felger and Kravets in 1925-28, represented an important landmark in the development of this form ofofficial administrative centre. Some twenty-five individual institutions — a Higher Council for the People's Economy, an Industrial Bank and various other industrial institutions - were housed in the vast Gosprom building consisting of three blocks linked by gangways. Each block had its own entrance hall, but they communicated internally and shared such facilities as a main conference hall, a dining room and a library.

The House of Industry at Sverdlovsk, the industrial headquarters for the Urals, closely resembled the Gosprom building in Kharkov. The competition for its design was carried out in two rounds: the first, in 1927, attracted entries from Alexander and Leonid Vesnin; Andrei Burov, Sinyavsky and Barshch; and Ivan Fomin, among others. The second, in 1931, received designs from Afanasev, Kornfeld and Milinis; and Ilya Golosov, among others.

A competition for a House of Industry in Moscow attracted entries from, among others, Panteleimon Golosov; Zalesskaya, Korzhev, Lissitzky and Prokhorova; Ladovsky and Fridman; and Simbirtsev. The most interesting design in this case originated from Leonidov. In it, each specialized category of employees, up to a maximum of 120, was accommodatedon a standard-size floor of the building, divided into areas of 5 m² per person. The floor was soundproofed and partitions between the individual areas were replaced by pot plants. One side of these areas was reserved for rest, physical exercise and a library, together with space reserved for consuming food sent from below, showers, a pool, paths, running tracks and recepres.Te sral m he ing and the walls could be moved aside in summertime. The lower, extended floor contained an assembly hall, a gymnasium, club premises, cloakrooms etc, and on its roof a movable pool, sports accommodation and running track. The top floor housed a hotel for visitors, and an intermediate open flor provided a restaurant and open-air walks.

Experimental designs for a standard House of Industry were carried out in Ladovsky's Vkhutemas-Vkhutein studio. Earlier designs in 1924-25 by Glushchenko, Volodko and Vitaly Lavrov for Vesenkha buildings had concentrated all the accommodation in skyscrapers built on a compact ground plan, but in Silchenkov' desin for  House of Industry and Trade he sa tial composition took account of both the connections between the various component institutions, and their separate identities, expressing these in a high-rise block for the central organi-
