Chapter 6/Development of communal supply and service systems

![](images/a7847ec32f121462a0515018eaecd2e26148c69f78d8a1e88676ef1c97090d01.jpg)

![](images/69661ed93eb93343b29b29fa88a69e875bd09481f44fb97656ace2b4e4f5b31c.jpg)

![](images/ef3688f696943151c1515a070a2535dc7aff5f0eb0f2a4f2018199267726a344.jpg)

![](images/6ed066152c8fe7404f3a4c5e60038ffe4b0b255cf95371cddb20dfa274a3edc5.jpg)

![](images/3faf0b400b427d1ab82e9a25ef8c9d9b82a3016d967265b0d985c884f17bd1db.jpg)

nial entrance hall, a covered swimming pool and a gymnasiu wel  enot d i sn r sa tors.

When the mass construction of communal buildings got into ie0ual lic baths became more realistic. Designs were produced for bathing establishments of various capacities, including some standard models, which provided bath and shower cubicles, as well as public facilities.

The extensive drive to provide public baths at the turn of the decade greatly contributed to the improvement of communal services for the workers. The development of a new type of public baths conceived as a complex communal building was also in progress. Designs produced in Vkhutemas and in Nikolsky's Leningrad studio did much to establish the architectural specifications involved.

The specifications for designs of public baths produced in 1926 by Zaltsman, Marmorshteinand others in Vkhutemas closely approximated to those of the earlier thermal establishments, in that the actual bathing facilities were subordinated to a group of sports premises, such as large swimming pools and gymnasiums.

The designs produced by Nikolsky in 1928 vividly illustrate the gap between the economic realities of that period and the quest for new types of public buildings. He designed public baths for two districts of Leningrad, which were duly built. Those for the Smolny district were housed in a two-storey building, with the bathing facilities arranged in a circular edifice round an inner courtyard, and an adjoining rectangular vestibule. The building for the Moscow-Narva district represented a complex spatial composition in which the simple outlines of the low and wide rectangular component blocks were contrasted with the convoluted feature at the angle of the building.

Nikolsky also produced an experimental sketch at this time for a public bathhouse designed for a daily intake of 4,000 users, as a spread-out single-storey circular building housing the baths and a large swimming pool at its centre, covered by a glass dome.

Thus, during the second half of the 1920s, a new type of building combining bathing and sports facilities was being intensively investigated, while the design and construction of public baths, as such, continued. The two concepts gradually converged, and the experimental designs shed any sports facilities, such as gymnasiums, that were unconnected with swimming. The result provided architectural specifications for a new type of municipal building - the public baths and swimming pool — on which the design of baths in various districts of Moscow, for instance, was based.

Two designs answering to this new requirement and submitin 8 or etiion to build bah in a paricular pt of the Zamoskvorechie district of Moscow are important in this connection. Nikolai Morozov's design provided for a three- and four-storey L-shaped building, consisting of two adjoining blocks and comprising: bathing accommodation for men and women; 225 shower cubicles; a sports swimming pool for use by 100 competitors and stands for 600 spectators; a section with fifty individual baths; a radiation and hydrotherapy section; and a 'while-you-wait' laundry. An open-air stadium was laid out next to the baths. In Dmitriev's design, the blocks housing the baths and swimming pool were laid out along a single axis, as part of one long building on a rectangular plan. Provision was made for a large adjacent playing field, as well as a sun roof, a doctor's surgery and physical training accommodation.

Several such combined public baths and swimming pools were built in Moscow. In 1930 alone, two large establishments were completed, one in the Proletarsky district, by Panin, and the other in the Bauman district, by Nikolai Gundorov. The latter, designed in 1928, was a two-storey brick building with separate bathing sections for men and woman. Inserted between these were a swimming pool with spectator stands (part cubicles and a diving board.

Combined public baths and swimming pools were also built in other cities, notable amongst them being that in Leningrad of the early 1930s, by Sergei Vasilkovsky.

![](images/e914cc82a2fab827785600520128f74aabb1b20f6fb4969507255f410ed78451.jpg)

Designing a new type of school

The reconstruction of the entire educational system, including secondary schools, specialized and higher educational establishments, began immediately after the October Revolution. In the studio headed by Zholtovsky in the Architectural Art Department of the People's Commissariat for Education, specifications for model school buildings were prepared and design competitions were launched, such as those for the Tolstoy Memorial School at Yasnaya Polyana in 1919 and for the Labour School, with entries by Ilya Golosov and Krinsky, in 1920.

The concept of the so-called Labour School, with an experimental team-method of tuition and a polytechnical approach, was a new idea of this time. Here, programmes and types of schools changed, as did the methodology of school work, from class work to tutorials and a combination of study and labour. All this was necessarily reflected in the design of school buildings.

As a rule, the schools built during the second half of the 1920s were designed on a pavilion pattern and an asymmetrical ground plan. Individual blocks were often linked at firstfloor level by covered gangways. The whole complex design was also frequently surmounted by the small cupola of the school's observatory, as though to mark its polytechnical character.

In addition to ordinary education, the new school was intended to impart physical, labour and socio-political training, and therefore contained not just classrooms, but also laboratories, studies, workshops, a gymnasium, library, dining hall, accommodation for pupils' associations and recreation halls, one of which could be adapted for meetings, shows and concerts. Sports grounds, a garden and a vegetable plot adjoined the school.

Ivan Rybchenko designed a model four-storey, ten-year school in Moscow in which the experimental tutorial method was to be applied with a bias towards production work, for which it provided carpentry and metal processing workshops, ativrt nd mus sudios,hys nd biemisboratories.

The following schools may be singled out among those built at this time: in Leningrad, the schools in Stachek Street, by Nikolsky in 1926-27; Lesnoi Prospect, by Nikolsky and Vladimir

Galperin in 1930-32; Tkachei Street, by Simonov in 1928-29; and in Stachek Street again, by Igor Fomin in 1930; in Moscow, the schools in Rusakovsky Street, by Fedorov in 192829; and Krymsky Square in 1930; and in Baku, the School Palace, by Tersakov in 1926-27.

Besides the design and construction of such mass models for school buildings, architectural research was also being undertaken in connection with the proposed radical reconstruction of family life and the application of new principles to the spatial organization of school buildings.

The first of these categories covered designs for Child Communes, with school, communal and dwelling accommodation, such as that by Petr Pomazanov in 1930, and children's educational combines providing tuition for children of all ages.

The second category was represented by the experimental designs produced in 1926-28 in Nikolsky's studio, for a new spatial treatment of school buildings. Instead of a multi-storey building with classrooms lit by windows from the side, Nikolsky offered a single-storey complex in which groups of classrooms and wide recreational areas, also serving as passages, were lit from above. The composition centred on the school hall, a vaulted and symmetrically sited building. The groups of structures housing the canteen and kitchens, specialized workshops and laboratories, were conceived as projections and functionally isolated from the rest of the complex.

## Workers' Preparatory Faculties, higher education establishments and technical schools

From its very start, the Soviet regime faced an acute shortage of trained cadres, and this situation deteriorated even further when industrialization speeded up throughout the country during the second half of the 1920s. It was also essential to provide the offspring of workers and peasants with a specialized secondary and higher education, as part of the important social task of creating a new working intelligentsia. This problem could only be resolved by a radical reconstruction of the ente educational system. During the very first years following the Revolution, a wide network of Rabfaks, or Workers' Preparatory Faculties,hadbeen created,in which workersand pents who had not received a secondary education were given intensive preparation for entry into higher education establishments. Thus, for instance, the Izo or Fine Arts Department at the Workers' Preparatory Faculty of the Arts, prepared students for entry into Vkhutemas. Many thousands of young workers and peasants passed through these faculties, and by the second half of the 1920s this was already altering the social mix of students in higher education.

The design of higher education establishments, usually with the inclusion of Workers' Preparatory Faculties, had begun in earnest in the middle of that decade. Among the first such competitions was that held in 1926 for the design of a university in Minsk. The specifications required a complex comprising: alecture block; blocks for individual faculties; a club block; a hostel for university and Workers' Preparatory Faculty students. The Constructivist designs by Ginzburg, Leonidov, Vladimirov and Vasily Krasilnikov, and Vegman were among the most interesting entries.

The designs carried out in Vkhutemas in 1926-27 for a Higher School of Art complex, including a teaching block, club premises, and student hostel, were also noteworthy. These were produced under the direction of two Rationalist leaders: Ladovsky, for the designs by Krutikov, Glushchenko and Vitaly Lavrov, and Dokuchaev, for those by Varentsov and Gelfeld.

A comparison of the Constructivist designs for Minsk University and those of the Rationalists for a Higher School of Art reveals strikingly different approaches to composition: the neat and functional efficiency of the ground plan and spatial organization in the former, and the search for a spatially expressive solution which characterized the latter.

A competition was held in 1927 for the design of a Polytechnical Institute, with Workers' Preparatory Faculty attached, at Ivanovo-Voznesensk. Ivan Fomin's design in his Proletarian Classical style,typical of that period, was selected and later carried out.

The design for a Higher Co-operative Institute in Moscow, produced in 1929 in Nikolsky's studio, is very important in terms of the search for a new spatial conception of an educational building. Just as in his experimental design for a school, Nikolsky proposed to accommodate laboratories and lecture halls in a single-storey structure lit from above, rather than in a multi-storey building. He saw a number of advantages in this arrangement because it provided more easily for the functional intercommunication and necessary grouping ofindividual premises while t ng as le cng or the snt and more evenly spread.

In 1930-31, Ginzburg headed a group of architects in the desi Trade TehnicalCombie or the taiigqualified cadres at the Chelyabinsk Tractor Factory. This complex was made up of a number of separate blocks linked by enclosed walkways: a single-storey production training block, a circular lecture block, ringed by a corridor, with sectional lecture rooms and a teachers' common room at the centre.

## Libraries

Libraries were regarded in the 1920s as one of the most important factors in the cultural revolution, on the same level as clubs and schools. Thousands of reading room cabins were opened in villages, as well as 'Red Corner' leisure rooms, libraries and reading rooms in towns and workers' settlements, during the early years of Soviet power.

Libraries stood for access to knowledge, and to the cultural achievements of the world at large, by broad sections of the working population, and they were awarded a place of honour among the socially new types of public buildings then being designed. It is thus not a matter of mere chance that the first task set by Nikolsky to senior Obmas students in Vkhutemas was to design a library as one ofa town's foremost social centres, complex in its composition and monumental in appearance. Proposals by Balikhin and Mochalov were amongst those resulting.

Work on a model city library continued in Vkhutemas-Vkhutein over the years. The difficulty of the brief was gradually increased, and the library turned into an elaborate complex combining the functions of an institution serving cultural, educational, scientific and study purposes. Hence the diploma subject set in 1927 for a Lenin Institute of Librarianship in Moscow, with designs by Leonidov and Viktor Pashkov.

A competition was held in 1928 for the design of the Lenin Library in Moscow. The first prize went to the entry by Dmitry Markov, Fridman and Fidman in which the multi-storey bookstore was symbolically shaped in the form of an open book.The Vesnins used a consistently functional approach in a paviliontype design in which superimposed specialized blocks were linked by corridors and gangways. Nikolsky offered a different type of composition in the spirit of Suprematist Constructivism, with a contrasting juxtaposition of variously shaped parallelepipeds, while Shchusev submitted an entry in the Constructive Style. The eclectic Neo-Classicist design by Shchuko and Gelfreikh was adopted and its execution began in 1930.

A competition was also held in 1928 for the design of a block for the Leningrad Public Library. Nikolsky provided the most interesting entry, with a round reading room as the basis of the composition.

## Scientific establishments

The cultural revolution and the reconstruction of the national economy were integrally linked with a quickening tempo of scientific work, which had virtually to be started from scratch in many areas. Great importance was attached to integrated scientific development, from basic research to the most downto-earth applications. The areas subject to the most rapid development were those upon which expansion of the national economy depended in industry, transport and agriculture.

Large scientific establishments were created or greatly expanded and new buildings began to be provided for them from the mid-1920s onwards. Among the first of these was the Institute of Mineral Raw Materials in Moscow: a competition was opened for it in 1925 and attracted entries from Ilya Golosov, Ginzburg and Fidman among others. The design ultimately used was by Viktor Vesnin.

Dung e hal e 0 graduates of the Moscow Higher Technical Institute, MVTU, under the direction of Alexander Kuznetsov, were adopted for the construction of Tsagi, the Central Institute of Aero- and Hydrodynamics, and of the All-Union Electro-Technical Institute. The functional specifications and architectural forms applied in both cases reflect the technological, production and scientific aspects involved.

On the other hand, the specifications for the Moscow Institute of Invalids, built in accordance with a design by Ginzburg in 1931, were wholly different. The Institute was conceived as a scientific establishment founded to devise methods of fitting the disabled into employment. It could be said to combine the functions of a scientific research institute, a training workshop, a university clinic and a cultural educational establishment. Ginzburg attempted to reconcile all these various functions, while recognizing the importance of providing healthy working conditions for the disabled whom the Institute was designed to serve. He therefore distributed blocks with different functional assignments amid vegetation and linked them together by enclosed gangways. The main block was presented side-on to the street, with a glazed screen supported by slender pillars.

Among the scientific research establishments built in the 1920s the following are worthy of note: the Experimental Veterinary Institute in Moscow, by Vladimirov and Vorotyntseva; and the Institute for Marxism-Leninism in Moscow by Chernyshev, of 1926-27. Notable amongst the unbuilt designs were those for a Mining Institute in Stalino by Shteinberg, of 1929, and for an Institute for Industrial Safety in Moscow, by Milinis, of 1929.

Sport and leisure

![](images/09c6e31a4f0fd163c19a27ef00a2ff09241a7d8eae1a359cbf281bf39a9d2ed9.jpg)

Sports installations

The involvement of the broad masses in sport became one of the more important measures in the field of health and culture undertaken by the Soviet regime during the earliest post-Revolutionary days.

The Programme for Universal Military Training of the Citizens of Soviet Russia, Vseobuch, did a great deal to bring sport into the workers' daily life during the Civil War. This movement, initiated by the workers and peasants for the creation of armed detachments in factories, cities and villages to defend the Proletarian Revolution, also created a large network of playing fields and promoted the construction of the first sports installations.

The foundation stone of the All-Russian Sports Stadium was laid in Moscow on the Lenin Hills in 1920 at the instigation of Nikolai Podvoisky, the Head of Vseobuch; this installation was soon thereafter renamed International Red Stadium, known as MKS. Specifications were prepared for the stadium, sketch designs were made, and monuments and statues were commissioned from sculptors.

A competition was launched in March 1924 for the design of the MKS, in which teams of students from Vkhutemas and the Moscow Institute of Civil Engineers, Migi, were invited to take part. This work was treated as an academic activity, with each student engaged on a separate item forming part of the project as a whole. Vkhutemas students were awarded prizes for two of their collective entries and were commissioned to continue the elaboration of the design under Ladovsky's direction.

The International Red Stadium was intended for All-Union and international sports competitions, such as the workers' equivalent of the Olympic Games, the Octobriads. It was to form a fully developed complex with sports, spectator, agitational and entertainment facilities, including a stadium, clubhouse, mass action theatre, landing stage, bath house, entrance arch, kiosks, and sports hostel.

In 1924, a group of Vkhutemas students under Ladovsky's direction designed a number of items for the sports complex, some of which were immediately erected, such as the entrance arch, kiosks and an amphitheatre for open-air plays in summer. Ladovsky, with Krinsky and the students, produced a detailed design for the actual stadium, set on a steep gradient and dropping down to the river in terraces. A model of the stadium was shown at the Paris International Exhibition in 1925. The MKS competition was the first occasion on which Asnova members worked together on a practical project. Among the designs produced in Ladovsky's Vkhutemas studio in the course of several years, the sports hostel by Krutikov and Valentin Popov, the clubhouse by Turkus and the mass action theatre by Lamtsov deserve special mention. The MKS theme was also set for diploma purposes in Ladovsky's studio, and Korzhev's work on the stands represented an important original contribution in this category.

Stadia began to be built throughout the country during the second half of the 1920s. They usually comprised an open-air playing field with surrounding stands and a clubhouse. Examples of typical stadia built in Leningrad from designs produced in Nikolsky's studio were the Red Sports International Stadium in 1927 and the Red Putilov Factory Worker Stadium in 1928. The most interesting work done in Nikolsky's studio concerned variations in the shape and structure of stands. Original types of ferro-concrete stands were designed, as well as a stand built on alluvial sand. The latter was designed for the bowlshaped stadium on Krestovsky Island in Leningrad during 1932-34, and was ultimately built with some alterations.

The two sports complexes of the Dynamo Society in Moscow, by Langman and Lazar Cherikover, and Leningrad, by Oleg Lyalin and Yakov Svirsky, stand out by their size among stadia built towards the end of the 1920s.

In addition to fully developed sports complexes, individual types of sports installations were also developed during the 1920s. Their design frequently included an original treatment of the roof area, with complex and expressive spatial compositions, such as the design for the Red Khamovniki clubhouse in Moscow, by Lamtsov and Viktor Petrov in 1928, and that of a swimming pool, by Mikhail Minkus in 1929.

The beginning and end of the 1920s were marked by the design of large sports complexes of international significance. The first of these was the International Red Stadium, the second was the All-Union Physical Training Combine in Moscow. The preliminary round of the competition to design the latter was held in 1931-32. The stadium and stands holding 120,000 places were intended for international Spartakiad Games and displays of the country's own sporting achievements. The most interesting among the four entries were probably the two submitted by teams of students from the Moscow

Part I/Social tasks of architecture

Chapter 8/Sport and leisure

Architectural Construction Institute, Asi, under the direction of Ilya Golosov. In these designs, the main stands served both the central stadium and the mass display field, as likewise could the stands round the football field through gaps along either its width or its length.

## Parks of Culture and Leisure

From the earliest years of the Soviet regime, the parks, gardens and squares that had previously been privately owned were thrown open to all citizens. As public parks were laid out, and club and sports complexes were organized, a new type of urban amenity gradually took shape, the Park ofCulture and Leisure.

Experiments carried out in the Central Park of Culture and Leisure in Moscow, which became a sort of laboratory for this purpose, provided a basis for the elaboration of specifications and functional planning methods for other parks of the new type elsewhere. A large tract along both banks of the Moscow River was set aside, including the area of the 1923 Agricultural Exhibition, the Neskuchny Garden, the Lenin Hills and Luzhniki. It was thought that such a park must include a sports base, a military cantonment, club premises, an art exhibition area, a children's village, public eating places and quiet areas.

Diploma tasks were set in Vkhutein in 1929 with the Park of Culture and Leisure in Moscow as an object. Two of the main problems encountered were those of convenient communications with the various districts of Moscow and the organic integration of various installations into the natural environment. A system of entrances conveniently connecting the park with the city at large had to be provided, as well as an entry arch for the recption oorganized bodies ofvisitors for official clebrations and parades.

In Mazmanyan's design, from Ladovsky's studio, a ceremonialroute fanked by stands led the group visitor toan unus ual triumphal arch, designed as a wide ramp on a figure-ofeight plan. The visitor approaching the park by this 'archramp' gradually climbed higher and higher and attained a panoramic view of the whole park by following the convolutions of the ramp. In Zalesskaya's design - also produced in Ladovsky's studio - the composition hinges on the contrast between two structural complexes, the first being the sports area in Luzhniki, at the park's lowest point, the second at its culmination on the Lenin Hills, in the club area. The children's villages are variously interpreted. Three age groups - babies, toddlers and schoolchildren - were to be provided for. Alabyan, from the same studio, designed the park crèche as a sort of human cloakroom: a long low block where the parents entering the park would hand over their babies. The kindergartens in this design were arranged so that each small group of children had an area of its own to play in.

A competition was held in 1931 for the actual design of the Moscow Central Park of Culture and Leisure, as a link in the complex chain of the city's parks. The entries concentrated on a search for the most rational ways of zoning the park area under existing circumstances. Designs by Melnikov and Ginzburg provided the most elegant solutions.

Melnikov centred his composition on Luzhniki and divided the semicircular area there into sectors or functional zones which converged towards the main entrance and fanned out in the direction of the river. The Neskuchny Garden and the mass of vegetation on the Lenin Hills were reserved for quiet leisure. Ginzburg's approach to zoning was different. He divided the park into a series of strips which hugged the curves of the river. The large number of entrances on the perimeter of the park allowed the visitor easy access to the functional zone of his or her choice by way of transverse alleys.

## Rest homes

Before the Revolution, the vast majority of the working population had no opportunity to use holiday resorts or rest homes. From the very first years of Soviet power, holiday resorts were put at the disposal of workers and peasants. A new way of using these and other leisure centres was gradually developed and perfected. The provision of rest homes by trade unions, large undertakings, ministries and other organizations, together with greatly reduced fares subsidized by social insurance, became widespread. Former country houses and palaces, boarding houses and hotels were re-equipped for this purpose, in addition to the construction of new accommodation in leisure centres.

One of the first competitions for the wholesale development of ret zone was held in 1926 for the construion o the Soci-Matsesta resort. Avraam Zilbert's design deserves to be singled out among the entries submitted. In it, the butt ends — cayinall t dwelling blocks, sited along the contours of the ground, faced the communal services block comprising a tall six-storey component.

In 1928, Vkhutein students were set the subject of 'A Holiday Hotel', comprising 200 bedrooms and a group of public rooms. In Alexander Vesnin's studio, Afanasev solved this complex set task by linking a high part of the shoreline with the sea via an extended hotel block which spanned the gradient of the shore, while the residential quarter descended in terraces along a sloping corridor. Nikolai Sokolov's design preserved the natural environment by scattering circular residential cabins of various sizes, suitable for one, two or three guests, and raised on a central pillar surrounded by a spiral flight of stairs, amid the surrounding vegetation. In Ladovsky's studio, Bunin suggested the construction of a permanent service centre accompanied by temporary living accommodation in its vicinity.

The Green City mentioned earlier, for which a competition was held in Moscow in 1930, was intended not as a city but as an unusual idea for a workers' recuperation area. As noted above, Ginzburg and Barshch departed from the specifications and treated Green City as part of a linear settlement. But their design also contained many interesting suggestions for the organization of leisure, such as stadia, tourist pavilions etc.

Ladovsky designed Green City as a city of rest and socialist living, an extensive holiday resort for Moscow workers. The axis of his plan was the motorway linking the resort with Moscow. The individual leisure districts would be linked with the motorway by access roads forming closed loops. The overall planning pattern and basic principle of development in his design are reminiscent of a tree, with the motorway as the trunk and the access roads as the branches, A railway station, central hotel, department store etc, were sited near the planning axis, while rest homes, a winter camp with tourist pavilions, a children's village and other installations would be scattered around the area.

Melnikov conceived Green City as a city of rationalized leisure,implying frst and foremost the rationalization o sleep, the main form of rest. In his design, the Green City area was enclosed within a ring of main transport routes and divided into sectors of forest, fields, gardens and vegetable plots, a zoo, a children's village and a social area, at the centre of which stood an Insttue  Man.Thevisitors relaxi among thereey had at their disposal mobile restaurants, libraries and sports installations. Various establishments were included, such as a railway station, a spa, hotels and tourist pavilion. But Melnikov chiefly concentrated on the sleeping accommodation. The dwelling blocks were equipped so as to enhance the quality of sleep, with specially designed beds, controlled pressure and composition of the air, piped music etc.

Fridman's design treated Green City as a forest resort. Most of the installations in it were to be assembled from standard components, with dwelling units linked to each other in long chains.

Ladovsky's design won, and he was commissioned to develop the settlement. In addition, all four competitors were enabled to test the dwelling units they had designed in fullscale models. The working design and construction of the project began in 1930-31 with the production of a scale model, the laying of the road and initial construction of a number ofinstallations, but was then wound up at the end of the second townplanning debate.

During the period under review the following buildings erected were of special interest: the sanatoria in Matsesta by Shchusev, of 1926-35, in Sochi by Miron Merzhanov, of 193133, and in Barvikha by Boris Iofan, of 192934; also the rest house of Lake Sevan by Kochar and Mazmanyan, of 193435.

The development of new forms of public building during this period mirrored the complex and inherently contradictory course of development of new public, administrative, educational and other establishments and the search for new kinds of mass cultural work, for education and for communal provision for leisure. Particular types of institution came to the fore at successive stages of this evolution, and their requirements were then embodied in architectural specifications for the new types of buildings needed, followed by their design and construction. Such new institutions, however, often substantially altered their original character as practices changed. In some cases they simply proved unviable, or, having fulfilled their intended funcon, werac by hrs  the next sag ess.

All this was equally reflected in the search for new types of public buildings, in changes in the specifications for them and in changing attitudes to certain types of structures. These considerations obtained with particular force in those areas of architecture where the development of new types of buildings was most intense, such as that of Palaces of Labour, Houses of Soviets, Workers' Clubs, mass action theatres, mass kitchens, swimming baths, labour schools, Workers' Preparatory Faculties and rest homes. These researches and experiments in the development of new types of public building played an important part in the establishment of the world's first socialist architecture. They helped to introduce a new culture into everyday life, and they laid a firm foundation for the further evolution of this area of architecture in the Soviet Union.

![](images/1410c13da25ab2afb4b9ec7a124aea9858f653f8451d148715a4875dc47a0ce1.jpg)

Masters and trends:

biographies, statements, manifestos

![](images/ed70b2f6f8b795971428ce5a29a3fb7556cf9a29118fd5d7adf73ffc66fbed41.jpg)

The new Soviet architecture of the 1920s is often treated in the foreign - and even domestic - architectural press as a rather amorphous phenomenon devoid of any clear-cut differences betweenindividual trends. Such an approach makes it imposeS y

At a certain stage of its development, every creative trend produces a theoretical credo as well as a specific system ofartistic devices and methods. This needs to be stressed in the present case because the existence of stylistic systems has usually only been recognized within the traditionalist Soviet architectural trends of this time, such as the Neo-Renaissance, Proletarian Classicist, Neo-Armenian schools, and so on.

It is true that those who favoured the new trends categorically denied that it was necessary to develop any specific system of compositional means and methods and insisted on the predominant place of a creative approach, such as the psychoanalytical or the Functional Method.

Nevertheless it was inevitable that the differences in aesthetic conceptions and methods of design between, say, Rationalism and Constructivism should be reflected in their manner of creating a spatial composition, in their attitude to particular shapes and details, and in the specific artistic means and methods which they employed.

The instances of Rationalist and Constructivist designs quoted in earlier chapters clearly demonstrate that - despite the stylistic unity of the new architecture as suchworks by the supporters of each of these trends display different formal and aesthetic characteristics.

Both Rationalists and Constructivists rejected Classicism and genuinely made no use of traditional forms in their work. Nevertheless, a comparison of the means they employed in the spatial composition of buildings and larger complexes undeniably suggests that the Constructivists were more closely tied to tradition. The geometrical orderliness of their compositions points to the indirect influence of Classicist aesthetic concepts. This inherited strand is obvious in the works of most leading Constructivists - Alexander Vesnin, Leonidov, Andrei Burov and others  even though they themselves were unaware of it.

This is clearly shown by comparing the entries of Osa Constructivists with those of Rationalists from Asnova and ARU, in competitions such as those for the Proletarsky District Palace of Culture, the All-Union Palace of the Arts, the Palace of Soviets in Moscow, the theatre in Kharkov, and for the planning and development of new towns, such as Magnitogorsk, Avo nKtsk  we  esor the uili a number of Moscow squares in 1931.

In the Constructivist designs, the compositional means employed clearly revealed Classicist attitudes. There was, of course, no question of any direct analogy. It was a matter of approach to the distribution of buildings in space and the means of composing an orderly composition for the whole complex. Classicist tradition is also perceptible in the rules applied to the spatial composition of single buildings.

The differences between the creative methods employed in either case are exemplified in the output of two of the most talented representatives of Rationalism and Constructivism, Melnikov and Leonidov.

Both produced designs that were entirely novel, entirely valid as works of art and devoid of any association with the past. Nevertheless, despite the modernity and exquisite quality of Leonidov's designs, a Classicist underlay is clearly perceptible in them, which is, also present, incidentally, in the work of Mies van der Rohe, but absent from the works of Melnikov.

Classicism also underlay Ilya Golosov's creative concepts, and in particular his theory of the construction of architectural organisms. Hence Golosov's Constructivist, rather than Rationalist position in the mid-1920s. He was in no sense by inclination a radical reformer of the methods and means of architecture. In his analysis of the properties of various geometric volumes, for instance, he came to the conclusion that certain solids, such as a vertical half-cylinder, a quarter-sphere etc, could not be used for architectural purposes on their own and needed to be 'supported' by other volumes. He also identified a group of "negative volumes', such as a reversed pyramid, an ellipsoid etc, which, in his opinion, were counterindicated for architectural purposes. Even when designing genuinely innovative, boldly shaped dynamic compositions, Golosov observed certain general principles in the construction of architectural organisms which were similarly basic in Classical terms. This set Golosov's output in early Soviet years apart from the experiments of Ladovsky and Krinsky, who were intent on widening the frontiers of what was 'permissible' in composition. It also marked the difference between his work and that of Melnikov, who refused to acknowledge the existence of 'negative spatial forms' in architecture and would select any shape and rotate it through space, regardless of accepted practice.

The creative positions of Rationalists and Constructivists differed as much in matters of form as they did in the field of spatial composition. The Constructivists primarily rejected all outward traditional forms and practices. They justified the new forms which they introduced by their constructional and functional efficiency. The Rationalists, on the other hand, may have plumbed deeper layers of the Classicist aesthetic concept by their rejection of compositional principles inherited from the past. They looked for renewal in terms of a human sense of space and form as such, on the level of universal perceptual rules.

While the Constructivists rejected traditional forms, they equally preserved much of the essence of Classicist artistic principles. The inevitable result was the development of a characteristically Constructivist compositional system, and even a form of near-Constructivist stylization - the Constructive Style. This situation was equally characteristic of European Functionalism as a whole. Its new forms and methods were just as quickly stereotyped, both generally and within individual trends such as the Bauhaus style, Le Corbusier's Five Principles, the Mies School, and so on.

The Rationalist conception, on the other hand, offered a far more open artistic approach, in that it seemed to lack those identifiably Classicist ingredients which made for formalization and stylization. Initially, the struggle of the new architecture against tradition was chiefly a matter of elaborating a new form and style. In fact, many innovativearchitects believed that the unprecedentedly functional nature of buildings was a crucial factor in the development of such a style. The Rationalists, however, never regarded questions of style as central  a position shared by Frank Lloyd Wright, whose views were close to theirs. Ladovsky treated the development of form, not as the mere outcome of the rapid contemporary evolution of functional and purely utilitarian structural considerations, but as the product of much less transitory human inspirations and perceptions in, for instance, the realm of space. Given such an approach, actual forms had no decisive significance. The Rationalists may be said to have broken through the stylistic outer surface, which was in dispute at that time between the supporters of traditional and novel forms, and to have penetrated right down to the underlying psychological requirements. Their concern was with the relationship between architectural forms and human perception.

Constructivism, like Functionalism as a whole, had to pass through an intermediate stage on the way to formulating its vision of the new architecture. Its link with Classicist aesthetic principles, indirect though this might have been, made it the more acceptableanti-traditional trend, despite its outright rejection of the Classical concept. It smashed the stereotypes, but took some minimal account of the continuity demanded by whatever might prove psychologically acceptable to the public at large.

In their attack on stereotypes, the Rationalists probably touched upon deeper layers of the traditionally accepted view. They may have found it difficult to compete with the Constructivists during the early stage of the new architecture's evolution, but their creative concept included much that gave their movement greater potential significance in restructuring formal traditional attitudes.

All this, of course, demands more detailed analysis. But the evidence adduced in the present volume shows that Rationalist artistic achievements  which had not received adequate attention until recently — were not inferior to those of the Constructivists in significance, artistic value and potential for the future.

Experiments by Soviet architects underwent a marked change of course during the period under review here. At the beginning of the 1920s, when the quest for innovation involved an embittered struggle against traditional architecture, the broad stream of new forms and compositional methods prevented any statement of a single stylistic direction for the new architecture. As the various innovative trends converged, however, it was not external differences in style, but deeper aspects which clearly divided Rationalists from Constructivists, such as their attitudes to space, to rules governing form and to systems of composition. This was particulary wellillustrated by the different approaches to the spatial organization of townplanning complexes in designs by Osa, Asnova and ARU teams early in the 1930s.

The development and organizational patterns of various innovative groups and associations during the 1920s were not merely a reflection of the existence of various trends and schools within the new architecture. They were also connected with rapid changes in the country as a whole and the statement of new requirements corresponding to various stages in the development of the new society. Thus Symbolist Realism reflected the revolutionary upsurge during the era of War Communism and the Civil War; Rationalism was largely con-

1494 Barutchev, for the Leningrad section of ARU. Competition design for a zoo, Leningrad, 1931. Pavilion of Man'

![](images/0b49986b8d45c14c1162413f1cb3c1821399e49070023f961ab2d0da36a97d40.jpg)

![](images/d70f936b328e9aed7d68f2bce16cd26a8235b0277f883431904750317765b5e7.jpg)

![](images/61b79b48d4d62a05bb385e5aaa565946c7e7dabb521225c50de3b1eccf2a5267.jpg)

![](images/7ba7ef981e1d58becbe4f521c637ceaaf1ca8984c8e5dd7821fa37954e444be2.jpg)

nected with the period when questions of cadres and professional competence became paramount; Constructivism took shape during the transition to actual construction work and the development of new types of buildings. ARU was founded during the First Five Year Plan, when the chiefrequirement was to build new towns, and Vopra arose in connection with a more intensive role for ideology in dealing with the artistic problems of architecture.

The different innovative trends and schools should not therefore be regarded as merely products of new and original artistic views, since their sequence was to some extent determined by the evolution of the new architecture as a whole. The successive stages in the development of the innovative movement were marked by the founding of new associations. Each of these loudly rejected the positions taken up by their predecessors, yet included in its own acts of faith many of the principles held earlier. Osa certainly relied upon the experience acquired by Asnova; ARU was in a direct line of succession from the latter and drew upon Constructivist achievements, and the positive part of Vopra's beliefs incorporated many tenets of Rationalism and Constructivism.

The continuous thread of general principles leading from one group to the next was often disguised by the bitter internecine polemics between these schools. This was especially true of Moscow, the centre where the main trends were formed, and of Vkhutemas in particular. In other cities, where the new movement established itself more or less slowly, each new trend arising in Moscow was simply regarded as the sum of all innovation so far. This does much to explain the mounting popularity of each successive grouping. It should be added that, as in Moscow, the statements of principle emanating from these groupings secured their greatest response among students and young architects.

The new architecture constantly extended its reach within the country. If Asnova virtually included only Muscovites, ARU had a branch in Leningrad and Osa branches in Leningrad, Tomsk, Kharkov, Kiev, Kazan, Sverdlovsk, Novosibirsk and Baku, so that architects there came to identify Constructivism with the new architecture as a whole in the struggle against eclecticism and stylization, rather then regarding it as merely one among other innovative trends. The same was true of Vopra outside Moscow, since it came into being when the strife between Rationalists and Constructivists had already prepared the ground for the conversion of most architects to the innovative movement. This goes a long way to account for the reputation widely gained by Vopra throughout the country, even though it had no set of creative principles ofits own, as opposed to Asnova, Osa and ARU. Local branches of Vopra were founded in the republics and many cities. They were most effective wherever conditions favouring the introduction of innovative trends had only appeared late in the 1920s, in other words, wherever Vopra represented the first and only new architectural trend.

In Leningrad, with its well-established Classical tradition rooted in its architectural practice and schools, only small branches of Osa and ARU established themselves, in the former case consisting of Nikolsky's group, and in the latter of Barutchev, Gilter, Meerzon and Rubanchik. The local branch of Vopra, on the other hand, called Lovopra, based in the Leningrad Institute of Civil Engineers, numbered more than thirty members, and was in fact the most influential of the local innovative groups in the fight against traditionalism.

The process of consolidation among innovative architects is well illustrated by the Armenian Opra, the first local innovative organization, which embraced all those who favoured the new architecture. Young architects coming home after graduating from Vkhutemas had studied under the leaders of Rationalism and Constructivism, Ladovsky and Alexander Vesnin. They set up a single innovative organization with guiding principle derived from all the experience acquired o far in the fight for the new architecture.

The emergence of more than one innovative trend in Soviet architecture during the 1920s was in itselfa major achievement in terms of twentieth-century architecture worldwide. The establishment of any school or trend is an extremely complex process which makes major demands on the creative energy of its founders and temporarily limits the contribution made by its most active supporters to actual construction work. Once it has found its shape, however, such a school comes to promote architectural progress and exerts a strong, though often only indirect influence on all creative work throughout the profession. The very fact of the emergence in Soviet architecture of trends such as Rationalism and Constructivism represents a most important development in its own right. It is not by chance that the principles advanced by these schools still attract the attention of both Soviet and foreign architects after so many years,

Pr II/Masters nd trens:bigraphies, stateents, mniesos

because, seen in perspective, their influence on the development of contemporary architecture becomes ever more clear.

This upsurge of creative schools and trends also called into being a particular kind of architect, in whom the roles of artist, teacher and theoretician were combined. Architects possessed of a vivid creative talent and a passionately polemical and promotional pen were typical of the 1920s. Virtually all the major innovative architects who took a hand in establishing Soviet architecture were also theoreticians and spent much time elaborating statements of their positions. This creative debate was generally conducted on a high intellectual level. A. Vesnin, Ladovsky, Lissitzky, Melnikov, I. Golosov, Leonidov, and Ginzburg raised the whole level of debate about artistic problems and thereby helped to lessen the gap between innovative experiment and design as a whole. It was important in this context that the main theoreticians of the new movement should also have been the leaders of the innovative trends. They challenged traditionalist concepts not only with theoretical statements, but also by their own highly distinguished artistic output.

Soviet architecture of this period is only now becoming the subject of deeper analysis. Much remains to be elicited and published, much will require reassessment and reinterpretation. Any objective appreciation of many aspects of Soviet architecture in the 1920s will call for just as much painstaking study as the elucidation of hitherto unknown facts, since a difficult and ambiguous problem underlies every important contemporary architectural phenomenon, be it artistic, social, functionally constructive or of some other kind.

Works that consciously pose or address problems are invariably of the utmost importance in terms of architectural progress, regardless of whether they were projects, or were actually built. The presence of that problem imparts a special quality to an architectural object, injects a potential charge into it, often makes it polemical, but invariably compels attention, especially if the problem concerned is stated sharply, boldly, topically and with a certain level of universality. And it can safely be said of Soviet architecture at that time that it was, by its nature, replete with problems.

![](images/3feae112d57f69e34a511e5432c767ce54b1da2509a8797f92669a889d453be1.jpg)

Ladovsky (1881-1941)

Nikolai Alexandrovich Ladovsky was the ideologist and acknowledged leader of Rationalism in Soviet architecture. He had for many years engaged in architectural design, even before receiving formal training in this field. He entered the Moscow College of Painting, Sculpture and Architecture in 1914 and graduated with a diploma of Artist Architect.

In 1918-22, he came under the direct influence of Zholtovsky and Cubo-Futurism. He was a member of Zhivskulptarkh in 191920 and took part in Inkhuk as a member of the Working Group forObjective Analysis, as Head of the Working Group of Architects and member of the Presidium. He headed Asnova from 1923, and ARU from 1928. From 1920, he was a professor, first at Vkhutemas-Vkhutein, then at Vasi, Asi and Mai - the architectural institutes which were derived consecutively from the Architectural Faculty of Vkhutein. He developed and applied a psychoanalytical teaching method, first at Obmas and then, from 1923, in the Spatial Studies Kontsentr of the Vkhutemas Basic Section, and also set up a Psycho-Technical Research Laboratory at Vkhutein in 1927. In addition, he evolved the town-planning concept of a dynamically developing city.

In 1919-20, he produced a series of sketch projects in pursuit of an image for such new building types as a communal house and a Temple of Communion Between Nations, and engaged in formal and aesthetic experiment. In 1924-25, he headed a team of students for the design of the International Red Stadium and also designed a dwelling complex in Moscow made up of standard three-pronged sections. He took part in many competitions, such as those for the design of the Soviet pavilion at the 1925 Paris International Exhibition, in 1924, for the Smolensk Market in Moscow in 1926, the Columbus Monument in Santo Domingo in 1929, a theatre in Sverdlovsk in 1931, the Palace of Soviets in Moscow in 1931-32, the Moscow District Soviet of Trade Unions Theatre in 1932, and many others.

The most interesting of Ladovsky's town-planning works included designs for the Kostino settlement in 1927 and for Green City in 1930, a model project for a developing city in 1930, a design for the reconstruction of Trubnaya Square in Moscow in 1931 and a competition entry for redesigning Moscow in 1932.

The major part of Ladovsky's designs remained on the drawing board. Among those carried out were a housing block in Gorky Street in Moscow in 1928-30, as well as the Krasnye Vorota (Red Gates) station building and the Dzerzhinsky Station on the Moscow Metro in 1934-35.

Ladovsky seldom went into print as the theoretician of Rationalism, although some unpublished minutes of meetings at Zhivskulptarkh and Inkhuk containing speeches by him have survived. He briefly summarized the principles of Rationalism in articles published in 1926 and at other times. Nor did Ladovsky leave a comprehensive account ot his psychoanalytical method, though many of the principles involved were publicized in the writings of colleagues and students, such as Dokuchaev, Krinsky, Lamtsov, Turkus and others, all of whom stressed Ladovsky's leading part in elaborating this method. His town-planning ideas were summarized in articles which he published in the late 1920s and early 1930s, as well as in various ARU statements and documents.

## Ladovsky's writings

## Extracts from speeches at Zhivskulptarkh meetings in 1919

'Architecture is an art operating with space. Sculpture is an art operating with form.'

'When an architect is contemplating any problem of building, he must initially organize only space, disregarding materials or construction, and only start to think about these later: this makes work easier, and the results will be neater and better.'

'Although space figures in all aspects of art, architecture alone makes it possible to read space correctly.

Construction only plays a part in architecture in so far as it defines the concept of space. A constructor's basic principle is to invest a minimum of materials and achieve maximum results. This has nothing in common with art and can only satisfy the requirements of architecture by chance.'

'Form may exist independently of material, since the existence of abstract form is possible.'

'The visual effect of weight is influenced not only by form, but by everyday experience. If we produce identical forms by means of two different materials, we will not achieve the same effect of weight.'

Par III/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

'It must be stressed that architecture as an art lives in accordance with laws of its own. This becomes clearly evident when an architect does not live by utilitarian laws.'

'A façade must not merely result from a building's inner content, but have its own inherent value.'

'How should forms be combined, and what forms should be regarded as simple and primary?.an elementary for will be such that a spectator cannot subdivide it.'

'One must start from the simplest form, a rectangle, for instance, and then introduce one that is less plai. A cube or par allelepiped may serve as examples of a clear form, since they consist of simpler forms . .  the transition to simpler, clearly readable forms will be easier in architecture, since architecture was not subjected to imitative art— to naturalism — which had a tremendous place in the fields of sculpture and painting. We now face the task of creating that conjunction of easily readable forms which will secure the expression of maximum movement.'

'A form depicted in art differs from a form in everyday life by being more clearly and definitely perceptible. Right forms are most easily perceived. A right form does not generate movement, but a conjunction of such forms results in movement. Thecombination of clearly legible right forms produces clearly expressed movement. Non-right forms are readily legible in so far as they approximate to right ones.'

'The cube provides an example of a readily legible form since, when we approach it from one side, and see only two of its faces and an edge, we clearly perceive the form as a whole. On the other hand, if we similarly observe a crumpled form, we would not perceive the form of the volume as a whole.'

(Zhivskulptarkh archive)

Note appended to the design of a communal house in 1920

Technology performs marvels.

The marvels of modern times soaring in space will be con-y rt lu mi  thct will be the amount of mind.

Painting in architecture must serve first the spatial quality, and then the sculptural form. It is in this order of their subordination that I acknowledge their synthesis within architecture.' (GNIMA, R.Ia.1644)

Extracts from the minutes of the Inkhuk Working Group   
for Objective Analysis, 1921   
Definition of technical construction

Technical construction is the conjunction of shaped material elements in accordance with a definite design scheme aimed at achieving an effect of force.

An ideal technical construction should comprise:

(a) material moments; these transform the [words missing - S.Kh.M.] of the forces operating. They serve as a relay point along their line of motion;

(b) tension (of the material elements); this must be near break  hiht parts);

(c) molecular forces of the material, which must be used in the direction of their greatest compatibility;

(d) there must be no excess materials or elements, and no material left over.'

'The main feature of a construction is that it should not contain surplus materials or elements. The chief distinguishing mark of a composition is hierarchy and subordination.'

(Inkhuk archive)

Extracts from the minutes of Inkhuk's Working Group of Architects, 1921

The task that faces us is the studyof the elements, qualities and properties of architecture. ... The most important elements in architecture are space, construction, form, and the rest of its elements merely follow these.'

'I am surprised that members of the group should still ask qt W h   e priority?The quality of space is architecture's unque concern, yet architecture does not undertake its study and makes poor use of it.' (Inkhuk archive)

Extracts from an article on

Fodations r he ConstructionThey Archue Under the BanRatalis hetic

'Architectural rationality, just like technical rationality, is based on an economic principle. The difference between them is that technical rationality is the economy of labour and materials in creating an appropriate structure, while architectural rationality is economy of psychic energy in perception of the structure's spatial and functional properties. A synthesis of th talit ihig u  Rtio-Architecture. ...

The architect constructs a form, introducing elements which are not technological or utilitarian in the strict sense and may be regarded as "architectural motifs". These "motifs" must be rational in an architectural sense and serve man's highest technological requirement  to find his direction in space

The work of an architect on the geometrical expressiveness of a form, which we always perceive in perspective, consists in getting the image resulting from the perception of real perspective as near as possible to the image conceived in the design. ..

Could-one allow an architect engaged in constructing a form to be ignorant of how it would be perceived by a spectator? Such a dereliction would imply a total lack of principle and the patent lack of any mastery whatsoever in the field of geometrical expressiveness. It is essential to assert that the architectural gemerc aspect oamaterial form should be treated in suc a way that the spectator actually sees its geometrical configuration to whatever extent the case concerned demands this.'

(Izvestiya Asnova [Asnova News], Moscow, 1926, pp.3-5)

## oal for Architecture (Posing the Question), 1926

'The architect must be aware, even if only in an elementary way, of the laws of perception and the means of achieving an impact, so that he may have at his disposal when practising his craft everything that modern science can offer. Among the sciences which assist in the development of architecture, a serious gap will be filled by the still new science of psycho-technology. . . .

The works that I - and subsequently also my colleagues - have produced in Vkhutemas since 1920 in the realm of architecture have been checked by psycho-technical means and will

Extract from Ladovsky's preface to Krutikov's article The Application of the Theory of Union to the Investigation and Measurement of Aptitude for Spatial Composition', 1929

'In establishing those features which define the concept of architect, it was essential first and foremost to define it as the basic feature of the contemporary architect's activity that he organizes the perception of space and spatial forms. In other words, it was necessary to formulate in a concrete and scientific way what is broadly defined nowadays by the concept of an architectural (i.e.spatial) composition.'

ArkhitekturaVkhutein [rchitcture and the hutei], No. 1, January 1929, p.5)

Extract from a lecture given at a conference of Vkhutein graduates, 1929

'If an architect is to provide for an individual's feelings, he must provide for them in such a way that these feelings are expended more economically, that an individual chiefly devotes his feelings to spatial entities, to spatial objects. It is on this particular level that the organization of his psychic energy takes place in accordance with the principle of economy of perception.'

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura [Contemporary Architecture], 1929, No.4, p. 144)

Extract from an article on "Historical" and "Socialist" Moscow', 1930

'The concept of a town's growth cannot be reduced to mere

Part III/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

terms of the mechanical increase of its territory, of the width of its thoroughfares, the height of its buildings etc. Growth must be regarded as organic and representing an organism that is not just quantitatively, but also qualitatively different at various stages in its development. ...

The radial-concentric form of city plan has many supporters in town-planning literature worldwide. It is therefore essential to examine it in greater detail. The medievel fortified town, Howard's garden city, Unwin's system of satellite towns and Le Corbusier's proposals for replanning Paris, all these planning compositions may be included in a single category of static forms, despite the apparent differences in form and purpose between them. . .. The grafting of these systems on to growing and active cities must inevitably provoke unhealthy symptoms in their growth.

A specific indication of their mechanical nature is that these systems are only capable of making sense during a minimal space of time if they are fully implemented, while in the very next moment of their growth it will become necessary to destroy them. In short, they do not provide for growth from a cell into a system, from a rudimentary system to another more elaborate one etc. While "static" factors predominated over dynamic ones in medieval times, i.e. given a relatively slow tempo of life and an inadequate sense of the passage of time, the radial-concentric system might have been capable of maintaining its place for planning purposes for a while, but, as capitalism developed and grew, it broke down everywhere.

. . . it was replaced by the grid system of planning as an expression of fluidity, a sort of ceaseless territorial conveyor belt which was more suited to the needs of capitalism and of mechanical, rather than organic growth. The most extreme expression of this fluidity is the concept of the linear city. These planning structures will inevitably prove to be weak organisms, because they represent an expression of utmost dynamism and reduce three-dimensional space to a single measure by stressing their linear quality. Yet modern material culture and technology make it possible to solve town-planning tasks in three dimensions by stressing "horizontal two-dimensionality".

. the geometric character of the circular territory reflects its spatially static qualiy, while the physical nature oits sructure only allows within it an increase in density at best . . . A sectoral system of growth might seem to be feasible in terms of radial-concentric planning, but essential dynamic geometrical considerations conflict with it, inevitably distort it and therefore make it impossible to implement. .. . On the other hand, one must decribe the growth of a city as organic when its growth as a whole makes provision for the growth of its different component organs with their various roles within a single economic time and space system. . . .

If one were to assume a total harmonization of form, i. e. if individual rings were to represent different organs — areas designated for particular purposes - the growth of any one of them would spell the decay of another. If, on the other hand, one were to abandon the difference of functional assignment between the various rings and accept their functional uniformity, a centripetal force would develop- all else being equal - on the strength of the economics inherent in the dynamic geometrical principle. This can be represented as an inward pressure of the rings upon each other towards the centre, while the central circle's tendency to grow would thus encounter the vast and insurmountable resistance of the rings.

in ring planning . . . the centre, which tends to develop naturally in a horizontal direction, encounters a resistance by the rings which it finds hard to surmount and the resolution of the most fundamental factor in the life of a city  the dialectic process of its growth-is not catered for by building on such a plan, since growth is impossible without causing the collapse of surrounding - and, it must be assumed, equally live - organs of the city....

. . The centre of a city must be able to grow not only in the third dimension, upwards, but equally in a horizontal direction, progressively outwards. Therefore, the centre of a city must consist of an axial line, not a static point. By breaking open the rings and bending them out into the shape of a horseshoe, we shall make it possible for the centre, as well as the corresponding ramifications of the former rings, to grow. The centre will become fan-shaped. Such a shape is more appropriate for a centre since, as the city grows, and as its dynamic increases and its organization becomes more complex, the centre will not be hemmed in, but be able instead to develop within the area of tIn  e sent a kind of stream which gradually widens.'

(Stroitelstvo Mosky [Moscow Construction], 1930, No. 1, pp.17-20)

in a Higher Educational Establishment

'The significance of planning as a separate discipline is only rather slowly being recognized by our architectural community. . . .

.. In our country, in the era of the building of socialism, architectural associations continue to structure their work from the particular to the general, from the building to the town, as our foreign colleagues are obliged to do in a capitalist orderof society.We cannot regard individual buildings as anything but particles of a city as a whole. Our associations must therefore restructure their organizational work and proceed from city to building, not the other way about. ...

Our builders and ideologists are mistaken when they equate structural with social types ofdwellings. Dwellings either identical in social terms, or of a disparate kind, such as individual flats or hostels with different degrees of communalization, may be accommodated in skyscrapers just as well as in any other type of known structure - blocks, axial buildings with spurs or grouped pavilions consisting of separate, spatially discrete bedrooms collected round the communal accommodation in a complex cluster.

It should be stressed that a dwelling for a more developed social order can be the more easily accommodated in buildings of diverse structural kinds, although differences would, of course, arise. But any such a difference would involve slight nuances in the pattern of daily life, rather than a difference in the central social aims pursued. ...

A worker's life and habits are far more complicated in their structure than might appear at first sight. Differences in trade, age, physique, psychology, senility, disablement etc, all require different, rather than identical forms of dwelling. And this implies alternatives, most easily provided by the free combination of all known - or recently invented - types of structure, than by an arid, functionally formalistic, self-imposed limitation and the cramming of the intricate organism of life into unbending frameworks devised by the meagre imagination of a designer or ideologist.'

(Sovetskaya arkhitektura [Soviet Architecture], 1931, Nos.1-2, pp.21-24)

Alexander Vesnin (1883-1959)

Alexander Alexandrovich Vesnin was the leader of Constructivism in Soviet architecture. He was born and spent his childhood in the ancient Russian town of Yurevets on the Volga. He graduated from the Moscow Practical Academy and entered the Petersburg Institute of Civil Engineers from which he graduated in 1912. With his elder brothers, Leonid and Viktor, he produced a number of architectural projects before the Revolution, several of which were carried out. Those designs which used the tradition of Russian Classicism displayed great mastery, while the Vesnin brothers were less successful when they worked in other styles.

Well before the Revolution, Alexander Vesnin became seriously involved in painting, at the same time as he was studying and doing architectural work. In painting, he studied first under Konstantin Yuon and E.Tsionglinsky in 1907-11, and then under Vladimir Tatlin in 191213. He painted the ceiling of the Sirotkin House in Nizhny-Novgorod in 1915.

During the early Soviet years, Alexander Vesnin worked as aCubist and abstract painter, and also as a theatrical designer. He took part in the exhibitions of the Left Federation of Artist-Painters and the Trade Union of Artist-Painters, both held in 1918; he showed in the First State Exhibition of Suprematist and Non-Objective Painting in 1919 and in the Constructivist Painters' show, '5×5=25', in 1921 in Moscow, as well as in a travelling exhibition of painters from the Russian Republic that went to Amsterdam, Venice and Rome in 1922. As a theatrical designer he worked on sets for the Maly Theatre: The Road to Glory and The Marriage of Figaro in 1919, The Government Inspector and sketches for Oliver Cromwell in 1920; for the Kamerny Theatre he did L'Annonce faite à Marie in 1920, sketches for Romeo and Juliet in 1921, Phèdre in 1922 and The Man who was Thursday in 1923; and, for the Children's Theatre, Adalmina's Pearl in 1921. He designed the decorations for the Kremlin and Red Square on the first May Day holiday after the Revolution, in 1918, and-jointly with Popova - the mass display on the Khodynskoe Field in Moscow in 1921. He also illustrated books. In 1921, Alexander Vesnin became a professor at Vkhutemas, where he first taught painting and drawing, and later headed an architectural studio. He was active at Inkhuk during 1921-24 and in the Architectural Group of Lef.

Alexander Vesnin was permanent chairman of the Con-

Part I/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

structivists' Union of Contemporary Architects, Osa, from 1925 to 1931, and chief editor of their periodical Sovuremennaya arkhitektura (Contemporary Architecture). From 1923, he did a great deal of architectural work in collaboration with either one or both of his brothers. Their principal designs during the period 1923-34 were: a Palace of Labour, 1923; the Leningradskaya Pravda and Arkos buildings and an aircraft hangar in 1924; a telegraph office in 1925; a department store at Krasnaya Presna in 1927, which was carried out; the Lenin Library, 1928; Housing Communes for Stalingrad and Kuznetsk in 1929-30; Workers' Clubs in Baku in 1928-32, which were carried out; a theatre in Kharkov, 1930; the Palace of Culture in Moscow in 1931-37, which was carried out; the Palace of Soviets in Moscow, 1931-32; and the People's Commissariat for Heavy Industry in Moscow, 1934.

In 1933, Alexander Vesnin and his brother Viktor took over one of the design studios which were set up at that time by the Moscow Soviet and soon afterwards transferred, first to the People's Commissariat for Heavy Industry, then to the People's Commissariat for the Oil Industry.

During the 1930s, Alexander Vesnin took an active part in aesthetic and professional debates but thereafter gradually withdrew from active contributions to theory and practice.

Alexander Vesnin's writings

'Credo' - April 1922

'The tempo of modern times is quick and dynamic; its rhythm is clear, accurate, straight-lined and mathematical. Materials and efficiency determine the make-up of an object produced by the modern artist.

It does not matter whether the object concerned is both efficient and useful - such as an engineering construction or a household article - or merely efficient, such as a laboratory project aimed at resolving problems posed by the new modern form. Every real object produced by the modern artist must play its part in life as an active force to organize the individual's consciousness, exert a psycho-physiological effect upon him and prompt him to energetic activity.

Clearly, objects producd by a modern artist must be pure constructions, freed of all representational ballast and devised in accordance with a direct geometric curve and the principle of economy of maximum effect.

Since the construction of any object is a matter of the strong bonding of the main elements of plasticity (material, colour, line, surface, texture . . .) the artist must treat their study as his first priority.

I regard all these elements as materialized energy endowed with dynamic qualities (movement, tension, weight, speed . . .) to be efficiently regulated by the artist.

Just as every part of an engine represents an active force, essential to the functioning of the given system and embodied in the appropriate shape and material, and these may not be wilfully altered without damaging the working of the entire system, so in every object constructed by an artist each component element represents a materialized force and cannot be discarded or altered without impeding the efficient functioning of the given system, i.e. the object.

T  h the steam locomotive, the aircraft, the crane..

The modern artist must produce objects equal to them in strength, tension and potential, as organizing principles in terms of their psycho-physiological impact on human consciousness.' (Inkhuk archive)

Extracts from the minutes of the Subsection of Modern Art,

Spi, Rakhn (Spatial Art Section,

Russian Academy for Artistic Science), 19 March 1929

'Introduction by A.A. Vesnin on "The Tasks of Modern Architecture"

A.A. Vesnin pointed out that the architect must engage in the active construction of the new life and share in the formulation of original architectural requirements, rather than accept their finished statement. The new life demands a new presentation, and this is only possible if new materials and the latest technical data are used.

N.I.Brunov asked A.A. Vesnin about the extent to which the architect takes account ofsensation and the extent to which architecture is dependent upon technology.

A.A. Vesnin explained that the basic task of architecture is this out. There can be no organization if antiquated technology is used. As to "sensation", he remarked that it did not enter into the task oarhitetur, but that it was an ievitable reul of any work the conception of which moved the spectator. A functional form, developed to perfection, would necessarily also be perfect for the spectator — as is an aircraft, for instance. The intuitive element would undoubtedly diminish as time went by. N.I.Brunov asked where the borderline lay between what was and was not art, if an aircraft was to be regarded as art.

A.A.Ve ere the iffnc t be purely qualtive. . . .

Theis   cu of life.'

(Iz istorii sovetskoi arkhitektury 1926-1932

gg. Dokumenty i materialy

[From the History of Soviet Architecture, 1926-1932.

Documents and papers],

Moscow, 1970, pp. 11314)

Extracts from a speech made at the debate in the Union of Soviet Architects on the subject of 'Creative Tasks of Soviet Architecture and the Problem of the Architectural Heritage', 1933

. . The lecturer said that Brunelleschi made creative use of Classicism and that we must therefore also make creative use of Classicism, yet we know that Brunelleschi introduced Roman capitals and cornices into architecture unaltered. That was perfectly natural at the time. .

Nowadays, however, the word "to use" should be chucked out and replaced by the word "to assimilate", i.e. to understand and surpass. But this certainly does not mean "to appropriate", as is now said and done. .

What should one gather from the history of architecture? First and foremost, one should be aware of the essence of architecture, i. e. of that basic feature which is peculiar to architecture. In this, the organization of life and living processes comes first. Architectonics come second, i.e, the laws of structuring space, and one must investigate how these develop and gradually change. ...

Secondly - concerning form and content . . . the vital processes of life enter into architecture, the task of organizing life itself and, on the other hand, so does ideological content.

Chapter 2/The leaders of the new direction

What therefore is form? It is space so organized in a material that it lends substance to the given content.

.. . We are concerned with elements of form to match our content since we incorporate life itself in our content, w need a dialectic understanding of its development, we need to unnit ogicly riclly.Wema in ths wy approach one of the basic problems of the new architecture.

The second lesson that the architectural heritages teaches us is the unity of architecture with technology.

(Arkhitektura SSSR [Architecture of the USSR],

1933, Nos.3-4, pp. 1415)

Extracts from an article entitled

The Problem of the Interior', 1934

'The plan is the basis and foundation of every architectural design. But we have become accustomed to making no artifical distinctions in design work and to carrying out the processes of analysis and synthesis simultaneously. The consideration of the ground plan, elevation, axonometric and perspective designs all take place in parallel; in other words, an integrated process of creating an architectural image is at work. This method of work has not always been practised. The architects of the old school  indeed many do this even now— divided up their work mechanically. The plan came first, then sections, elevations, a model, axonometry and then, at last, perspective, which often brought to light all the mistakes inherent in that architectural conception. Our own practice has convinced us that an organically integrated synthetic method of design, from initial sketch plans to overall perspectives, is the most rational, economic and artistically fruitful approach. .

Both Western and Soviet modern architecture have introduced important changes in the field of design work. I have in mind the principle of so-called free planning, when an architect resolves the organization of interior space purely on the strength of the everyday intellectual and social requirements of human beings. .. .
