Finally, the system of interior organization in accordance with the principle of "free-flowing spaces" produces a certain illusion of compositional variations within a structure, thereby increasing both its artistic expressiveness and its functional efficiency.

Using the term "function"or "functionalism"as frequently as I do, I begin to fear that I will be misunderstood. Modern

Par III/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

Functionalism in architecture has never claimed the two deplorable teachings which theiropponents lay at its door. It has never pretended to have invented new principles unprecedented in architectural practice. All true masters and architects of all countries in all ages have always given thought to the efficient resolution of functional tasks. We have also never said that the function of a structure, in the narrow sense of the word, must govern all other architectural considerations, i. e. the unity of composition of its image, its artistic purpose or even its role as a decorative element within the general conception of a street, a square, or a city. We have merely maintained that one must not neglect to the point ofinsensitivity the functional purpose of buildings, since they are being erected for living human beings with practical - even if exceedingly complex - intellectual, social and living requirements. Thus Functionalism is, in essence, no more than the rebirth of the immemorial primacy of function in architecture as opposed to scholastic, decorative academicism, now applied to the significantly enlarged and more complex operational base of modern man  the citizen of a socialist country. ...'

(Arkhitektura SSSR, 1934, No.7, pp. 3-4)

Extracts from an article entitled

'Debatable Questions', 1934

A certain confusion may be observedat present among many Soviet architects . ..

Something quite absurd has happened to the assimilation of the architectural heritage. The majority of architects approach the heritage as though it was a layette put by in a trunk. Each one of them pulls whatever he fancies from it and sticks it into his design. . ..

The fight against vulgarization often comes down to a fight against serious, simple and shapely architectural Constructivism, and, moreover, what is described as Constructivism has nothing in common with it as a form of architecture. The very great achievements of Soviet architecture . . . and uninspired trash are lumped together and described as "Constructivism", "boxes" and "vulgarization".

Architectural opulence is taken to mean neither opulence of content, nor thepulenceosimple, shapely forms, butamean ingless, disjointed piling-on of details.

The fight for quality often comes down to a fight for the quality of technical execution, either in reality or on paper, and the quality of the architectural treatment is ignored. ...

Symbolism is often substituted for imagery in architecture.' (Literaturnaya gazeta [Literary Gazette], No.135 (45), 8 October 1934)

Extracts from a speech at a Moscow-wide convention of architects in February 1936

'The problem of form is one of the basic elements in every sort of art, while Formalism is a definitely negative phenomenon against which it is essential to struggle. Yet these two concepts are very often confused with each other. .

We are searching for new forms. This search must start from the content of life and take account of the functional aspect, as well as of perception etc. All this is part of the content . . . The content is not simply a utilitarian function, and functions of an ideological order also have their share in it..

A few words about simplicity and simplification or vulgarization. These two concepts are often confused. Simplicity is a great achievement, it is our ideal. .

It would point to the Parthenon as a good example of simplicity. It is both exceptionally simple, and richly loaded with content. Then I would point to Brunelleschi's Pazzi Chapel [Mordvinov: 'And to Le Corbusier on the Myasnitskaya?']. ' And to Le Corbusier on the Myasnitskaya. I consider that a number ofLeCorbusier's works are on a level with those of Brunelleschi.

A number of the Constructivist propositions on which we have been working remain true even now. . . . First and foremost, the organic nature of architecture. We set ourselves the task of discovering forms which derived from the function of the materials and the structure. This proposition remains valid, bu i ne  eosi rl eh functions meant both utilitarian and ideogical functions to us, we neverthee vyla.

The quest oranew socialist model which was to developinto the quest for a new image, also stands. Unfortunately, there also remins the ruge is ccticmThe ins the e f te

In the past, some of our comrades, though admittedly a minority of them, definitely deviated in matters of art. They claime that r s   the ps labour in its place. Those comrades, however, who did not agree with them, myselfincluded, did not fight against this deviation actively enough. But we never allowed the slogan "Away with Art" to appear in our journal. . . .

Constructivism must constantly develop and move forward. ..

(Arkhitekturnaya gazeta,

No.13 (85), 3 March 1936)

Melnikov (1890-1974)

Konstantin Stepanovich Melnikov was born in Moscow, entered the General Educational Section of the College of Painting, Sculpture and Architecture and, on completing this course, continued his education, first in the College's Painting Section and then in that for Architecture, from which he graduated in 1917.

In early Soviet years, Melnikov worked in the Moscow Soviet's architectural studio and contributed to the Architectural Department of the People's Commissariat for Education. In 1920-21, he was one of the organizers of an Architects' Section in the Union of Art Workers and became a member of the Section's Presidium. He was a professor in the Architecture Faculty of Vkhutemas from 1921 to 1924.

In 1922-23, Melnikov took part in three major architectural events of the early years of the decade, with entries for a Palace of Labour and a dwelling complex competition, and the Moscow exhibition's Makhorka pavilion. His design of the sarcophagus in the Lenin Mausoleum was executed in 1924, as was that of the Novo-Sukharevsky Market in the same year and that of the Soviet pavilion at the Paris International Exhibition in 1925. Melnikov was one of the acknowledged leaders of the new direction. He was active as a contributor to competitions and carried out numerous commissions. Five clubs, four garages and his own house were built from his designs. He produced an experimental design for garages in Paris in 1925; competition entries for the Leningradskaya Pravda building in Moscow in 1924; for the Columbus Monument in Santo Domingo in 1929; for Green City in 1930; for the Moscow Central Park of Culture and Leisure, the Moscow District Soviet of Trade Unions Theatre and the Palace of Nations (a counterproposal in the Palace of Labour competition) in 1931; for the Ministry of Heavy Industry in Moscow in 1934; and others. In

Chapter 2/The leaders of the new direction

1931, Melnikov put forward a proposal for redesigning the Arbat Square in Moscow.

His artistic position was close to that of the Rationalists, and he even joined Asnova for a while. As an architect with a markedly individual approach, however, he did not regard himself as belonging to any particular trend.

In 1933-38, Melnikov headed one of the Moscow Soviet's architectural studios where he produced a number ofdesigns, among them those for a Palace of Culture in Tashkent in 1933 and the development of the Kotelnicheskaya and Goncharnaya Embankments in Moscow in 1934. He was also teaching during this period at the Moscow Architectural Institute. Towards the end of the 1930s, Melnikov carried out a number of commissions and devoted much attention to painting.

In subsequent years, Melnikov was mainly engaged in teaching, but also took part in some competitions, such as that for a Palace of Soviets in 1958 and a children's cinema on the Arbat in Moscow in 1967.

Melnikov's writings

Extracts from an article entitled

'The Delineation of a Design', 1933

'There is no obligatory sequence whatsoever for the creative processes applied in the initial stage of work on any design. Very much depends on intuition and what is still broadly known as "creative imagination". No work ofany kind is possible, of course, on the conception of any structure without some preliminary study of the technical and economic features of the task in hand. But every now and again the spatial treatment and composition take shape in the architect's mind before the detailed work on the economic and technical considerations has started. .. .

When one is considering a design, ideas dart about in the constant search for a solution. Much has repeatedly to be reconsidered as diametrically opposite possibilities are rejected. Personally, I make no use of drawings and sketches of any kind in the course of my work. Not only have I no urge to do so, but I regard it as simply unnecessary, and even counterproductive. A drawing hampers the free play of ideas by promoting a ready-made treatment in graphic form, even though the mind may have rejected it. Drawings hamper me particularly at the initial stage of work on a design. All purely technical  as opposed to compositional —sketches are another matter: they are both useful and unavoidable in the architect's work.

I have never had occasion to study any pattern books or manuals, or pay special attention to other people's constructions. The true architect has no need for this at all, even when he sets out to satisfy a particular stylistic canon. Nevertheless, since every architect has studied at a particular school, has been trained in particular tastes and has favourite masters, it is entirely natural that in the course of his creative activity he should, often quite unconsciously, be drawn to the classics or the influence of his contemporaries. It is appropriate at this point to examine two closely connected, but mutally incompatible concepts: the "related" and the "imitated". A master may be so familiar with the culture of former centuries that he quite unfailingly - one describes it as acting organically - becomes the bearer of the stylistic traditions of many generations. That is the quintessence of any culture, and it is therefore understandable that any master may see himself as a "relation" of some classical or even closely contemporary artist. An "imitator" is something else again. He merely copies mechanically and produces pale reflections, impotent tracings of what he has either never perceived or never studied.

The most painful stage of the creative process is provided by the final touches to a design. How is one to picture to oneself the future structure in its natural environment, so as to decide whether it may not need some alterations or additions? However exquisitely a drawing is executed, it never satisfies the architect. A model provides an even more imperfect rendering of nature. That is why I regard it as obligatory that the architect should share in every stage of the construction from his design. ...

I should like to say a few words about the architect's collaborators. Though an architect's assistant may not act purely as a technical collaborator, his basic tasks will still be the elaboration of individual details, estimates etc. In fact, one sometimes wonders what to do with oneself while most important compositional details are being elaborated  how one is to work hand in hand with an assistant . . . Even when an assistant improves upon individual compositional details in a design, he may very often pervert the architect's basic idea. An assistant must have a particularly congenial nature, if he is to carry out joint creative work with an architect.'

(Arkhitektura SSSR, 1933, No.5, p.35)

Extracts from an article entitled The Assimilation of New Materials in Architecture', 1934

'It is not necessary to demonstrate that the latest building materials and structures influence the emergence of new styles of architectural construction. So much is obvious. ...

Ferro-concrete is a material which has been tried out in the most varied contextes. A caveat of a different sort must, however, be entered here: this building material has not as yet been fully mastered. I am convinced that it holds a multitude of possibilities and a great power of architectural expression, which our master builders still need to discover in this most flexible of building materials. ...

I am aware that an opinion is current among architects according to which we should, for all intents and purposes, have the leading and controlling part reserved to us in the production of building materials and structural components. I am not convinced that this opinion is correct. In the light of economic and technological circumstances, and bearing in mind the requirements of our building work, the construction industry itself must manufacture and market on a mass scale the requisite types of building materials and structural components. It is merely up to the architect to make skilled and artistically effective use of them.' (Arkhitektura SSSR, 1934, No.4, p.37)

Extract from the article on 'The First Place for Architecture', 1934

That which is described as architecture is majestic. The loud language of granite in architecture arouses a millennial echo in the people's hearts. Architectural forms send forth a great clamour from one century to the next and preserve the freshness and glory of their times. The value ofarchitecture lies in its bold creations.' (Stroitelstoo Moskvy, 1934, No.1, p.9)

1934

design on the building site.'

(Arkhitekura SSSR, 1934, No.9, p.10)

1936

Arcitctural ceativity s an extreely cle rocs, synthesis of art, science and technology, involving both the participation of a large number of ancillary workers, and workers i ctrucion, whopent theeer'- tion and the quality and selection of building materials.

As we see it, the retarding principle in architecture dehistorial evolution and subject to the laws of permanence, are so exquisite that they hold their own further development captive. . ..

Only the genuine talent of a design artist endows his works with the right to live. Yet among us architects, the number of works of compilation  some of which are formally brilliant has reached enormous proportions. As we see it, these works will soon bedeprived of ther value by ltsel

Only that architect will be worthy of our era who can encompass this great era of heroic struggle for the building of socialism and is willing and able to strive for genuine creativity. Given a talented architect, genuine creativity is destined to create a new era in the evolution of architectural art. It must provide humanity with monuments which will make it possible to appreciate the heroism of our time.

It is essential to study the laws of proportion and composition embodied in Greek temples, and the entire architectural experience accumulated by humanity in the course of previous eras.

But it would be as untoward to allow an architect whose output is arcterles topp acual building work, asit is to see a painter unskilled in drawing.'

(Raboty arkhitekturnykh masterskikh The Output fArchitectural Studios], Collected Articles, vol. 2, Studio No.7, pp.3-4)

## Leonidov (1902-59)

Ivan Ilich Leonidov was born at the Vlasikh farmstead in Tver Province, as the son of a forester. He spent his childhood in the country; was apprenticed to an icon painter; entered, first, the Free Art Studios in Tver in 1920 and then the Moscow Vkhutemas in 1921, where he joined the Faculty of Painting, and later that of Architecture in Alexander Vesnin's studio.

Leonidov designed competition entries from 1925: for an improved peasant hut and dwellings at Ivanovo-Voznesensk in 1925, and for a university in Minsk, as well as for model Workers' Clubs with 500 and 1,000 members, in 1926. While still a student, he joined the Constructivist organization, Osa, and contributed to their periodical SA. His 1927 diploma design for a Lenin Institute represents an important landmark in the evolution of Constructivism. After graduation, he remained at Vkhutemas as a postgraduate student, where he also worked as an assistant in Alexander Vesnin's studio. Soon afterwards, he headed a studio of his own in the Architectural Faculty.

Leonidov's most active and fruitful years extended from 1927 to 1930. He was active in Osa, debated and produced experimental designs and competition entries, many of the latter on behalf of Osa, including a film factory in Moscow, a socially new type of club, a Government House in Alma-Ata and a Tsentrosoyuz building in Moscow, in 1928; a House of Industry in Moscow, in 1929-30; the Columbus Monument, in 1929; a socialist settlement for the Magnitogorsk Industrial Combine, and the Palace of Culture in Moscow, in 1930.

At the end of 1930, Vopra set off a debate about 'Leonidovism', in the course of which his experimental designs were sued to devastain it an he was fore o ive up teaching.

During the early 1930s, Leonidov worked in various design organizations on the planning and construction of the town of Igarka and on designs for Serpukhovo Gate Square in Moscow, for the reconstruction of Moscow, for the Pravda newspaper's club ec. He produced one of his best works in the design for the People's Commissariat for Heavy Industry in Moscow, in 1934.

During the second half of the 1930s, he worked on designs for the Klyuchiki dwelling complex near Nizhny Tagil, the Artek Pioneers' Camp, the Usole settlement in the Urals, and on a number of interiors.

In the post-war period, Leonidov overcame the creative crisis which had fettered his artistic imagination over several years, and sketched out a number of designs which bear witness to a renewed creative drive, including designs for a town of the future, Sun Towon; a United Nations building; a Palace of

Part III/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

Soviets; a Moscow World Exhibition; a Forum of Arts, and various others.

Leonidov's writings

'A Note Concerning the Problem of the Monument', 1929,

about the Columbus Monument design

Aim:

The nations in their millions must be informed about the great man of action and his historic part in the development of modern culture.

The monument must act as a condenser of all the achievements of worldwide progress, a site for the dissemination of information about the life and deeds of the man of action and the flow of world history.

## Means:

This aim cannot be kept to a "monument" restricted in its means of logical impact and its small radius of action.

The present day, with its unlimited scientific and technological progress, makes it possible to provide both for the aim and for the means of implementing it in universal terms.

Radio and pictures carried by radio over a distance, an air and sea port condense the movement of universal culture.

Radio, radio pictures, an air and sea port, the cinema, a museum etc, are means for informing the world about the life and rol heman ion nd thehiment niverlo gress.

The museum is the centre of the monument, where all the memorabilia connected with Columbus' life, as well as his body brought from the "shrine", are kept.

## Construction:

The large area of the museum is covered by reinforced glass. Bearing in mind the nature of a tropical climate, and the purpose of the museum, a powerful jet of air is released in place of walls, thereby providing the requisite insulation. On hot days, an artificial stream of air also passes over the roof. The chapel is covered by a glass dome surrounded by a spiral ramp which enables one to look into it from every angle.

The scientific laboratories represent the brain of the monument, where its plan of work is processed. We have: () the Radio Centre; (2) the Film Department; (3) the Observatory; () the Institute for Interplanetary Communications, the task of which is to solve the problems of interplanetary communications by means of the latest achievements of science and technology: the demonstration area for the latter is provided by the airfield and the surrounding terrain, as well as by a part of the sea; (5) a hall for world scientific and technological congresses organized by means of radio.'

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1929, No.4, p. 148)

## Extracts from the lecture "The Socially New Type of Club' given at the First Congress of Osa, 1929

. . . The culturally educational work of the trade unions must provide an extensive service to meet the direct requirements and needs of the working masses, by creating cultural living conditions which ensure comprehensive educative development for the workers, and organizing cultural leisure and entertainment for them. In this connection, the cultural educational work of the trade unions must genuinely cover the widest masses of the working class, eliminate vestiges of apolitical attitudes and restrictive culture-mongering, reshape its method from the start and actually take first place in the whole work of the trade unions.

...The trade unions must find their support among the most conscious, leading strata of working men and women and carry on active systematic work to develop the elements of the new way of life.

.. .In order to involve strata of workers who are not so far being catered forit is essentil not to restric cultual work with the framework of the clubs, but to develop it within the enterprises themselves, the workshops, workers' barracks and hostels, and workers' settlements, as well as setting up cultural educational work in the countryside in those places where industrial workers and seasonal workers are congregated, while closely linkng this with the whole mass political and propagandist work of the Party.'

(Souremennaya arkhitektura, 1929, No.3, pp. 103, 108, 110)

Extracts from Leonido's answers to questions put to hm at the First Osa Congress, 1929

'Question: Will the worker rest in your club? AnsrThe worker will rest in the club, at the sanatorm, the r house and in his own welling.There is o such thing as absolute rest. Whatever a person does, tires him. But relative rest from one kind of work is possible by engaging in another (rest from"physical" work by engaging, for example, inmen tal" work). A person's working day, cultural development and leisure can ony be rganized on the bass of the prosse olabour.

Question: How soon will economic and technological circumstances allow such clubs to be built?

Answer: Today. Only sceptics and conservatives and lovers of tradition can ignore the scientific-technical facts available to us today, and fail to understand that with their help any cultural work desired can be organized. Those who do not see further than their own noses, who suggest that the cultural revolution should be carried out in the old-fashioned way, that the land should be tilled with a wooden plough instead of a tractor and that the masses should be inoculated with enthusiasm for industrialization through "art", while industrialization is implemented in the handicraft way  only such would regard today's means of cultural organization as cloud-cuckoo-land.

Question: Do you take into account the effect of light on organisms, and how is one to manage with your glass walls in Baku: how will people shield themselves there against strong sunlight?

Answer: Climatic conditions must, of course, be reflected in the make-up of walls, and one cannot mindlessly transfer to Baku what has been done for Moscow.

Question: How is one to account for the identical forms which you introduce for different functions, otherwise than by aesthetic formal considerations?

Answer: The question indicates that the person putting it is primarily interested in outer form, in tasting rather than in organizing. Such a question is appropriate wherever there is concern for idealist architecture "as an art", while we are concerned with form as a corollary of the organization and functional interdependence of the workers and structural considerations. One should consider and criticze the methods of cultural organizations, not the form.

Question: What is this - a piece of fiction or a design? Answer: That depends on the degree of understanding: there are those for whom Soviet power is not power, but a piece of fiction.

Question: What size of site should be set aside for your club? Answer: The hygienic and operational considerations involved in the organization of a club require first and foremost a site well hielded from dirt and dust a park,the utskirts o y e It is therefore clear that the more room, the better. The minimum area is two hectares. ...

Question: Do you take into account the effect of light on an individual's psychology?

Answer: Light undoubtedly influences an individual's psychology and the whole question is how to switch from the unconscious play of light to scientifically informed work with light. Question: How do you organize an individual's emotions?

Answer: Emotions and feelings are by no means abstractions incapable of scientific analysis, and the organization of emotions and feelings is primarily the organization of human consciousness.

Quo  y el  i - tions?

Answer: It is not a matter of organizing visual perceptions, but of the general organization of human consciousness. The eye is an accurate mechanism which transmits the visual image to consciousness. The negative and positive nuances of assessment of visual perceptions depend upon the individual and social class experience of that consciousness.'

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1929, No.3, pp.110-11)

## Ex he for a Palace of Culture, 1930

(1) To impart a definite sense of purpose to all cultural work. To create the conditions for a hundred percent coverage of workers by polytechnic and political education. (2) To produce a clear sense of organization promoting the manifestation of initiative and self-help among those workers visiting the Palace. (3) To make the Palace of Culture not only a location for concentrated mass cultural work and leisure, but also the directing centre of cultural work in the entire working-class district. . . (5) To apply to the fulfilment of these socio-political tasks the most powerful resources of science and technology, and novel ways of conducting mass cultural work.' (Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1930, No.5, p.4)

Extract fom the explanatrynote attache to the enty for the House of Industry competition

'In our conditions, every new structure is a step towards socialism and must meet the new conditions of work and everyday

Part II/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

life. An architect who disregards these conditions is a conservative.

Work is not a regrettable necessity, but a sense of purpose in life.

Work as a physical and psychological condition must be totally organized.

Indices of the new organization of buildings

Organized work, work and physical training exercises, light, air, organized rest and food, heightened vitality.

Indices of the old organization of buildings

Confined courtyards, lack of vistas, pokey rooms, lack of adequate ventilation and light, barrack-like corridors. Absence of planning organization. Nervousness, piles, lowered vitality, lowered productivity.'

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1930, No.4, p. 1)

## Extracts from the explanatory note

attached to the design for a socialist settlement at the

Magnitogorsk Industrial Combine, 1930

'A socialist settlement is not the old random accumulation of urban districts and barracks, divorced from nature, haphazardly tied to industry, robbing people of their vitality by its dullness...

Asocialist settlement is a carefully thought-out organization of industry and agriculture, culture and leisure—of everything that informs human consciousness and life.

A settlement constructed on the basis of the foremost socialist technology....

A habitat should be conceived not as a kind of masonry holdall, with noisy corridors, where thousands of people dwell deprived of light and individual leisure, divorced from nature, but as a means of organizing small communities in which one personality is not lost among a thousand others but, instead, has the opportunity to develop to the utmost and to communicate with people (proceeding from a small community to larger ones). Habitats surrounded by gardens, sports grounds and swimming pools which dispense with the need to arrange rest homes outside the city. Habitats in which work, leisure and culture are organically linked.'

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1930, No.3, p. 1)

Extract from the explanatory note attached to the competition e desgthe eple Cissarat r Heavy Indury (Narkomtyazhprom), 1934

'Until now the Kremlin and the Church of St Basil have served as the architectural centre of Moscow. Naturally, the erection of a new, grandiose building on Red Square will in its turn influence the part played by individual monuments in the general effect of this central complex of Moscow.

I consider that the architecture of the Kremlin and St Basil must be subordinated to the architecture of the House of the Narkomtyazhprom and that the NKTP building should itself occupy the central position in the city.

The architecture of Red Square and the Kremlin is delicate and majestic music. The introduction into this symphony of an instrument of vast size and powerful sound is only permissible if such an instrument takes the lead and surpasses all the other buildings in the ensemble by its architectural quality.

Neither pomp nor overblown tawdriness in details and forms should provide the basis of the NKTP House composition, but, instead, simplicity, strictness, harmonious dynamism and wealth of meaning. The historical patterns must be subordinated for purposes of composition to this outstanding object, in accordance with the principle of artistic contrast.'

(Arkhitektura SSSR, 1934, No.10, p. 14)

Extracts from an article entitled 'The Architect's Palette', 1934

'The technology of building materials and structural elements is one of the decisive factors  together with the social conditions of everyday life — which determines the style and general composition of an architectural construction.

Everybody knows that is was our Functionalists who first uh  l cu to point in this connection to a cardinal sin committed by contemporary archietects, both in the USSR and in the West. Their approach to the latest building materials has been purely mechanical. They started exclusively from a primitive view of creatively to the newrganization ofhumanity in terms offunction and way of life, and this led to a certain utilitarianism in their design solutions. The artistic - and even, to some extent, technological- potential of the latest building materials has not been fully understood.

Architects make an extremely ponderous use of such a marcrete....

The architect should not approach building technology from a narrowly structural point o view. He must familiarize himself in a philosophical sense - if one may use such an expression in this context - with the potentialities of building technology. He must create new forms and structures from the material concerned. This is an elementary creative need which requires definite boldness in the search for new forms. But this will also in itselfsuggest new means ofresolving the spatial and general stylistic tasks facing the architect. ...

The production of building materials and structural elements must involve the close co-operation of the architect. He must act as a consultant or even control this branch of our industry...

It is essential to clarify the question of standardized production of individual structural elements and large building components. Some designers also have in mind the mass production oflargertistic" building components.This is a typcally eclecticapproach toarchitectural problems

Plastics will undoubtedly come to occupy in future a place of honour among our latest building materials. Plastics open unlimited opportunities for the widespread and skilful use of new materials and one can boldly assert that they will play a part just as revolutionary as that of ferro-concrete at an earlier time..

The colour treatment of façades, including, of course, those built of concrete-either by colour rendering or some other form of protective material - is obligatory. The colour, however, should not disguise the building material by overlaying it, but should stress its features, so that they may achieve the greatest possible effect.'(Arkhitektura SSSR, 1934, No.4, pp:32-33)

xel, February 1936

'I have been tagged with a number of "isms" - I am a Constructivist, and a Formalist, and a Schematist, and so on. In fact, I have been a Constructivist all along, and have worked in the Constructivist Group. ...

There is enormous confusion concerning creative method. An architect's working method is primarily the method of an artist, and there is no standard for the method of an artist. To-

Chapter /The leaders of the new direction

day I achieved a splendid ground plan, and tomorrow I will achieve a splendid elevation to go with this plan, o theher way about.

..I do everything in my power to make things realand concrete.

.. . One should believe in socialism — and it is hardly a fault to dream a little in this connection."

(Arkhitekturnaya gazeta, No.13 (85), 3 March 1936)

## Lissitzky (1890-1941)

Lazar Markovich Lissitsky was one of the most diversely gifted artists of the twentieth century, who made major contributions to architecture, painting, theatre design, photomontage, industrial design, book production and exhibition design. He studied at the Architectural Faculty of the Higher Technical School in Darmstadt, 1909-14, and at the Riga Polytechnical Institute, 191518; was one of the founders of Unovis; taught at the Free State Art Studios, SGKhM, 191920; and was a member of the Moscow Inkhuk in 1921.

Lissitzky was instrumental in the incorporation into architecture of the achievements of Leftist painters  principally the Suprematists - with his series of Proun paintings and drawings in 1919-24. He lived in Germany and underwent medical treatment in Switzerland during 1921-25, but maintained his artistic links with the USSR and promoted Soviet art - in particular, Constructivist work. He was in close contact with a number of European architects and artists, was a member of the Dutch De Stijl group, a co-founderof the periodical Veshch/Objet/Gegenstand, jointly with Ilya Ehrenburg, in Berlin, 1922, as well as of ABC, jointly with Mart Stam and Hans Schmidt, in Zürich, 1924, and he represented Asnova abroad.

During the early Soviet years, Agitational Art played an important part in Lissitzky's output, well-known examples being the poster Cleave the Whites with the Red Wedge of 1919, and the Lenin Tribune of 1920-24.

In 1923-24, he worked on problems of the vertical zoning of a city area in his designs for horizontal skyscrapers' in Moscow.

After returning from abroad, Lissitzky was active, both creatively —with designs for a yacht club in 1923 and the House of In-

Pr II/Masts n: baphit

dustry in 1930, among others — and editorially, on behalf of Asnova-with the publication ofone issue of Izvestiya Asnova (Asnova News) in 1926, together with Ladovsky. He took part in architectural competitions, with designs for the House of Textiles in Moscow, 1925, dwelling complexes in Ivanovo-Voznesensk, 1926, and the Pravda newspaper complex in Moscow, 1930. He taught the design of multi-purpose furniture and built-in equipment for standard dwelling units at Vkhutemas-Vkhutein in 1925-30, and introduced a number of radical innovations in exhibition design at Soviet pavilions abroad, in Cologne in 1928, and Leipzig and Dresden in 1930, at the All-Union Polygraphic Exhibition in Moscow in 1927, and others. He also contributed to the interior spatial organization of theatres, producing, for examples, the design for I Want a Child at the Meyerhold Theatre in 1928-29. He perfected a method of visual and spatial book design with Skaz o dvukh kvadratakh (The Tale of Two Squares), 1922, Dlya golosa (For the Voice), 1923, and Khorosho! (Good!), 1927, by Mayakovsky, in the periodical ssSR na stroike (USSR on the Building Site), 193240, and other publications.

Lissizky's writings

## x he g 19202

Not universal illusions, but universal reality

is what we called the sation on the way to the cnstru tion ofa new form....From being a copyist, the artist becomes a constructor of the forms of a new world of objects. This world is not built in competition with the engineer. The paths of art have not yet crossed the paths of science.

2.A Proun is a creative structuring of form (proceeding from mastery over space) with the aid ofan economic construction of the path of concrete creativity, and not the demonstration, explanation or popularization of life.

The    he mented individual scientifc disciplines the constructor concentrates all these within his experimental experience.

The Po's pah is not t l the ouenal path clear path of influence acknowledged of reality.

4.We have investigated the first stages of our construction, confined to two dimensions, and found that it is just as solid and resistant as the Earth. One may construct here as one would do in three-dimensional space and the tension of forces between individual parts must therefore equally be brought into balance. The conjunction of the resultants of individual forces manifests itself in a novel way in the Proun. We have seen that the plane of the Proun ceases to be a painting and becomes a structure which one must contemplate in the round from every side, examine from above and investigate from below. It follows that the only axis of the painting perpendicular to the horizon has been destroyed. As we circle around, we spiral inwards into space.

Until now, we projected space directly on to the plane. Through the Proun, we are put in the position of having to effect our escape from this plane of projection. We have set the Proun in motion and have thereby acquired a multiplicity of axes of projection; we stand among them and push them asunder. As we stand within this framework, so we must begin to define it. .

The Prou is viriledynamically active.The Pronmoves from one stage to the next, along a line, towards perfection.

The Proun leads to the genesis of the future, it attracts a new plane, then switches to the modelling of space and beyond this to the construction of all forms of life in the widest sense of the word.

The Proun alters the accustomed forms of art and leaves the picture to the individualist, a scarcely perceived phenomenon, wo sitting infont  his easel in a closstustart an - ishes it alone.

The future life is the ferro-concrete foundation of commu-W the integrated city commune in which all humanity is to dwell will be erected on this foundation.

And so the Proun passes on beyond the painting and its painte  ne  the maine d the eern the he, and strides out armed with new elements for the creation of a new space, articulates it by means of elements of 1, 2 and 3 dimensions and constructs a new, multifarious, but integrated image of our nature.'

1921

We  n  he  e pation in the ceation o material cule.

Architecture has always been an art which imparted matechitecture . . .?

Ever since our living, natural, creative activity has been stuffed into academic hothouses, allthat was truly creative has bypassed these musty boxes. Yet those who found canvas and clay a constraint and went on to create structures, found themselves captives in the incantatory ring of an infinite number of bookish teachings, with which the solidly established professors crammed them and, slowly but surely, bottled up the whole creative urge of their apprentices. And thus monsters grew up among us, walking encyclopedias of all bygone centuries. . ..

But then fresh winds began to blow in the West.

. a new image of the new bourgeois, capitalist world came into being via the image of its city. When imperialist capital began to tread on the corns of its younger brother - the petit bourgeois — the taste for nature and philanthropy was born. In London, Ebenezer Howard invented the "garden city", small, comfortable houses on the outskirts where those for whom there was no room at the centre might seek their salvation. The German Krupps and their peers seized upon this splendid idea, and began to philanthropize their workers with such colonies.

Such are the worlds whence the wind wafted us our living corpses, who are now preparing to force on communist Russia the capitalist imperialist city, with its stock exchange at its centre (the City) and, swept away to the fringes from where no breeze blows, its manufacturing back alleys, its philanthropic little houses for the "working" classes, and so forth and so on.

They are now trying to devise a standard single-family worker's house.

... But do we need this? Do we need a separate house of our own . . .

. . . would labouring Russia be content with this? ...

It now turns out, however, that our venerable ones are engaged in the art of architecture, while all the rest is just the art of building. Let the builders build, let them put up the living body of a factory or a popular dwelling and the artist-architects

Chapter 2/The leaders of the new direction

will then come along with the powder and make-up and wigs aber ylin  pla the builder's inartistic product.

... Is not architecture in these circumstances a parasite on the sound body of a structure?

And yet life is crying out for creators. Where can we expect them to come from in the art of construction, that field which faces the tremendous task of reconstructing the entire body of life in accordance with the new content that is being created for it.' (Gazeta Izo', 1921, No. 1)

Extracts from the periodical Merz, 1924

'It is more than ENOUGH, this constant MACHINE MACHINE MACHINE MACHINE

as soon as one comes to modern art. The machine is no more than rush,  y iv ne t h, wi whi te canvas of the world's picture is being painted. ...

It would b a toal waste  tme, to put it mildy, to  t prove nowadays that, given the existence of the typewriter, it is no longer necessary to write in one's own blood with a goose quill. Trying to prove nowadays that the task of any creativity, including art, is not representation, but affirmation, is also a total waste of time.

The machine has not divorced us from nature. We have discovered by means of it a new, previously unsuspected nature.

Modern art has reached the same conclusions as modern science by entirely intuitive and independent means.

,: . . And in so doing both have reached the same formula: EVERY FORM IS THE PETRIFIED SNAPSHOT OF A PROCESS. THEREFORE, A WORK IS A STATION IN EVOLUTION AND NOT ITS PETRIFIED AIM.

We acknowledge works which embrace a system, but a system that has become manifest in the course of production, not before it. ..

Our work is no philosophy, nor a system of natural science, it is a branch of nature and only as such can it become an object of cognition.' (Merz, Hanover, April/July 1924, No.8/9)

Par II/Masters and trends:biographies, statements, manifestos

Extract from a letter to J.J.P. Oud, 1925

'I am convinced that art arises wherever we least anticipate it. I go to the utmost pains to think out and make use ofevery aspect of the functions, constraints, material possibilities etc, but I only experience satisfaction if a work results which surprises me when I am confronted by it. I believe that all my organs taken together are more correctly on course than my brain is on its own. If I lacked this conviction, I would rather become a cobbler tomorrow.'

Extracts fromanarticle entitle 'Americanism and European Architecture', 1925

'The words "America" and "American" are linked in the Old World, in Europe, with the notion of something extra-perfect, rational, fficient and universal. All these are concepts alien to the old artists of Europe. .

Our era has invented a new material- concrete. A house can be poured from it like a statue in bronze. It has some drawbacks, however, for housing purposes. At present, work is in hand everywhere on the development of new materials that will be light, easily processed, thermally insulting etc. And, in this, Europe is beginning to outstrip America. It is in the solution to this problem that the centre of gravity of work devoted to the creation of a new form also resides. . . .

Europe takes over organizational and practical ideas from America, then purifies and refines them. In this, Europe sets itself the target of meeting more than the demands of economy, efficiency and hygiene. European architects are convinced that by designing and organizing the house afresh they will be sharing in the process of organizing a new, more conscious way of life.

We have had occasions to meet in Europe a number of excellent masters of the new architecture and discover how difficult their situation is. They are surrounded by a chauvinist, reactionary, individualistic society which regards as alien and hostile these internationally minded, cheerfully active and collectively inclined people. That is why they follow with such attention the progress of our life, and all believe that the future belongs, not to the USA, but to the USSR.

(Krasnaya niva [The Red Cornfield], 1925, No.49, pp.1188-89)

## Extract from the article 'The Film of El's Life up to 1926'

. . . Proun. The picture canvas has become too narrow for me. The circle of colour-harmony epicures has become too narrow for me, and I created the Proun as a transfer station from painting to architecture. I have treated the canvas and the wood panel as sites on which my structural ideas would meet with the smallest number of obstacles laid across their path. I have used the black-and-white scale (with red highlights) as substance and material. In this way, a reality will be created which will hold no ambiguity for anyone.'

(l Lissitzky. Maler, Architekt, Typograf, Fotograf. Erinerungen,   
Briefe,SchrienUbegenvn Sophe Lissy-Kupper lLisy.   
Painter, Architect, Typographer, Photographer. Reminiscences, Letters, Writings. Related by Sophie Lissitzky-Kuppers], Dresden, 1976, p.329)

## Extracts from an article entitled 'The Culture of the Habitat', 1926

'Salons, halls, boudoirs, drawing rooms . .  all have been gardly, cramped living space is already beginning to grow into the new Soviet habitat. Forms of dwelling are the material expression of essentials of the new way of life, and they demand our attention. Let it be understood that the habitat, its plan and its organization, represent the basic cell out of which houses are formed houses which in their turn form streets and squares, in other words the city as a whole. This is growth in the direction of "large architecture".But "small architecture" also develops from the organization of the habitat, its interior equipment, its furniture. . . . the present time does not merely - ment, for new furniture.

The fudamentals our way of le reas follows:euzation of the requirements of all layers of the population; a standard amount of living space; and the new Soviet family. Equalization of requirements is the starting point in the elaboration of a standard dwelling, as opposed to bourgeois countries where total anarchy prevails in respect of requirements. The standard and the definite limits demand inventiveness directed at rationalization, planning and equipment. A modern lodging must be designed like the very best modern suitcase, taking into account all the necessities which must find a place in it, and making useof every cubic centimetre—al else apart, this is dictated for the present by the need for economy. The new Soviet family, with factually equal rights for husband and wie,a cords the wife far more rights than merely those of nurse and cook, and imposes more obligations on her, which, in turn, se new requirements in terms of habitat

. . . Our basic task is to plan the habitat so that the bulk of equipment and furniture are built-in together with the house, as a single entity . .. All the kitchen equipment . . . the cupboards, partitions, folding tables, besallthis can quite smply be built as part of interior space. Such a task requires the joint efforts of an architect and a designer of domestic equipment.' (Stroitelnaya promyshlennost [Building Industry],

1926, No. 11, pp.877-81)

## Extracts from the Asnova slogans, 1926

'Our grandmothers believed that the Earth was the centre of the universe and that Man was the measure  all things. Learn to see what is in front of your eyes. Man is the tailor's measure and architecture must be measured by architecture.'

(Izvestiya Asnova, Moscow, 1926, p.8)

Extracts from an article on 'The Architecture of the Steel and Ferro-Concrete Framework', 1926

'Functionalism, Constructivism, Horizontalism and Verticalism . . . such are the slogans round which architectural thought revolves today. Construction by means of new materials- steel and ferro-concrete — has resulted in horizontal and vertical articulation of the skeleton of a house. This skeleton has evolved from the new requirements set for a modern house, from its novel functions....

The plan and its system, that is what determines the total outcome. Let us take note of the most characteristic feature of a modern plan,which is the proportion between black and white; the proportion between the total sectional area of walls, supports and partitions and the area of living space. The more modern the plan, the smaller this fraction will be. It is thus that we come right to the heart of the new method of construction. ...

And so, the unchallenged master of modernity - the engineer — has created the steel and ferro-concrete framework. Ar-

Chapter The leaders of the new direction

hind modernity, must take on such construction. Nothing comes into the world of its own accord, and architecture will not result from a single structure. That would necessitate a whole series of other, no less important conditions.

Our architecture must bring forth its own masters. Expen e masers.Expc cea acl b work....

Here we still regard as novelty designs on paper. The steel and ferro-concrete framework predominates in them, but it is a graphic rather than a material framework, and therefore practice alone will succeed in providing us with genuine criteria. . ..

We are faced with the task of creating spatial architecture wc  e and not only touched by the hands, as in sculpture, but among which people live and move - an architecture of space and time. To this end, steel and ferro-concrete frames may provide us with excellent tools.'

(Stroitelnaya promyshlennost, 1926, No. I, pp.59-63)

## llya Golosov (1883-1945)

Ilya Alexandrovich Golosov was born in Moscow as the son of a priest. After leaving school, he entered the Moscow Stroganov Industrial Art College and then, in 1907, the Moscow College of Painting, Sculpture and Architecture, from which he graduated in 1912 as an architect. As a student and immediately after graduation, he worked as an assistant for a number of architects and artists, surveyed architectural monuments and was engaged in book production. Private houses in Kirzhach and Moscow were built to his designs in 1912 and 1913 respectively. During the First World War, he built various structures for use by the army's rearguard forces.

During the first years after the Revolution, he worked in the architectural studio of the Moscow Soviet and taught both at the Moscow Polytechnical Institute and at Vkhutemas. In 1918-19, he produced a number of designs in the Classical mode, such as a hospital and crematorium in Moscow, and a Leo Tolstoy Memorial School at Yasnaya Polyana. In the early 1920s, Golosov adopted Symbolist Romanticism, developed a novel theory concerning the construction of architectural organisms and introduced a new method of tuition in the Vkhute-

Part III/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

mas studio which he jointly directed with Melnikov. The most characteristic of his experimental designs and competition entries produced in 192024 included those for a bakery, a radio station, an observatory, a workshop with water pump; for the Ostankino stud, the Palace of Labour in Moscow, the Far Eastern pavilion at the 1923 Exhibition and for the Soviet pavilion at the Paris Exhibition, as well as for the Arkos and Leningradskaya Pravda buildings.

In the mid-1920s, Golosov switched to Constructivism and went on to produce, until the early 1930s, a large number of outline sketches and competition designs - many of which won prizes and were carried out-for buildings and complexes of various kinds. Among these were office buildings, such as the House of Textiles in 1925, the Elektrobank and Rusgertorg buildings in 1926, the House of Military Industry Trusts in 1929 and the House of Industry in Sverdlovsk in 1931;Palaces of Labour like that for Rostov-on-Don in 1925; Workers' Clubs and Houses of Culture, for Moscow in 1927-29 and Stalingrad in 1928; communal houses and dwelling combines for Moscow in 1927-28, Ivanovo-Voznesensk in 1929-32 and Stalingrad in 1930, Houses of Soviets for Bryansk in 1924, Khabarovsk in 1928-30, Rostov-on-Don in 1930 and Elista in 1928-32; and mass action theatres, notably for Ivanovo-Voznesensk in 1931, Sverdlovsk in 1932 and Moscow in 1933.

From 1933, Golosov headed one of the Moscow Soviet's architectural studios. During the first half of the 1930s, he took part in debates about the creative orientation of Soviet architecture, and criticized both Constructivism and the direct application of Classicism. He made increasing use of traditional forms in his designs dating from the 1930s, such as those for: the Palace of Soviets in 1932; dwelling houses in Moscow on Yauza Boulevard in 1933-35 and Kalyaevskaya Street in 1936; the Tass building in 1934; the House of the Book in 1934; the Moscow Trade Union High School in 1938; and a dwelling house in Gorky in 1936.

During the Second World War, Golosov designed a large number of memorial structures, such as graves, mausoleums, pantheons and arches. During the last years of his life, he worked hard on a major book, Osnovy sovremennoi arkhitekturnoi kompozitsii (Foundations of Modern Architectural Composition), which he never completed.

Ilya Golosov's writings

Extracts from manuscripts dating from the early 1920s

'Architecture, like every science, must have firm principles of its own. . . . It abounds with laws concealed from us until now. Common sense demands that these laws be elicited and obeyed.'

'Architectural form cannot be achieved through knowledge alone. Sensitivity and artistic intuition must also be present. Yet artistic intuition develops through knowledge.'

'Intuition is a capital that is accumulated gradually, but can be spent at one go.'

'When composing a group of structures, or a single structure, it is essential to distinguish mass from form. . . . we will assume the former term to cover any volume of the most rudimentary kind, devoid ofany inner meaning; in other words, not resulting from any particular subjective architectural idea.

An architectural mass as such is without content and is not the expression of a particular idea-it is therefore totally freein the forms it adopts and bears no responsibility for them.

We shall contemplate every architectural structure in terms of both mass and form, without confusing these two concepts.

When considering an architectural structure from the point oi omass wemust wholly put aside its conception, hereby freeing ourselves from the responsibility of impinging on its subjective form, and approach it from a purely objective tion.

Ino wors, te"ual mas" thes three-al cual ing whatsoever and quite independent from such a meaning. ...

If mass carries no inner meaning, bears no responsibility toward the latter an is wholly indeendent, thepie is tue of form: it is dependent upon and responsible to the meaning which has brought it into existence. When we perceive a form, we experience the inner meaning.

There can be no architectural form without a definite meaninsincorm lacsmeanigtmust impl eecib as an architectural mass and, conversely, if an architectural mass is invested with a definite meaning, it ceases to be a mass and becomes a form. ...

Consequently, any architectural structure can be regarded s a ms n  sn  sace nd orm r cmplex of them) bearing the imprint of a particular functional character derived from its inner meaning.

'Mass is a phenomenon the chief property of which is power, weightiness .. it can be contemplated independently of form..

Form is the expression of essencethe meaning of a particua. It rt te lit  eAh ral form is the expression of architectural thought.'

'When one analyses architectural mass and form, one cantion to the principle of movement.

In every mass or form a correlation of forces expressing this principle is ways preent in nehapenother

One of the main conditions governing meaningful composition in architecture is an understanding of the properties of the configurations of masses or forms employed, in relation to their repose or movement, and inherent in them in accordance with their nature.

. . . Every structure as a whole has a dominant direction of movement of its own.

. . . it is essential to postulate in this context that movement must be understood, not as a change of location, but . . . as the conjunction of movement with a static scale, i.e. a structure which embodies clearly expressed movement is nevertheless seen as strictly static.'

'The majority of monumental buildings must incorporate movement and the concept of total repose can only be applied in very rare cases — it is permissible only in those cases when a single structure brings together a very large number of buildings and is sited apart and entirely independently.'

'Achieving that harmony among masses which must be present in every structure means achieving harmony among all movements.'

'There is no limit to the number of ways in which a cube can be used- each of its surfaces can provide the basis for the whole form. A prism is less adaptable in terms of position. An eightsided prism cannot adopt a prone position, because its base would lack an area of adequate size. A cylinder is similar in this to an eight-sided prism. A pyramid is dependent on the number of its faces.'

'Life - the world rotates faster and faster. .

Repose in antiquity - movement in modern times.

The need to subordinate architecture to a clearly marked

Chapter 2/The leaders of the new direction

rhythm becomes ever more obvious and the brilliance with ture.

In terms of expression, architecture has perceptibly shifted in recent years from detailed forms to general masses.

Our era is gradually destroying an earlier concern for details and history. Life requires construction, the artistic creation of that which is complete and individual - mainly the construction of interrelated masses.

The value of this very kind of construction abolishes nowadays any more thorough study of a structure's details as a starting point for architectural composition.

The rhythm of masses - that is where compositional innovation in architecture finds its start.'

'When composing any group of separate structures linked by a common purpose, the architect is at liberty, within the limits of a rational treatment, to choose any particular general composition to cover all the structures.'

(Tsgali [Central State Archive for Literature and Art], Fond 1979, Schedule I)

Extracts from a notebook entitled Ideas, Statements, Aphorisms, 1920s

'The creative drive, crushed by the majesty and beauty of Classicism, can create nothing new.'

'General architectural composition devoid of principle cannot be redeemed by any embellishments, by any exquisite details.'

'The modern reproductions of Classical architectural models resemble an adaptation of Procrustus' bed to the human frame.'

'Plastic quality is among the basic requirements of an architectural building.'

'Repetition is fundamental in art.

Size (scale) can suppress worth.

'The confusion, mystique and vagueness of the theory about a golden mean in architecture are features which forbid one to accept it unquestioningly. On the contrary, everything indicates that such a theory fetters living creative forces.'

'Nature abhors a vacuum. In architecture, as in all manifestations of life, gaps, empty spaces ask to be filled: an architect must therefore learn to . . . see these gaps so as to fill them.'

(Ilya Golosov archive)

Part III/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

Extracts from a lectue entited The New Ways to rchue December 1922

. . . The architect must be free of style in the old, historical meaning of this term, and must himself create a style resulting from an architectural form that matches the meaning or purpose (idea) of the object.

Guiding rules and laws must be provided for this purpose so as to make it easier in each particular case for the architect to pick the right path towards a solution of the given artistically creative task. ...

It is essential to lay down only constant principles, tha are irrefutable, true and immutable.

There are very many such principles, and these principles, which have an absolute intrinsic value, are certainly equally valid for Classical architecture and for the architecture of our time. All the rest is a matter of intuitive creativity and cannot be confined to the hard-and-fast framework of law. .

The first and immutable law of architectural artistic construction is the law of artistic necessity.

Other extremely useful laws are those which concern spatial positions and the interrelations of architectural masses; the proportional artistic relation of volumes; artistic perception; the expression of the psychological effect of artistic-architectural masses and forms; the independence of certain phenomena; and many other laws.

There are certain given natural effects over which the artist has no control whatsoever, since he is only in a position to make a certain use of their actuality, such as, for example, the effect of horizontal and vertical, light and shade etc. There are principles which can only be assessed in objective artistic terms.

Every work of art must manifest the artist's individuality, but such manifestation will only be justified and artistically meaningful if it expresses an objective value, since an unknown quantity can only be determined by means of a known quantity. This obliges us to adopt laws with an absolute value that constantly and immutably underpin every work of art.'

Materialy idokumenty, Moscow, 1963, p.29

Extracts from an article entitled 'The Large Architectural Form', 1933

. Architecture is one of the most complex forms of art. . icntctuie i unt U

When I set to work on a design, I tackle the volumes, the elevation and the plan jointly, rather than mechanically distinguishing between them. Starting from a general conception of the task, I move to the composition of a plan.

. . . Every architect must give thought to the connection between the volumetric solution and the handling of the ground plan, while taking account of the functional purpose of the structure and its general social and aesthetic conception.

I always start on a design by making a close study of the technological and economic particulars of the task. This very often even happens at the expense of a certain "impressive" quality in the design, which, on the other hand, becomes considerably more realistic. Generally speaking, the constant revision of individual parts of the design is inevitable. And this is where the preliminary sketch or drawing plays a colossal part.

The drawing gives concrete expression to the individual stages of an architect's work and informs him of the correctness or otherwise of the treatment applied.

A drawing is especially necessary and profitable at the initial stage of design work. . .. To an architect, the ability to draw matches the ability to assemble. An architect who is not skilled in drawing will never be able to compose.'

(Arkhitektura SSSR, 1933, No.5, p.34)

First Moscow Architects' Studio (Work Guild), 1919.

## Ginzburg (18921946)

Moisei Yakovlevich Ginzburg was born in Minsk as the son of an architect. He graduated from the Milan Academy of Arts in 1914 and the Riga Polytechnical Institute in 1917; carried out his design for a private house in Evpatoriya and investigated Tatar national architecture in 1917-21; taught at Vkhutemas from 1921 and became professor of Architectural History and of the Theory of Architectural Composition there from 1923. Simultaneously, he taught at the Moscow Institute of Civil Engineers - Migi, later MVTU - and was confirmed as a full member of the Russian Academy of Artistic Sciences- Rakhn. During the first half of the 1920s, he worked on problems of architectural composition, as in his book Ritm  arkhitekture

RinArchteueof 923.He was activ in the tru r the new architecture in editorial articles for the periodical Arkhitektura in 1923 and in his book Stil i epokha (Style and Epoch) in 1924. He was one of the organizers ofOsa in 1925 and joint editor of their periodical Sovremennaya arkhitektura (Contemporary Architecture) -SA -in 1926-30. During 1926-27, he published in SA a series of articles on theory in which he set out and promoted the fundamental positions of the Functional Method - the creative statement of Constructivist tenets.

During the years 1928-32, Ginzburg headed a team of architects whose aim was to study the problem of a scientificorganization of the way of life and of the standardization of housing. The team produced designs for new types of economic, rationally planned dwelling units and houses provided with communal services.Ginzburg later summarized his conclusions from this work in his book Zhilishche (Habitat), published in 1934. During the 1930s, Ginzburg went deeply into problems of socialist settlement and district planning, producing designs such as that for Green City in 1930, the planning of the Chernikov industrial district in 1931 and the regional planning of the southern shore of the Crimea in 1935-37.

Throughout the twenties and thirties, Ginzburg produced a large number of competition entries, including the Palace of Labour in 1922-23, the House of Textiles in 1925 and the Orgametal House in 1926, in Moscow; the House of Soviets in Makhachkala in 1926; a Housing Commune in 1927 and the Government House in Alma-Ata in 1927-31, which was carried out; a theatre in Sverdlovsk in 1931; the Palace of Soviets in 1932, the Nemirovich-Danchenko Theatre in 1933, the Narkomtyazhprom building in 1934 and the Izvestiya combine in 1936, all in Moscow. An apartment house was built in Moscow in1926-27, and three experimental communal houses of a transitional type were erected in Moscow and Sverdlovsk in 1928-29 to designs by Ginzburg.In 1935-37, Ginzburg headed the team which constructed a sanatorium complex in Kislovodsk.

Towards the end of the 1930s, Ginzburg was in charge of work on a multi-volume General History of Architecture at the USSR Academy of Architecture. During the war, he dealt with the standardization and industrialization of building work. Towards the end of his life, he started on the reconstruction of Sebastopol and two sanatoriums, at Kislovodsk and Oreanda in the Crimea, which were not completed until after his death.

His major theoretical work dealing with architectural creativity also remained unfinished.

## Ginzburg's writings

## Extracts from Ritm v arkhitekture (Moscow, 1923)

'The history of styles as conceived until quite recently was merely a history of the evolution of architectural form. The compositional methods which went into the construction of actual monuments were left in the background. Yet, here too, the characteristics of these laws of composition must be elicited if a style is to be fully understood.' (pp.7172)

Thehistory o styles, like everyother history is bjective. It does not recognize the existence of "better" or "worse" styles. A style ultimately works itself to death. . . . A new influx of creative power is needed in order to start afresh. . . .

Yet what is dead will not rise again. Identical problems endlessly gain in complexity, the methods for their solution change. . . . And the task of modern architecture is, of course, to discover those formal elements and those laws governing their combination through which the rhythmic beat of our time will be revealed.'(p.116)

## Extract from an article entitled 'The Old and the New', 1923

'We can distinguish two driving principles whenever a new style makes its appearance: the law of continuity, which reinforces our artistic invention and imagination, while economizing our creative ability and energy, and the law of independence of the new idea, which finds its support in the new life that emerges around the artist and which imparts to the work the edge of novelty and that feeling for rhythm of modern times without which art simply ceases to be art.'

(Arkhitektura, 1923, Nos. 3-5, p. 3)

## Extract from an article entitled 'The Aesthetics of the Present', 1923

'If before the war and the Revolution we could still hope for success in searching out all kinds of Renaissances and Classicisms, the last few years have, of course, made us clearly aware of the obvious truth that going forward is easier if one also looks ahead. However half-baked, confused and far-fetched architectural ideas among the young may be, they nevertheless relentlessly point to the fact that some very marked change has taken

Part III/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

place in the nature of human emotion and its attendant aesthetics, a change which, whether it be good or bad, is certainly reflected in the still inchoate explorations of young art.'

(Arkhitektura, 1923, Nos. 1-2, pp.34)

## Extracts from Stil i epokha (Moscowe, 1924)

'Experience, backed by the creativity of past centuries, clearly points the way for modern artists: boldness in experimentation, daring in the quest for novelty, joy in creative discovery; that long thorny path that leads to victory, provided only that sincerity is in the movement, and determination shines brightly, and that both are carried to the shore by a wave that does not falter and meets the needs of our time.

That was the way of art in all the best phases of humanity's existence and that, ofcourse, is what it should be today.(p.12) . . . the system of an architectural style arises in answer to a whole number of problems  those of space and those of volume, which represent the treatment of the same task from inside and outside.' (p.23)

'In addition to the assessment of a work of art or an entire style in terms of history, i.e. in its relation to the environment which brought it into being, another method of objective assessment arises in genetic terms, aimed at establishing the significance of a phenomenon for the further development of that style and the evolution of the process as a whole.' (p.24)

'An excellent indication of the creative impotence of our times is provided by the very notion of making new city districts fit in with the style of old existing ones, however formally excellent these might be. This is a notion which has established itself very firmly in the minds of the best architects of the last decade and has often forced them to subordinate entire city quarters and districts to the formal features of some group of stylistically earlier monuments, instead of subordinating them to the organic reality of that city, something that lies entirely beyond the formal features of any particular style.' (p.27)

Almost invariably, a new culture is brought into being by the appearance on the stage ofhistory of a new nation, ethnicity or social group. . ..

Certain forms of artistic organism are most representative of pyaelyy tive drive....

The component of life which the new active social element of modern times - the working class - has promoted to first place has been labour because it represents the main ingredient of life for this element, its unifying feature. . . . Thus the main problems which come to the fore today concern those architectural organisms which involve the concept of labour  the workers' house and the house of work - and the multiplicity of problems they pose.

And the logical conclusion to be drawn from this in the light of a study of history, is that the significance of these problems will outgrow their initial solution and will provide the basic elements of a new generally applicable style.' (pp. 77-80)

The problem of the workers' house was posed by life long before the war. . . .

Even at these early stages, we encounter the spirit of collectivism, a sweeping increase in architectural scale which favours lapidary and energetic statements. We have no concrete idea of what the workers' house of the future will be like, but one can foretell without much fear of being proved wrong that it is precisely these qualities, derived from the characteristics of the workers' dwelling as such, which will provide its essentials.'(pp.80 and 82)

'Quite apart from a speculative perception of the laws of statics and mechanics, every individual has a purely intuitive understanding of these laws..

Thus, on the strength of our perceptual experience and psycol i tem, self-contained but derived from and dependent upon constructions in the world of form  strictly speaking, a system of aesthetics.' (pp.112 and 114)

'When a new stylistic idiom makes its appearance and its new elements are formulated, there is clearly no need for extraneous accretions - whatever is new most often arises from a constructive or practical requirement without any decorative addition. Later, decorative elements gradually appear, at first without disrupting the organic life of the monument concerned, until their abundance oversteps these limits and turns into a self-involved interplay of decorative elements. The youth decorative." (pp.119 and 120)

if during the last century, when life meant something iffen tu e the public building which exerted an influence on industrial architeturedietricallyposite henmenon is nowki place: industrial architecture, which is more closely related to the sources of modern perception of form, must exert its influence on the architecture of dwellings, which is dominated to a greater extent by tradition and inertia.' (p. 134)

'Dealing with everyday matters in close conjunction with the foreman and the constructor, an architect cannot help catching their methods of working. Like them, his aim will be a clear-cut solution of a task embodying particular data and particular unknowns, rather than the unbridled fantasy of an immaterial conception. The architect will then come to regard himself not as a decorator, but as an organizer of life. The spiritual energy and creative imagination thus released will not, however, desert his work but will merely be directed into another channel, with imagination turning into inventiveness. . . . In this way, subconscious, impulsive creativity will come to be replaced by a clear and distinct organizational method.' (pp.141-42)

## Extracts from an article entitled New Methods of Architectural Thought', 1926

'Instead of the old system of architectural work, in which the ground plan, construction and outer aspect of a building were constantly at war with each other and the architect was, in so far as he was able, the arbiter of all these unresolved conflicts, the foremost feature of new architectural creativity is its single, undivided, integrated drive forward, in the course of which the task is evolved organically and which represents the creative process from start to finish.

The quality of a modern architect's work is radically altered by the fact that he does not regard his activity as the fulfilment of individual commissions, but as a matter of establishing architectural standards governing the production of new dwellings and cities, and of constantly perfecting these standards. . . . As socialism is currently being built up, every new solution provided by the architect-for a dwelling, a club, a factory -is perceived by us as the invention of an entirely new model, appropriate to its task and suitable for reproduction in whatever numbers the State may require, This deflects the architect's energy at the outset from hankering after individual and idiosyncratic treatment, and directs it towards the improvement of his standard and the refinement and maximum standardization of all his details. ...

The new architect, free from all vestiges of the past, prejudices and bias, analyses every aspect of his task and its special features, reduces its component elements, groups these by function and organizes his solution in accordance with these factors. ...

As a result, we observe in the output of modern architects the egence n entirey new,argely symerical ki plan.

.. . Only functional architectural thinking rigorously fixes spatial organization at the start of work. . . . It is from this that the modern architect principally proceeds. It forces him to develop his idea outwards from within, and not the other way about, as was the practice in periods of eclecticism.'

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura, No. I, pp. 1-3)

## Extract from an article on The International Front of Modern Architecture', 1926

'Modern Soviet architecture, at least such of it as is grouped around our periodical . . . does not contain any nihilism whatsoever and does not, under any circumstances, reject the requirements of formal expressiveness. It is, however, entirely based upon the functional aspects of a building as a whole, and of its constituent elements. Our line on modern architecture is based on the principle that a finished work of architecture, just as any other genuinely modern object, is not a building or an object comprising some sort of aesthetic additive, but a concrete task, organized in a rational and planned way, the very method of organization of which provides its maximum potential of expression.'

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1925, No.2, p.44)

Extracts from an article on 'The Functional Method and Form', 1926

The Functional Method, correctly understood, requires from the modern architect, in every case without exception, a material presentation arising from the new conditions of life.

If an atavistic form ultimately results from the architect's work, the Functonal Method must have beenincorrectly applied and the solution is faulty in some respect.

. . . It should be most categorically stated that the Functional approach does not . . . abrogate the highly important task of architectural presentation. It merely establishes the laws of such presentation, and forces the architect to look for it in those elements that are functionally justified, by removing him from

Part III/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

the realm of unrelated aesthetic additions and derivation, to the organization of the purely architectural task Itself and the formal use of all the practical and structural opportunities which are always available within any architectural project.'

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1926, No.4, pp.89 and 91)

## Extract from an article on The National Architecture of the Peoples of the USSR', 1926

The Functional Method, correctly applied, should also provide an answer to the question of a national image of architecture.
