All the conditions determining the contemporary image of the National Soviet Republics must be taken into account: (1) the conditions determining the character of the secular way of life and climate, a character which defines the individual national image of a republic; (2) the conditions determining the new social structure, the new ways of life . . . and the achievements of modern technology, all of which are common to and alike in the USSR as a whole, and define the growth of the Union-wide forces of socialism under construction.

A correct solution can only be arrived at by taking both categories of conditions into account.

From this point of view, of course, the revival of old architectural forms of decoration appropriate to one or another of the national styles cannot be justified in any way.

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1926, Nos.5-6, pp.11314)

Extracts froman article entitld Purpose inModern Archtecture 1927

'Functional architecture could not exist without a clearly and newly stated purpose.

This purpose, which is often only conveyed by a single word factory, club, dwelling etc  must first be carefully analysed by the architect, and then given substance and broken down into a system of clear production and domestic processes.

Production or labour processes are usually associated in our minds with a factoryrindustrial plant, while socially domestc ones are connected with dwellings and public buildings.

.. . in fact, during the construction of socialism, socially domestic processes must, of course, be treated with as much care and attention as production processes.'

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1927, No.1, pp.4-5)

Extract from an article entitled 'Constructivism as a Method of Laboratory and Educational Work', 1927

'Any study of the elements of architecture involves a danger that certain forms may be canonized and pass into the architect's vocabulary for everyday use. Constructivism wages war against this phenomenon and treats the basic elements of architecture as material that is constantly changing in accordance with the changing conditions of the generation of form.'

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1927, No.6, p. 165)

## Ex ntCuvie1928

'The theory of Constructivism may be subdivided into three parts: the Constructivist attitude to the aim, to the means and to the form. . . . the aim, in so far as we are concerned, is the absolute forceful demolition of old concepts. This is usually a programmed process within which the architect must display his creative ability. . . . Our aim is not, in fact, the execution of a commission, as such, but working together with the proletariat on the tasks involved in the building of a new existence, of a new way of life. . . . technology is for us no more than a means which we press into service. . . . All the achievements of modern science must be subordinated to our work, must be tied in to this work for a new social aim by the new architect. . . . A fairly widelyhedopinion is current that Constructivism reresents artistic nihilism and is the rejection of form, an unwillingness to take account of form. In reality, however, Constructivism is a working method for searching out the most reliable and correct path to a new form, which most closely corresponds to the new social content. . . . we approach form through the li, must constantly be sought and newly defined each time by an aim that is clear and bears the imprint of the Revolution.'

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1928, No.5, p.145)

in91.He went to Italy for yearateraduatintosty chitectural monuments, then taught at the Institute and, as an assistant to the architects Vasily Kosyakov and Alexei Bubyr, helped to design and erect various buildings in Petersburg, Reval (now Talinn) and elsewhere. During the first years following the Revolution, Nikolsky was engaged in the design and buildin  cvilin ruture on theilways. In 120, esumed his teaching work at the Institute of Civil Engineers, and also engaged in practical work.

At the start of the 1920s, Nikolsky was influenced by Cubo-Futurism and Zhivskulptarkh, as in his design for a District Executive Committee building in 1921.

After 1926, Nikolsky went on teaching, but also took charge of an architectural design studio, whose members formed something like a Leningrad branch of Osa. A whole series of experimental and competition designs were produced here, such as a tram stop, a hall for public meetings, a club, a crematorium, a cinema with dining hall etc, all of which may be classified as examples of Suprematist Constructivism.

During the 1920s, Nikolsky was much concerned with the development of models for socially new types of structures. In the housing field he did a Housing Commune. Amongst communal facilities he did baths and a swimming pool; in the field of public buildings he designed clubs, schools, higher educational establishments and sports complexes. Amongst his projects actually executed in Leningrad were baths in Lesnoe and in Ushakov Street, schools in Lesnoe and in Stachek Street, dwellings on Traktor Street, clubs for the Krasny Putilovets factory and the shipbuilding wharfs, and stadia for the Krasny Sportintern and Krasny Putilovets.

Nikolsky took part in a large number of competitions, among them that for the Central Telegraph Office in Moscow in 1925, a communal house for workers - an Osa competition — in 1927, the Tsentrosoyuz building and the Lenin Library in Moscow in 1928, a public library in Leningrad in 1928 and the first round of the Palace of Soviets in 1931.

## Nikolsky (1884-1953)

Alexander Sergeevich Nikolsky was born in Saratov, the son of a doctor. He completed his secondary-modern education in 1902 in Petersburg and entered the Petersburg Institute ofCivil Engineers, from the Architectural Section ofwhich he graduated

His main work during the 1930s was a stadium for 100,000 spectators at the point of Krestovsky Island in Leningrad, as part of a large Park of Culture and Leisure complex.

During the war, he worked on the camouflage of a number of military targets in Leningrad, prepared a set of drawings of that city at war and designed a number of memorials such as monuments and arches.

Chapter The leaders of the new direction

During the 1930s and the posar years, Nikolsky was vey active in the Union of Soviet Architects.

## Nikolsky's writings

## Extract from a lecture on the new construction of schools, given at the First Conference of Osa, 1928

'As one of the most important factors in the country's economic life, the construction of schools and cultural educational establishments, including clubs, adult education and pre-school institutions, is very closely conditioned by the present economic situation and the availability of funds. Together, these will determine the direction which building for cultural purposes is to take in the immediate future. It must be carried out in the most closely planned and rigidly economical fashion.

In this connection, and in view of the limited resources at present allocated by the State for the needs of national education, the rationalization of a model school building and a design calculated to bring it up to modern requirements acquire special significance, starting from the planning and construction of the building itself and ending with the provision of special equipment.'

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1928, No.4, p.114)

## Extracts from an article on Natural Lighting for Interiors', 1929

'The question of the natural lighting of interior premises in a multi-storey building, except for industrial purposes, had until now raised no questions and was resolved by the only means possible, with the openings situated in the lateral outer walls. If the nature of the premises required much light, the window space was increased, and vice-versa. As to the distribution of the apertures admitting light, this has followed the set pattern inherited from the brick wall.

... I have posed the question of a rational distribution of lighting areas to suit the needs of one form of inner space or another. The attempt to secure an equal amount of light by means of a smaller, more rationally distributed area of lighting takes the problem somewhat further, since it involves not only rationality, but economy in the use of natural light.

..work on the study and rationalization of natural lighting should not be restricted to the multi-storey structure with its obligatory lighting through lateral outer walls.

Part III/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

On the assumption that the ideal method of illumination of interior space is overhead lighting, a critical study of this possibility must be made in connection with the use of multi-storeybuildings.' (Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1929, No.2, p.57)

## Extracts from an article 'About the Works from My Studio', 1934

'Factors in architectural work such as building materials, structural elements, questions of cost, engineering and technological considerations, public health requirements and functional domestic installations have been subjected to some study and their treatment has, one way and another, been put on a scientific foundation. Architecture as a spatial art, on the other hand, in terms of form and proportions, as an art uniting all its component parts in a harmonious whole, has not yet become a subject for scientific study. ...

A theory of architecture must be created, an architectual grammar must be formulated.

Everybody needs such a grammarbook as a matter of basic information. Yet for us as architects, this is not a case of knowledge but intuition. A systematic grammar is the key to a reading of the past and the future. .

. . . The social and economic circumstances in which we live pe    n chitectural style. .

. . . I would like to dwell . . . on the study of the problem of natural lighting of premises and the changes in the exterior aspect of a building connected with this. This work revealed to me what architectural innovations, what unexpected and varied possibilities, what a broadening of the creative range can open beret i  efm of traditions of every kind.'

Akademiarkhitekury [Academy ofArchectue],1934, Nos.1-2, pp.9293)

## Extracts from an article entitled 'Creative Report', 1935

'We are experiencing years of historical transition in the evolution ofour rchitectural thought and practice

On looking back now one might, of course, claim that the posions taken up were not ideal, the standard o equipment was poor, there was no wholesale co-ordination between the operations of individual units, that internal frictions and quarrels even occurred and that, as a result, the losses were too high. But it is beyond question that the aim and the plan of campaign were correct, that the tactics, generally speaking, met the needs of the situation and that the moral spirit of fighters and commanders was beyond reproach. That is why, as I see it, these honourable positions and glorious battlefields should be respected, the size and significance of the losses should not be exaggerated, and what has been conquered should be favourably assessed.

In short, the architectural trends of the post-Revolutionary era were quite predictably conditioned by economic, social and technological changes.

The struggle of "Leftist architecture" against epigonist apeing, eclecticism and the uncritical adoption of Classical models from West and East was equally predictable and correct.

What was, however, wholly incorrect was the indiscriminate rejection of art in general, together with the architecture of the past, as being ideologically hostile and technologically outdated forms of art. ..

A certain reaction against vulgarization, joyless schematicism and other youthful sins is psychologically understandable. But when as a result of such reaction we are presented with a series of designs and buildings that are overloaded, excessively pompous, eclectic, pseudo-monumental and pseudodecorative, a serious architect can only be moved to regret, even if the "beauteousness" of these buildings continues to impress the public at large for some time to come.'

(Arkhitektura SSSR, 1935, No.4, p.52)

## Extracts from notes written at various times

'A modern artist may not take an "heir" unto himself. The modern architect must bring forth his own children, not take them on temporary lease. Thus, for instance, a House of Culture must grow out of the demands of modern life, and not out of the rejigging of a gambling club and an Imperial Theatre.' (1933)

'The architect must be capable of finding his way without difficulty in all social, political, economic and technical questions of the present time. He must fully take into account territorial, climatic, ethnographic and other conditions in each individual case.Thearchitect must make use oall thertuities offered to him by modern science and technology.' (1933)

'Economy is the first word inscribed on the architect's banner. Economy is the direct meaning of the word  economy in the means of expression etc.'

'As a doctor who examines, auscultates and investigates a patient in an attempt to discover any weak spots, entirely so as to strengthen what is debilitated, cure what is sick and remove whatever is incurable, so I  having received a theme — check the organism of a building for soundness: I look to see whether there are not some sick or ailing places, and whether some requirement may not be dictated by decadent, and therefore unhealthy, traditions. In a word, I take the task in hand and improve it to the best of my ability.'

'Life calls upon the architect to take a creative and active part in its reshaping, to assist in the birth of new types of buildings. It drives architecture forward, in hot pursuit of technology, science and social ideas which have all forged ahead of it.'

(Quoted by A. A.Ol in his essay 'The Creative Views of A.S. i Problems of Soviet Architecture], Leningrad, 1956, pp.85-106)

## Viktor Vesnin (1882-1950)

Viktor Alexandrovich Vesnin was born in Yurevets and graduated from the Moscow Practical Academy, and in 1912 from the Petersburg Institute of Civil Engineers. He worked on a whole series of architectural designs during the years 190814, in conjunction with his brothers Alexander and Leonid. During the First World War and the early years after the Revolution, he worked mainly on his own and specialized increasingly in industrial architecture, with designs for chemical factories in Kineshma and Tambov Province in 1915, the Zhilevsky chemical factory near Moscow in 1916, the building complex of the Great Kineshma Tomna factory in 1916-17, the superphosphate factory at Chernorechensk in Nizhny-Novgorod Province in 1918-19, the sulphuric acid factory in Saratov in 1920-22, and the rosin and turpentine factory in Vakhtan in 1922-24. Features characteristic of Rational Architecture were most prominent in these works by Viktor Vesnin, and this trend not only influenced some of the designs for civil buildings undertaken jointly by the three brothers before the Revolution, but represented one of the sources of Constructivism.

Chapter 2/The leaders of the new direction

Viktor Vesnin began to teach at the Architectural Section of the Moscow Higher Technical Institute - MVTU - in 1923, where, together with Alexander Kuznetsov, he decisively influenced the formation of a Soviet school of industrial architecture.

Vesnin worked in close collaboration with his brothers in 1923-24, but also produced a number of individual designs during this period, among which the most interesting of those actually built were the Institute of Mineral Raw Materials in Moscow in 1925-28, the Agricultural Bank in Ivanovo-Voznesensk in 1926-27 and the Dneproges power station in 1929-30, in collaboration with Nikolai Koll, Georgy Orlov and Sergei Andrievsky.

In the 1930s and 1940s, Viktor Vesnin took a leading part in founding the Union of Soviet Architects and furthering architectural science. He also came to the fore as a prominent social and official personality. From 1932 onwards, he was chairman of the Organizing Committee, and then chief secretary, of the Union of Soviet Architects, as well as one of the organizers - and the first president in 1938-49 - of the Academy of Architecture of the USSR.

## Viktor Vesnin's writings

## Extract from a speech made at the discussion ofthe competition entries for Dneproges, 1929

'I belong to that trend and that group which considers it impossible to draw a line between architectural and engineering elements in a structure. In this case, form is no more than a byproduct, and if we work on the form at all, it is only in the sense of clarifying and perfecting it in processing the data which provides us with the basic, functional solution. . > . •

Quoted by A.G.Chinyakov, in Bratya Vesniny The Vesnin Brothers], Moscow, 1970, p.124

## Concerning the Functional Method

'The widely used old method of phased and fragmented design, eal i the pter lly, the elevation and perspective, is basically unsound.'

The connection between all the elements of a design is indissoluble, and any alteration of one of these invariably involves the alteration of another. ..

Every work of architecture is three-dimensional and one must therefore train oneself to design by working on the plan, sections, elevations and perspective simultaneously, in parallel and multilaterally. One must teach oneself to think not about separate projections and parts, but globally, about the structure as a whole.' (Chinyakov, op. cit. p.90)

Extract from an article "The Language of the Epoch', 1932

'The language of Classical forms, however perfect the latter may be, is the language of the past and cannot be used to express the present.'

(Sovetskoe iskusstvo [Soviet Art], 15 March 1932)

From a speech made at a discussion on art in May 1934

'Too rapid a transition was made in architecture from the slogan "Impermissible" to "Everything is allowed". The transition from ultra-rationalism to ultra-non-rationalism has been too sharp, and yet, as I see it, the majority of architects have gone down this path of unbounded Decorativism. I describe this asShchusism",and it is,in my opinion, a very dang ous phenomenon, maybe even more dangerous than Retrospectivism..

It is said that richness is needed in architecture. Yes, richness is indeed necessary. But what we are being offered is not a richness of our own. It is the richness of the merchant's taste.

Why do we find this richness alien? It is richness withoutul   I  , the richness of make-believe.'

(Arkhitektura SSSR, 1934, No.6, p.6)

## Krinsky (1890-1971)

Vladimir Fedorovich Krinsky was born in Ryazan. After completing his secondary education in Petersburg he studied first at the Art School of the Society for the Promotion of the Arts, then at the Petersburg Academy of Arts, initially as a painter and later as an architect, in 191017.

In 1918, Krinsky worked in the Directorate for the Restoration of Yaroslavl. In 1919, he joined Zholtovsky's architectural studio at the People's Commissariat for Education and became a member of Sinskulptarkh-Zhivskulptarkh at the same time.

He joined the Working Group for Objective Analysis at the end of 1920 and the Working Group of Architects of Inkhuk early in 1921. He headed the United Leftist Studios - Obmas - in Vkhutemas, jointly with Ladovsky and Dokuchaev. After the restructuring of Vkhutemas in 1923 and the creation of the Basic Section - the Introductory Course - Krinsky was in charge of the general teaching of 'Space' as a subject. Krinsky, together with Lamtsov and Turkus, summarized his experiences in teaching this discipline in a manual - Elementy arkhitekturno-prostranstvennoi kompozitsii (Elements of Architectural-Spatial Composition), published in 1934. His teaching work extended over more than half a century, until 1971, bridging the successive transformations of Vkhutemas into Vkhutein, Vasi and the Moscow Architectural Institute.

During the early Soviet years, Krinsky produced a series of competition entries and experimental designs which reflected Symbolist Romanticist tendencies: a crematorium, a kiosk, a temple of the new cult, a Temple of Communion Between Nations, a communal house and a speaker's platform.

During the 1920s and early 1930s, Krinsky, apart from teaching, also turned out a large number of designs, some in collaboration with Rukhlyadev, such as those for the skyscraper of the Supreme Soviet for the National Economy - Vesenkha-in 1922-23; the Arkos building in Moscow, the Soviet pavilion in Paris, the Lenin House of the People in Ivanovo-Voznesensk and the House of Soviets in Bryansk, all in 1924; the Mausoleum to Lenin and the Heroes of the Revolution in Odessa in 1925; a sports arena in 1928; the Columbus Monument in Santo Domingo in 1929; the Palace of Arts, as a member of the Asnova team, in 1930; and the Palace of Soviets in 1931, both of the latter in Moscow.

During the 1930s, in line with the general orientation of Soviet architecture at that period, Krinsky made great use of Classical architectural forms in his designs, such as that for the Komsomolskaya Metro Station, with Rukhlyadev, and the locks on the Moscow-Volga Canal.

As one of the leaders of Rationalism in the 1920s, Krinsky m ui hulate - retical tenets of this trend at the time ofits genesis in the Inkhuk Working Group of Architects.

Krinsky's writings

x W AyTalysCo on anCanh h ry-April 1921

'As distinct from the architect, the engineer only uses material in its unprocessed form and only sets himself a utilitarian task when he devises a structure.'

'A Greek temple is a stone construction and modern architecture perpetrates a lie when it uses the proportions of a stone construction. Such architecture disfigures and destroys the essence of the material.'

'Construction — in the sense of the configuration of a building - incorporates material. I regard a well-defined construction as one that involves the configuration as a whole, while the combination and arrangement of supports represents composition. The conjunction and direction of forces, on the other hand, represents construction.'

There can be no merging of technical, i. e. utilitarian, with artistic construction, in the sense of any theoretical subordination, or of an integration of the latter into the former.

A variety of aims is obvious.

The main principle of construction in making both abstract and utilitarian things is economy.

The constructive principle can be traced in composition as the essence of design. In painting, composition lies along the path to liberation from representation and turns to construction for the basic answer: that is why it is so difficult to separate composition from construction in painting (and sculpture).

In architecture, construction is inevitably present as a technical and mechanical basis - the very basis of composition.'

'Construction implies a movement of force, or a direction, which is schematically expressed by lines. A system oflines is in itself a bare construction and every system of planes or spatial forms, when each of these is represented in motion, is a constructive configuration.

A system of configuration involves the law of interaction between the elements of a construction.

Architectural construction relies upon physical laws of stress and balance which, within a structure, determine the interaction of that construction's components with each other.

(Inkhuk archive)

x   hWT Path of Architecture', May 1921

'Since art differs from nature by the fact that it is of "man" what could be more human in man than thought ...

As to architecture, there is no need to demonstrate that perception  ismpossibleithoutunderstanding thel treatment.

tion, is the architectural idea.

There is no need to demonstrate that the perception ofa particular structure is inseparable from an awareness of its technical efficiency and of its appropriateness and reality at that moment. A disturbance of the harmony between the perception of it as a pure form and the perception ofit as a dwelling, when the latter is absent, makes for unreality and leads to a purely aesthetic perception which is no more than superficial or, as the saying goes, formal. We cannot fully perceive the architectural concept as an answer in this case, because one of the essential conditions of this answer is missing. . . .

And so architecture always has a living theme. Living architecture consists in creating the present: And the architect has one weapon in this creative act — form.

Form is usually described as utilitarian if its purpose is to fulfil a given practical purpose. It is said that the construction engineer's concept is material and that he resorts to a utilitarian form, as opposed to the architect, whose ideals are a matter of art and who employs artistic form..

We cannot conceive of a utilitarian form which would allow no latitude for its treatment as a form. In the majority of cases, an infinity of means is available for altering the given, wholly utilitarian treatment one way or another in terms of form. In other words, every utilitarian treatment is also a treatment of form.

It could hardly be otherwise, since we impart form to everything that we do. Man finds his way about in the "external" world owing to form. It would be impossible to create anything without formulating it, because we could otherwise not understand it.

Our understanding of form depends upon some of its particular qualities. .. . Line, plane and geometrical surface - such as a sphere, a cylinder or a cone — are eternal. They represent the laws of our thought in perception. The means of finding one's direction in the world consist of an order of perception

Part III/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

whic e tablis.That is why we y that thee is o will aly l.

... Is any preconceived idea, determined in advance, in fprh ditions of the task are known and, consequently, before the theme has been stated?

All these aesthetic axioms and preconceptions entering into modern architecture are generally abstract and unreal, since they exist outside real tasks, so it is natural that they should deal with abstract, extra-utilitarian form in general. This form is defined as having only one purpose - its impact on a human being. We have seen that real form also has an impact, since we would otherwise not perceive it. But abstract form has nothing besides its impact. We also know, however, that, just as art, its impact is achieved solely by its "meaning"', i.e. in terms of thought and content. Meaningless beauty is air, sun, whatever animals may perceive. Art is human. What then is resolved by an abstract form? It indubitably has only one aim: the problem of the perception of form in architecture, sculpture and painting comes down to a perception of space. The aim of abstract form is purely theoretical. It is the theory of form in generalized terms. The scientific discipline is to think in space.One cannot apply abstract form to reality, or utilize it. A cube, a cylinder or a cone are not forms, but concepts by means of which we resolve a given form. This is not a predetermined idea. It is our way of speculating about real space. Yet every abstract form resolved represents a formulation of our experience of space. And we are thus forced to recognize that there can be no predetermined idea before a set architectural task has been resolved and its conditions have been studied.

It may happen that an abstract form appears to be impaired by practical application, but this only goes to show that the task has not been solved correctly. An answer obviously results from the conditions, and conditions cannot be made to fit the answer. And to debate what leads the architect to select an answer, whether it is art, i. e. form, or material considerations, i.e. the utilitarian purpose, is like arguing about what came first, the chicken or the egg. Imparting form to real life - that is the architect's sole aim.' (Inkhuk archive)

Extract from Krinsky's 'Credo', December 19213

'Art escapes from the blind alley of aestheticism as from those of humanism and romanticism, by ceasing to be an end in itself. Form is an invention, not a cult. Its value is proved in action.

The abandonment of aestheticism, even if it involves a total rejection of art, is already a sign of health; but pure utilitarianism is no way out either; with it, darkness sets in again, and the concept of bare efficiency is equally meaningless.

I leave aside, as far as possible, everything that is not connected with architecture, assuming that the latter includes the whole cycle of spatial problems connected with the organization of the space that surrounds us. This is, of course, factually indistinguishable from what we call building work, from architecture as a technique.

The architecture which was and is cultivated in academies, and is labelled as art, is alsolodged in the blind alley ofaestheticism.

If we try to establish what leads to this dead end, we shall encounter the main stream of academic training and building practice, both of which originate from the Renaissance.

We must assert that the Renaissance style, as it has been allowed to develop over the years through inertia, is a brake and an enemy to architecture, in so far as it represents an active factor within it.

The Renaissance style can be no more than decoration, and is at best mere romanticism.

The roots of architecture are lost in its case. As manifestaons orchiteture, the Classial ndRenaissance tye fetishes.

It seems to many people that by destroying these fetishes we also destroy the very essence of architecture, and deprive it of all they value most. And one often runs across the honest query: "But what, then, is left?"

What is casual and temporary has become fundamental, kind of eternal value, is a fog that must be dispersed for good, and only by dispersing it will we emerge from our dead end, and our field of action will become enormous.

We must inevitably be a bit barbarian: the work of fine adjustment lies before future generations, while now is the time for basic questions. We are left with much to do, our tasks grow imurably n eica the ei schools, factories, railway stations, power stations, gardens, villages, streets, squares and citWHAT fa, WHAT cities? One cannot retreat from this into the past.

The other basic question which architecture still harbours is - HOW is all this to be done?

In this respec we prod from the basiclaws our perceptionfeturederstanding, and find our way about the space that surrounds us. . ..

Astraight line and a plane; further, the regular arrangement o planes and lines a volume; further,systems of sequenial arrangements of particular distances  rhythm.

In parallel with this, come the essentials ofmaterial conguration - theoretical mechanics and construction.

Questions of form must be worked out architecturally. Form cannot be excluded from technology. Mastering technology without mastering form would mean always remaining a slave to tradition. Pure utilitarianism cannot be established as architecture's guiding thought: that would amount to as much of a fiction as pure art. An assertion that questions of form can be shrugged off during the resolution of utilitarian tasks would imply that the existing form was accepted as axiomatic, i. e that one was in the grip of the identical old aestheticism.

The theory of abstract form is for consideration as a discipline of spatial thinking and points to the way of solving real problems.

Chapter 2/The leaders of the new direction

Abstract solutions of form are self-sufficient, but in terms of life s a whole this is no more than laboratory activity for the formulation of our spatial impressions.

In the last resort, the sole material ofarchitectural composition is our spatial experience.' (Inkhuk archive)

## Architectural associations of the new direction

![](images/11102c4c6a8bd52ba3478b534eae164689b84fef086af2c93b5911c391a78c06.jpg)

## Working Group of Architects in Inkhuk

Set up in March 1921. Members of the group were Ladovsky, Alexander Efimov, Krinsky, Alexander Petrov, Dokuchaev and Mapu.

Extracts from the Programme of the Working Group of Architects', 1921

(1) The Working Group of Architects is engaged in the analysis and synthesis of the elements of architecture as means of expression.

(2) The Group considers the chief, basic elements of architecture to be: space, form and construction.

(3) Its secondary elements and means of expression are recognized as: mass, weight, volume, colour, proportions, movement and rhythm.

(4) Since the essence of architectural solutions comes down to an ordered sequence of spatial quantities, the problem of the space used by architects as their material is by far the most important of its basic problems, and is therefore subject to investigation by the Working Group first and foremost.

(5) Subsequent tasks will be: form and construction; all the remaining elements of architecture will follow later.

(8) The psychology of perception to which the means of architectural expression ultimately appeal cannot be overlooked in the Group's research work.

(9) The Group does not lay down as permanent any one method of research, on the assumption that  given the fundamental proposition that the means of architectural expression are, in essence, means of affecting individual perception - all possible methods and their combined use will be equally applicable and necessary for the investigation of the means of architecture.' (Inkhuk archive)

## Asnova: Association of New Architects

Asnova gradually came together in the course of the development of Zhivskulptarkh (1919-20), Obmas (1920-23) and the Working Group of Architects in Inkhuk (1921-23). Its constitution was registered on 23 July 1923 and its founders were Ladovsky, Dokuchaev, Krinsky, Rukhlyadev, Alexander Efimov, Fidman, Mochalov and Balikhin, together with the engineers Loleit and Kuzmin. The leader of Asnova was Ladovsky. Other members included Bolbashevsky, Borisovsky, Budo, Bunin, Bykova, Varentsov, Volodko, Gelfeld, Zalesskaya, Korzhev, Kruglova, Krutikov, Vitaly Lavrov, Lamtsov, Lissitzky, Melnikov, Viktor Petrov, Valentin Popov, Prokhorova, Silchenkov, Spassky, Travin, Turkus and others. The first and only issue of Izvestiya Asnova, edited by Lissitzky and Ladovsky, appeared in 1926. Ladovsky left Asnova with a group of his followers in 1928, when Balikhin became its leader. It became a sector of Movano in May 1930 and its 'Programmatic-Ideological Position' was published in 1931. Asnova was dissolvedin 1932, along with all other groupings.

## Extracts from the "'Constitution of Asnova', 1923

(1) Asnova pursues the aim of uniting Rationalist architects, and workers of a like mind in the field of architecture and construction, with the intention of raising architecture as an art to a level matching the present state of both technology and science. . ..

(2) In accordance with its aims, the activity of Asnova is divi into () work within the Association, where an exchange of information and experience takes place through lectures, papers, debates, seminars, the organization of courses and schools etc; (b) work outside the Association: a consultations concerning various questions of architecture, town planning and construction in general: b the drafting of architectural structural designs, estimates, costings etc: c the directing of architectural building work on site in accordance with the tasks formulated in para. I of the Constitution: d the area of activity covers the city of Moscow. ...

(The membership of Asnova consists of:

a honorary members,

b ordinary members,

c candidate members.

Honorary members are elected through secret ballot by a General Meting from individuals who may prove useful to Asnova by their activities. .

Only those individuals may become ordinary members who by their ideology and activity support the Rationalist Architects and possess a scientific qualification equivalent to a completed education at higher technical or art college, and sufficient practical experience. ...

.Students attending senior courses at higher educational establishments may only become candidate members.

... Students in their last year at higher educational establishments may become ordinary members of Asnova. . . .

The activities of Asnova are managed by:

a the general meeting of members,

b the Architectural Council,

c the Directorate,

d the Auditing Commission.

..The general meeting consists of honorary, ordinary and candidate members. ..

The Architectural Council consists of the members of Asnova.

To conduct its activities, the Architectural Council elects a Directorate consisting of: the chairman, two of his colleagues and a secretary.' (From Nikolsky's archive)

Extracts from the slogans and statements published in Izvestiya Asnova, 1926

'The USSR is the builder of a new way of life.

The USSR is the collector of class energy for the building of a classless society.

The USSR also sets new tasks for architecture.

The USSR represents rationalized labour in unity with science and highly developed technology.

Asnova considers that its foundation is the material embodiment in architecture of the principles of the USSR.

Asnova considers it urgent that architecture should be armed with the latest equipment and methods of modern science.

Asnova considers that in order to move modern architecture forward, the present, strategic moment requires mutually creative labour on the part of architectural producers on the one hand and the labouring masses of consumers on the other. The practical work of today will culminate in a theoretical system tomorrow.

Asnova, bearing in mind the rate of modern invention, when every passing day confronts the architect with new technological organisms, considers that in the existing situation it is most important to establish generally applicable principles in architecture and rid it of atrophying forms.

Asnova is working on the establishment of a precise and scientific terminology in contemporary architecture, as it considers that this would offer substantial means for its improvement.

For whom is Asnova working? Asnova is working for the mass which demands: a standard architecture, on a par with a motorcar or a shoe; b more from the habitat than the bare equipment required for the fulfilment of natural needs;

cfrom architecture, a rational justification of its features;   
d craftsmanship, not dilettantism.

Who works in Asnova?

All architects who recognize their responsibility towards the icesi vle o tucion,  is tee anarchy.

Al oe l  hi u to the level of art.

All builders of all that is new, whose paths cross that ofarchitecture.' (Izvestiya Asnova, Moscow, 1926, p. 1)

Extracts from the 'Programmatic-Ideological Position of the Asnova Sector', 1931

. The architecture of the epoch of proletarian dictatorship ical factor in the building of socialism.

By actively revolutionizing and shaping the psychology and ideology of the proletarian masses, architecture must intensify their creative initiative and activity on the path to socialist construction by creating an environment favourable to creative activity. Such a view of architecture obliges one, when engaged in the resolution of concrete tasks of socialist construction, not to be content to serve the process of production and ordinary life by elementary planning in space, and not to allow material spatial form to serve in a purely passive way, but to create  by the full conjunction of artistic and spatially plastic qualities and images - a powerful organizing stimulus for the ideology of the masses. ...

.. . Proletarian architecture, when it is resolving the tasks set by the reconstruction of productivity and everyday life, must provide a dialectical synthesis of the economic, technical, plastic and ideological factors....

Part III/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

.. . The plastic and ideological expressiveness of architecture should not be regarded as an external addition, an ornament or a luxury which may or may not be permissible depending on economic circumstances: proletarian architecture must be penetrated through and through by plastic and ideological expressiveness in all conditions and in every concrete situation; its plastic and ideological effect must be organically grounded in the conjunction of the essential parts of an architectural whole.

No quality is absolute, no model is supreme, and no standard achieves utmost excellence, but the highest quality must be attained by a perfect organization of all that the architecture conveys in the given concrete conditions and situation.

.. . as part of the resolution of any concrete task, the class ideological function of architecture must be organically incorporated on equal terms into the complex of conditions determining the architectural form.

## The features o poletarian tecue

Socialist planning determines the completeness of the dialectical synthesis in terms of economics, technology, plasticity, and ideology. In its turn, this synthesis determines the highest degree of integration of all the elements of proletarian architecture.

T the following considerations:

(1) the unity of plastic form and productive functions, i.e. form rationally determines the productive or domestic process, organically corresponds to it and supports it psychologically;

(2) the unity of the plastic features of form and the technological construction from which the construction gains expression, The construction becomes a factor of a powerful class ideological impact;

(3) the plastic unity of all the elements of the architectural ensemble, fromindividual structures to.thecomplex  scialist city;

(4) an active plastic connection between architectural form and its environment (architecture and nature);

(5) the organic conformity of the architectural form's plastic features with the form of pedestrian traffi passing by and the nature of individual perception of architectural form, as well as the position and role of such form within the general system of the architectural ensemble concerned. This imposes the obligation of adopting a particular type of composition as a solution based on principle in each actual case;

(6) dynamism as a plastic feature, whereby the higher degrees of ideological expressiveness can be achieved and active orientation can be imparted to an architectural complex;

(7) tension as the apex of plastic activity, to stimulate creativeactivity and arouse a feeling of cheerfulness, clarity,assurance, strength and joy;

(8) deliberate asymmetry as a principle of composition, on the strength of which the supreme unity of a complex of architectural objects can be achieved in full accordance with the impetus and scale of the processes of socialist production;

(9)a deliberate organic synthesis ofarchitecture with figurative sculpture and painting, aimed at intensifying the plastic and ideological power of expression of architecture, so as to fill it with images of revolutionary struggle and construction.

The degree of principle and fullness of treatment supplied by the tuov ter ih thy, novelty, grandeur and wisdom of the compositional approach, determine the fullness and force of the proletarian architecture's ideological power of expression.'

(Sovetskaya arkhitektura, 1931-32, Nos.1-2, pp.46-47)

## Osa Union of Contemporary Architects

Osa was founded at the end of 1925, with Alexander Vesnin as chairman, Ginzburg and Viktor Vesnin as deputy chairmen, and Orlov as secretary. The membership of Osa included Barshch, Andrei Burov, Vegman, Leonid Vesnin, Vladimirov, Gan, Ilya Golosov, Nikolai Krasilnikov, Komarova, Leonidov, Loleit, Nikolsky, Nikolaev, Nikolai Sokolov, Sobolev, Fisenko, Kholostenko, Khiger, Yalovkin and others.

The periodical Sovremennaya arkhitektura was published by as editors.

The First Osa Conference was held in April 1928.

The First Osa Congress was held in May 1929.

In 1930, Osa became a sector of Movano, was reorganized as Sass-Sektor Arkhitektorov Sotsialisticheskogo Stroitelstva (Sector of Architects for Socialist Construction) -and was dissolved in 1932.

Extracts from the statement of the Os oup the Aat Sectiohe Moco February 1926

the proposed organization, as distinct from those already in eise wl nirchit  o worker th whom they are linked, on the Left Front of architecture.

The Union proposes to resolve and implement collectively, wcrucvcrylicspliciyu vance, all the architectural constructional problems arising from the present times, the features of socialist construction in the Republic and the general tempo of the country's productive life.

.. the Union of Contemporary Architects proposes to include in its membership not only professional architects, but also individuals closely connected with building production and united with us by a common ideology. ...'

192193g. Dokumentymaterialy, Moscow, 1970, p.68

Extract from Osa's statement to the Art Department,

Chief Administration for Science,

People's Commissariat for Education, 1926

we think it necessary to point to the following substantial differences.

The Moscow Architectural Society includes a large number of architects with diverse ideologies and, for this reason, the ideology of modern architecture essential for the expression of socialist tasks cannot be formulated within it with sufficient clarity.

Osa brings together people closely bound by a single ideology and conducts collective-theoretical scientific research and practical work on a well-defined plane in a struggle against inertia and survivals from the past.

Asnova is the union of a small group of architects with a definite ideology, the essence of which is the quest for an abstract form in purely aesthetic terms.

Osa collectively resolves, and implements in practice, the new architectural form functionally derived from the purpose of a given structure, its materials, construction and other production conditions, and thereby responds to the concrete tasks set by the socialist development of the country.'

(Iz istorii sovetskoi arkhitektury..op.cit., p.69)

CAaln the

Editorial on The Tenth Anniversary of October', 1927

'There is no other field of artistic labour in which the events of Ocr uc  eciive ticl  eol change as in architecture.

The October Revolution embodied the significance of a new historical phenomenon of unprecedented sweep and scale and was destined to demolish by its very nature the outdated principles of pre-Revolutionary architecture.

Once it had abolished private property, the Revolution threw open to Soviet architects prospects of grandiose planned work, work on the formulation of new models, new architectural organisms, new complexes and sets of buildings, instead of the narrowly individualistic tasks dictated by the pre-Revolutionary customer.

The abolition of private ownership of land provided the widest possible opportunities for the new planning of cities and settlements, while the generally positive and creative trends introduced by October brought forth the idea of Constructivism as an idea of life-building and life-organizing labour, in place of the ornamental and decorative varieties of old art.

The first phase in the development of architectural Constructivism was negative, in line with the initial stage of post-October culture as a whole. Its cutting edge was set to the struggle against layers of inertia and atavism, and to the destruction of surviving traditions, whose tendrils were greedily grasping at the shoots of innovative thought.

The second, positive phase of Constructivism - in parallel with the economic growth of the Soviet Union and the constructive phase of our entire national economy - has already clearly left its mark on the movement's creative activity, by clarifying for it the purposive involvement of the Soviet architect in the establishment of the social condensers of our era, an involvement which sets our Constructivism apart from all Leftist trends and groupings in Western Europe and America.

In step with the sweeping increase in the rate of construction work in the Soviet Union, Constructivism is opening up for itself, on the tenth anniversary of October, prospects of strenuous work in connection with the creation of a high level of new architecturalclturen thebashis -publicpuroulne, coverii equal measure questions of art and questions of utilitarian technology, so as to solve them monistically, by the total fusion of new content and new form. This tenth anniversary must become

Part III/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

the date marking the completeliquidation of pre-Revolutionary eclecticism and retrospectivism, the date of the intensive development of genuinely new construction which will make use of all the grandiose opportunities that the October Revolution offers it.' (Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1927, Nos.4-5, p.111)

Extracts from the resolutions concerning the reports

othe Ideological Section adopted at the First Osa Conference, April 1928

'The First Conference of the Union of Contemporary Architects stresses its complete unanimity and adopts the ideological position and production programme of Constructivism in architecture.

.. .Constructivism evolves methodological ways and means of social-artistic labour, and creates new manifestations of it to counter aspects of idealistic art surviving from the past and prevailing even now.

we, as Constructivists, contrast the passive execution of a commission on the basis of old pre-October types of architecture with the study of the socially domestic and productive mutual relationships of the post-October era, and of their active embodiment in a series of new types of architecture on the basis of comprehensively planned socialist construction.

We set the new type of communal dwelling, the new type of club, Palace of Labour, Executive Committee building, factory etc, which are to become the conductors and condensers of socialist culture, against the pre-Revolutionary types of the "investment property", the private house, the "Noble Assembly" building etc, which resulted from pre-Revolutionary social, economic and technological conditions, but serve to this day as a basis for the architecture that is being erected in the USSR.

Against the passive use of old, archaic materials, structures and methods of construction, which arose in the circumstances of a socio-economic system that is now defunct in our terms; which were the heritage of our pre-Revolutionary destitution and inertia and which now hamper the rate and quality of the new socialist culture's growth, we set the determined conquest of our backwardness; the active and scientific acquisition of all the achievements of worldwide technology in the field of the latest materials, structures, mechanization and standardization of building production; and the planned introduction of all these achievements into our everyday building practice, due account being taken of the USSR's special economic features. In matters of form, we equally categorically reject:

(1) the ignorant attitude of builders and engineers alien to questions of the socially artistic quality of architecture;

(2) the unprincipled eclecticism of the "decorators" of architecture, who clothe every kind of architectural social content in the garb of ready-made styles from the past;

(3) abstract forays into new form divorced from the social purpose of architecture and any realistic means of execution;

(4) naive dilettantism seeking to reflect one or another outlook on the world by means of decorative architectural form;

(5) work in the so-called "new style", using the elements of the new architecture as means of "contemporizing" and prettifying essentially outdated structures.

We set against all this the organic growth of Soviet architecture, which arises from the specific features peculiar to the new social model and the technically perfected methods of building production.

We resolve questions concerned with the effect—ideological, emotional etc-upon the user, not by the addition of decorative elements, but by:

(1) the very system governing the structuring of the new social model, the clarity of the articulation of social domestic functions of various kinds and the general sense of purpose of the entire architectural organism;

(2) the highest standard of quality of all elements and structural parts without any exception, as required by their social and technical purposes;

(3) the employment of all the specific features of various elements ofarchitecture, such as plane, volume, spatial relationship, scale, texture, light etc, while treating one or other of their properties, not as a self-sufficient abstract value, but as a continuously variable quantity, resolved anew in each case, depending on one or other of the conditions governing the aim, purpose and concrete possibilities of execution involved.

Against the work by architects in the National Republics of the Soviet Union which amounts to resurrecting the national styles of the old bourgeois culture, we advance work that is direc  ng ly ue tk in the individual republics, on the basis ofa radical transformation of social class relationships.

As to questions of new architecture in the West, we note lements of kinship between our work and the area of activity of the most progressive architects of the West in which the highest achievements of world science and technology have taken effect, and our complete divergence with regard to the purposive approach of our architecture, a divergence which manifests most vividlyhewhoffenctenhegs cl ur  he pl Wn the  c i in proletarian country engaged in building socialism.'

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1928, No.3, p.78)

Housing, 1928

'The First Conference of the Union of Contemporary Archiand construction of new housing to change from individual flats to new communal dwellings with a clear distribution of individual and communalized functions, aimed at the greatest possible communalization and collectivization of the processes of everyday life.

In the course of this work, the new architect must take close acunt o the need to raise the socal quality of the housing intended to promote the organization and progress of the new way of live, but also of the need for the lowest possible cost of housing, its further reduction by all available means, and the improvement of its efficiency.

In terms of the planning of populated areas, we set against the chaotic, random building work in cities, and against the act of planning in accordance with predetermined schemes derived from a graphic starting point, or from long since outdated ways of life, the thorough study of a city's functions, not in their static condition, but in terms of a city in motion, in its dialectic, taking account of its maximum possibilities of further development. We contemplate the planning of a new city only by taking account of all the factors bearing on a city's character and role, of the commitment to the socialist reconstruction of the country and of work on the selection of materials and structures that will ensure elasticity and the possibility of the most painless changes in planning both of the city as a whole and of individual buildings.'

(Sovremennaya arkhitektura, 1928, No.4, p.123)

Chapter 3/Architectural associations of the new direction

Extracts from the Sass theses At a New Stage, 1931

() The Osa slogans were directed along four lines: a Creative-methodological;

b Design;

cConstructional-technological;

d Socialist settlement.

The six years during which Osa has been in existence have tested the viability of these slogans and shown what was correct, and what was incorrect in them. ...

(4) It is however as clear to Osa as ever that an architectural construction must result from the study of the processes of everyday life; social and production processes; the technological and static properties of building materials; the means of building production in the context of the economy of the reconstruction period; and the socialist reorganization of production and the way of life in the given economy.

The new content of architecture must derive from and be determined by the socialist reorganization of production, culture and the way of life. The new form of architecture, being the outcome of the spatial reorganization of social processes, must derive from and be determined by a switch from the existing method of building to a standardization and typification of buildings which would make it possible to go over from seasonal construction work to mechanized assembly all year round. ...

(5) Osa considers that the slogan of the Party and working class: "To catch up with and overtake in a technological-economic respect the leading capitalist countries" can only be fulfilled given the greatest possible use of engineering in architecture and the provision of a modern scientific technological basis for the latter. . ..

(6) Osa is against the view of architecture as "an art of images" propagated by Vopra and AKhR; Osa is for architecture as organized scientific activity. ...

(8) In the field of architectural design, Osa has fought, on the basis of the Functional Method, for socially new types of buildings, for new economic principles governing design in all aspects of building work and for the most rapid possible reconstruction of the way of life in accordance with socialist principles:

a for an economic dwelling corresponding to the new socialist conditions and forms of labour, leisure and everyday life;

Part III/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

b for new public buildings maximally conducive to the organization and intensification of the activity of the labouring masses and their level of culture;

c for a socialist type of industrial building that will provide the workers with the maximum facilities in both production and sanitary respects, and the proletariat with the maximum scope for creativity, initiative and self-help;

d for the socialist planning of population settlement on the basis of Marxist-Leninist theory. ...

(14) Osa, which regards questions of socialist settlement as vances the following propositions.

During the pre-reconstruction period, when it was a matter of re-establishing the wrecked economy, it was obviously not possible to raise questions concerning a new location for industry, or socialist settlement, or socialist aspects of economy and service in general . . .

(15) the reconstruction period set entirely new tasks to the architect intent on fighting for the building socialism

The task had now become no less than the creation of an integrated s tt whuha cialism...

(36) the basic feature of the socialist economic servicing of dwellings is the network, district, non-domestic character of such service .

(38) all aspects of economic and cultural supply must, of course, be included in the system of public service networks within an area. The following are essential:

a road and transport network;

b communications network - post, telegraph and radio;

c network of dwellings;

d food supply network;

e public health network;

f sewage network;

g consumer goods distribution network;

h socialist education network -childrens' and pre-school services;

i network of polytechnical eduaction;

jcultural and socio-political service network;

k physical training and tourist service network;

I medical service network - surgeries, hospitals, sanatoriums and spas.

A fully developed system of socialist settlement implies the creation and full operation of the entire system of network services . . .

(48) In all these links of the habitat system, the basic and first task of socialist settlement will be a drive for the complete elimination of individual private domestic arrangements and servicing, as being the most backward and primitive - and hence an unproductive waste of human energy.

The socialist dwelling may remain an individual dwelling, but it must not represent an individual domestic economy.'

(Sovetskaya arkhitektura, 1931, Nos.1-2, pp.97-101)

## ARU: Union of Architect-Planners

The first organizational meeting of ARU at which it adopted its First Declaration, was held on 7 November 1928, with Ladovsky as chairman, plus Fridman, Grinberg and Glushchenko. When published, the Declaration listed as founders of ARU: Ladovsky, Fridman, Zazersky, Vitaly Lavrov, Glushchenko, Grinberg, Krutikov, Sergei Lopatin, Saishnikov, Sakulin, the public health physicians Nekrasov and Kovalev, and the economist Zhmudsky. Other members of ARU were: Barutchev, Gilter, Grinshpun, Iosif Dlugach, Evgeny Iokheles, Kalmykov, Kaplun, Meerzon, Osipov, Valentin Popov, Rubanchik, Alexander Shevtsov and others.

According to the ARU Constitution, members of the group had to have a higher education course in architecture or construction to their credit. A temporary Presidium of ARU, with Ladovsky as chairman and Vitaly Lavrov as secretary, was formed in December 1928, and 'Rules for Conducting Work in ARU on the Drafting of Commissioned Designs and Participation in Paid and Unpaid Competitions' - which provided, among all else, for internal competitions to develop design ideas— were also drawn up then. The Permanent Directorate of ARU was elected in October 1929 with Ladovsky as chairman, and Fridman and Zazersky, as well as Vitaly Lavrov from April 1930 onwards, as his deputies. From August 1930, ARU became a sector of Movano. The Second Declaration of ARU was adopted and published in 1931. There was also an ARU group in Leningrad. Along with all other groupings, ARU was dissolved in 1932.

Extracts from the First Declaration of ARU, 1928

'(1) The general rate of socialist construction and economic deeffect on the growth of towns.

The essential characteristic determining the character of the modern town is the intensive growth of is dynamism

(2) The basic features of a town in the socialist system, which differentiate it from the theoretical and practical conceptions chiefly current in the West, derive from the determination to destroy all social inequality within the population; the simplification and ultimate withering away of the class structure of society; the nationalization of land and the elimination in this connection of land rents and opportunities for speculation in land, all of which throw open a broad path to the rational replanning and development of existing towns. ..

(3) In connection with this, it has now become necessary to p pricuttenin  theural  e tion and development.

.. . Modern man is consistently obliged to operate within an architectural environment. The architectural structures of a city, quite freely perceived, will have a direct impact on the feelings of a "consumer" of architecture and call forth specific perceptions of the surrounding world by their aspect and forms. The Soviet State, which puts planned regulation first among its the psyche of the masses.

A definite direction must be imparted to the impact of architualje omey arpabil ities.

(4) Architecture, understood as a single spatial continuum, must resolve not merely the task of designing individual structures, but connect all groups of structures into a single spatial system within which the individual structures represent no more than parts of a more general architectural entity. Such an interpretation of town planning provides the only reliable starting point for dealing with architectural tasks as systems designed to produce a psychological and ideological impact on the social unity of a city. These considerations make it necessary, when defining a particular architectural task for the design of an individual structure, first to resolve the more general task of architecturally systematizing the urban entity.

(5).. .The Union of Architect-Planners (ARU) sets itself the task

Chapter 3/Architectural associations of the new direction

lat lcnheol

(6) . .ARU considers it essential to put forward the following initial questions for comprehensive theoretical and practical study:

a the planning and architectural design of cities as a social and psychological factor in the education of the masses;

b the planning and architectural design of cities as the prime organizational form of bringing architecture to life, in connection with the elaboration of modern principles for the regulation of form in architectural planning;

c the general concept of the spatial design of a city and its linkage with therchitecture oindividual structures;

d the need to create a specialized higher educational establishment for the study of the entire body of questions connected with town planning, and a comprehensive statement of aim for the organization of a city in a socialist system;

e the development and organization of existing courses concerned with town planning in specialized higher educational establishments and the introduction of a course on urbanism in non-specialist establishments;

f the creation of a special organ for the statement and formulation of theoretical problems and for information concerning practical town-planning work in the USSR, Western Europe and America.'

(ArkitkturaVhutein [Architcure andutin], First Issue, January 1929, p.8)

## Extracts from the Second Declaration of ARU, 1931

() . . . In the conditions of socialist construction, the work of architectural organizations which treat individual structures as parts of a whole is appropriate. ARU is the first architectural union organized in accordance with this principle.

(2) . . . The correct resolution of modern tasks of socialist settlement can be achieved on the basis of the scientific study of questions connected with urban construction.

(4) ARU regards town planning as a scientific discipline the aim of which is to discover the general rules governing the organization of human settlements in the different forms and developments they adopt, and to establish laws on the strength of which planned forecasting of the processes leading to their formation would become possible, and the random nature of this

Part III/Masters and trends: biographies, statements, manifestos

process would be overcome, i.e. it would be switched to the rails of socialist planning.

. A definition of the paths of development of socialist settlement must be based on a dialectic consideration of the factors acting as town-generating principles, while differentiating between these factors in the light of conditions operating at each particular place and time. .

(5) ARU regards as incorrect attempts to define the development of a city in terms of the application and implementation of a single organizational form of settlement, since a solution of the problem of socialist settlements cannot be based exclusively on a single particular principle.

ARU considers it essential to differentiate between varieties of settlements by highlighting the specific features peculiar to a given variety and to proceed from the combination of factors that determine each individual variety of settlement.

ARU stresses the differential significance of inhabited localities and the need for a differentiated approach to the stimuli and opportunities for their development; and considers that it is necessary to create organisms that are flexible in social and spatial terms, capable of evolutionary growth within the limits of the given social system and capable of orderly reconstruction.

In accordance with this stated position, it is opportune to raise the question of the typological classification of inhabited localities. On its own, the typification and fabrication of single dwellings, or even separate dwelling complexes or districts, carried out in a number of design and construction organizations, cannot bring about a radical solution of the question of the rationalization of planning, in view of the flawed character of the method applied to the work. According to this method, the question is resolved starting from the particular — a house taken in isolation, or a district —and proceeding to the general an inhabited location - no account being taken of the qualitative dfferences in the ructure of such a inhabited loliy, which depend upon the number of elements of which the locality is composed.

(6) The Soviet State, which sets planned regulation as the chief cornerstone of its activity, must also use architecture as a powerful means for the psycho-ideological organization of the masses. . .

(7) . . . ARU considers that among the questions which an quence, first place must be given to the social human being in his full, modern, class concreteness. One may provisionally differentiate, within the whole set of questions which an architectplanner is called upon to consider, between those concerning spatial organization, and those concerning spatial composition. It has to be stressed that the first category of questions need not be resolved by the architect acting as a specialist, while the participation of an architect is essential in the case of the second category of questions. The degree of priority of these two categories varies in each actual case. In one case, questions of the organization of space may be placed first. In another, questions relevant to the second category may be of decisive significance. But in either case, both these principles must be considered as part of a complex and not in isolation.'

(Sovetskaya arkhitektura, 1931, Nos.1-2, p.19-20)

## Vopra: All-Union Proletarian Architects' Association

Vopra was organized in 1929 and published a declaration signed by, as founder members, Alabyan, Dmitry Babenkov, Baburov, Vlasov, Elegont Deryabin, Nikolai Zapletin, Zaslavsky, Zilbert, Ivanov, Kozelkov, Kochar, Alexander Krestin, Kryukov, Mikhail Kupovsky, Mazmanyan, Matsa, Mikhailov, Mordvinov, Polyakov, Terekhin, Simbirtsev, Grigory Solodovnik and Avraam Faifel. Its directorate comprised Matsa as chairman, Zaslavsky as deputy chairman, Baburov as secretary, as well as Kozelkov, Mordvinov, Simbirtsev and Deryabin.

Vopra's largest organization was located in Moscow, where ibertyebers  0hil bs were set up in the Ukraine, Georgia, Armenia, Leningrad and Tomsk. Vopra teams took part in many open competitions during the period 1930-31. Documents emanating from Vopra sharply criticized the Osa Constructivists, the Asnova Raalis ormalistthe ececticsts an stylizers in Mao all of whose work was regarded as failing to meet the class requirements of the proletariat.

Vopra was dissolved in 1932.

Extracts from the Vopra Declaration, 1929

'At all stages of class society, architecture has served the interests of the ruling classes.

The architecture of the capitalist era is characterized by decorativeness and a recourse to complacent formalism and technicism.

In the USSR, in conditions of proletarian revolution and the building osocialism, architecture stil emains in thegroov of bourgeois art: Constructivism, Formalism and, especially, eclecticism are currently the dominating tendencies in architecture. These trends, identical with the same trends in the capitalist West, have altered their outward appearance and adapted themselves to our circumstances under the impact of our reality, but have remained essentially alien to it.

At the same time, proletarian architecture is arising from the economic foundations of the transitional period.

We reject eclectic architecture and the methods of the eclectics, who mechanically copy the old architecture and blindly subject themselves to Classical canons and schemas. Eclecticism was especially widespread in the architecture of the period of mercantile industrial capital. ...

We reject Formalism in architecture, which made its appearance during the period of developed industrial capitalism. . . . Formalism was the consequence ofa collision between the psycho-ideology and habits of the petit-bourgeois intelligentsia with the monopolistic tendencies of the period of financial capitalism. .

We recognize the positive historical role of Constructivism (as indeed of Formalism) in the process of overcoming eclecticism and the mindless application of technological routine, in the promotion of questions concerning the rationalization, mechanization and standardization of construction, in propagandizing industrial technology etc. But we note at the same time that, in the conditions prevailing in the USSR, though Constructivism achieved positive results in terms of the critique of eclecticism and pre-industrial forms of architecture, it has nevertheless proved unable in its own theory and practice to go beyond mere Leftist mouthing and "revolutionary" clichés.

We reject Constructivism, which has arisen on the basis of fi-

Chapter 3/Architectural associations of the new direction

nnalcapialThendamental ature opolistcitalism  a bias in favour of capitalist planned arrangements, rationalization and powerful industrialization - have determined the nature of this architecture. ...

We reject every sort of attempt to blur the class role of architecture and to saddle the proletariat with an architecture unconnected with class, such as could only be realized in the conditions of a communist society. We consider that in the period of the dictatorship of the proletariat and the struggle for the socialist rearrangement of the world, architecture must belong to class in its content and form, that it must . .  participate in the class struggle on the side of the fighting proletariat by all the means at its disposal.

We consider that proletarian architecture must develop its thy ep he of dialectical materialism. ..

We are in favour of a proletarian class architecture, of a constructively and formally integrated art which shows vitality and organizes the will of the masses for struggle and labour. We are against a divorce of form from content and form from construction: we are in favour of their organic unity. For us, form is not a canon, nor an abstract symbol or construction  not an aim in itself, but the means of expressing a concrete significance. An architect must, however, achieve formal, compositional and constructional mastery. We are in favour of assimilation of the culture of the past, but on the basis of Marxist methods of analysis and of the critical use of historical experience in the process of creating a proletarian architecture; not its imitation or mechanical copying as in eclecticism. We are in l cess of creating a proletarian architecture. We are in favour of a dialectial use and application to architectural creativity of all achievements of modern science bearing on form, colour etc.

And so our method in architecture is based upon a comprehensive view of the object of architecture, upon taking account of the largest possible number of elements which make up architecture in their interconnections, contradictions and reciprocal effects upon each other, in social-economic, emotionalideological, constructive-technical and other terms.'

(Stroitelstvo Moskay, 1929, No.8, pp.25-26)

The following abbreviations have been used:

arch. architect (This concept covers the following diploma classifications: architect, architect-artist and civil engineer)

art, - artist

eng. - engineer

eng,-art. - engineer-artist

grad. - graduate

AKh - Academy of Arts

Api - Azerbaidzhan Polytechnical Institute (Architecture and Building Faculty, founded in 1920)

Egu - Erevan State University (included an Architecture Section in its Technical Faculty)

First SGKhM -First Free State Art Studios in Moscow (formed in 1918 from the Stroganov Industrial Art College - Skhpu)

Ivpi -Ivanovo-Voznesensk Polytechnical Institute (formed in 1918 from the Riga Polytechnical Institute - RPI - evacuated to Moscow)

Khisi -Kharkov Institute of Constructional Engineers (founded in 1930)

Khkhi - Kharkov Art Institute (formed in 1927 from the Kharkov Art Technical College - Khkht)

Khkht - Kharkov Art Technical College (formed in 1921 from the Kharkov Art College)

Kkhi - Kiev Art Institute (Architecture Faculty founded in 1924)

Lakh - Leningrad Academy of Arts (formerly Petrograd Academy of Arts - Pakh)

Ligi -Leningrad Institute of Civil Engineers (formerly Petrograd Institute of Civil Engineers - Pigi)

Liiks-Leningrad Institute of Communal-Construction Engineers (formed from Ligi in 1931)

Mai (Markhi) - Moscow Architectural Institute (formed in 1933 from Vasi)

MGU - Moscow State University

Migi - Moscow Institute of Civil Engineers (formed in 1922 from the Moscow Polytechnical Institute - MPI)

MP1-Moscow Polytechnical Institute (formed in 1918 from the Polytechnical Institute for Women)

Muzhvz - Moscow College of Painting, Sculpture, and Architecture (founded in 1832 and formed in 1918 into the Second Free State Art Studios - SGKhM)

MVTU - Moscow Higher Technical Institute (Architecture Faculty)

Oii - Odessa Institute for Figurative Art (formed from the Odessa Art Institute)

Okhi - Odessa Art Institute (formed in 1922 from the Higher Art College - Okhu)

Okhu - Odessa Art College, founded in 1889, 1918-20 known as the Higher Art College

Pakh - Petersburg (later Petrograd) Academy of Arts (founded in 1757)

PI - Polytechnical Institute

Pigi - Petersburg (later Petrograd) Institute of Civil Engineers (founded 1832)

Piips - Petersburg Institute of Communications Engineers

RPI - Riga Polytechnical Institute (founded in 1896, and included a Building and Architecture Faculty

Second PPI - Second Petrograd Polytechnical Institute

Skhpu - Stroganov Industrial Art College, Moscow (founded 1825)

Vasi - Higher Institute for Architecture and Construction, Moscow (founded in 1930 on the basis of the Vkhutein Architectural Faculty)

Vkhpi - Vitebsk Artistic Practical Institute (formed in 1921-23 from the Vitebsk Art Studios)

Vkhutein - Higher State Artistic Technical Institute, Moscow (formed in 1927 from the Higher State Artistic Technical Studios (Vkhutemas) in Moscow)

Vkhutemas - Higher State Artistic Technical Studios, Moscow (formed as a result of the merger in 1920 between the First and Second Free State Art Studios — SGKhM)

Note: Numbers in italics refer to captions to the illustrat
